• BCO 12-5.d See 28-72, ffl, 4, p. 281

PART TWO
MINUTES, TWENTY-EIGHTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY
First Session - Tuesday Evening
June 20, 2000

28-1 Assembly Called to Order and Opening Worship
The Twenty-Eighth General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America
gathered for the opening worship service at 7 30 PM on Tuesday, June 20, 2000 in the
Ballroom at the Tampa Bay Convention Center, Tampa, Florida

Prelude Prelude and Fugue (St. Anne) in E-l 'Jat Major - js bach
Calling of the Assembly to Order “O Man, Bewail Thy Grevious Fall’' js bach
Call to Worship From Psalm 100
Trio Praise Father, Son and Holy Ghost
O enter then His gates with joy, within His courts His praise proclaim
Let thankful songs your tongues employ O bless and magnify His name
Because the Lord our God is good His mercy is forever sure His truth at all
times firmly stood And shall from age to age endure
Praise God from Whom all blessings flow, Praise Him all creatures here
below Praise Him above ye heavenly host Praise Father. Son and Holy Ghost
Amen
Assembly Praise God from Whom all blessings flow Praise Him all creatures here
below Praise Him above ye heavenly host Praise Father, Son and Holy Ghost
Trio Praise Father, Son and Holy Ghost Amen

Invocation - In Unison
Holy, holy, holy, LORD God Almighty, Who was and is and is to com e1 You
are worthy, O Lord, to receive glory and honor and power, for You created all things,
and by Your will they exist and w-ere created You are the Alpha and the Omega, the
Beginning and the End, who is and who was and who is to come, the Almighty You are
the one who rules the nations with a rod of iron and shall one daybreak into pieces all
nations and peoples who refuse Your sovereign sway
You are worthy, O Lord, to take the scroll of history and to open its seals, for
You were slain, and have redeemed us to god by your blood out of every tribe and
tongue and people and nation, and have made us kings and priests to our God, and we
shall reign on the earth
Worthy is the Lamb who was slain to receive power and riches and wisdom and
strength and honor and glory and blessing! Blessing and honor and glory and power be
to Him who sits on the throne, and to the Lamb, forever and ever Amen! Blessing and
glory and wisdom, thanksgiving and honor and power and might, be to our God forever
and ever Amen!

<a id="ga28-p20"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=22 printed_page=20 -->

All praise we would render, 0 help us to see, ‘tis only the splendor of light hide th
Thee May we bask in Your effulgent glory as we worship You with one heart and mind
and voice, even as You sanctify our minds and hearts and wills Teach us to pray from
our hearts, even as Jesus taught His disciples to pray, saying .
The Lord’s Prayer
Hymns of Praise to God Holy, Holy, Holy Hymn 100
O Father, You Are Sovereign Hymn 75
The Reading of and Meditation upon the Law of God Exodus 20:1-7
Corporate Confession of Sin - A Puritan’s Prayer
Assurance of Pardon Romans 3:19-28
Chorus of Praise o fora thousand tongues to sing
Confession of Faith - A Westminster Creed
Hymn of Preparation be thou my vision Hymn 642
Scripture Reading Matthew 18 1-10
Introduction of the Speaker RE Thomas F Leopard, Retiring Moderator
Sermon “Chainsaw Repentance” TE Joe Novenson
pastor of lookout mountain pc A, Lookout Mountain, TN
Offertory Hark' A Voice Saith, all are mortal - J S. Bach
Trio - Medley of Communion Hymns Don Krafft, Ron Phillips and Eric Dickinson
Holy Communion - words of institution TE David McWilliams
pastor of covenant pc A, Lakeland, Florida
Psalter Selection 51
Hymn of Meditation rock of ages Hymn 500
Benediction
Postlude Prelude in B Minor - J S. Bach

Following worship, the Assembly recessed for 15 minutes at 9 55 PM.

28-2 Declaration of Quorum and Enrollment
Moderator RE Tom Leopard reconvened the Assembly for business at 10:10 PM
with prayer byte frank barker the moderator declared a quorum present with 872
Teaching Elders and 416 Ruling Elders

28-3 Adoption of the Docket
The Fourth draft of the docket was declared adopted with the following
amendments: the addition of a partial report of the bills and overtures committee
immediately preceding the report of the Standing Judicial Commission (presumably
Wednesday, June 21, 2000 at 2:00 PM), and of a partial report of the bills and
overtures committee immediately following the report of the committee of
Commissioners on Covenant College (presumably Thursday, June 21, 2000 at 9:45 AM)

<a id="ga28-p47"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=49 printed_page=47 -->

28-4 Election of Moderator
the moderator opened the floor for nominations for moderator of the 28th
General Assembly TE Edmund Clowney placed in nomination TE Joseph “Skip” Ryan
TE Joseph Pipa placed in nomination TE Morton Smith In accordance with RAO 1-4,
ballots were distributed
The Assembly sang “The Spacious Firmament on High” . TE Morton Smith was
elected Moderator 502-391 The Vice-Chairman of the administrative Committee, RE
William “Bingy” Moore, presented to the retiring Moderator RE Tom Leopard a plaque
in token of the Assembly’s appreciation for his year of service as moderator

28-5 point of information
during the counting of the ballots, RE Michael Proffitt asked permission to
announce the availability of a “voters guide” with biographical sketches of men who
would be nominated from the floor. retiring moderator leopard ruled this out of order
in that the only documents before the Assembly are printed in the Handbook and all floor
nominations are accompanied by biographical sketches provided by the Nominating
committee as apart of their report. Upon challenge, the Moderator’s ruling was
sustained

28-6 Election of Recording and Assistant Clerks
On nomination by the Stated Clerk, TEs David Dively, J Robert Fiol, and TE
Steven Meyerhoff were elected recording clerks, RE Walter Lastovica was appointed
timekeeper, Frank M. Barker III and RE Ed Nalley were appointed Sound Engineers,
Dale Carrell was appointed A-V Engineer; and TE James A, Smith was appointed
chairman of the floor clerks andre richard springer vice Chairman.

28-7 Recess
The Assembly recessed at 11:07 PM with prayer by the Moderator. TE Larry
Roff, Assembly organist, and Dr. Phillip Ryken, staff member at Tenth Presbyteian
Church, introduced several hymns written by the late Dr. James Montgomery Boice and
the assembly was led in the singing of “Hallelujah!”.

Second Session - Wednesday Morning
June 21, 2000

28-8 Assembly Reconvenes
The Assembly reconvened at 8.00 AM on June 21, 2000 with prayer by teal
Lutz, who included in his prayer petitions for a former teaching elder who was recently
excommunicated, and his wife and children. The Assembly sang ‘Tairest Lord Jesus.”

<a id="ga28-p48"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=50 printed_page=48 -->

28-9 Appointment of Assistant Parliamentarians
On nomination by the Moderator, TE Frank Moser and RE Sam Duncan were
appointed assistant parliamentarians

28-10 Partial Report of the Stated Clerk
TE L. Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk, led the Assembly in prayer and presented his
report (see Appendix A, p. 349). He mentioned he has been asked by the Christian and
Missionary Alliance Church to submit “friend of the court” briefs in the matter of
newport church of the nazarene vs. Hensley before the Oregon Supreme Court, but
has declined because the Assembly has not authorized him to do so
the stated clerk reviewed the statistical portions of his reports. He read the list
of churches which were added to the denominations inc ethel a st Assembly:

## COMMUNICATION 2 From Trent Lott of the United States Senate
"Response to GA From Senator Lott Regarding Abortion"

Dear Dr. Taylor

Thank you for your letter regarding the sanctity of human life and our meeting at the University of Southern Mississippi football game. I appreciate your taking the time to contact me concerning the position of the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America regarding the sanctity of human life.

I appreciate your communicating the position of your church. In my view, the Supreme Court tragically erred in its *Roe v. Wade* decision in 1973. Throughout my tenure in both the House and Senate, I have supported pro-life legislative efforts. I oppose the use of tax dollars to fund abortions. I have also strongly supported banning the “partial-birth” abortion procedure. Please be assured that I will continue working to protect children before birth, and will remember your concern as these issues come before the Senate.

Again, thank you for contacting me concerning this important matter. With best personal regards, I am

Sincerely, yours,
/s/ Trent Lott

## COMMUNICATION 5 From the Reformed Presbyterian Church of North America
"Greetings"

### INTERCHURCH COMMITTEE
Of the Synod of the Reformed Presbyterian Church of North America
April 1, 2000

Dear Roy,

Please convey our warm and hearty greetings to your General Assembly as we serve together in the cause of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. In the midst of a materialistic and immoral culture, and conflicting and humanistic theologies, we praise God that we can stand together with confidence in the triumph of Christ and His church.

Since we sent a fraternal delegate to your General Assembly last year, in keeping with NAPARC procedure, we will not be sending a fraternal delegate this year.

Also, as I announced in the meeting of NAPARC, our Synod will not meet this year. This is a procedure beginning in 1996 which eliminates the meeting of Synod

<a id="ga28-p53"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=55 printed_page=53 -->

every fourth year to encourage full participation at the Reformed Presbyterian International Convention which we anticipate will be attended by over 25% of our total membership. However, each of our Boards and Committees will prepare a report which will be placed in a volume to be distributed to the Sessions of the Church in July. If you are led to send a Greeting to our Synod in care of our Church Relations Committee before May 1, we will include it in the Report of our Committee.

We praise God for the life and testimony of your Church, and pray for the leading of our Sovereign Lord in your ministry and the deliberations of your Synod.

Cordially in Christ,

/s/ Bruce C. Stewart, Secretary

Interchurch Relations Committee

## COMMUNICATION 6 From the Reformed Church in the United States

Monday, May 1, 2000

Dear Brother:

Greetings in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ!

Thank you for your very kind invitation to send an observer to your 28th General Assembly. We regret however that we will be unable to take you up on your invitation because of budget constraints. The budget for the Interchurch Relations Committee of the RCUS is set a year or so in advance and thus no funds were allocated to send an observer to the PCA GA this year.

It is my understanding that the PCA operates on an invitation cycle of two or three years. Perhaps when we meet at NAPARC in the fall we could discuss your cycle so that we can be prepared to accept an invitation from you in the future should one be forthcoming.

We do wish you god’s blessing on your deliberation as a General Assembly and will look forward to hearing your report on this GA to NAPARC in November. If a report on your GA is printed before that time we would be thankful to receive a copy.

We are thankful for your fellowship in the gospel and for your laboring together with the NAPARC churches in this country to further the glorious work of our Lord.

In the bonds of Christ I remain,

Respectfully yours,

/s/ Rev. Ron Potter

## 28-12 Presbytery Votes on BCO Amendments

The Assembly moved to consideration of amendments to the Book of Church Order proposed by the Twenty-Seventh General Assembly. Items 1-4 having received the concurrence of the necessary number of presbyteries, were adopted by the

<a id="ga28-p54"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=56 printed_page=54 -->

Assembly. Thirty-nine (39) Presbyteries must approve a proposed amendment to the *BC(O)(BC(O)26-2)*.

**SPECIAL ITEM**: That *BC(O)* 24-1 be amended as follows:
[see *M26GA*, 26-55, III, 14, p. 208ff, deferred by the 27th GA, 27-12, Item 4, p. 59]

A. Substitute the following for the first paragraph:

**24-1.** Every church shall elect persons to the office of ruling elder and deacon in the following manner: At such times as determined by the Session, communicant members of the congregation may submit names to the Session, keeping in mind that each prospective officer shall be an active male member who meets the qualifications set forth in I Timothy 3 and Titus 1. After the close of the nomination period nominees for the office of ruling elder and/or deacon shall receive instruction in the qualifications and work of the office. Each nominee shall then be examined in:
[NOTE: continue with areas of examination.]

B. Substitute for second paragraph (i.e., “The Session shall examine. . .”) the following:
If there are candidates eligible for election, the Session shall report to the congregation those eligible, giving at least 30 days prior notice of the time and place of a congregational meeting for elections.

**Grounds**:
*BC(O)* 24-1 requires public notice of the time, place and purpose at least one month prior to the appointed time of electing officers, during which time the congregation is asked to submit names to the Session.
*BC(O)* 24-1 requires the Session to examine those nominated and then report to the congregation before election day those eligible for election.
These two requirements can place undue time constraints upon the Session to examine candidates and report to the congregation before the election day.
*Deferred to the 28th General Assembly [BC(O)26-2; 26-6]*

**NOTE**: The following presbyteries are “urged to report their judgment to the next General Assembly”: Illiana, Korean Eastern, Korean Northwest, Korean Southern, Korean Southwest, Siouxlands, Southwest Florida.
*Adopted at the 28th GA*
[See 28-72, p. 275 for Assembly action]

ITEM 1. That BCO 13-12 be amended by substituting for the whole the following:
see minutes of the 27th GA, 27-44, III, 1. p 155

“The Presbytery shall meet at least twice a year on its own adjournment
The Moderator shall call a special meeting at the request or with the concurrence
of three Teaching Elders and three Ruling Elders from at least three
different churches Should the Moderator be for any reason unable to act, the
Stated Clerk shall, under the same requirements, issue the call If both
Moderator and Stated Clerk are unable to act, any three Teaching Elders and
three Ruling Elders of at least three different churches shall have power to
call a meeting However, any Presbytery may prescribe in its rules its own
requirements for calling a special meeting, provided that those
requirements are not less than those stated in this section notice of the
special meeting shall be sent not less than 10 days in advance to each Teaching
elder and to the clerk of session of every church in the notice the purpose of
the meeting shall be stated, and no business, other than that named in the notice
is to be transacted The Presbytery also shall convene when directed to do so by
the General Assembly, for the transaction of designated business only ”
Adopted

ITEM 2: That BCO 15-1 be changed as follows.
see minutes of the 27th GA, 27-44, III, 4, p. 162

15-1. a commission differs from an ordinary committee in that while a
committee is appointed to examine, consider and report, a commission is
authorized to deliberate upon and conclude the business referred to it, except in
the case of judicial commissions of a presbytery appointed under BCO 15-3 A
commissions hall keep a full record of its proceedings, which shall be submitted

<a id="ga28-p58"></a>

to the court appointing it Upon such submission this record shall be entered on
the minutes of the court appointing, except in the case of a presbytery
commissions ervin gas a session ora judicial commission asset forth in BCO
15-3 When a commission is appointed to serve as an interim Session, its
actions are the actions of a Session, not a Presbytery. every commission of a
presbytery or session must submit complete minutes and a report of its activities
at least once annually to the court which commissioned it
Adopted

ITEM 3: That BCO 38-1 be changed as follows:

See Minutes of the 27th GA, 27-44, III, 5, p. 163

38-1. When any person shall come forward and make his offense known to the court, a full statement of the facts shall be recorded and judgment rendered without process. In handling a confession of guilt, it is essential that the person intends to confess and permit the court to render judgment without process. Statements made by him in the presence of the court must not be taken as the basis of a judgment without process except by his consent. In the event a confession is intended, a full statement of the facts should be approved by the accused, and by the court, before the court proceeds to a judgment. The accused has the right of complaint against the judgment.

Adopted

3. that the General Assembly establish two levels of relations with other
denominations: Adopted
a Fraternal Relations - The General Assembly may maintain a
fraternal relationship with other Presbyterian/Reformed
denominations that are voting members of the north american
Presbyterian and Reformed Council and with other such
Churches with whom the General Assembly wishes to establish
fraternal relations unilaterally. This would involve the exchange
of fraternal delegates, exchange of General Assembly or general
Synod minutes, communications on matters of mutual concern,
and other matters that may arise from time to time,
b. Corresponding Relations - The General Assembly may maintain
corresponding relation with other evangelical Churches in North
America and in other continents for exchanging greetings and
letters of encouragement. This may include the exchange of
official observers at the broadest assemblies, and
communications on issues of common concern.
4. a. That the IRC be instructed to inform the NAE that we strongly
disapprove of the change inn ae bylaw B-7 and ask that the
change be rescinded Adopted
b. That the IRC be instructed to report fully to the 29th GA on the
NAE’s response and its rationale for this action Adopted
5. Overture 8-2000 from Illiana Presbytery, “Evangelical Presbyterian
Church Receiving a Suspended PCA Teaching Elder” be answered by
the General Assembly’s adopting the following response to Overture 8
a.) That the 28th General Assembly of the PCA recognizes that,
while the EPC may have acted within its own constitutional
authority when it received into its membership, Rev. David Baer,
ate under the discipline of illiana presbytery of the PCA,
nevertheless, such action by the EPC represents a disregard of
biblical judicial process and the judgment that our Lord declares
to be His own (Matt. 18:15-18). This action undermines the
goodwill that has characterized the relationship between our
denominations. Adopted
b.) that the General Assembly of the PCA requests that thee pc
honor the judicial process and the ecclesiastical censures of the
courts of this church Adopted
c.) that the General Assembly direct the stated clerk of the PCA
and the chairman of the PCA inter church relations committee
to meet with the stated clerk of the epc and the chairman of
their corresponding committee to find a way to respect each
other’s judicial processes in similar situations for the reputation

<a id="ga28-p64"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=66 printed_page=64 -->

of Christ and the peace of His Church and to likewise address other Reformed bodies who apparently disregard our discipline when requested by a PCA presbytery and propose resolution to like disciplinary issues.
Adopted as amended

##### OVERTURE 8 From Illiana Presbytery

"Evangelical Presbyterian Church Receiving Suspended PCA Teaching Elder"

Whereas, Illiana Presbytery imposed a censure of indefinite suspension on TE David W. Baer in 1998, and

Whereas, Illiana Presbytery’s action of censure was communicated in the Spring of 1999 to TE Corbett Heimburger, Chairman of the Evangelical Presbyterian Church’s Mid-America Presbytery’s Candidates and Credentials Committee, and

Whereas, TE Heimburger indicated that the EPC would not receive TE Baer as long as he was under discipline in the PCA, and

Whereas, that censure has not been lifted by Illiana Presbytery but has been left in force by a Subcommittee of the General Assembly’s Standing Judicial Commission which declined the appeal of TE Baer in June of 1999, and

Whereas, the EPC Mid-America Presbytery examined and voted to receive TE Baer into their fellowship on October 23, 1999 despite his still being under censure in the PCA, and

Whereas, the EPC Mid-America Presbytery at the same meeting approved the appointment of TE Baer as organizing pastor of Trinity Fellowship Church, composed of people who had left TE Baer’s former PCA Church, Westminster PCA, in Godfrey, Illinois, and

Whereas, the EPC Mid-America Presbytery rejected the Illiana Presbytery’s Censure and the SJC Subcommittee’s decision as being in error,

Therefore, Illiana Presbytery respectfully overtures the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to take this matter into advisement in regard to current or future relations with the EPC and to assign the Interchurch Relations Committee to investigate this action by the EPC and to recommend an appropriate response and/or actions to be taken by PCA churches that have relations with EPC denominational churches in their area.

Adopted at the Winter Stated Meeting of Illiana Presbytery on January 15, 2000.
Attested by: /s/ Dale L. Eisenreich, Stated Clerk

6. That the Permanent Committee of the IRC investigate and clarify the relationship between the PCA and the World Fellowship of Reformed Churches (WFRC) and report their findings to the 29th GA.
Adopted

**28-19 Report of Committee on Constitutional Business**

TE Ric Cannada, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the Report. After reviewing the report, the Assembly **received** it as information.

**I. Introduction**

Your Committee on Constitutional Business (CCB) met one time subsequent to the 27th General Assembly, on April 17 - 18, 2000 in Atlanta, Georgia. The CCB was able at that meeting to complete all of the work then assigned to it. Attendance was as follows:

**Present:**

TE Robert C. Cannada, Jr. (00), Chairman
TE Craig D. Childs, Sr. (03)
TE Bryan S. Chapell (02)
TE Morton H. Smith (01)
TE Karl F. McCallister (alternate)
Excused: RE Daniel D. Hall (00)
RE Richard A. Springer (03)
RE Joe B. Johnson (02)
RE Calvin Poole III (01)
RE Frederick Neikirk (alternate)

In the absence of RE Hall, RE Neikirk was seated, by proper motion, as a voting member. A quorum was present and the Committee convened to conduct business.

<a id="ga28-p68"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=70 printed_page=68 -->

## II. Minutes of the Standing Judicial Commission

The CCB approved the minutes of the March 2, 2000 meeting of the Standing Judicial Commission as being in conformity to the Manual of the SJC (20-5) and RAO 15.

We do acknowledge that in the Minutes of the Case (or Matter) 99-1 that the confusion of the terms “case” and “matter” both in the Commission’s work, and the possibly varying uses of the words “case” and “matter” in our Constitutional Documents and Manuals of Operation of (cf. *BCO* 34-1 and 15-4; RAO 15-1; Manual of SJC 6-1 and 6-2) made the Commission’s work difficult.

If the issues before the SJC did not constitute a “judicial case” then it is questionable whether there was specific RAO or *BCO* language allowing the disqualification of specific parties of the SJC who may have been considered parties to the “case” (see Manual of the SJC 6-2; although there is apparent allowance for the disqualification from a “matter” also in Manual of the SJC 6-1, first sentence). Further, if the issues did not constitute a “case” then no members of the SJC were eligible to adjudicate the matter, since they only take vows for a “case” (RAO 15-1). Voting to change the language of its minutes so that all references to “case” were changed to “matter” further clouded the SJC’s authority to disqualify members. Our candid assessment is that the *BCO*, RAO and SJC Manual use the terms “matter” and “case” without strict definition (and they may be synonymous) and, therefore, given the way Commission resolved these matters by correct parliamentary steps within its deliberations causes us to see no reason for taking exception to the SJC’s minutes. For example, the use of the word “case” needs clarification in *BCO* 34-1 and elsewhere (see above references).

Further, it is our conclusion that guidelines need to be drafted to determine how the General Assembly will specifically deal with the actions described in *BCO* 34-1.

We suggest that minutes of the SJC reflect more fully the actions of the commission so that they can be more adequately assessed (e.g. making sure that changes of wording to commission actions are recorded in the minutes).

## NOTATIONS

1. There is no record in the minutes of the SJC’s having received and/or dealt with the materials submitted by Western Carolina Presbytery and Ascension Presbytery in an effort to meet/respond to the officers’ original finding. (SJC Manual 10-2)

2. There is no record in the minutes as to how the SJC received information about the action of Tennessee Valley Presbytery upon which the SJC based their decision.

3. There is no record as to who closed the meeting in prayer.

The entire action was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0.

## III. Non-Judicial References

### Reference 1 – Presbytery of Western Carolina:

**Question 1.** When presbyteries act under the provisions of *BCO* 34-1, may the case be declared out of order without being heard, or does the word “shall” in 34-1 indicate that the General Assembly is obligated to take up the case and hear evidence?

<a id="ga28-p69"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=71 printed_page=69 -->

Response – When Presbyteries act under the provisions of *BCO* 34-1 the word “shall” in the last clause does obligate the General Assembly to act on the matter. The way in which the Assembly shall act on the matter is by referring it to the Standing Judicial Commission (see *BCO* 15-4) which shall process the matter according to its regular procedures which shall include the options of administratively ruling the matter out of order if there is sufficient cause or, if the matter is in order, taking up the case and hearing the evidence.

Question 2. Does the reference to the General Assembly in 34-1 mean that it is the Assembly “as a whole” which must determine to take up the case, or may the SJC make that determination?

Response – The matter shall be referred to the SJC (see the answer to question number 1 above).

Question 3. May the case of original jurisdiction under *BCO* 34-1 be declared judicially out of order under the provisions of *BCO* 41, 42 or 43 or must the SJC (or Assembly) hear it?

Response – There are no operating provisions in the Manual of the SJC for dealing with *BCO* 34-1. However, the *BCO* does assume that the SJC has the right to determine whether all matters coming before it are in order (RAO 15-2). Although the provisions of the *BCO* 41, 42 and 43 dealing with references, appeals and complaints do not directly apply, they are the only analogy the SJC currently has for making such determinations. More specific operational guidelines dealing with 34-1 are needed, but until they are provided the SJC can only operate with its ordinary procedures.

Question 4. In a case arising under the provisions of *BCO* 34-1, may the SJC or its officers determine all or some of the “facts” of the case as a basis for a determination as to whether a case is judicially in order without hearing evidence and arguments from both parties, or must the case go forward (assuming it is administratively in order) so that such evidence may be presented under the provisions of *BCO* 32-13, 32-14, 32-18 and 35?

Response – Under the provisions of 34-1 and in accord with the Manual of the SJC (10-1) the officers of the SJC or the impaneled SJC may determine if a matter is administratively out of order without hearing evidence and arguments from both parties based on timeliness of filing, proper paperwork, etc. The impaneled SJC may determine if a matter is judicially out of order prior to hearing full evidence and arguments from both parties if the SJC believes it has sufficient initial evidence to make such a determination (cf. Manual of the SJC 10-1b). Otherwise the SJC could be forced to listen to matters at length, which were not properly before it.

Question 5. In the event that the matter is assumed as a case of original jurisdiction, then since the Presbyterian Church in America versus the individuals are the parties of the case, would members of those presbyteries that have been

<a id="ga28-p70"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=72 printed_page=70 -->

involved in bringing the issue to the Assembly be required to be excluded from participating in the trial?

Response -- Because the only parties to a case that goes to the SJC are the Presbyterian Church in America and the accused (BCO 31-3), the mere membership of an SJC member in a presbytery that may have brought charges against the accused does not disqualify that SJC member from hearing and voting upon the case in which the SJC has assumed original jurisdiction. However, the rules for the conduct of SJC members who are qualified for adjudicating a case (Manual of the SJC 6 and 7), will ordinarily disqualify the SJC participation of members of presbyteries who have brought charges resulting in the SJC becoming the court of original jurisdiction. These rules, for example, indicate that it is not appropriate for any member of the commission to discuss the merits of the case with any party to the case or any person other than a member of the commission, or to express an opinion on the merits of the case prior to the record being completed, or to consult or advise regarding any matter that may come before the commission. While it is conceivable that members of presbyteries bringing charges may not be disqualified from SJC participation for these or similar reasons, it is unlikely and, therefore, members of presbyteries bringing charges should ordinarily recuse themselves or be disqualified.

The entire response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0.

## Reference 2 — Presbytery of the Ascension:

Question 1 – When presbyteries act under the provisions of BCO 34-1, may the case be declared out of order without being heard, or does the word “must” in 34-1 indicate that the General Assembly is obligated to take up the case and hear evidence?

Response – See answer to question #1 of Western Carolina Presbytery Reference for Advice dated Dec. 28, 1999.

Question 2 – Does the reference to the General Assembly in 34-1 mean that it is the Assembly as a whole which must determine to take up the case, or may the SJC make that determination (implicit in this is the corollary question – is this even a case before it is received by the General Assembly itself)?

Response – Since BCO 15-4 assigns all judicial cases to the SJC for adjudication it is the duty of the SJC to handle such matters.

Question 3 – May the SJC (or the Assembly as a whole) declare as judicially out of order any case which comes to the General Assembly under a provision not covered by one of those chapters (e.g., an action arising on the basis of BCO 34-1), or must the SJC or the Assembly hear such a case if it is administratively in order?

Response – See answer to question #3 of Western Carolina Presbytery Reference for Advice dated Dec. 28, 1999.

<a id="ga28-p71"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=73 printed_page=71 -->

Question 4 – In a case arising under the provisions of *BCO* 34-1, may the SJC or its officers determine all or some of the “facts” of the case as a basis for a determination as to whether a case is judicially in order without hearing evidence and arguments from both parties, or must the case go forward (assuming it is administratively in order) so that such evidence may be presented under the provisions of *BCO* 32-13, 32-14, 32-18 and 35?

Response – See answer to question #4 of Western Carolina Presbytery Reference for Advice dated Dec. 28, 1999.

The entire response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0

## Reference 3 – (Communication 4) Presbytery of the Ascension re: RAO 4-2; 4-3; 4-4 and 13-2:

Question – Which of the entities listed in RAO 4-2, 4-3 and 4-4 are covered by the “ineligibility provision” of RAO 13-2, and why?

Response – It is the advice of the Committee that the term “permanent committee” is a technical term referring only to the four committees mentioned in RAO 4-2 (cf. *BCO* 14-1.12). Therefore, only those serving on those four committees (or their sub-committees and staffs) are barred from serving on committees of commissioners (cf. RAO 4-10.a). Service on any other committee, commission or agency of the General Assembly would not, under our current rules, bar one from serving on a committee of commissioners unless he would be serving on a committee of commissioners that oversees the committee or agency on which he serves (see Roberts Rules sec. 44).

The response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0.

## COMMUNICATION 4 From the Presbytery of the Ascension (to CCB)
“Regarding the Interpretation of RAO 4-2; 4-3; 4-4; and 13-2”

Whereas, RAO 13-2 states “Commissioners serving on permanent committee or sub-committees of the Assembly or the staffs thereof are not eligible to serve on any Committees of Commissioners;” [emphasis added] and

Whereas, this has traditionally been the basis for barring ones serving the entities discussed in RAO 4-2 and 4-3 from service on Committees of Commissioners; and

Whereas, at one recent Assembly commissioners were told at the Committees of Commissioners’ briefing that this provision also barred ones serving on the Committee on Constitutional Business, Nominating Committee, Review Committee, Standing Judicial Commission, Theological Examining Committee, etc., from serving on Committees of Commissioners, thus disenfranchising some who fell into this category and had been elected by their presbyteries to serve at that Assembly; and

Whereas, the July 1999 edition of the *Handbook for Presbytery Clerks* states that RAO 13-2 “excludes SJC members from serving” while “[m]embers of the Nominating Committee and Committee on Review of Presbytery Records are eligible to serve;” and

<a id="ga28-p72"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=74 printed_page=72 -->

Whereas, the Constitutional basis for this distinction is not clear, nor does it provide guidance as to the eligibility of members of some of the other committees noted above; and

Whereas, BCO 14-1.12 states “The Assembly permanent committees are the Administrative Committee of General Assembly, Committee on Christian Education and Publications, Committee on Mission to North America, and Committee on Mission to the World;” and

Whereas, RAO 4-2 also designates these four committees as the “permanent committees;” and

Whereas, this limited definition of “permanent committees” seems to mean that RAO 13-2 can only be seen as limiting persons serving on four committees (and their sub-committees and staffs) from service on Committees of Commissioners, which conclusion is much narrower than past practices; and

Whereas, we are therefore confused as to the proper understanding of RAO 13-2; and

Whereas, we do not wish to run the risk of being disenfranchised from Committees of Commissioners either because we elected someone to serve who would be excluded under one of the broader understandings of RAO 13-2, or because we did not elect someone for fear he would be excluded when he is in fact eligible; and

Whereas, we therefore need clarification on these matters before we can choose our members for the Committees of Commissioners of the 28th General Assembly; and

Whereas, the Stated Clerk of the General Assembly advised our Stated Clerk that this question should be addressed to the Committee on Constitutional Business; and

Whereas, BCO 41-2 gives us among the proper subjects for reference “…questions involving the Constitution and legal procedures respecting which the lower court feels the need of guidance;”

Therefore Be in Resolved that the Presbytery of the Ascension address the following non-judicial reference to the General Assembly (and its Committee on Constitutional Business): “Which of the entities listed in RAO 4-2, 4-3, and 4-4 are covered by the ‘ineligibility provision’ of RAO 13-2 and why?”

Adopted at the January Stated Meeting of the Presbytery of the Ascension on January 29, 2000. Attested by: /s/ Frederick R. Neikirk, Stated Clerk

## Reference 4 – James River Presbytery:

Question – “Does the current printing of BCO 57-5 (using “Church” in the introduction to the profession of faith, and “church” in vows 4 and 5 accurately reflect BCO 57-5 when it was duly adopted?”

<a id="ga28-p73"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=75 printed_page=73 -->

Response – It is the advice of the Committee that the original language of *BCO* 57-5 used an upper case “C” in both vows 4 and 5. The change to lower case was apparently the result of a typographical error at some point. The Committee recommends that this be corrected. The Committee does not believe it is appropriate for us to recommend whether any changes ought to be made in the language of the vows.

The response was adopted by proper motion, 7-0-0.

## IV. Constitutional Inquiries

Note: In view of some confusion that seems to exist in the Committee and the Church at large, we recommend that the term “constitutional inquiry” be reserved for questions referred to the Committee by the General Assembly [see RAO 7-2, second (2)], while matters coming to the Committee directly from the lower courts be referred to as “non-judicial references” [see RAO 7-2, first (2)].

## Inquiry 1 – Ascension Presbytery:

Question 1 – When presbyteries act under the provisions of *BCO* 34-1, may the case be declared out of order without being heard, or does the word “must” in 34-1 indicate that the General Assembly is obligated to take up the case and hear evidence?

Response – See answer to question #1 of Western Carolina Presbytery Reference for Advice dated Dec. 28, 1999.

Question 2 – Does the reference to the General Assembly in 34-1 mean that it is the Assembly as a whole which must determine to take up the case, or may the SJC make that determination (implicit in this is the corollary question – is this even a case before it is received by the General Assembly itself)?

Response – Since *BCO* 15-4 assigns *all* judicial cases to the SJC for adjudication it is the duty of the SJC to handle such matters.

Question 3 – May the SJC (or the Assembly as a whole) declare as judicially out of order any case which comes to the General Assembly under a provision not covered by one of those chapters (e.g., an action arising on the basis of *BCO* 34-1), or must the SJC or the Assembly hear such a case if it is administratively in order?

Response – See answer to question #3 of Western Carolina Presbytery Reference for Advice dated Dec. 28, 1999.

Question 4 – In a case arising under the provisions of *BCO* 34-1, may the SJC or its officers determine all or some of the “facts” of the case as a basis for a determination as to whether a case is judicially in order without hearing evidence and arguments from both parties, or must the case go forward (assuming it is administratively in order) so that such evidence may be presented under the provisions of *BCO* 32-13, 32-14, 32-18 and 35?

<a id="ga28-p74"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=76 printed_page=74 -->

Response – See answer to question of #4 of Western Carolina Presbytery Reference for Advice dated Dec. 28, 1999.

The entire response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0.

## Inquiry #2, Louisiana Presbytery dated April 15, 2000

In response to the hypothetical questions posed:

The charge of the CCB is to give advice regarding actual matters before the Church. The CCB is directly charged not to introduce new business, and therefore it cannot answer a series of hypothetical questions that force us to create procedures or suggest courses of action that are not directly addressed in our Constitution. Answers to such questions must be determined by overture or judicial processes that establish the policies of the Church (cf. *BCO* 40).

The response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0.

Whereas, many in the Presbyterian Church in America have serious doubts about the ability of the Standing Judicial Commission (SJC) effectively to represent the denomination as a whole; and

Whereas, the decision of the Standing Judicial Commission in RE the judicial case of Presbyterian Church in America vs. John Wood is, in the minds of many in our denomination, seriously flawed; and

Whereas, we desire to proceed in these matters in such a way as to address our concerns in a proper and, so far as possible, a peaceful manner, with full submission to the wisdom of the General Assembly; and

Whereas, the toleration of a situation in which a woman is allowed to fill a PCA pulpit is divisive, and will, if not satisfactorily redressed, lead to division of our beloved church;

Now therefore, be it resolved, that Louisiana Presbytery directs the following questions to the Committee on Constitutional Business:

1. Suppose that a presbytery declined to proceed judicially against a session with regard to an apparent violation of our Standards (for example, allowing a woman to preach), and suppose further there that no one in the presbytery filed a complaint against that willful failure by the presbytery. What are the possible recourses for others in the Presbyterian Church in America in order to deal with the situation? For example, would it be appropriate or legitimate for charges to be brought against the presbytery, seeking its discipline since it had failed to uphold our church’s Standards?

2. Suppose that the Standing Judicial Commission was derelict in its duty, whether deliberately or because of incompetence. What possible recourses are there? (A.) For example, would it be appropriate or legitimate for judicial charges to be brought against the individual members of the SJC? (B.) Would it be appropriate or legitimate for administrative charges to be brought against the individual members of the SJC? (Presumably, this would require a rescinding of the vote to elect them to their post.) How would an administrative trial be

<a id="ga28-p75"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=77 printed_page=75 -->

conducted? If prior notice were given, would a vote to remove require a simple majority?

3. If a court is responsible for a particular matte, may charges be brought not only against the court corporately but also against the individual members thereof who approved of that action? For example, our *Book of Church Order* says that the session is responsible for the conduct of public worship in the congregation. Suppose that a session authorized a known homosexual to preach; could charges be brought against the individual members of the session who voted for that action?

4. What would be the effect of an Assembly declining to approve the motion, that it declare the SJC to be its commission (BCO 15-4)?

5. Historically, Presbyterianism has allowed one General Assembly to condemn the opinion of a prior Assembly (see M12GA, p. 140: “Properly speaking no action of previous General Assemblies may be amended, rescinded, or annulled. A subsequent General Assembly may take a contrary position and condemn the action of a previous Assembly...”). Since an SJC decision is the position of the previous General Assembly (BCO 15-4, “...and each subsequent Assembly should declare the Standing Judicial Commission as a whole to be its commission”), may the current General Assembly condemn the opinion of the SJC which is reported in the current year?

Adopted by Louisiana Presbytery at the 63rd Stated Meeting, Opelousas, Louisiana
Attested by: /s/ James A. Jones, Jr., Stated Clerk

## V. Overtures

**OVERTURE 1 – From Heartland Presbytery, “Declare Full Preterism a Heresy.”**

**Response** – It is the opinion of the Committee that Overture 1 is out of order in its present form.

The Overture needs to be referred back to Heartland Presbytery for revision before it comes to the floor of the Assembly. It appears that the Overture states the position of Heartland Presbytery on the matter of preterism but is not clear what action, if any, the Overture is requesting the Assembly to take.

The Committee agrees that the General Assembly has the authority (BCO 14-6a) to, “bear testimony against error in doctrine and immorality in practice, injuriously affecting the Church,” and “to decide in all controversies respecting doctrine and discipline.”

The response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0. [Text and action at 28-72, III, 17, p. 297.]

**OVERTURE 2 – From Grace Presbytery, “Grant Full Constitutional Status to the Entire Directory of Worship.”**

**Response** – It is the opinion of the Committee that this Overture is not in conflict with other parts of the constitution. The committee notes that every amendment to

<a id="ga28-p76"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=78 printed_page=76 -->

the $BCO$ (which this is) must be submitted for adoption to the Presbyteries, as per the normal amendment process ($BCO$ 26-2).

The response was adopted by proper motion, 7-1-0. (See Majority and Minority Reports Below regarding the status of the DFW, p. 80). [Text and action at 28-72, III, 3, p. 280.]

OVERTURE 4 – From James River Presbytery, “Clarify Use of Professional Counsel in Cases of Process.”

Response – It is the opinion of the Committee that the Overture presented is not in conflict with any other provision of the Constitution.

The response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0. [Text and action at 28-72, III, 12, p. 288.]

OVERTURE 5 – From Louisiana Presbytery – “Amend BCO 38-4 by Deletion.”

Response – It is the opinion of the Committee that the Overture presented is not in conflict with any other provision of the Constitution.

The response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0. [Text and action at 28-72, III, 15, p. 295.]

OVERTURE 6 – From Cherokee Presbyterian Church, “Amend BCO 16-4 To Allow that Exceptions May Not be Taught.”

Response – It is the opinion of the Committee that the Overture presented is not in conflict with any other provision of the Constitution. It is the recommendation of the Committee that this language, if it is approved, be included in Chapter 21 either as a part of section 4 or immediately following. If approved, there should also be a reference in BCO 13-6 to this new language.

The response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0. [Text and action at 28-72, III, 10, p. 286.]

OVERTURE 9 – From Presbytery of the Ascension, “Amend BCO 24-1.”

Response – It is the opinion of the Committee that the proposed new language is not properly before the Committee since it seeks to amend language that is not part of our constitution. RAO 13-6 specifies that proposed amendments, when passed down to the Presbyteries and subsequently returned to the General Assembly for approval, must retain the explicit language approved. In order to accomplish the intent of the overture, General Assembly would have to suspend the RAO during consideration.

The response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0. [Text and action at 28-72, III, 2, p. 278.]

OVERTURE 10 – From Presbytery of Mississippi Valley, “Restrict Preaching or Exhorting to Qualified Men Only.”

Response – It is the opinion of the committee that this Overture is not in conflict with other parts of the constitution. We note that the Directory of Worship was

<a id="ga28-p77"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=79 printed_page=77 -->

adopted by the 3rd GA and is part of the Book of Church Order, as adopted by the Church (see Prefatory Statement of the Directory of Worship, which leaves its constitutional status confusing). The procedures of *BCO* 26-2 must be followed to amend it. However, the Assembly must decide whether or not the use of the term “exhort” is more restrictive than scripture itself (*BCO* Preface II Preliminary Principles1).

The response was adopted by proper motion, 7-1-0. (See Majority and Minority Reports Below regarding the status of the DFW, p. 80). [Text and action at 28-72, III, 5, p. 281.]

## OVERTURE 12 – From Potomac Presbytery, “Prohibit Women From Preaching In Public Worship.”

**Response** – It is the opinion of the Committee that the Overture presented is not in conflict with any specific provision of the Constitution. However, the Assembly must decide whether or not the use of the term “expounding” is more restrictive than scripture, itself. (*BCO* Preface II. Preliminary Principles 1.)

The response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0. [Text and action at 28-72, III, 4, p. 281.]

## OVERTURE 13 – From Susquehanna Valley Presbytery, “Re-ordination of Former PCA Ministers.”

**Response** – It is the opinion of the Committee that Overture 13 as presented is not in technical conflict with any other provisions of the Constitution. However, the Committee would point out that *BCO* 34-10 does not mandate divestiture in every case (“...it shall be the duty of the Presbytery...to inquire...and, if necessary, to institute judicial proceedings...If it shall appear that his neglect proceeds only from his lack of acceptance to the church, Presbytery may...divest him of his office...”). The Committee is also concerned that the language proposed for the new *BCO* 21-5 raises questions about the nature and importance of the call to the ministry (“...simply a lack of call...”). Finally, the Committee notes the apparent inconsistency in the handling of one who has been removed from office by discipline (deposition – *BCO* 36-7) who would be restored as per *BCO* 37-5 and one who is divested without censure (*BCO* 34-10) who could be restored only after reexamination and re-ordination. Perhaps a proposed amendment clarifying the relationship between divestiture and deposition would be helpful.

The response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0. [Text and action at 28-72, III, 11, p. 287.]

## OVERTURE 15 and 16 – From New Jersey Presbytery to Revise *BCO* 56-5

**Response** – It is the opinion of the Committee that Overtures 15 and 16 are not in order for the following reasons:

1. The Presbytery did not adopt the original Overtures and there is no evidence that the provisions of RAO 10-10 were followed with regard to the handling of overtures that were rejected by a presbytery.

<a id="ga28-p78"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=80 printed_page=78 -->

2. If the Presbytery is seeking the formation of a study committee, as indicated by the note at the bottom of the page, there is no discussion of funding as required by RAO 10-11.

The response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0. [Text and action at 28-72, III, 6 and 7, p. 283 ff.]

## OVERTURE 17 – From Philadelphia Presbytery, “Add Vows for Elders When Receiving Members.”

**Response** – It is the opinion of the Committee that Overture 17 as presented is not in conflict with any other portion of the Constitution.

The response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0. [Text and action at 28-72, III, 8, p. 284.]

## OVERTURE 18 – From Ellisville PCA, “Amend BCO 13-1 to Increase Number of RE Commissioners to Presbytery Meetings.”

**Response** – It is the opinion of the Committee that Overture 18 as presented is not in conflict with any other portion of the Constitution.

The response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0. [Text and action at 28-72, III, 9, p. 285.]

## OVERTURE 21 – From Louisiana Presbytery, “Disciplinary Measures Against Tennessee Valley Presbytery.”

**Response** – It is the opinion of the CCB that Overture 21 as worded is not consistent with our Constitution since it pre-determines the guilt of a presbytery and prescribes censures. In order for such a determination to be made, and such a censure to be administered, then the procedures of *BCO* 40-4 through 40-6 would need to be followed. These procedures always provide for a hearing of specific charges in an appropriate court and with opportunity for defense before any individual or entity is reprimanded or rebuked. The Review and Control process is one way that such a matter might properly come before the Assembly so that a presbytery’s actions could be reviewed.

Additionally our Constitution does not allow for the “the conduct of a trial on the floor of the Assembly” since all judicial matters are referred to the SJC (*BCO* 15-1); and this matter could be referred to the SJC by the GA (*BCO* 15-4).

The response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0.

##### OVERTURE 21 From Louisiana Presbytery

“Disciplinary Measures Against Tennessee Valley Presbytery”

**Whereas**, several presbyteries of the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) have formally brought to the attention of Tennessee Valley Presbytery (TVP) the fact that one of its churches has allowed women to preach; and

**Whereas**, TVP, to date, has refused to deal adequately with the situation; and

<a id="ga28-p79"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=81 printed_page=79 -->

Whereas, our Constitution tells us that it is “incumbent” upon a higher court
that has been “well advised” of serious neglect or irregularity, to “take
cognizance of the same, and to examine, deliberate and judge in the
whole matter” (BCO 40-4), and

Whereas, the 14th General Assembly, in approving the report of the blue-ribbon
Ad Interim Committee on the General Assembly, approved the following
principle: “In the event the lower court does nothing [with regard to
discipline], by virtue of its ecclesiastical authority the higher court may
(a) ignore the failure to act, or (b) counsel, advise, exhort, and urge the
lower court to comply, or (c) reprimand or rebuke the lower court, or (d)
suspend one or all of the ecclesiastical privileges of the lower court with
respect to the higher courts -- e.g., to overture or reference a matter to
the higher courts, to vote upon amendments to the Standards, to vote at
the higher courts, or even to have commissioners at the higher courts, or
(e) as a last resort ‘act against’ the lower court by dismissing it from the
fellowship” (M14GA, pp. 103, 436); and

Whereas, given the great turmoil which TVP’s failure to act has engendered
throughout the PCA, it does not seem prudent for the General Assembly
to ignore this willful failure, and

Whereas, since sister presbyteries have already counseled and urged TVP to
act in a satisfactory manner and it has refused to heed this advice, it
seems that the second option has already, in essence, been exercised, and

Whereas, we do not want to proceed immediately to the drastic measure of
exscinding TVP from our fellowship without giving it at least one more
opportunity to repent;

Now Therefore, Be it Resolved, that Louisiana Presbytery hereby overtures
the 28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to take
appropriate disciplinary measures against Tennessee Valley Presbytery
for its failure to uphold the standards of our church, said disciplinary
measures being that of a reprimand or rebuke, along with the
pronouncement that if TVP does not act in a satisfactory fashion by the
time of the 29th General Assembly, its ecclesiastical privileges of having
commissioners seated at the General Assembly and of having
representatives from the Presbytery to be nominated to any General Assembly committees, commission, board or agency, will be revoked;
And Be It Further Resolved, that Louisiana Presbytery hereby overtures the
28th General Assembly to enact these measures by any lawful means
possible, including, if deemed necessary, the conduct of a trial on the
floor of the assembly (BCO 40-5 and 40-6).

Adopted by Louisiana Presbytery at the 63rd Stated Meeting, Opelousas,
Louisiana, on April 15, 2000 Attested by: /s/ James A. Jones, Jr., Stated Clerk

<a id="ga28-p80"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=82 printed_page=80 -->

## OVERTURE 22 – From Louisiana Presbytery, “Condemn the SJC Decision in the John Wood Case.”

**Response** – It is the opinion of the CCB that Overture 22 is not properly before us since it does not call for any constitutional advice. The matter should be sent to the Bills and Overtures Committee of Commissioners for processing.

[Text and action at 28-72, III, 1, p. 275.]

The response was adopted by proper motion, 8-0-0.

Respectfully Submitted:
/s/ TE Robert C. Cannada, Jr., Chairman
/s/ RE Richard Springer, Secretary

## Majority Rationale of the Committee on Constitutional Business to Overture 2 from Grace Presbytery and Overture 10 From Mississippi Valley Presbytery in this matter of the Directory for Worship of the Book of Church Order

This majority rationale was prepared after the meeting of the committee and with the help of a number of the committee members; but it was not finally discussed, debated and approved in an open committee meeting prior to the meeting of the 28th GA.

### Background:

The Committee acknowledges the unique character of the Directory for Worship (DFW) and understands the difference of perceptions regarding its relationship to the constitutional documents of the church and the church-at-large. Beginning with the 3rd General Assembly, the adoption of the language of the DFW has continued to be a controversial matter. Some of that controversy finds its origins in the various philosophies with respect to the nature of “constitutional authority” and what it means that language may, or may not, have the “force of law” or be “obligatory.” It is beyond the scope of this report to deal with these issues and the Committee simply refers the Assembly to *BCO* 11-1; 11-2; 25-10 and 25-11, among others.

The very nature of proceedings in our first three General Assemblies required procedures of an extraordinary character. They were necessary, however, to get the organization functioning and move ahead with the business of the church (which at times may disregard the details of administration and “minutia”). And, we were, after all, men of good will having in our hearts the desire to serve Christ in complete conformity to His will, as revealed to us in Scripture. There was no intention or desire to confuse or confound the work of the Assembly; in fact, just the opposite was true.

The 3rd GA, then, approved the DFW in the same way that the first two Assemblies had approved the Form of Government (FOG) and the Rules of Discipline (ROD) – that is, acting as a constitutional convention. That procedure was clearly understood to be a suspension of the rules which suspension was necessary to get things started. A close comparison of the minutes of the First, Second and Third General Assembly demonstrates that there was no difference in the process used in the adoption of FOG

<a id="ga28-p81"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=83 printed_page=81 -->

and rod thant hat in the case oft hed fw the original statement for how the BCO
was to be adopted clearly covered all three parts of the document

the action of the third General Assembly is recorded as follows

"Recommendation # 3 was adopted: Recommendation # 3 - The Committee
recommends the adoption of the proposed Directory for Worship and that upon its
adoption the Assembly declare the Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America
to be fully adopted, and that the suspension of paragraph 27-2 approved by the First
General Assembly now be ended." M3GA. 3-15. p 55)
it is important to note that t hed fw was adopted ads er ia tim somewhere in the middle
of this process, (it is often difficult to perfectly understand the chronology of events
when reading minutes since their nature is to record final actions taken as opposed to
serving as a transcript of proceedings) the “Temporary Statement” now serving as part
of the Preface was adopted (M3GA 88, 3-70). of greater relevance is the fact that there
was no distinction of procedure for those parts of dfw that were adopted prior to the
adoption of the “Temporary Statement” and those after. The whole was concluded by
the action taken in “recommendation 3,” noted above.

This temporary statement, created during the process of crafting the constitutional
documents at precisely the point of the consideration of the DFW, is at the crux of much
of the current discussion. It must be kept in mind that the Constitutional Documents
Committee was charged by the 3rd GA with developing a statement on the “relative
authority” of the constitutional documents. This “temporary statement” was adopted,
“until a more precise statement can be prepared...” This permitted the Assembly to
move on and complete the constitutional documents. The vacuum that had been created
by the 1st GA wherein the Assembly was free to mold the constitution without
encumbering the process with the 2/3’s vote of the presbyteries was held open until, and
only until, the 3r ga completed the adoption of the final piece - that is, the DFW. At
that point, the Assembly declared the special process (which had suspended the normal
procedure for amendment of the constitution) closed, the constitution complete and the
amendment process in full force and effect. From this point on, all changes to the
constitutional documents must be sent to the Presbyteries.

an interesting action is recorded in the minutes of the 6th GA. In 6-11 (Report of the
Clerk) section D, there are recorded matters carried over from previous Assemblies.
one of those noted, found on page 44 of the minutes, is:
“3-70 ...A temporary statement was adopted to be placed at the beginning of the
directory of worship until a more precise statement can be prepared by the
Constitutional Documents Committee The Constitutional Documents Committee was
directed to prepare a statement on the relative authority of the Constitutional
documents, which is to be returned to the next Assembly ” (M6GA, p 44)
However, 6-107 (4) on page 108 of the same minutes reports:
“The recommendation of the Constitutional Documents Committee that it continue its
work was rejected, and the Committee was dismissed with the thanks of the Assembly.”
(M6GA p. 108)
There is no evidence in the minutes that the CDC had made any recommendations or
done any more work at the 6th Assembly on the statement regarding relative authority

<a id="ga28-p82"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=84 printed_page=82 -->

In the report of the Stated Clerk to the 7th Assembly, it is noted that this matter was referred to the Sub-Committee on Judicial Business.

“Concerning the 3rd GA’s direction to the Constitutional Documents Committee to prepare a statement on the relative authority of the constitutional documents to replace the temporary statement which now appears at the beginning of the Directory of Worship, paragraph 3-70, Minutes of the Third General Assembly. That the matter be referred to the Permanent Committee on Judicial Business for appropriate action.” (M7GA, 7-41-III-22, p. 104)

Nothing is reported about this matter in the report of the SCJB to the 7th General Assembly.

The 8th Assembly took up the matter once again. Here, action was taken to change the status of three chapters of BCO. The recommendation is,

“That Chapters 57, 58, and 59 [apparently the chapter order of the Directory for Worship has also been altered at some point or else they got the numbering wrong -- the next year they are referred to as 56, 57, and 58] of the BCO be adopted as constitutional and sent down to the Presbyteries for their advice and consent, and that the SuBCO Committee on Judicial Business rewrite the Directory of Worship.” (M8GA, 8-88-III-9, p. 118)

[Note that these chapters were also referred to in the record of the Third General Assembly as numbers 57, 58 &amp; 59.]

The matter was sent to the Presbyteries and the 9th General Assembly approved the change and finalized the matter.

“That chapters 56, 57, and 58 of the BCO be adopted as constitutional. (Clerk's note: These chapters have already been approved as a part of the Book of Church Order, but due to the temporary notation at the beginning of the Directory for Worship, the constitutional authority of this portion of the Book of Church Order has been questioned, and thus these particular chapters are being submitted for the advice and consent of the presbyteries in order to give them full constitutional authority. Minor editorial corrections in punctuation have been made by the Clerk.)” (M9GA, 9-65-III-I-item 6, p. 137)

Following that action, the language of the three chapters of the DOW (including the upper case “C’s” in vows 4 and 5) is recorded.

The Preface of the DFW currently states that “BCO 56, 57 &amp; 58 have been given full constitutional authority by the Eleventh General Assembly ...” This is a curious statement in that no such action seems to be recorded in the minutes of the 11th GA. The Subcommittee on Judicial Business submitted a report to the 11th Assembly in the matter of the DFW. This report did not include the language of the current Preface, but it did include specific language in a lengthy Appendix. (M11GA p. 220). The formal action that was taken was simply to receive that as information, to refer the Presbyteries to the Westminster Directory of Worship and to invite responses from the churches.

<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=85 printed_page=83 -->

General Assembly regarding the temporary statement which serves as a
preface to the directory of worship
Little or no progress has been made each year since the 7th
Assembly Each year, the General Assembly has authorized the
Suft( YJmmittee on Judicial Business to continue working on the matter
This year, it was concluded that a fresh beginning might be helpful The

The Subcommittee was generally pleased with the progress made
