to the attention of the assembly and the church for reaction and
comment ”

Apparently, the SCJB felt that its function was to submit language not only for a
prefatory statement but for specific chapters of the DFW, as well

Facts not in dispute
based on the historical evidence of the minutes of the Third, Sixth, Seventh, Eighth.
Ninth, and Eleventh Assemblies, we may draw a statement of facts that the majority and
minority will agree to

1 TheFirst, Second and Third Assemblies suspended the normal amendment
procedure as part of the process of crafting the constitutional documents
2 The Third Assembly created no special procedure for consideration and
adopting the DFW that was different from that used to adopt the FOG and
ROD
3. The Third Assembly felt it necessary to attach a temporary Prefatory
Statement to the DFW prior to its being adopted
4 The Third Assembly charged the Committee on Constitutional Documents
with the task of developing a statement on the “relative authority of the
Constitutional Documents ”
5 Upon the adoption of the DFW, including its Preface, the Third Assembly
declared the constitution to be fully adopted and the suspension of the rules
for amendment (BCO 27-2) was ended
6 The GA conducted a continuing effort, under the auspices of the Subcommittee on Judicial Business, to bring revised language to the floor of
assemblies the purpose of this revised language was to replace either the
temporary prefatory language adopted at the 3rd ga or specific language of

<a id="ga28-p84"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=86 printed_page=84 -->

the actual chapters of the DOW adopted at the 3rd GA. (Editorial note, the Directory has been referred to as the Directory of Worship at some Assemblies and as the Directory for Worship at others. It is currently titled Directory for Worship.)

7. At some point prior to or during the 8th GA, questions regarding the status and authority of the DFW caused the Assembly to send parts of it to the Presbyteries in order to remove their temporary status and invest them with “full constitutional authority.” Three chapters of the DFW in BCO enjoy this special status and they are 56, 57 &amp; 58 (though in early Assemblies they were numbered 57, 58 &amp; 59).

8. Since the 3rd GA had restored BCO 27-2, any amendments to BCO subsequent to that Assembly had to follow the procedures specified therein.

9. Any amendments proposed to BCO, including 47-55 and 59-63, by the 28th GA will have to follow the procedures of BCO 27-2.

10. Three chapters of the DFW have “full constitutional authority” and the balance have some other status that is described as not having the “force of law” and not considered “obligatory in all its parts.”

## The Majority Conclusion

The majority of the Committee believes that the actions taken by the first three Assemblies demonstrate that the commissioners understood that they were creating a document that came to be entitled the Book of Church Order. It is clear that this Book has three major sections and that in developing it they were working on the creation of one of the constitutional documents. This is further demonstrated by the adoption of the temporary statement, in the first place. Commissioners were not happy with the language of the Directory for a number of reasons.

During the Third GA it became apparent that the process was going to be much slower than anyone anticipated. The first two segments of the BCO were in place but some strategy was needed to break the impasse over the DFW. There was concern that if this language were part of the BCO and therefore the constitutional documents it would be enforceable and they would be obliged to it (in the same sense that any other language of the BCO is enforceable and obligatory), possibly in violation of their consciences. The way to include the DOW in the BCO and constitutional documents came in the form of the temporary statement. Those who did not like the language were satisfied that they would not be held to it when it became part of BCO. The DOW would be included in BCO but not be considered enforceable or obligatory (whatever that means). This compromise worked and the language was approved as part of the BCO. The language would be an approved guide and those who liked it could embrace it.

The minority opinion at this point invokes the definition of the Constitution and hangs onto the final phrase. The Preface to BCO, III defines the constitution as:

“The Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America, which is subject to and subordinate to the Scriptures of the Old and New Testaments, the inerrant Word Of God, consists of its doctrinal standards set forth in the Westminster Confession of Faith, together with the Larger and Shorter Catechisms, and the

<a id="ga28-p85"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=87 printed_page=85 -->

Book of Church Order, comprising the Form of Government, the Rules of Discipline and the Directory for Worship, *all as adopted by the Church* (Emphasis added)

The minority would agree that, in fact, the DFW was adopted, but it was adopted as an approved guide (taken from the “Temporary Statement” of the Preface of the DFW). This point we do not argue. The 3rd GA did, in fact, adopt the DFW as a temporary guide without the “force of law” and not “obligatory in all its parts.” None-the-less, it adopted it. And, it adopted it right smack in the middle of completing work on the BCO and under the special rules (suspension of the amendment process), which it created just for that task. So, the constitution of the church is comprised of *all* those documents listed in the Preface to BCO, “all as adopted by the Church.” All of the documents that it adopted which are listed in the Preface, by definition, make up the constitutional documents of the PCA. None of those documents are part of the constitution unless and until they are adopted, and no other document may be adopted as part of the constitution without changing this Preface. Granted that the framers of our constitution enacted language and then stripped it of its “enforcement authority” and “obligatory nature,” but such was the necessary compromise needed to complete the crafting of the constitution and finish the work begun by the 1st GA. But these are the items that make up our constitution, all as adopted by the court. Some were adopted without the force of law and obligation, but all are part of the constitution, “all,” as they were “adopted by the Church.”

At this point, the Committee is compelled to remind the Assembly that this report is not dealing with the “relative authority” of the constitutional documents nor the nature of the phases “force of law” and “obligatory in all its parts.” Care must be exercised not to characterize any of this discussion as addressing those concepts.

The fact that the Constitutional Documents Committee was charged to draft the temporary statement, . . .”until a more precise statement can be prepared” indicates that the language was a work in process. Indeed, the objective that the Assembly had in mind was a statement on the “relative authority” of the constitutional documents. Just how authoritative are these documents that we are including into the constitution? Will all portions of the BCO be as binding and authoritative as others – or as binding and authoritative as the WCF or Scripture? These were some of the questions leading up to the charge to the CDC. As a result, the DOW, which was adopted, was understood to be part of BCO but still a work that would undergo continued development by future Assemblies. It was as good as it could be at the moment and it was adopted into BCO as an approved guide but with language that would permit those with reservations to “live with it.” And that work did continue until the Assembly dismantled the SCJB replacing it with the SJC and CCB. There is currently no Assembly committee or agency formally charged with the continuing development of the DFW.

Even if one were to carve the DFW out of the constitution because it is not “enforceable” or “obligatory”, it would be very difficult to see how it is not part of the

<a id="ga28-p86"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=88 printed_page=86 -->

$BCO$. All of the actions from the $1^{\text{st}}$ to the 3rd GA assumed that the DFW was part of the $BCO$. Every reference to these chapters in the records of the General Assemblies is a reference to “$BCO$ XX,” not to “Approved Guide chapter XX.”

The actions of the $8^{\text{th}}$ and $9^{\text{th}}$ GA’s granting three chapters “full” constitutional authority did bring the matter into sharper focus. This action granted a greater measure of accountability from our churches with respect to Chapters 56, 57 &amp; 58 as the Assembly changed the relationship of those chapters to our churches. This action was required by a need for clarity on the validity and obligatory nature of the vows contained in these three chapters. These parts of the $BCO$, indeed, these parts of the constitutional documents, are now invested with the force of law and obligation (in the same way that it is understood for the rest of $BCO$). This is what prompted the Stated Clerk to add the note of explanation to the minutes and that note was not challenged as to its precision. The note reminded the Assembly that the $3^{\text{rd}}$ GA had already adopted the DOW (now the DFW) as part of the $BCO$ making it an integral part of the constitutional documents. The time to clarify the action of the Assembly was at that precise moment. If that was not the intent or understanding of the GA, it should have taken action to say so. At this point, the approved, adopted, accepted, operative and historic language is in place and we must be guided by it unless and until future Assemblies act otherwise.

Further, it was necessary to send this change to the Presbyteries since granting this full authority required a change to the Preface. $BCO$ 27-2 was in full effect and the Assembly had no authority to act as it had formerly during the first three meetings. The three chapters were not being amended to the constitution or $BCO$ since they were already part of it but the new relationship of authority and obligation to the churches (in particular the vows in those chapters) required the churches to adopt the change to the Preface and the status of the language. The promise made earlier by the Assembly to the commissioners and churches was being annulled with respect to these three chapters and the constitutional change required the process of $BCO$ 27-2. Though the language of the three chapters was not being changed (though the process would allow changes if they conformed to the amendment process) the “relative authority” of these chapters to the constitutional documents was changing. These three chapters ceased to be a work in process and their temporary nature was removed. The balance of the language of the DFW retained its status as adopted by the 3rd GA. That point needs to be stressed – the action to change Chapters 56, 57 &amp; 58 did not in any way alter the nature, status or relationship to the Church of any other part of the DFW from its original adoption by the $3^{\text{rd}}$ GA.

The concern has also been raised that the DFW may not be amended under the provisions of $BCO$ 27-2 since it is not actually a part of the $BCO$ or constitutional documents. The actions of a number of Assemblies noted above demonstrate that the DFW is seen as part of the $BCO$ and therefore subject to the procedure of $BCO$ 27-2. There is also some concern that if the GA submits some portion of the DFW to the Presbyteries as part of an amendment procedure that any amendment would automatically be granted “full constitutional authority” in the same way that Chapters

<a id="ga28-p87"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=89 printed_page=87 -->

56, 57 &amp; 58 were. This is not the case. The Presbyteries would have to take the same action as was done between the 8th and 9th Assemblies and vote on precise language to grant “full” constitutional authority. Indeed, the Preface language would also have to be removed or amended before any action to invest parts or all of Chapters 47-55 and 59-63 with “full” constitutional authority. This is an obligation that the PCA assumed back in the 3rd GA. A solemn promise was made to the churches and commissioners that the language of the DFW, though part of the BCO and constitutional documents, would be held as not enforceable or obligatory until more precise language could be developed and adopted by the normal amendment procedure. Great care must be exercised to guard that trust and obligation.

The Committee, therefore, respectfully submits its proposed responses to the 28th General Assembly in the matters of Overture 2 from Grace Presbytery and Overture 10 from Mississippi Valley Presbytery.

## Minority Report to the Answer of CCB to Overture 2 from Grace Presbytery and Overture 10 from Mississippi Valley Presbytery

### In the matter of the Directory for Worship of the Book of Church Order

In the CCB discussion of Overtures 2 and 10, serious questions arose concerning whether or not the Directory for Worship (DFW) chapters 47-55 and 59-63 have ever been adopted as part of our constitutional standards. The unique, even peculiar, nature of the "Temporary statement" which serves as a preface to the DFW has produced a situation, which members of the CCB often spoke of as a quagmire. Some members felt that DFW 47-55 and 59-63 were part of the constitution but unenforceable; other members felt that those paragraphs were somehow quasi-constitutional or partially constitutional; the Stated Clerk referred to them as having the authority of an "in thesi" paper; many members simply concluded that it is unclear or confusing as to whether or not the paragraphs are part of the constitutional standards of the P.C.A.

To help the Bills and Overtures Committee of Commissioners understand why the confusion exists, it helps to see the relevant portions of the BCO together.

## Preface: III The Constitution Defined

The Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America, which is subject to and subordinate to the Scriptures of the Old and New Testaments, the inerrant Word Of God, consists of its doctrinal standards set forth in the Westminster Confession of Faith, together with the Larger and Shorter Catechisms, and the Book of Church Order, comprising the Form of Government, the Rules of Discipline and the Directory for Worship; all as adopted by the Church.

## Preface to the DFW

Temporary statement adopted by the Third General Assembly to preface the Directory for Worship. The Directory for Worship is an approved guide and should be taken seriously as the mind of the Church agreeable to the Standards. However, it does not

<a id="ga28-p88"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=90 printed_page=88 -->

have the force of law and is not to be considered obligatory in all its parts. BCO 56, 57, and 58 have been given full constitutional authority by the Eleventh General Assembly after being submitted to the Presbyteries and receiving the necessary two-thirds (2/3) approval of the Presbyteries.

While the CCB did finally agree to conclude that these portions of the DFW seem to be constitutional, this minority of one was not persuaded to agree. It is the position of this minority of one that DFW chapters 47-55 and 59-63 are not part of our constitutional standards for the following reasons:

1. In "III. The Constitution Defined", it specifically delineates that the DFW is a component part of our constitution. However, it clearly qualifies this assertion by inserting that very important criterion: "All as adopted by the church!" The critical question for us to consider is not whether or not the church has ever adopted DFW 47-55 and 59-63, because it has -- this was done at our Third General Assembly.

The real question is, what were they adopted as? Were they adopted as part of our constitution? The answer is clearly - no. The "Temporary statement" Preface to the DFW is the umbrella under whose shadow all of the DFW was adopted at the Third Assembly. The "Temporary statement" specifically limits the nature of the adoption, which occurred at that Third Assembly:

- The DFW was adopted only as "an approved guide"

- No paragraph of the DFW was given constitutional authority until the Tenth and Eleventh Assemblies acted to grant such status to BCO 56, 57, and 58. (The status changed was only granted after paragraphs 56, 57, and 58 were subjected to the normal BCO amendment status as prescribed in BCO 26-2.)

2. The "Temporary statement" takes a second step to make it clear that the DFW 47-55 and 59-63 are not part of our constitutional standards by specifically stating "it does not have the force of law." It seems to this minority of one that the very nature of the constitution is that it consists of the standards of our church which do in fact function to have the force of law upon us. If it does not have the force of law upon us, then it can not be a component of our constitutional standards.

3. The "Temporary statement" takes a third step to insure that everybody will understand that DFW 47-55 and 59-63 can not possibly be misunderstood to be a part of our constitutional standards. The "Temporary statement" clearly teaches that this DFW is "agreeable to the Standards". This statement is compelling to this minority of one. Logic absolutely demands that if something is "agreeable to the Standards" then they are not in fact part of the Standards themselves.

4. The "Temporary statement" delineates that the three paragraphs (56-58), which have become part of our constitutional standards did so by going through the BCO 26-2 process. These three paragraphs had no privileged status, which one might expect if somehow they were already part of the constitution. These three paragraphs went through the exact same process that any other non-constitutional language must go through to be adopted as constitutional

<a id="ga28-p89"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=91 printed_page=89 -->

language. These three paragraphs were new amendments to our constitution and had to be adopted as such.

Given this conclusion that the DFW 47-55 and 59-63 have no constitutional status as they presently sit, then it is the opinion of this minority of one that the CCB should have answered Overture 2 (from Grace Presbytery) and Overture 10 (from Mississippi Valley Presbytery) as follows:

## CCB Minority Report:

### Proposed Answer to Overture 2 from Grace Presbytery

It is the opinion of the minority that the request of Overture 2 (from Grace Presbytery) to rescind the current prefatory statement to the Directory for Worship (DFW) will not create conflicts with other portions of our Constitution.

It is the opinion of the minority, however, that the end effect of rescinding the prefatory statement will be radically different from the effect anticipated by Grace Presbytery. Grace Presbytery is mistaken when she assumes that the effect of rescinding the prefatory statement will be to grant the entire Directory of Worship full constitutional status and weight. Chapters 47-55 and 59-63 have never been adopted as paragraphs with full constitutional status. They have only been adopted as an "approved guide". While they are "agreeable to the Standards", they are not part of the Standards. Certainly, the procedure used to adopt BCO 56-58 as having full constitutional authority would also need to be used to adopt 47-55 and 59-63 as part of our constitutional authority. The procedure used to adopt BCO 56-58 is the normal BCO amendment process prescribed in BCO 26-2; this is a process which allows consideration, deliberation, debate, and amendment upon each chapter, and if need be each paragraph, of the newly proposed language. The real effect of rescinding the prefatory statement and doing nothing else will be to effectively remove all references to 47-55 and 59-63 from the printed pages of our BCO since the umbrella under which they existed in our book has now been rescinded.

It is the opinion of the minority that if Grace Presbytery wishes to accomplish her desired end, then she must make two proposals at the same time:

1. Request General Assembly to rescind the preface "Temporary statement" and
2. Request General Assembly to amend the BCO by adopting the present language of paragraphs 47-55 and 59-63 to become part of our constitution. (Each paragraph, of course, would be subject to the normal debate and amendment processes.)

## CCB Minority Report:

### Proposed Answer to Overture 10 from Mississippi Valley Presbytery

It is the opinion of the minority that there is no constitutional mechanism in place to change the language of DFW 53-6. The process prescribed in BCO 26-2 would not be appropriate to apply to DFW 53-6 unless it was the desire of Mississippi Valley Presbytery to see the General Assembly add 53-6 to the other three DFW paragraphs

<a id="ga28-p90"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=92 printed_page=90 -->

(56, 57, 58) as part of our constitutional standards. This certainly would not be wise to do unless all of the rest of chapter 53 was also included.

Our Stated Clerk suggested at our meeting that these DFW 47-55 and 59-63 paragraphs might well be treated as we would an *in thexi* paper such as the Pastoral Letter on Spiritual Gifts or the Paper on Freemasonry. While there may be little purpose in seeking to change the language of such documents, it might be accomplished by asking the General Assembly to reconsider the document and then to change the language. The *BCO* 26-2 process would not be required -- only by majority vote at one meeting of our General Assembly.

Submitted by Dr. Craig D. Childs, Sr., Teaching Elder, Member of CCB

## SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT OF THE COMMITTEE ON CONSTITUTIONAL BUSINESS

### I. Introduction

Your Committee on Constitutional Business (CCB) met prior to the 28th General Assembly, on June 20, 2000 in Tampa, Florida. The CCB was able at that meeting to complete all of the work then assigned to it. Attendance was as follows:

### III. Non-Judicial References

**Reformed University Ministries as a Permanent Committee of GA**

Moved, seconded and it carried that it be found not in conflict with any other part of the constitution. [See text and action at 28-31, III, 12 p. 105ff] 8-0-0

<a id="ga28-p91"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=93 printed_page=91 -->

IV. Overtures
OVERTURE 26 - South Coast Presbyter) “ Request for General Assembly Action
Regarding Central Florida Presbytery's Receiving Former Member of
South Coast Presbytery”
It was moved, seconded and it carried to advise the Stated Clerk that the Committee
concurs with his advice in this matter to refer this to the SJC [BCO 15-4) 8-0-0
[Action referred to SJC to report to the 29* GA]

OVERTURE (MEMORIAL) 26 From South Coast Presbyters
(to CCB & SJC)
“Request for General Assembly Action Regarding Central Florida
Presbytery’s receiving former member of south coast Presbytery”

Whereas, the General Assembly has the responsibility to take cognizance of any
neglect or irregularity that has taken place ina presbytery of which it is
well advised, and to examine, deliberate and judge the whole matter
(BCO 40-4),

Whereas, central florida presbytery of the PCA at their meeting on april 18.
2000, did receive Dr Mark Futato by transfer as a minister in good
standing, laboring out of bounds at reformed theological Seminary,
Orlando,

Whereas, there remain charges outstanding against Dr Futato. which were
found to be in order a ta called meeting of south coast presbytery of the
PCA on April 10, 1999,

Whereas, after the delivery of those charges and before that meeting Dr Futato
apparently broke his fourth ordination vow and did renounce the
jurisdiction of the PCA in order to avoid facing the properly-constituted
charges.

Whereas, the existence of these charges was communicated to the independent
charismatic church which received Dr Futato (and which now no longer
exists), but the right to adjudicate the charges should Dr Futato ever
return to the PCA was not relinquished by South Coast Presbytery.

Whereas, Central Florida Presbytery, although made aware of the existence of
the charges by several individual members of south coast Presbytery,
made no effort to inquire of south coast presbytery whether the issues
involved in the charges had ever been addressed and settled, and did not
direct Dr Futato to repent of his independent spirit and refusal to submit
himself to his brethren, prior to consideration of his reception by their
Presbytery,

Whereas, the Stated Clerk of the PCA, expressed his opinion on this matter in a
letter to Dr Luder Whitlock dated February 28, 2000, that Dr. Futato
“ should provide the Presbytery in Florida, a copy of the charges from
South Coast Presbytery, and request that the Presbytery in Florida deal
with the case ” Nonetheless, even should South Coast Presbytery agree
to remit the case to Central Florida Presbytery, such an adjudication ina

<a id="ga28-p92"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=94 printed_page=92 -->

case with process must necessarily involve a trial in accordance with the provisions of *BCO* 32.

**Therefore**, we the members of South Coast Presbytery of the PCA, hereby memorialize General Assembly and protest against the grossly unconstitutional proceedings of Central Florida Presbytery of the PCA in receiving Dr. Futato without adequate investigation of his status, thereby exhibiting a blatant disregard for the legitimate concerns of a fellow Presbytery. We further make the following overtures to the General Assembly:

1. That the General Assembly act to reverse the April 18, 2000, decision of Central Florida Presbytery of the PCA in receiving Dr. Futato as a minister of the Presbyterian Church in America (*BCO* 40-5);

2. That the General Assembly take appropriate disciplinary measures against Central Florida Presbytery for its willful disregard of the properly-constituted charges against Dr. Futato, which remain to be answered, if Central Florida Presbytery does not act in a satisfactory fashion by the time of the 29th General Assembly.

3. That the General Assembly instruct the Central Florida Presbytery to inform Dr. Futato of the necessity for him to be cleared of charges against him before the South Coast Presbytery prior to his being received by Central Florida Presbytery.

4. That the General Assembly should enact these measures by any lawful means possible, including, if deemed necessary, the conduct of a trial on the floor of General Assembly (*BCO* 40-5 and 40-6).

We bring these overtures to General Assembly with a deep sense of concern. Our desire is to promote the purity, peace, and unity of the church. Nevertheless, if in order to avoid legitimately constituted charges, a minister has simply to renounce the jurisdiction of one Presbytery in the PCA and after one year without regard to the pending charges be received by another Presbytery of the PCA, there is no possibility of effective church discipline.

If this action by Central Florida Presbytery is allowed to stand, we will be a Presbyterian Church in name only, not in fact. The appearance will be that individual Presbyteries will have been granted the right to do whatever seems right in their own eyes, with no reference to the legitimate concerns of their fellow Presbyteries.

Apart from any technicalities of the case, Central Florida Presbytery has treated the court system of the PCA with disdain by neglecting the interests of South Coast Presbytery.

In the light of the seriousness of these matters and the fact that continual vigilance is required to combat false doctrine (Acts 20:28-32), we request all members of General Assembly to devote themselves to persistent, humble

<a id="ga28-p93"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=95 printed_page=93 -->

prayer, seeking God’s wisdom, love, and guidance regarding General Assembly’s discussion and decisions concerning these issues.

Adopted by South Coast Presbytery on May 1, 2000
Attested by: /s/ TE Ron Gleason, Recording Clerk of South Coast Presbytery

## OVERTURE 1 – Heartland Presbytery “Declare Full Preterism a Heresy”

It was moved, seconded and it carried that the Committee respond that the process of overture to ask the General Assembly to “bear testimony against error in doctrine,” (BCO 14-6,a.), is not in conflict with any part of the constitution. We further advise that the normal mechanism to declare doctrinal positions is through amendments to the constitutional standards, through judicial process or through study committees appointed by the General Assembly. These processes permit a full study of all the particulars being addressed. [Text and action at 28-72, III, 17, p. 297.] 8-0-0

It was moved, seconded and it carried that the Committee finds the recommendation from Bills &amp; Overtures related to Overture 4, from James River Presbytery, is not in conflict with any other provision of the constitution. 8-0-0

[Text and action at 28-72, III, 12, p 288]

[Text and action at 28-72, III, 2, p 278]

[Text and action at 28-72, III, 4, p. 281.]

Respectfully submitted,

28-23 Committee of Commissioners on Covenant Theological Seminary

TE Dale Welden, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the Report. The report was closed with prayer by TE Welden, and the report of the Seminary was postponed until later. [See also Appendix F, p. 468.]

#### I. Business Referred to the Committee

a The Report of CTS
b The minutes of the following meetings of the Board of Trustees:
1) May 14-15, 1999
2) September 24-25, 1999
3) January 28-29, 2000
c The minutes of the following meetings of the executive committee of the Board of Trustees:
1) December 3, 1999
2) March 3, 2000
d The financial audit for CTS and the proposed 2000-20001 CTS budget

#### II. Statement Of Major Issues Discussed

Following President Bryan Chapell’s report to the Committee of Commissioners he was asked a number of questions in various areas. Questions were raised concerning CTS’s theological positions on such matters as the role of women in the church, creation, and the regulative principle of worship. Other areas discussed included the exciting new Youth in Ministry Institute (YIMI) now being developed through a 1.2 million dollar Lilly endowment, the need for the Seminary’s faculty, staff, and Board to reflect a broader racial mix, the decision as of now not to establish multiple campuses (although CTS does operate courses in multiple sites), the faculty-student ratio in light of the tremendous 11 year growth in the student body (ratio goal of 1:16 is presently at 1:16.5), the continued desire of CTS to be held accountable to the PCA and its Reformed standards, and the spiritual climate of the faculty and student body.

#### III. Recommendations

1 That the General Assembly give thanks to God for the ministry of Covenant Theological Seminary, for its faithfulness to the Scriptures, the Reformed faith, and the Great Commission, for its students and graduates, its faculty and staff,

<a id="ga28-p95"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=97 printed_page=95 -->

its Trustees, and for those who support the Seminary through their prayers and
gifts. Adopted
2. that the General Assembly encourage the congregations of the Presbyterian Church in America to support the ministry of covenant theological seminary
through their prayers and gifts, and by recommending the Seminary to
prospective students Adopted
3. that the General Assembly encourage the congregations of the Presbyterian Church in America to support the ministry of covenant theological seminary
by contributing the Partnership Shares approved by the General Assembly
Adopted
4. that the minutes of the meetings of the board of trustees of covenant
Theological Seminary for May 14-15, 1999, September 24-25, 1999, and

8 The following motion was approved by the 27th General Assembly: That the

Churches to the General Assembly and to report its findings to the 28th General Assembly.
The GA/MNA Permanent Committee recommends that the 28th General Assembly adopt the following responses:
• the purpose of the coalition of korean presbyterian churches of the PCA,
as stated in its Bylaws, is “to maintain Christian fellowship and close
relationships among Korean churches in the PCA and to discuss and carry
out ministry activities in cooperation.”
• The 28th General Assembly commends the korean language churches of the
PCA for their labors in the Gospel.

encourages the Coalition to bring to the GA/MNA Permanent Committee,
through TE Henry Koh as GA/MNA Korean Ministries Coordinator, any
desires the coalition may have for communication with the General ssembly.
Adopted
9. The 27th General Assembly instructed the GA/MNA Permanent Committee to
study the issue of women speaking and teaching in public, and present clear
guidelines to the 28th General Assembly of their application of i timothy 2: 11-
12 and other scripture when selecting women for such ministry.

The GA/MNA permanent committee recommends approval of the following
response:
This instruction to the GA/MNA Committee was issued in response to a
specific instance of a woman teaching in an GA/MNA conference in February
1999. For this reason, and because GA/MNA does not possess the authority to
selector assign people to other types of speaking and teaching ministries (except
assigning teaching elders as church planters), we understand the parameters of
our response to be limited to the issue of choosing women for speaking and
teaching in seminars, conferences and worship services conducted under the
auspices of GA/MNA

<a id="ga28-p102"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=104 printed_page=102 -->

GA/MNA conducts two kinds of activities at its conferences and seminars that are addressed by this motion. One activity is worship. The Scriptures, as interpreted by the subordinate standards, give sufficient standards for the ordering of public worship. The preaching of the Word in worship is to be protected, and that protection is assured as the preaching of the Word is conducted by elders. GA/MNA adheres to this standard in the worship services that it conducts.

The other activity is seminars designed to provide church planters and other leaders with greater insights and skills for their ministry. While GA/MNA is careful to assure that worthy material is presented in these seminars, the context is not that of a worship service, and therefore the subject matter does not require the same degree of protection as the content of worship.

The primary difference between the preaching of the Word in worship and the subject matter of seminars has at its heart the issue of authority. The content of seminars is not intended to carry the weight to which the description “teach or have authority over” (1 Timothy 2:11-12) would apply. The context of this passage is the Apostle’s presentation of instructions for the proper conduct of public worship and not that of the more informal seminar which is generally more subjective, informal and based on personal experience than is the preaching of the Word in worship.

In most cases, teaching and ruling elders present the content in the seminar context. However, from time to time, GA/MNA may ask that godly men and women give presentations even though they are not elders, but who in God’s providence may have insights and experience which may prove helpful and instructive to those who lead in ministry.
Adopted

Time expired for debate, and the Moderator ruled that included all matters before the court. His ruling was challenged, and he was sustained.

The following amendment was defeated: That the GA give the following instructions to the MNA Committee:

1. The MNA Committee will not allow women to teach or preach in worship services as this is clearly forbidden by God’s word, and is contrary to our subordinate standards. Worship services shall be conducted by the elders of the Church. (1 Timothy 2:11-12/WCF XXI/BCO 8, 12, 53).

2. The MNA Committee will not employ women as keynote speakers or expositors of the Bible in seminars except when those seminars have women as the primary audience. (1 Timothy 2:11-12; Titus 2:2-3/BCO 12).

3. The MNA Committee may from time to time invite godly women, who in God’s providence may have insights and experience which may prove helpful to those who lead in ministry, to give informational presentations to audiences comprised of men or women, in a seminar or similar context. (Romans 12:10-13/WCF XXVI/BCO 12)

<a id="ga28-p103"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=105 printed_page=103 -->

*Defeated 381-599*

## MNA MINORITY REPORT #1 ON OVERTURE #3

**Whereas** the church is exhorted to pursue Christian unity for the glory of Christ and the extension of His kingdom, (John 17:21)

**Whereas** Christian union within a denomination is predicated upon a common confession (2 Cor.13:11, Rom.16:17-18, WCF 26.1)

**Whereas** to affirm division within a denomination on the basis of “doctrinal differences” is to affirm schism (1Cor.1:10, Rom.14:19)

**THEREFORE**, be it resolved, that Overture #3 be deferred for one year, and that a commission be erected by the moderator to visit Westminster Presbytery as soon as possible and as often as necessary pursuant to BCO 14-6.d, g, i, k; 15-2 to take necessary measures to bring reconciliation among the brethren, and that the Church call for a day of fasting and prayer by all PCA officers with a view to bringing about reconciliation between our brethren in Westminster Presbytery.

|  /s/ TE Paul Hahn | /s/ TE James Phillis  |
| --- | --- |
|  /s/ RE Don Maurer | /s/ RE Darryl Richards  |
|  /s/ RE Christian Lewis | /s/ TE Scott R. Wright  |
|  Minority Report #1 was defeated  |   |

## MINORITY REPORT #2, OVERTURE #3

**Whereas** the church is exhorted to pursue Christian unity for the glory of Christ and the extension of His kingdom, (Jn.17:21)

**Whereas** Christian union within a denomination is predicated upon a common confession of Faith (2Cor.13:11, Rom.16:17-18, WCF 26.1)

**Whereas** to affirm division within a denomination on the basis of “doctrinal differences” is to affirm schism (1Cor.1:10, Rom.14:19)

**Therefore**, be it resolved to answer Overture #3 in the affirmative as amended:

<a id="ga28-p104"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=106 printed_page=104 -->

##### OVERTURE 3 From Westminster Presbytery

“Divide Westminster Presbytery into Two Presbyteries”

Whereas, Westminster has grown to include 26 churches; and

Whereas, there are significant doctrinal differences among the brethren within
Westminster Presbytery, and

Whereas^ these doctrinal differences produce significant differences in philosophy and
practice of ministry; and

Whereas, we have sought for years to reconcile our differences, but have come to the
conclusion—that—the—differences—betw een—as—afe—tee—fundamental— for
reconciliation; and

Whereas, these differences have so divided us that we are no longer able to work
together effectively as a court of the church, and

Whereas, such present theological division prevents us from promoting the kingdom of
God in all respects, including church planting, evangelism, Christian education;
u llli

Whereas, at the present -time , we are able to separate ourselves in a respectful manner
without bitterness or rancor, and

Whereas, there is a lack of regional cohesiveness in the presbytery due to its spanning
two states and historical precedence for a geographical division along these
lines; and

Whereas, present boundaries do not promote shared ministries due to distance and
travel time required, and

Whereas, these differences in our Presbytery generally exist between churches that are
separated along geographical boundaries, and

Whereas, for the common good of the churches within westminster Presbytery;

Now therefore, be it resolved, that Westminster Presbytery hereby overtures the 28th
General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to make a
geographical division of westminster Presbytery, effective September 1, 2000,
as follows:
the churches and ministers in the tennessee counties of Hancock,
Hawkins, Greene, Washington, Sullivan, Carter, Unicoi and Johnson
will comprise one new Presbytery,
the churches and ministers in the virginia counties of Buchanan,
Tazewell, Scott, Bland, Dickinson, Wythe, Grayson, Lee, Smyth,
Russell, Wise and Washington will comprise Westminster Presbytery;
the churches and ministers in the virginia counties of pulaski and
Carroll will be transferred to New River Presbytery;
The churohes, ministers, candidates and licentiates bearing unique
ties to either Presbytery shaH choose the particular Presbytery with
which they shall affiliate by September i , 2000.

/s/ TE Preston Graham, Jr. Is/ TE Ken Cross
/s/ RE Frank Young, Jr. /s/ RE Dennis Snider
Defeated

<a id="ga28-p105"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=107 printed_page=105 -->

11 That the General Assembly note that the entire 2000 budget of GA/MNA

12. The 24th and 25th General Assemblies received overtures from at least four
presbyteries requesting the separation of reformed university ministries from
mission to north america for the purpose of establishing reformed university
ministries as a permanent committee of the PCA.

Included in the General Assembly’s responses to these overtures is this motion,
approved by the 24th General Assembly:
The General Assembly believes that constituting Reformed University
Ministries as a new permanent committee may have significant advantages and
disadvantages for Reformed University Ministries, GA/MNA and for the General Assembly of the PCA. Further, the change proposed has never been formally
considered by GA/MNA or by General Assembly.
Therefore, the General Assembly recognizes that GA/MNA Committee has
appointed a study committee to study the relationship of reformed university
Ministries and GA/MNA, and awaits the report of its findings and recommendations to
the 25th General Assembly, by normal process, through the GA/MNA Committee.

For the following reasons, the GA/MNA Committee believes it is expedient that the
GA/MNA committee be released from responsibility for the oversight of reformed
University Ministries:
■ the rapid growth of reformed university ministries has been nurtured by the
protection and wisdom of GA/MNA during its critical formative years.
Reformed University Ministries will continue to mature as it assumes full
responsibility under the Lord for its future.
■ The size, scope and budget of reformed university ministries have grown to a
place of parity with that of GA/MNA, which legitimizes a move from a
subordinate ministry relationship to a full ministry partnership.
■ Reformed University Ministries’ distinctive mission, philosophy of ministry,
and leadership have matured to the place where further expansion (both national
and international), require that Reformed University Ministries fully accept the
privileges and responsibilities of a permanent program committee, accountable
to the PCA General Assembly.
■ The 24th and 25th General Assemblies received overtures from PCA Presbyteries
urging that Reformed University Ministries be permitted to become a permanent
program committee of the Presbyterian Church in America. This
recommendation affirms the (grassroots) wisdom that was brought forward
from the churches and local campus subcommittees.
■ the primary ministry of GA/MNA is the planting of churches, while campus
ministry is related, it is not an integral part of the church planting process. In

<a id="ga28-p106"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=108 printed_page=106 -->

particular, the GA/MNA Committee has far more expertise in governing church
planting than it does in governing campus ministries. Leaders who have the
strongest interest in the latter generally are elected to the GA/MNA campus
ministries subcommittee rather than the GA/MNA Committee. Functions that
are related, such as campus ministers moving into church planting, could be
facilitated just as easily if the two ministries were separate.
* In most cases, the presbyteries have separated the supervision of church planting
and campus ministries, so that a campus subcommittee reports to the presbytery
through presbytery GA/MNA, but the presbytery GA/MNA committee is little
involved with the leadership of the campus ministries.
■ much of the management of the two ministries is already separated internally,
such as accounting and audits, office space, staff roles. Current Reformed
University Ministries staff and subcommittee prefer to separate all aspects of
management.
■ in the perception of many even in the PCA membership, and certainly among
non-PCA students and others, there is little or no awareness of a connection
between the two ministries.
■ the financial support of reformed university ministries is already separate
from GA/MNA, except for the $100,000 that GA/MNA contributes annually to
Reformed University Ministries from Partnership Share receipts. This
commitment brings a limitation to GA/MNA fund raising, in that GA/MNA
must provide support, but cannot address the Reformed University Ministries
constituency to raise this support.
■ the field management structures of church planting and campus ministries are
quite different. Church planters are placed and become self-supporting, not
maintaining any formal relationship with GA/MNA on a long-term basis,
whereas campus ministries remain permanently in an affiliated status. This
means that the two organizations have different styles of operation, both
internally and externally.
■ The above factor also means that whereas GA/MNA may well fulfill its mission
on a long-term basis without seeing very much organizational growth, Reformed
University Ministries will grow ever larger as an organization as more campus
ministries begin. If current growth trends continue, the Reformed University
Ministries administrative staff will eventually be larger than GA/MNA staff.
■ The campus ministries subcommittee already has a defined role in selecting the
campus ministries coordinator such that the GA/MNA Coordinator cannot
unilaterally make his own choice of the person to fill this position, as he is
authorized by the GA/MNA Committee to do with all other staff.
■ For public relations and fund raising purposes, Reformed University Ministries
would be able to project a stronger image if it were separate from GA/MNA.

in light of the overtures received by the 24th and 25th General Assemblies, and the
considerations listed above, that Reformed University Ministries be separated from
the GA/MNA committee and be established as a permanent program committee of the

<a id="ga28-p107"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=109 printed_page=107 -->

General Assembly effective at the 29th General Assembly (2001), after due
constitutional process Adopted

ATTACHMENT A

in order to accomplish this separation the committee of commissioners approves
the following recommendation of the rum transition Committee:

1 accept the following recommendations of the rum transition committee on
behalf of the committee on mission to north america asset forth in approved
GA/MNA Committee minutes 3-00-92
3-00-92 MS&C: That the MNA Reformed University Ministries Campus Study
Committee, working with the Reformed University Ministries staff and
the MNA Reformed University Ministries Campus Subcommittee, be
commissioned to bring its recommendations concerning the future
relationship of reformed university ministries to the General Assembly,
at the 28‘ Genera1 Assembly, directly to the GA/MNA committee of
( 'ommissioners.
2. Recommend to the General Assembly that Reformed University Ministries be
established as a permanent committee of the GA/PCA and be named Committee
on Reformed University Ministries (ATTACHMENT A)
3 recommend to the General Assembly the Book of Church Order and Rules of Assembly Operations changes (amendments). (ATTACHMENT B)
4 Request that the respective presbyteries nominate, through the 2000-20001 GA
Nominating Committee, the individuals named for the particular classes in the
Proposed Initial Committee Structure, for the Committee on Reformed
University Ministries at the 29th General Assembly. (as amended on
ATTACHMENT C)

reformed university ministries
as a permanent committee of the PCA

I. In a cooperative spirit, the process of separation has been initiated by GA/MNA
in concurrence with Reformed University Ministries.
II. the rapid growth of reformed university ministries has been nurtured by the
protection and wisdom of GA/MNA during its critical formative years
Reformed University Ministries will continue to mature as it assumes full
responsibility under the Lord for its future
III. The size, scope and budget of reformed university ministries have grown to a
place of parity with that of GA/MNA, which legitimizes a move from a
subordinate ministry relationship to a full ministry partnership
IV. Reformed University Ministries’ distinctive mission, philosophy of ministry,
and leadership have matured to the place where further expansion (both national

<a id="ga28-p108"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=110 printed_page=108 -->

and international), require that Reformed University Ministries fully accept
privileges and responsibilities of a permanent program committee, accountable
to the PCA General Assembly
V. The 24th and 25th General Assemblies received overtures from at least four
PCA Presbyteries urging that Reformed University Ministries be permitted to
become a permanent program committee of the PCA this recommendation
affirms the (grassroots) wisdom that was brought forward from the churches and
local campus subcommittee.
VI the primary ministry of GA/MNA is the planting of churches; while campus
ministry is related, it is not an integral part of the church planting process in
particular, the GA/MNA Committee has far more expertise in governing church
planting than it does in governing campus ministries. Leaders who have the
strongest interest in the latter generally are elected to the GA/MNA campus
ministries subcommittee rather than the GA/MNA Committee. Functions that
are related, such as campus ministers moving into church planting, could be
facilitated just as easily if the two ministries separate
VII. In most cases, the presbyteries have separated the supervision of church planting
and campus ministries, so that a campus sub-committee reports to the presbytery
through presbytery MNA, but the presbytery MNA committee is little involved
with the leadership of the campus ministries
viii much of the management of the two ministries is already separated internally,
such as accounting and audits, office space, staff roles. Current Reformed
University Ministries staff and subcommittee prefer to separate all aspects of
management
IX. in the perception of many even in the PCA membership, and certainly among
non-PCA students and others, there is little or no awareness of a connection
between the two ministries.
X. the field management structures of church planting and campus ministries are
quite different. Church planters are placed and become self-supporting, not
maintaining any formal relationship with GA/MNA on a long-term basis,
whereas campus ministries remain permanently in an affiliated status This
means that the two organizations have different styles of operation, both
internally and externally.
XI The above factor also means that whereas GA/MNA may well fulfill its mission
on a long-term basis without seeing very much organizational growth, Reformed
University Ministries will grow ever larger as an organization as more campus
ministries begin If current growth trends continue, the Reformed University
Ministries administrative staff will eventually be larger than other GA/MNA
staff.
XII. For public relations and fund raising purposes, Reformed University Ministries
would be able to project a stronger image if it were separate from GA/MNA

<a id="ga28-p109"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=111 printed_page=109 -->

attachment c

reformed university ministries
proposed initial committee structure

I. since there is no precedent for the formation of anew permanent committee in
the PCA, it is the recommendation of the transition committee that primarily,
members of the current MNA subcommittee on campus ministries be assigned
to classes and constitute the new permanent Committee on Reformed University
Ministries After the first year, this committee will then be subject to the
nominating process of the GA. This will allow for a smoother transition and
necessary continuity in the formative years.
II. in making recommendations about the composition of the committee on
Reformed University Ministries, and the future committee make-up, several
factors must be acknowledged and balanced to ensure equity and good-judgment
in these next several years:
A. Consistent with the Overture arising from Missouri Presbytery to the
General Assembly of 2000, five classes are being recommended with
three men in each class
B. A balance of Ruling and Teaching Elders is essential for the well-being
of rum and the acceptance of the PCA:
1. To ensure the balance, classes will alternate between one Ruling and
two Teaching Elders, and one Teaching and two Ruling Elders
2. Because there have been far more Teaching Elders on the previously
existing Subcommittee for Campus Work, the first class
recommended will be one Ruling and two Teaching Elders The

<a id="ga28-p111"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=113 printed_page=111 -->

following class will implement the alternate of one teaching and two
Ruling Elders
C. Obviously, Reformed University Ministries had its origin in the
southeastern United States Therefore, the concentration of available and
knowledgeable Teaching and Ruling Elders is in the southeast The fact
cannot be denied However, to demonstrate the Biblical, magnanimous
and Presbyterian spirit, the representation on this Committee must also
be as representative of the denomination as possible. Both factors must
be held as equally important.
D. certain elders have been very involved in the development of reformed
University Ministries through the years, and carry a plethora of
understanding of rum these individuals should have a major role in
helping RUM be established as a standing committee amidst the General Assembly.
E. Elders who have served on affiliated committees, and, especially those
who have served on the General Assembly MNA Campus
Subcommittee, have information and expertise to contribute in behalf of
the General Assembly for the well-being of campus ministry

With such factors in mind, the following list is an attempt to recommend names for the
yet-to-be-established Permanent Committee on Reformed University Ministries.

2 TE John G ess...................Fellowship Presbytery, SC

2. RE Glenn Bledsoe...........Southeast Alabama Presbytery, AL

2. TE Andy Phillips........... Susquehanna Valley Presbytery, PA
3. RE Dave Brow n.............Great Lakes Presbytery, OH

15. That Overture 19 from Northeast Presbytery requesting to form three new
Presbyteries (Southern New England, New York State and Northern New
England) out of the existing northeast presbytery (thereby dissolving the
existing Northeast Presbytery), be answered in the affirmative. The newly
formed new york presbytery would be limited to areas of new york previously
in the Northeast Presbytery. Adopted

##### OVERTURE 19 From Northeast Presbytery

“Dissolve Northeast Presbytery; Form Three New Presbyteries”

For the 28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to form
three new presbyteries out of the existing northeast presbytery (thereby
dissolving the existing Northeast Presbytery) as follows:
1. Southern New England Presbytery—
Geographical Boundaries: Connecticut (excluding the CT "panhandle"
that is currently in Metro Presbytery), Rhode Island and Massachusetts.
2. New York State Presbytery—
Geographical Boundaries:
The boundaries include all the Churches, ministers and territories in New
York State, excluding those churches, ministers and territories under the
jurisdiction of metropolitan new york presbytery and ascension
Presbytery ; and further, subject to the approval of ascension Presbytery,

<a id="ga28-p113"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=115 printed_page=113 -->

to include the churches, ministers and territories within New York State
but are currently under the jurisdiction of ascension Presbytery.

3. Northen New England Presbytery—
Geographical Boundaries:
Maine, New Hampshire, and Vermont

Rationale:
1. Geographical-

Whereas, the formation of the above constituted presbyteries would
significantly ease the burden of vast geographical distances between
churches and would have access to the major travel corridors that move
through the region; and

Whereas, the above stated boundaries are reflective of natural travel corridors
as follows:
Southern New England—A Southern New England Presbytery would
significantly ease the burden of vast geographical distances between
churches and would have access to the major travel corridors that move
through the tri-state region (1-95,1-91,1-84).
Northern New England— (1-95,1-93,1-89)
New York State—
There are some cities and towns in upstate New York which are not
presently under any presbytery jurisdiction (Utica, Syracuse, Watertown,
Elmira, Coming) and having no PCA churches therein, and these same
cities and towns are connected by major interstates (1-90, 1-81 and 1-17)
providing opportunities for population growth; and furthermore, there
are other cities (Rochester and Buffalo) under the jurisdiction of
Ascension Presbytery but have no PCA churches.

2. Mission—
whereas the great commission of our savior mandates that the church of jesus
christ is to make disciples of all nations and that church planting is the
most effective way of obeying this commandment, and

Whereas church planting is best accomplished as from local and regional
initiatives that will be further facilitated by more frequent and more
regionally based meetings; and
whereas the demographic realities of church planting are better understood and
accounted for by those closest to that demographic reality, and

Whereas it is more likely for churches if they sense a mission in their own
shared regional context to work toward the planting of new churches
through a coordinated strategy that is informed by a relatively precise
consensus about the demographic realities, and
whereas the formation of the above constituted presbyteries would move us
closer to a "reasonable size" that would allow for greater participation

<a id="ga28-p114"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=116 printed_page=114 -->

and cooperation in mission, prevenient shepherding, leadership
development and worship

3 History--

Whereas the Northeast Presbytery has spent the last two years working in
regional committees in preparation for the creation of three new
presbyteries
Explanation:
in the process leading to the formation of the metro new york
Presbytery in 1997, a general consensus was formed about the meaning
of presbytery in order to reflect a more authentic regional church model
the notion of "Presbytery" as a "regional church" pushed us to think of
the Presbytery not simply in judicial terms but also in ministry and
mission terms such as to more truly reflect the theology of an organically
connected church subsequent to the formation of the metro new york
Presbytery, a series of "vision meetings" were held (some in New Haven,
others on the floor of presbytery in special session) A consensus was
formed for a regional church model that more authentically expressed the
prophetic, priestly and kingly dynamics of church ministry (as related
then to the ministry of word, worship/prayer and diaconal/shepherding
kinds of activities). In order to better approximate these kinds of
ministry dynamics in our presbytery, it was decided at the September,
1998 presbytery meeting to restructure the Northeast Presbytery into
three sub-regional committees (New York State, Northern New England,
and Southern New England) The restructuring resulted in a committee
structure which was less "task" based and more "regional" based which
was intended to encourage a more holistic approach to doing the work of
the gospel as a regional church
it is therefore the judgment of the northeast presbytery that the
time has now come for these three regional committees to make our new
working relationship official with the creation of three new presbyteries,

Whereas, the consent of ascension presbytery is being sought for the release of
various churches and territories toward the goal of forming anew york
Presbytery.

RECOMMENDATION:

Therefore, be it resolved that the 28th General Assembly create three new
presbyteries, after the September, 2000 meeting of northeast Presbytery,
with the following boundaries:
Northern New England Presbytery: including the states of Maine,
New Hampshire and Vermont, and
Southern New England Presbytery: including the states of
Massachusetts, Rhode Island, and that portion of connecticut not
included in the Metro Presbytery, and

<a id="ga28-p115"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=117 printed_page=115 -->

new york state presbytery including that portion of new york state
not included in Metro Presbytery,
And be it further resolved that the current Northeast Presbytery be dissolved,
after the September 2000 meeting of northeast presbytery

## 28-32 Partial Report of Committee of Commissioners on Administration

RE John Mardirosian, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the partial report regarding the relocation of the PCA Office Building. He yielded to TE L. Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk, who provided background for the Assembly on the issues dealing with the proposals concerning the New Office Buildings. TE Paul Kooistra, Coordinator of MTW, assured the Assembly of the genuine progress made in cooperating together to address the various concerns and to present a plan. He also spoke of God’s evident blessing in the financial provisions surrounding this plan. RE Brian Terrell, who works for the Staubach Company (real estate broker and consultant), presented the details of the plan to sell the current building and build a new two

<a id="ga28-p118"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=120 printed_page=118 -->

building campus 17 miles northeast of the current building. TE John Robertson explained how the buildings will be financed.

**Recommendations 1-8** were **adopted** at this time. [Text and action of the complete report for Administration is at 28-67, p 263.]

## 28-33 Procedural Motions

On motion, the Assembly **postponed** the partial report of the Bills and Overtures Committee and the Standing Judicial Commission Report until the commissioners have had the written reports in their hands for one hour.

On motion, the Assembly set as orders of the day these two reports for Wednesday evening 8:30 PM.

## 28-34 Committee of Commissioners on Mission to North America

The Chairman of the Committee of Commissioners on MNA, TE Steven Meyerhoff, led in prayer, remembering before the Lord Dr. Boice’s family and Mrs. R. Laird Harris, who had a stroke last week.

The Omnibus motions 1-5, 7-11, 13-16 were approved at this time. [For action on Recommendation 10, see 28-44, p. 213.] [Text and action of complete report on Mission to North America, see 28-31, p. 98.]

## 28-35 Report of the Creation Study Committee

The Moderator yielded the chair to TE Paul Settle. Chairman of the CSC, RE Sam Duncan, led the Assembly in prayer. He then explained the proposed procedure for handling the report. The Moderator ruled that the effect of the Committee’s recommendation is to suspend some of the provisions of *Roberts Rules of Order*, thus requiring a 2/3 vote. The procedure was **adopted**. (See 28-38, p. 119, for the text and p. 184 for the action of the Creation Study Report.)

## 28-36 Recess

The Assembly recessed at 5:35 PM to reconvene for business at 7:30 PM, with prayer by TE David Coffin.

## Fourth Session – Wednesday Evening
June 21, 2000

## 28-37 Assembly Reconvenes

The Assembly reconvened at 7:30 PM with prayer by TE Larry Roff, and the reading of the introduction to and the singing of Dr. Boice’s hymn “Give Praise to God.”

<a id="ga28-p119"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=121 printed_page=119 -->

28-38 Report of the Creation Study Committee (continued)
Chairman RE Sam Duncan led the Assembly in prayer, and Assembly
proceeded to hold the previously appointed hour of discussion of the report. At 8:30 PM
the Assembly by a 2/3 vote postponed the order of the day to complete the report.
at the end of the hour, upon motion the hour of discussion was determined
complete and the Assembly rose from “informal consideration” . The Assembly
proceeded to discuss the three recommendations as a unit. Upon motion the question
was called. Recommendations 1-3 as a unit were defeated Recommendations 1 and
3 were moved, and Recommendation 2 was amended by the addition of the new
number 2. [see p. 184 for Recommendations ]
The following commissioner requested that his negative vote on the new
Recommendation 2, which was adopted, be recorded: TE David Coffin.

The following substitute motion was made for the motion as amended and was defeated:

<a id="ga28-p121"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=123 printed_page=121 -->

The Twenty-Eighth General Assembly of the PCA endorses the following interim advice to sessions and presbyteries as a sense of the Assembly resolution:

With charity toward all our members and with fidelity to the original authors of our confessional standards, our understanding is that, until evidence to the contrary is found, the Westminster divines intended the confessional phrase “in the space of six days” to specify that the six days of creation were days of normal duration with evening and morning. Accordingly, for the peace of the church, any future candidates who differ with this original meaning should request an exception to this sense of the Confession until documentation that the Westminster divines held other views is firmly established. We also urge courts of original jurisdiction to make these future determinations with care, charity, honesty, and impartiality.
