REPORT OF THE STANDING JUDICIAL COMMISSION

A. INTRODUCTION
The Standing Judicial Commission (SJC) held its annual meeting on March 2,
2000. the october meeting oft hes jc was cancelled by the officers due to there being
insufficient business to justify the meeting.

n. JUDICIAL CASES
the following is a list of judicial cases before the commission over the period
since the last General Assembly:

1. Case 98-8 Beverly R. Smith vs Southwest Presbytery (Appeal)
2. Case 98-9 David Baer vs Illiana Presbytery (Appeal)
3. Case98-10 Byron Curtis vs Eastern Carolina Presbytery (Reference)
4. Case 99-1 Request to General Assembly to Assume Original Jurisdiction in
the case of a teaching elder from western carolina Presbytery,
Et. al (Judicial Matter)
5. Case 99-2 Aureliano Tan vs South Texas Presbytery (Complaint)
6. Case 99-3 Joseph Gatis vs Northeast Presbytery (Appeal)
7. Case 99-4 Edward Fitzsimmons vs Evangel Presbytery (Appeal)
8. Case 99-5 Lawrence Rountree vs Covenant Presbytery (Appeal)
9. Case 99-6 Robert M. Shive vs Central Carolina Presbytery (Appeal)
10. Case 99-7 Jeffrey M. Black vs Eastern Carolina Presbytery (Complaint)
11. Case 00-1 Erwin Morrison, et al. vs Philadelphia Presbytery (Complaint)
12. Case 00-2 Philip J. Adams vs Northeast Presbytery (Complaint)

of those, Cases 99-3, 99-5, 99-6, 99-7, 00-1, and 00-2 are in process with panels
having been appointed to hear the cases. The Standing Judicial Commission has
completed its work on Cases 98-8, 98-9, 98-10, 99-1, 99-2, and 99-4 and the report on
those are as follows:

<a id="ga28-p218"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=220 printed_page=218 -->

### B. REPORT OF THE CASES

### JUDICIAL CASE 98-8
### APPEAL OF BEVERLY R. SMITH VS SOUTHWEST PRESBYTERY

## I. Summary of the Facts

Mrs. Beverly Smith is a member of Covenant Presbyterian Church (CPC) in Sun City, AZ. Her husband is a retired minister and a member of a non-PCA presbytery. He regularly attended CPC with his wife. For 14 years, from 1979 to 1993, Rev. Donald Esty was pastor of CPC. After he retired in 1993, CPC had an interim pastor for a few years. Then, in 1996, Rev. Gerald Partain was called as pastor.

In December 1996, some members of CPC petitioned the Session to call a congregational meeting to “consider the future of Covenant Presbyterian Church under the leadership of the current Pastor.” (ROC 67) The meeting was held in January 1997, and the motion to recommend to presbytery that it dissolve the pastoral relationship failed. Mrs. Smith then filed two complaints alleging that the meeting was improperly called and alleging that the moderator erred by not allowing Mr. Smith the privilege of the floor at the meeting. Both complaints were denied. In addition to these complaints, Mrs. Smith filed four other complaints between January and March 1997.

At some point after the congregational meeting, Southwest Presbytery (SWP) formed a Commission to help resolve conflict within CPC. In November 1997, the Commission reported that after months of investigation, it had determined what it believed to be the cause of the “divisions currently found at Covenant PCA.” (ROC 111) The report stated:

“CPC was deeply divided at the end of Rev. Don Esty’s tenure as pastor of CPC. The Commission is convinced that both sides of this conflict have sinned and continue to do so by failing to forgive and seek forgiveness from one another. Both sides maintain that the problem is primarily that of the other side. The truth is, however, that Pastor Partain’s authoritarian and confrontational style has been like gasoline to the fire of the unsubmissive, belligerent attitudes and actions of some opposing Pastor Partain’s continuation as Pastor. These conclusions have led the Commission to the following decision. It is our exhortation that anyone who is unwilling to follow its directives should out of love for Christ and his Church leave the membership or leadership of CPC and join another evangelical church... (The Commission then outlined six directives.) (111-112)

Two months later, in January 1998, Mrs. Smith was cited to appear to hear charges brought against her by the Session of CPC. In the citation letter, Smith was also notified that the Session had placed her under suspension from the sacraments until the charges against her were adjudicated and removed. Session cited BCO 33-4. The charges were not revealed in the citation letter. This citation was made approximately one year after the congregational meeting that considered dissolving the pastoral relationship of TE Partain.

In February 1998, Mrs. Smith met with the Session, heard the four charges, and pled “not guilty” to each charge: (1) factiousness/divisiveness, (2) gossip/slander, (3) breaking of membership vows, and (4) contumacy.

<a id="ga28-p219"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=221 printed_page=219 -->

In March 1998, the session of c pc (one TE and three RE’s) held a trial and
found smith guilty of all four charges. In April 1998, Smith filed an appeal to SWP
alleging 14 specifications of error. Approximately six months later, in September 1998,
Smith’s appeal was heard before the entire Presbytery at its fall Stated Meeting SWP
sustained three specifications of error, but denied the appeal as a whole and wrote:

"The reason for the denial of the appeal as a whole is that the presbytery has concluded
that although there were procedural errors on the part of the session, there is sufficient
evidence from the record that the session was correct in its conclusion that Mrs Smith
was guilty of the charges ” (20)

In October 1998, Smith filed an appeal to the General Assembly On April 30,
1999, thirteen months after the trial and seven months after the appeal before
Presbytery, a panel of the Standing Judicial Commission heard the appeal

#### II. Statement of the Issue

did southwest presbytery clearly err when it did not sustain the appeal of mrs
Beverly Smith?

II Judgment in the Case
Yes The Presbytery clearly erred in judgment by not sustaining the appeal and
by not reversing the judgment of the session The Session clearly erred in both
judgment and procedure Therefore, the finding of guilt is reversed and the discipline of
suspension from the sacraments is annulled

III. Reasoning & Opinion
this court fully appreciates the high standards of review required by BCO 39-3
However, in this particular case, it is the opinion of this court that the judgments of both
lower courts should be reversed Both lower courts clearly erred in their judgment that
the evidence presented at trial, as contained in the Record of the Case, supported the
finding of guilt
Furthermore, the Session clearly erred in procedure While the Presbytery noted
these errors in procedure, the Presbytery erred in judgment by not finding these
procedural errors to be significant enough to require remanding for retrial (assuming
that the evidence had supported the charges).

A. The Evidence Did Not Prove the Charges
both lower courts concluded that the evidence supported a finding of guilt on all
four charges We disagree Because there were no witnesses called to testify at the trial
(a fact discussed later in this opinion), the prosecution’s case rested exclusively on
documentary evidence The documentary evidence, however, is clearly insufficient to
support the finding of guilt
Mrs. Smith was charged with four offenses: (1) facetiousness/divisiveness, (2)
gossip and slander, (3) breaking of membership vows, and (4) contumacy (ROC 80)
To establish the four charges, ten specifications with 20 supporting documents were
offered as evidence We will briefly examine each specification and its accompanying

<a id="ga28-p222"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=222 printed_page=null -->

documents In summary, none of the documents prove the allegations contained in the
specifications.

Specification 1 “That on or about January 17, 1997, the said Beverly R. Smith
did demonstrate divisiveness and broke her membership vows by
not accepting the will of the congregation in the january 15,
1997 vote to retain the pastor by writing two complaints seeking
to overturn the outcome of the meeting. ”
(Documents 1, 2, 3, 4 - ROC 83, 85, 86, 89)

Documents 1, 2, and 3 were formal complaints written by the Appellant against
three different actions of the Session. The complaints were written in temperate
language the filing of complaints is not, per se, proof of divisiveness or proof of the
breaking of membership vows. Document 1 complained that improper procedures were
followed in calling the congregational meeting. Document 2 did not pertain to the
meeting Document 3 complained that the Appellant’s non-member, but regularly attending husband was denied the privilege of the floor during the congregational
meeting
Document 4 was a handwritten letter to the Session that expressed concern about
“problems in the relationship between the pastor and the congregation” and asked “that
the session take action to request that Southwest Presbytery create a commission to
investigate these problems and then counsel with the pastor and the congregation.”
Document 4 appears to be seeking a solution to division rather than being proof of
divisiveness Indeed, Presbytery did eventually form such a commission to investigate
and counsel with the church These four documents are insufficient to prove the charge
of divisiveness or of the breaking of membership vows

Specification 2 (vacated prior to trial)

Specification 3 "That on or about February 22, 1997, the said Beverly R. Smith
did show her divisive spirit by impugning the PSW, accusing the
members of presbytery of violating the BCO 43-7, -8, -9, and
accusing the clerk of the congregational meeting of falsifying the
minutes. ”
(Document 6, ROC 90)
Document 6 is a letter from them rs smith to TE mike biggs of the presbytery
It is written in respectful and temperate language Apparently, the session of c pc
forwarded one of Mrs. Smith’s complaints to the Presbytery for its consideration. (This
washer complaint that her husband was denied the privilege of the floor at the
congregational meeting on January 15.) Presbytery denied her complaint, and she wrote
in Document 6 that their “denial was accepted ” However, Mrs. Smith had never been
notified beforehand that her complaint was forwarded to Presbytery. The Presbytery
considered her complaint without her being present. Therefore, Mrs. Smith was not
impugning the presbytery or falsely accusing them of violating the BCO. It appears that
the presbytery did violate those sections of the BCO, though it remains unclear exactly

2 20

<a id="ga28-p221"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=223 printed_page=221 -->

how Mrs Smith’s complaint got to Presbytery in the first place She was never given
the opportunity to argue her complaint before the Session
Furthermore, shed id not accuse the clerk of the congregational meeting of
falsifying the minutes Her letter simply stated that “the minutes of the congregational
meeting must be wrong concerning this matter ” This appears to be nothing but a
difference in people’s recollections of what transpired at the meeting there is no proof
of a divisiveness spirit in this letter

Specification 4 “That during March 1997, the said Beverly R. Smith
demonstrated contumacy and divisiveness by her refusal to come
to a session meeting on march 13, 1997 to discuss with Session
the situation in the Church and her part in it; and by impugning
the actions of the Session. ”
(Documents 7, 8, 9, 10 - ROC 91, 92, 93, 95)
In March 1997, the Session sent letters to the Smiths, the Richards and the Bols
expressing concern about things that had taken place before and after the congregational
meeting of january 15, 1997 The Session wanted to “discuss the situation” with each
(91 & 44) The Session requested that the members come to separate Session meetings
for interaction on this matter The Record does not contain the Session’s letter to Mrs
Smith Apparently, she was invited to a Session meeting held on March 13
Document 7 is the Smith’s response to this letter from the Session All three
couples, including Beverly and James Smith, signed Document 7 This joint letter
stated that the couples “would be more than happy to meet with the Session as a group”
and requested a copy of the agenda for such a meeting this was not a refusal to meet,
but simply a request fora different format for the meeting Furthermore, there appears
to be some confusion regarding the Session’s use of the term “contumacy ” This term is
used in the BCO in the context of an accused person who refuses to obey a second
citation to appear before a church court in formal judicial process (BCO 32-6, 33-2, 34-
4) When Mrs. Smith signed Document 7, she was not formally accused of anything
nor had she been cited to appear ina case of process it was a request to meet for
discussion, not a citation her request fora different format is not contumacy as the
term is used in the BCO
Documents 8, 9, and 10 are formal complaints against actions of the session
These complaints pertained to the Session’s decision not to request Presbytery to
appoint a commission, a Session policy regarding letter distribution among the
congregation, and the Session’s alleged threat to remove Mrs. Smith from the roll if she
did not meet with them by herself As previously stated, the filing of complaints is not,
per se, proof of divisiveness (even the filing of six complaints ina two month period )

Specification 5 “That since January 15, 1997 Beverly R. Smith has demonstrated
divisiveness and has broken her vows of membership by not
accepting the will of the congregation in the vote to retain the
pastor, and in that she has neglected the Church, making only

<a id="ga28-p222"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=224 printed_page=222 -->

sporadic visits, and then only in the presence of those who voted
negatively in the meeting. "
(Document 11 - ROC 97)
Document 11 is merely a copy of chapter 57 from the BCO. It is not clear how
this document alone could be evidence of any of the allegations contained in this
specification There was no testimony presented regarding how often Mrs Smith
attended worship at CPC At the trial, her representative stated that during 1997 she
had been actively helping a PCA church planter in Phoenix. (47)

Specification 6 “That prior to April 15, 1997, the said Beverly R. Smith did
demonstrate her divisive spirit and committed slander when she
wrote in a letter to the PSW unsubstantiated and slanderous
comments about the pastor of CPC. The comments were never
brought to Pastor Partain in accordance with Matthew 18 first,
but were sent directly to the PSW Officers, Churches, and
Nominations Committee. In the April 1997 Presbytery meeting
TE G. K. part a in was cleared of such offenses. "
(Documents 12 & 13 - ROC 98-101, 102)
Document 12 is a letter from TE Steele to TE Partain TE Steele was the
chairman of the Presbytery’s Officers, Churches and Nominations Committee (OC&N)
that was responsible to investigate allegations against TE Partain Eleven families in
the Covenant Presbyterian Church alleged to the Presbytery that TE Partain had
committed various offenses the smiths were one of the eleven in his letter tote
Partain, TE Steele apparently quoted from a letter written by the Smiths, but their letter
is not contained in the Record That quote is as follows:

"Mr Partain speaks about love in his sermons but seems to lack sincerity and is selective
in showing that love to all members of the congregation. Mr. Partain ... sees the
congregation dn ided into two groups with the former pastor being the dividing factor.
He has aligned himself with one alleged group, largely ignoring the other group The
truth is that the congregation is divided over Mr. Partain s ministry and he is creating
division Mr. Partain's view of the division within the church has been expressed .. as
follows: 1 am going to stay, let those who are opposed leave, only then will there be
peace within the church ' He promotes division in the church around himself . In a
meeting with session members and others in the fall of 1996 ... Mr. Partain in
speaking to a fellow minister of the Gospel, who has been very active in the life of CPC.
has filled the pulpit, taught Sunday Bible Classes, led Wednesday night prayer sessions,
and been a great asset to the church. Mr. Partain was rude, was confrontational, was
argumentative, was not patient, was not kind, did not show humility or compassion That
minister. Rev Jim Austin, deserves better treatment.”

First, the accuracy of this quotation was never established. Second, there was no
evidence offered to establish the allegation of slander. The term “slander” applies to
false, verbal statements. In addition, for either a verbal statement to be slander ora
written statement to be libel, it must be known to be false and must be published to a
third party There was no evidence presented that the Smith’s comments were false.
Furthermore, it is not entirely clear that the Presbytery should be considered as a “third
party ” BCO 34-3 states that “If anyone knows a minister to be guilty of a private

<a id="ga28-p223"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=225 printed_page=223 -->

offense, he should warn him in private. But if the offense be persisted in, or become public, he should bring the case to the attention of some other minister of the Presbytery.” The letter from the Smiths may simply have been complying with the provisions of *BCO* 34-3 and there was no evidence offered to the contrary. Document 12 does not establish a divisive spirit.

Document 13 is a letter from TE Lundgaard, the clerk of SWP, to the Session of CPC. He reported that the Presbytery had adopted the report of the OC&amp;N committee that found “no strong presumption of guilt” on the part of TE Partain, with reference to the 11 letters containing allegations of offenses. The fact that the committee did not indict TE Partain on charges does not mean that the writers of the letters wrote “unsubstantiated and slanderous comments.” None of the letter writers was interviewed by the OC&amp;N Committee nor admonished by the Presbytery.

## Specification 7

“That on or about April 19 and April 24, 1997, the said Beverly R. Smith did demonstrate divisiveness, gossip, slander and facetiousness by writing and circularizing slanderous and divisive Letters of Offense against the Pastor and Session of CPC to the Southwest Presbytery and to the people and Session of CPC. She further solicited 27 members (including Mrs. Smith), two associate members &amp; eleven non-members of CPC to sign the letter of April 19. The letter of April 24, 1997 was signed by Marge Bol, Marge Richard and Beverly R. Smith with a cover letter by PCUSA TE James M. Smith. None of the signatories of the April 19 letter were identified as to membership status.”

(Docs 14, 15, 16 – ROC 103-105, 107-109, 110-112)

The April 19 letter (Document 14) was addressed to the Presbytery and had about 40 signatures. The April 24 letter (Document 15) was addressed to the Session and had three signatures. Mrs. Smith signed both, but there was no evidence presented that Mrs. Smith wrote, circularized or solicited signatures for either of these April letters. On neither letter is her signature the first one listed.

Document 14 alleged that the Session had wrongly indicted two ruling elders, then dropped the indictments and administratively removed them from the membership roll in a manner allegedly contrary to the *BCO*. This letter was signed by 27 communicant members – about 40% of the communicant membership of CPC. This letter appears to comply with *BCO* 40-5 as one satisfactory method by which to advise a higher court of “any important delinquency or grossly unconstitutional proceeding” of a lower court. Furthermore, seven months after this allegation was made, the Commission of SWP reported in November 1997, that “administrative removal became the standard operating procedure for the Session as they dealt with those whom they assumed did not support Pastor Partain.” The Commission also directed that the Session “reinstate all members removed from the roll administratively who have not absented themselves for at least one year.” (ROC 112)

Document 15, signed by three women, was sent to the Session and addressed the same concerns as Document 14, as well as a few others. Document 15 was later sent to the members of the congregation under a cover letter signed by the Appellant’s

<a id="ga28-p224"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=226 printed_page=224 -->

husband. However, there was no evidence that Mrs. Smith was involved in any way in this congregational mailing.

Document 16 is a three-page report from the SWP Judicial Commission concerning CPC. The report does not allege that Mrs. Smith was guilty of the offenses mentioned in Specification 7.

## Specification 8

“That on or about January 8 to February 1, 1998, the said Beverly R. Smith did demonstrate contumacy and contempt for the leadership of Covenant Presbyterian Church when she, with her husband James M. Smith, partook of the sacraments at another church, after having been suspended from the sacraments by the Session of CPC pending adjudication of charges against her.” (Document 17 – ROC 114-117)

Document 17 is a four-page letter signed by Mr. James Smith and addressed to “my friends in the Congregation of Covenant Presbyterian Church.” This letter was written after Mrs. Smith was notified that she had been indicted and suspended from the sacraments, but before she had learned what the charges and specifications were. In the letter, Mr. Smith complained to his friends in the congregation that his wife was being mistreated by the Session. He informed them that he and his wife had recently celebrated the Lord’s Supper in another church. Mrs. Smith contends that she was unsure of the parameters of the suspension. At the time she participated in the Lord’s Supper, she did not yet even know what she was being charged with. Furthermore, there was no evidence offered that she participated in the Lord’s Supper at any other time during her 16-month suspension (January 1998 – May 1999). This document does not prove the allegation of contempt and contumacy.

## Specification 9

“That on or about Feb. 22 through Feb. 26, 1998 the said Beverly Smith did demonstrate divisiveness, gossip and slander, and contumacy by writing/approving and circularizing divisive, gossipy and slanderous, and contumacious letters to some members of the congregation and perhaps to others. These letters were in defense of the charges brought against her by the Session and were an attempt to sinfully influence the court and the congregation in these matters.”

(Documents 18, 19 – ROC 122-123, 124-125)

Again, Documents 18 and 19 were letters signed by the Appellant’s husband and addressed to his “friends in the congregation.” There was no evidence offered to establish the allegation that Mrs. Smith wrote, approved, or circularized either of these letters. In fact, the trial transcript indicates that she claimed she had nothing to do with their “construction or distribution” (ROC 50). In her written appeals to Presbytery and to the General Assembly, she claimed that she had never even seen Documents 18 and 19. (ROC 6 &amp; 9)

## Specification 10

“That on Feb. 25, 1998 at the Annual Congregational Meeting the said Beverly Smith did demonstrate contumacy by sinfully

<a id="ga28-p225"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=227 printed_page=225 -->

disrupting the meeting by speaking along with two others without
being recognized and refusing to stop speaking when requested
toby the moderator fora period of time. ”
(Document 20 - ROC 126-127)
Document 20 is the minutes from the meeting at which the alleged contumacy
occurred. However, the minutes seem to vindicate Mrs. Smith. at the time of the
meeting, Mrs. Smith was under suspension from the sacraments, but had not yet been
tried on the four charges. The minutes record that three women spoke without being
recognized by the Moderator. They were told that as suspended members, they could
not vote and the minutes record the names of two of the women who refused to be
silent. Mrs. Smith complied. The Moderator also ordered the three women to “seat
themselves behind the first four rows with other non-voting members.” The minutes
record the name of one woman who refused to move. Again, Mrs. Smith complied.

B. Procedural Errors of the Session
The Session made several procedural errors that were significant enough to
warrant remanding for retrial. However, we do not recommend remanding since the
evidence in the record does not establish the guilt of the accused. While not
recommending remand, we believe it is important to address these procedural errors
because they involve constitutional interpretation.
While not sustaining the appeal as a whole, the presbytery did sustain three of the
Appellant’s specifications of error. The errors involved (1) the lack of witnesses, (2)
prejudice and (3) the presumption of guilt (as related to the burden of proof)
The Session’s fourth procedural error involved a lack of reasonable indulgence.
In her appeal, Mrs. smith claimed in specification of error #4 that the Session was
unfair when it ruled that her written defense statement was inadmissible. The Appellant
called this document the “Response to Specifications.” The Presbytery did not sustain
Smith’s specification #4. In not sustaining this specification, the Presbytery erred in
judgment.
The Session’s fifth procedural error occurred in the manner in which it suspended
Mrs. Smith from the sacraments. While this error would not, by itself, warrant
remanding, it is serious and should be addressed.

1. No Witnesses
The Appellant claimed that BCO 35-3 was violated by the Session. BCO 35-3
requires that:

The testimony of more than one witness shall be necessary in order to establish any
charge; yet if, in addition to the testimony of one witness, corroborative evidence be
produced, the offense may be considered to be proved.

the presbytery sustained this specification of error and wrote, “In addition to the
corroborating documents, the session is required to produce witnesses in the case.”
(ROC 19) However, the Presbytery did not judge that this procedural error was
significant enough to warrant remanding. We disagree. Even if the evidence had

<a id="ga28-p226"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=228 printed_page=226 -->

supported the charges, this procedural error alone would have warranted remanding for
a new trial.
In the Primary Brief filed by the Presbytery prior to the hearing before the SJC
Panel, the Appellee stated that

“During the trial there were several opportunities for the defense to object to the fact that
there were no witnesses brought forward by the prosecutor By his lack of objection to
there being no witnesses called (BCO 32-14), it would stand to reason that he consented
to the exclusive use of documentary evidence.”

This is not accurate. On the contrary, in his opening comments at the trial before
the Session, defense counsel RE Moore said the following:

“The other portion that was passed over pretty lightly was BCO 35-3. I don't see any
exceptions to the fact that more than one witness or one witness and corroborated
evidence. It has been stated that the documents are self-explanatory, but a number of
statements have been made which are of avery controversial nature which i will get to.
The fact that no witnesses are presented should invalidate this proceeding.” (ROC 44)

The BCO is clear; at least one witness is required, regardless of the amount of
corroborative evidence.

2 & 3. Prejudice & the Presumption of Guilt
the presbytery sustained two more of the Appellant’s specifications of error (# 9
& 10, see ROC 10) and wrote:

“It is the opinion of the presbytery that the session was to some extent prejudiced. The
evidence from the record of the trial points to the session’s failure fully to set aside this
prejudice during the trial. Although the session must presume guilt for instituting
process, BCO 31-2, paragraph 2, the prosecution is responsible to prove guilt, BCO 31-3,
31-9.” (ROC 20)

The Session judged that there was a “strong presumption of guilt” with regard to
each of the four charges. The BCO term ‘strong presumption of guilt,’ has to do only
with indictments and required probable cause before prosecution of the case can begin.
Once a case proceeds to trial, the burden of proof of guilt re stent i er ly upon the
prosecution.. The Session assumed, at that point in the process, that the burden of proof
was on the accused rather than on the prosecution. This assumption was wrong. The
presumption of guilt does not relieve the prosecution of the burden of proving guilt.
Even though there must be probable cause to indict, the accused is still considered
innocent until proven guilty.
Several times during the trial before the Session, this mistake was made During
his opening remarks, the moderator, TE Partain, instructed everyone “that the
prosecution does not have to prove guilt but the defense has to prove the innocence of
the accused.” (ROC 21) In his closing remarks, the prosecutor stated: “I remind the
court and i remind the accused that the burden of the accused is to say they didn’t do
these things. the presumption of guilt is upon them. They have not done that. They
have not met that burden... the accused has failed to remove the presumption of guilt.”

<a id="ga28-p227"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=229 printed_page=227 -->

(ROC 52, 53) The prosecutor made other similar statements during the trial. (ROC 22,
24)
the fact that the burden of proof is on the prosecution is clear from several
procedures in our BCO. First, the reason why the prosecution argues first at trial, and
has the closing remarks, is because the burden of proof is on the prosecution. Second,
the accused is not required to testify and the defense need not even present a case. The
prosecution, however, must present a case Third, if an accused person ignores repeated
citations to plead, or to appear for trial, that person can be censured for contumacy. He
is not, however, censured for the offense on which he was indicted because his guilt on
that charge has not yet been proven.
Before a person can be indicted on charges, BCO 31-2 requires that some kind of
preliminary investigation should first result in a “strong presumption of guilt.” BCO
31-9 uses the phrase “probable cause” in a similar vein. Therefore, it is reasonable that
a member of the session will have already formed some opinion if he votes to indict the
accused. However, when he is called upon to sit as judge at the trial, if he cannot judge
with a fair mind, and judge based on the case presented at trial, then he should recuse
himself. And if a Session cannot sufficiently set aside prejudice, then the case should
be referred to presbytery in accordance with BCO 43.

4. Lack of Reasonable Indulgence
In her opening remarks at the Session trial, Mrs. Smith stated: “My defense has
been prepared in writing to be presented at the proper time by my counsel.” She then
requested and was granted permission to be excused for health reasons. The moderator
then stated that Mrs. Smith “wishes the written statement which is a summary of her
defense by RE joe moore to be part of the record.” (ROC 23-24)
at the beginning of the trial, the Session agreed to admit her written statement
over the objections of the prosecutor. Later in the trial, after learning that she had
signed the statement, the Session then considered the document to be a “deposition”
which the prosecution had no opportunity to cross-examine. Based on this judgment,
the Session ruled the document to be “invalid as a defense.” (ROC 38-39)
However, the clerk’s comments in the trial transcript indicate that the Session
reviewed the entire document and “judged what parts may be allowed as arguments of
the defense.” The Record does not indicate which parts were allowed and Mrs. Smith
was never informed which parts were allowed.
After opening remarks, Mrs. Smith left the trial, with the court’s permission,
believing that her written statement was accepted as her defense. It was clearly unfair
of the session to subsequently disallow it and to proceed with the trial. At the very
least, the trial should have been postponed and Mrs. Smith informed that her written
statement was not acceptable. She would then have the opportunity to make right
whatever deficiency existed

5. Suspension from the Lord’s Supper
In the Session’s letter to Mrs. Smith that cited her to appear and hear charges, the
Session cited BCO 33-4 and placed her under suspension from the sacraments until the
charges against her were “adjudicated and removed.” (ROC 70) However, the charges

<a id="ga28-p228"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=230 printed_page=228 -->

were not revealed to her until six weeks later. Therefore, she was suspended before she
even knew why, despite repeated requests to learn what the charges were. (ROC 77,
120)
BCO 33-4 states:

When it is impracticable immediately to commence process against an accused church
member, the Session may, if it thinks the edification of the church requires it. prevent the
accused from approaching the Lord's table until the charges against him can be examined.

However, the conditions required by BCO 33-4 were not met. There is no
indication that it was “impracticable immediately to commence process” against Mrs.
Smith In fact, process was commenced within a reasonable time.
Furthermore, there is no indication that such a suspension, prior to trial, was
required for the edification of the church. the church was apparently not notified of
Mrs. Smith’s suspension, so it is unclear how the church could have been edified
After being judged guilty on all four charges, Mrs. Smith received the censure of
suspension from the sacraments. After she notified the Session that she was filing an
appeal to Presbytery, the Session wrote to her and said: “Because of your Appeal, the
BCO states that you must be returned to your former status. That status is one of
suspension from the sacraments without censure.” (ROC 64) The Session, however,
did not comply with BCO 42-6 when it continued the suspension from the sacraments:

Notice of appeal shall have the effect of suspending the judgment of the lower court until
the case has been finally decided in the higher court. However, the court of original
jurisdiction may, for sufficient reasons duly recorded, prevent the appellant from
approaching the Lord's Table, and if an officer, prevent him from exercising some or all
his official functions, until the case is finally decided (cf. BCO 31-10; 33-4). This shall
never be done in the way of censure. (BCO 42-6. emphasis added)

It does not appear that “sufficient reasons” were ever offered or recorded It
appears to this court that the continued suspension was “done in the way of censure.”
Mrs. Smith’s suspension from the sacraments was continued for more than a year
while her appeal was being heard in the higher courts. this case is an example of why
the judgment of a lower court should normally be suspended until the appeal has been
finally decided in the higher court

Conclusion
In conclusion, it is the Panel’s sincere hope that our heavenly Father will grant to
Covenant Presbyterian Church, and to all involved with this case, the blessing for which
our Savior prayed:

“May they be brought to complete unity to let the world know that
you sent me and have loved them even as you have loved me.”
John 17:23
Note: This decision was written by RE J Howard Donahoe, TE Dr Paul B. Fowler
and RE J Grant McCabe

<a id="ga28-p229"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=231 printed_page=229 -->

## Roll Call Votes on Case as a Whole:

**Absent**: TE Dominic A. Aquila, TE David W. Hall, RE Thomas F. Leopard

**Concur**: RE Mark Belz, RE J. Howard Donahoe, RE Samuel J. Duncan, TE Robert M. Ferguson, TE Paul B. Fowler, RE Eugene H. Friedline, TE Benedict W. Konopa, TE Paul D. Kooistra, RE John W. Lane, TE Lawrence N. Lunceford, RE Robert H. Miller, TE John M. McArthur, RE J. Grant McCabe, TE Charles E. McGowan, TE David P. Peterson, TE G. Dewey Roberts, RE Edward J. Robeson, TE James L. Smith, RE John Van Voorhis, RE John B. White, Jr., RE W. Jack Williamson

Total 21 Concur, 3 Absent

## JUDICIAL CASE 98-9
## APPEAL OF TE DAVID BAER VS ILLIANA PRESBYTERY

### I. A Summary of the Facts

On October 1, 1997 The Session of Westminster Presbyterian Church (hereinafter WPC) of Godfrey, IL (three Ruling Elder members functioning at that time) met with the Appellant and the Youth Pastor and differences between the two were discussed. They were instructed, according to approved Session Minutes, to apologize to the congregation and indicate their support for one another.

On October 9, 1997, after the Appellant had made a statement to the congregation on October 5th, the Session met again and expressed concern about the statement. They had understood that Appellant and the Youth Pastor were to meet together and then make individual statements to the congregation. Contrary to their understanding, the Appellant had made his own statement without showing it to the Youth Pastor or having it reviewed by Session. A motion was passed agreeing that the Appellant, Youth Pastor, and their spouses would get together “for fellowship and healing” before October 19th.

On October 15, 1997 a called meeting of the Session was held to discuss the “ongoing conflict” between Appellant and the Youth Pastor and “some long term concerns” with the Appellant’s ministry. The Session and Appellant discussed areas of disagreement, including the Session’s perception of the Appellant’s “lack of majority congregational support.”

On October 25, 1997 Appellant wrote to Session members, informing them that he had asked assistance from Illiana Presbytery and expected the Shepherding Committee would meet with him soon. He asserted that since he was a member of Presbytery and the Youth Pastor was under care, the Presbytery would have jurisdiction of their difficulties.

On October 30, 1997 a called meeting of Session was held to discuss Appellant’s ministry. A docket had been sent out which had been prepared by the Clerk of Session and which included several items of business, but Appellant asserted he had the right (presumably as Moderator) to set the docket and length of meeting. Therefore, he declared that the only item to be discussed would be his ministry. Unannounced, he also brought a Teaching Elder from Presbytery to the meeting, which at least one

<a id="ga28-p230"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=232 printed_page=230 -->

Ruling Elder considered inappropriate. After discussion, two of the three Ruling Elders stated that because of a lack of trust in the Appellant, his ministry at WPC was “done.” The third Ruling Elder was not yet ready to join in that conclusion.

At a regular meeting of Session on November 6, 1997, Appellant reported that he had met with Illiana Presbytery’s Shepherding Committee and that they had advised that “his ministry was done at Westminster and he should get counseling immediately.” According to Session records, Appellant rejected their counsel on the ground that they were “not probably qualified” to give such advice. A second option proposed by the Shepherding Committee, that of Appellant’s taking a leave of absence for counseling and correction of his problems, was said by the Committee not to be the best solution, and they further doubted that Session would support it. More discussion of differences was held and all three Ruling Elder members of Session recommended that the Appellant resign on November 9th, that he attempt to bring about healing during his remaining time at the church, and that he seek professional counseling.

During a “business meeting” of the congregation on November 9, 1997, a member of the congregation asked for the floor and announced that Appellant had been asked to resign. This was denied by one of the Ruling Elders but he stated there had been many meetings and that the congregation would be notified shortly. In a letter that same day, but not delivered until November 10th, the Session formally requested Appellant’s resignation. They noted that if he did not comply, they would be forced to call a special congregational meeting to dissolve the pastoral relationship.

During a called meeting of Session on November 13th, Appellant stated that he declined to resign because it was a matter of “standing for the truth.” When pressed as to the Shepherding Committee’s advice re professional counseling, Appellant noted that the committee had thought that Session would not go along with the idea of an approved leave period while he sought professional counseling, and Appellant therefore concluded there “was no real need for it.” One Session member expressed “strong disagreement with the truthfulness” of Appellant’s statement. Appellant felt that the Session should have told him of problems earlier, but Session believed they had been telling him for several years.

On November 23, 1997, a lengthy congregational meeting was held regarding Session’s request that Appellant resign. Appellant declared: “[I]t is six weeks now they have been demanding my resignation” and later in the meeting stated: “Did you know the Session was demanding my resignation when it first started some six weeks ago?” Session members admitted that for weeks the Session had been asking Appellant to “resign on his own” and even “begging, basically begging him to resign on his own” but had not “told him from us that we were telling him to resign.” It was denied that Session had refused to let Appellant go to the Shepherding Committee. With respect to the difference between an initial and finally approved draft of Session minutes regarding Appellant’s agreement to speak to the congregation about his differences with the Youth Pastor, Appellant stated that “the minutes all of a sudden were rewritten to be in conformity with the way the elders saw things.”

At the conclusion of the congregational meeting, the vote to request Appellant’s resignation was 39 yes and 32 no. Since a 2/3 majority was required by the WPC bylaws, the request for the Appellant’s resignation was not carried.

<a id="ga28-p231"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=233 printed_page=231 -->

On December 6, 1997, approximately two weeks after the congregational
meeting, the three ruling elders of w pc petitioned the presbytery to dissolve the
pastoral relationship of appellant to WPC. They stated: “His conduct demonstrating
continuing misstatements and lack of credibility, along with his unwillingness to submit
to us, has caused us to lose trust in him and has caused irreparable injury to the
Church. ” They believed that expeditious action was required because WPC was in
danger of losing many of its congregation
Session noted that Appellant had met twice with the Shepherding Committee
and once with them and that the Committee had recommended that Appellant resign
because of the deep division between session and Appellant.
it is further noted in the petition that a ta called meeting of the session on
November 28, 1997 (Minutes of this meeting not in the ROC) Appellant had been
presented with what Session considered “his many inaccuracies” and that his responses
had been “totally unsatisfactory.”
Session stated that they were filing a petition rather than formal charges out of
deference to some on the Shepherding Committee who believed there might be reasons
other than intentional sin which contributed to Appellant’s behavior.
a special meeting of illiana presbytery was called for december 22, 1997.
After much discussion, by a vote of 22-2-2 a committee was appointed to investigate
the relationship between Appellant and congregation and Appellant was suspended
from all pastoral duties until the next meeting on January 10, 1998. In the debate
Appellant repeatedly indicated his support for an investigation because he felt aggrieved
by injurious reports of his character.
On January 5, 1998 the moderator of presbytery asked the committee to also
investigate injurious reports about Appellant and his character and report to the January
10th meeting At the Presbytery’s meeting on January 10, 1998, it was decided on
motion to continue the investigation and the Appellant agreed to continue to remove
himself from pastoral duties in the interim.
On February 7, 1998 a special meeting of presbytery was held. The Special
Investigative Committee reported, inter alia, that there was a “strong presumption of
guilt” and recommended that a prosecutor be appointed. A report concerning the
“injurious reports” issue was circulated but not made public and was approved by a
majority. The committee further recommended that Appellant resign as pastor
immediately, and that if he did not do so, the Presbytery dissolve his pastoral
relationship. Appellant agreed to resign with conditions, including one that no charges
be filed against him. A motion to this effect was defeated 2-22-1. Presbytery then
dissolved the pastoral relationship by a vote of 24-1-0, a Judicial Commission was
appointed, and it was agreed that the Moderator would appoint a Prosecutor.
At Presbytery’s April 18, 1998 meeting it was reported that two committees had
been appointed, one a Judicial Committee with Prosecutor, and the other a Special
Committee under BCO 31 -7. It was also reported that in response to a request from
appellant for all tapes and minutes of meetings and interviews by the special
Committee, Appellant had been informed that tapes had been destroyed and that there
were only records instead of minutes. On motion it was agreed that Appellant would
only be given information supporting specific charges.

<a id="ga28-p232"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=234 printed_page=232 -->

On August 28, 1998 appellant filed a notice of intention to appeal the
Presbytery’s judgment. It is not entirely clear from the record what the disposition of
this notice was and there is no specification given therein of the grounds for such an
appeal. On September 23, 1998 Appellant filed a second document called an “Appeal”
against the judgment of the judicial Commission, contending that the Judicial
commissioner red i nits judgment and censure of august 26, 1998. The ROC contains
no record of any such judgment and censure and we can only conclude that these may
refer to actions of presbytery described in its minutes of september 3, 1998 (see ROC
205), which are not before us for action since they do not constitute apart of the ROC.
We must assume that Appellant’s complaints with respect thereto may be subsumed in
the later hearings and actions of presbytery
On October 12, 1998 the Judicial Commission heard charges against Appellant
Four charges were withdrawn by the Prosecutor and one was ruled invalid by the
Commission. It was noted that Appellant had confessed guilt to five charges on May
26, 1998. The two remaining charges were heard. One charge, which stated that
Appellant “wrongfully implied that the elders illegally changed” their record, was not
sustained because the word “illegally” had not been used by the Appellant.
The second charge, that Appellant violated the 9th commandment, the 4th and 7th
ordination vows, and the 3rd installation vow by overstating the Session’s request for his
resignation in saying they had “demanded” his resignation for six weeks”, was
sustained. The Judicial Commission definitely suspended Appellant from the exercise
of his pastoral office until the next presbytery meeting.
a tits regular meeting of october 17, 1998 Presbytery approved the Judicial
Commission’s judgment unanimously. A vote on the recommended censure carried 12-
7-1. It was ruled that Appellant would remain under indefinite suspension until he
demonstrated repentance.
Appellant, contrary to the instructions of the judicial Commission, was not
present for the Presbytery meeting. he had asked for excuse by letter of october 14,
1998, stating that he would be attending another Presbytery (not specified as a
presbytery of the PCA) out of state. The Presbytery voted not to approve his request for
excuse.
On November 17, 1998 Appellant appealed to the SJC on the grounds that
Illiana Presbytery committed irregularities in the proceedings, manifested prejudice, and
committed injustice in the judgment and censure.
in his brief appellant argues that the charge of which he was found guilty (see
above) is “essentially false” and that, with respect to those charges to which he pled
guilty, he has been denied justice in that the ro c does not contain a fuller statement of
the facts which accompanied his confessions of guilt. as to the charge of lying about
the Session’s “demanding” his resignation, he contends that he should not be adjudged
as guilty because one dictionary definition could include the words of session members
before they formally requested that he resign. However, he admits that “if i had it to do
over again, i would not use the word demand because i believe it is an emotionally
charged word and inflamed passions rather than cooling them.”
as to the charges of which he pled guilty, Appellant cites various facts and
circumstances, some not believed to be in the ROC and therefore not properly to be

<a id="ga28-p233"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=235 printed_page=233 -->

considered, to justify his words and actions. However, he admits: “I do not in my appeal retract the items to which I have pled guilty.” He argues that the censure of suspension is unduly harsh for what he considers sins of lesser severity, and he asked in his brief that the Presbytery’s action be reversed and the suspension be lifted.

At the hearing on June 1, 1999 at the Hazelwood PCA church in the St. Louis, MO area, Appellant’s representative stated that Appellant’s request was simple and straightforward. Appellant does not wish to return to his former church or to the Illiana Presbytery, but is hindered in his efforts to seek a call by the “indefinite suspension” censure imposed by the Presbytery.

The representative argued for a lack of evidence to clearly show Appellant guilty of lying and requested that the Presbytery be instructed to change the censure to “definite suspension” and promptly lift that censure in light of Appellant’s demonstrated repentance and acts of seeking forgiveness. Appellant himself asserted that he has confessed guilt and asked forgiveness, and expressed concern that he may not be able to perform the repentance which Presbytery is seeking. It was stated, though outside the record, that Presbytery has continued to work with Appellant in seeking to resolve this matter.

Appellee stated the Presbytery through a committee is still seeking to counsel Appellant. However, Appellee emphasized that the one charge of which Appellant was found guilty is not the sole issue herein, but must be considered in combination with the other charges to which Appellant pled guilty. He contended that Appellant’s misstatements of fact may have seriously misled the congregation when a majority, but not two-thirds, voted to request Appellant’s resignation.

Appellee averred that he was not aware of relevant letters not being included in the ROC, that verbatim and detailed records associated with guilty pleas are not required to be kept in a record, that testimony obtained by the investigative committee was given based on a promise of confidentiality and served only to provide a basis for determining whether there might be a case, that prejudice has not been established, that Matthew 18 procedures were not required for the type of public offenses involved herein, and that if the sins which Appellant describes as being minor are ‘frivolous’, they should be easy to confess and repent of. It is urged by the Appellee that the Presbytery’s judgment and censure be sustained.

## II. Statement of the Issues

A. Did Illiana Presbytery err in concluding that Appellant violated the 9th commandment, the 4th and 7th ordinations vows, and the 3rd installation vow by erroneously informing the congregation of WPC that the Session had “demanded” his resignation six weeks before the congregational meeting?

B. Did Illiana Presbytery commit such irregularities, manifest such prejudice, and commit such actions by their judgment and censure of the Appellant as to deprive him of the due process of the Book of Church Order and of righteous and Biblical judgment as is required by Scripture and the Constitution of the PCA?

<a id="ga28-p234"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=236 printed_page=234 -->

## III. Judgment in the Case

A. No.
B. No.

## IV. Reasoning and Opinion of the Court

A. Much attention has been given to the meaning of the word “demand” as used by the Appellant on two occasions during the congregational meeting called to determine whether or not his resignation would be required. It cannot be denied that individual Session members had earlier expressed themselves strongly that for various reasons the Appellant should resign and that he should consider his ministry among the people of WPC as ended. Indeed, one elder admitted that he virtually “begged” Appellant to resign for the good of the congregation and its unity.

It is also accurate to say, from a technical standpoint, that one definition of the word “demand” would perhaps approximate the language used at times by individual Session members.

However, the Session as a whole took no formal action until just after the congregational meeting, and the previous viewpoints of individual members must be considered only as opinions of particular Session members at certain times.

Whether or not the Presbytery, through its Commission, was justified in finding the statement by the Appellant to be a violation of the 9th commandment must be evaluated in light of the circumstances in which the statement was made. In the stress and pressure of a lengthy and volatile congregational meeting, the Appellant should have been zealous not to overstate his position or inaccurately state it. Instead, his action in stating and then reiterating that the Session had “demanded” his resignation for six weeks, when in fact it had obviously sought to achieve its goal through voluntary resignation so as to maintain congregational peace and unity, served to put the Session’s actions in a much more unfavorable light than was justified by the overall circumstances and cannot be condoned. Indeed, Appellant has now admitted that the word “demanded” was an “emotionally charged word” which inflamed passions. The 77th question and answer of the Westminster Shorter Catechism declares that the 9th commandment requires “the maintaining and promoting of truth between man and man, and of our own and our neighbor’s good name, especially in witness bearing” (Emphasis added). Appellant was surely bearing witness, though not under oath, before the congregation in a matter of great significance to the people, and he had a duty to maintain a much higher standard than he did. We conclude, therefore, that Presbytery did not err in this specification.

B. Appellant avers that the charges to which he earlier pled guilty were essentially minor and he has attempted to explain them as such. However, in rendering its judgment and censure the Presbytery acted upon the total picture. We find no convincing reasons for overturning the Presbytery’s conclusion that he was guilty of the sins charged against him or for faulting their infliction of the censure of suspension until such time as Appellant repents. Presbytery and

<a id="ga28-p235"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=237 printed_page=235 -->

Appellant continue to seek resolution of their difficulty, but their current relationship is outside the scope of this judicial case.

We have considered Appellant’s specific complaints as set forth in his appeal, brief, and argument. We find no convincing support for the allegation that any specific material available to the Presbytery has been improperly withheld from the Appellant, or, in any event, would be likely to have any significant impact upon the final judgment herein.

We likewise are not persuaded by written evidence or oral argument that any “missing” evidence, if such exists, would have any substantial impact upon our ultimate conclusion. There are, no doubt, some minor flaws in Presbytery’s records, but this is understandable in a case as prolonged and complex as the one before us. The record as a whole demonstrates a Presbytery which has not arbitrarily or summarily dealt with Appellant with a lack of concern for justice and pastoral concern. It has dealt with this matter over a prolonged period, utilizing its Shepherding Committee and an investigative committee before making the matter a specific judicial case. We cannot conclude the record justifies the SJC in substituting its judgment for that of the Presbytery in a matter of this type.

We find the principles set forth in *BCO* 39-3 to be very applicable. The SJC is required to “limit itself to issues raised by the parties in the case in the original (lower) court” and to give “great deference to a lower court regarding those factual matters which the lower court is more competent to determine, because of its proximity to the events in question, and because of its personal knowledge and observations of the parties and witnesses involved.” The same is true as to “matters of discretion and judgment.” We have sought to take very seriously these provisions and their application to this particular case. Therefore, while it may be argued that there were minor actions of the lower court which might have been better handled, we find no such flaws in the Presbytery’s overall handling of this case as to justify reversing of their judgment and censure. Accordingly, the Judicial Panel recommends to the Standing Judicial Commission that the appeal be denied.

**NOTE:** This decision was written by RE John H. Van Voorhis with the concurrence of TE Charles E. McGowan and RE Edward J. Robeson.

## Roll Call Votes on Case as a Whole:

Miller, TE John M. McArthur, RE J. Grant McCabe, TE Charles E. McGowan, TE David P. Peterson, TE G. Dewey Roberts, RE Edward J. Robeson, TE James L. Smith, RE John Van Voorhis, RE John B. White, Jr., RE W. Jack Williamson

**Total 19 Concur, 2 Recused, 3 Absent**

<a id="ga28-p236"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=238 printed_page=236 -->

# CASE 98-10
## REFERENCE OF TE BYRON CURTIS VS EASTERN CAROLINA PRESBYTERY

### I. Statement of Facts

1. Byron Curtis was examined and approved for ordination by Eastern Carolina Presbytery on July 20, 1985, followed by ordination and installation on August 18, 1985. At the time of his examination TE Curtis stated orally four exceptions to the *Westminster Confession of Faith* and the *Book of Church Order*, including one on the six days of creation.

2. In the summer of 1991, TE Curtis received a faculty appointment as assistant professor of Biblical Studies at Geneva College.

3. In the Summer/Fall of 1992, TE Curtis applied for transfer to Ascension Presbytery.

4. TE Curtis was denied transfer by Ascension on the basis of his exceptions to *WCF* and *BCO*, but was granted permission to labor in its bounds.

5. At its meeting on October 16, 1993, ECP approved the call of TE Curtis to his position at Geneva College and granted him permission to labor outside the bounds of ECP. Presbytery also attached to its minutes a written copy of TE Curtis’ prepared statement of exceptions.

6. On January 24, 1998, ECP approved a motion to establish a committee to investigate the theological views and related issues of TE Byron Curtis.

7. At its next meeting on April 18, 1998, ECP sustained a complaint against the action creating this investigating committee and dissolved the committee. ECP subsequently passed a motion requesting from TE Curtis clarification of his exceptions.

8. On July 18, 1998, a motion for ECP to adopt a policy of not receiving any men for licensure or installation to ministry into the presbytery who take exceptions to the Standards was defeated. At the same meeting a motion to prohibit TE Curtis from teaching his exceptions also was defeated.

9. TE Jeff Black complained against ECP’s action in not prohibiting TE Curtis from teaching his exceptions. His complaint was docketed for the next meeting of presbytery on October 17, 1998. Unable to complete its business, the complaint was carried over to an adjourned meeting on November 7, 1998. By a vote of 12 to 10 ECP sustained TE Black’s complaint and prohibited TE Curtis from teaching his exceptions. TE Curtis was not present at the November 7, 1998 meeting of Presbytery.

10. TE Curtis forwarded a complaint to General Assembly against the ECP action and sent a copy to ECP. After receiving TE Curtis’ complaint, ECP sent that complaint along with TE Black’s sustained complaint as a reference to GA.

<a id="ga28-p237"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=239 printed_page=237 -->

## II. Recommendation

The panel recommends to the Standing Judicial Commission that the reference from Eastern Carolina Presbytery, entitled Case 98-10, be returned to Eastern Carolina Presbytery with the instruction that the Complaint of TE Byron Curtis be heard. (BCO 43-1 and 43-2.)

## III. Reasoning and Opinion

Eastern Carolina Presbytery referred this matter to the Standing Judicial Commission (ROC page 199). The SJC Officers acceded to this reference and referred this matter to the Panel for consideration. While the complaint of TE Byron Curtis was prematurely directed to the Standing Judicial Commission because it was not first considered by the lower court, it was also mailed to and received by ECP in a timely manner. After receiving TE Curtis’ complaint, ECP formally referred the complaint to the SJC. In addition, ECP also referred the complaint of TE Jeffrey Black it had already sustained.

The Respondents for ECP subsequently argued to the SJC that what ECP had intended to do was to refer the matter as a “non-judicial” reference to the Constitutional Business Committee for advice. However, since the matters referred related to judicial complaints already in process, these cases cannot be considered “non-judicial” and, therefore, they could only be referred to the Standing Judicial Commission.

While the case before the Panel is “judicial” and is ready to be adjudicated, it is also clear that the Respondents do have some grounds for arguing that ECP did ask specific questions for advice. (ROC 199). Yet, this case cannot be referred for mere “advice” (either from the SJC or CCB). Given this element of uncertainty and the principle that the lower court ought to hear cases before it, it is the judgment of this panel that this case be returned to ECP for adjudication at the next available stated meeting and that the substance of TE Curtis’ complaint be heard.

**Note:** This Opinion was written by RE John B. White, Jr., with the concurrence of TE David Hall and TE Robert Ferguson.

## Roll Call Votes on Case as a Whole:

**Absent:** TE Dominic A. Aquila, TE David W. Hall

**Dissent:** RE Mark Belz, RE J. Howard Donahoe, TE Paul B. Fowler, TE John M. McArthur, TE Charles E. McGowan

**Concur:** RE Samuel J. Duncan, TE Robert M. Ferguson, RE Eugene H. Friedline, TE Benedict W. Konopa, TE Paul D. Kooistra, RE John W. Lane, TE Lawrence N. Lunceford, RE Robert H. Miller, RE J. Grant McCabe, TE G. Dewey Roberts, RE Edward J. Robeson, RE John Van Voorhis, RE John B. White, Jr., RE W. Jack Williamson

**Recused:** RE Thomas F. Leopard, TE David P. Peterson, TE James L. Smith

**Total 14 Concur, 5 dissent, 3 Recused, 2 Absent**

<a id="ga28-p238"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=240 printed_page=238 -->

JUDICIAL MATTER 99-1
REQUEST TO GENERAL ASSEMBLY TO ASSUME ORIGINAL
JURISDICTION IN THE MATTER OF A TEACHING ELDER
FROM WESTERN CAROLINA PRESBYTERY, ET. AL

I. Background Information
1. At its March 1999 stated meeting, Western Carolina Presbytery asked Tennessee
Valley Presbytery (TVP) to investigate allegations that “Cedar Springs
Presbyterian Church, Knoxville, Tennessee, has had women preachers to fill its
pulpits.” The alleged actions took place at successive Sunday evening worship
services on August 16 and 23, 1998.
2. At its spring 1999 stated meeting, TVP, having been advised about Western
Carolina Presbytery’s request and concern, discussed the matter. There was
further discussion on this matter at TVP’s summer 1999 stated meeting (see text
of overture #1 adopted by TVP on 10/12/99). the stated clerk oft vp
formally acknowledged receipt of western Carolina Presbytery’s
communication by a letter dated August 3, 1999.
3. It was reported to TVP at its fall meeting (October 12, 1999) that three teaching
elders from the presbytery “met with TE John Wood, moderator of the cedar
Springs Session, to thoroughly discuss this decision” [i.e., “the decision of the
session of cedar springs pc to allow a woman on staff, under their authority
and guidance, to speak from the pulpit on two Sunday evenings, to explain her
biblical approach to Christian Education”].
4. Western Carolina Presbytery (on July 20, 1999), Calvary Presbytery (on July 24,
1999) and Ascension Presbytery (on July 31, 1999) adopted resolutions
requesting the General Assembly to assume original jurisdiction over a teaching
elder whom they allege allowed women to fill the pulpit in a PCA church. They
were invoking the provisions of BCO 34-1, which deals with process against a
minister.
5. At its fall stated meeting (October 12,1999), TVP discussed the matter again and
adopted the following resolutions:
a that the session of cedar springs presbyterian church
(1) is in conformity with the standards of the PCA;
(2) has not acted in anyway injurious to the testimony of christ or his
church, and
(3) is in submission to their fellow elders, and are eager to maintain the
peace and purity of the church.
B. That TVP openly and plainly declares that it is not proper for women to
preach and teach in the church’s corporate worship.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

Is this matter properly before the General Assembly?

#### III. Judgment of the Case

No. This matter is not properly before the General Assembly.

<a id="ga28-p239"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=241 printed_page=239 -->

## IV. Reasoning and Opinion

The matter is not administratively in order and not properly before the General Assembly:

1. The Session has jurisdiction over worship in the local church.

The wording of the resolutions adopted by the three Presbyteries requested the General Assembly to assume original jurisdiction over a teaching elder against whom allegations of public scandal were raised. The Presbyteries allege that the teaching elder “violated the Biblical teaching on worship in having a woman filling the pulpit, preach, give instruction and exhort the congregation from the Word of God.” However, the conduct of worship services in the local church is under the jurisdiction of the Session and under its responsibility (BCO 12-5 [e]). The teaching elder cannot be held liable for actions that are properly the responsibility of the Session. If the alleged incidents of women preaching from the pulpit did occur, the matter must be addressed to the Session.

2. The Presbytery did not refuse to act.

BCO 34-1 deals with process against a minister. When a minister is accused of doctrinal error or having engaged in matters perceived to lead to public scandal, then his presbytery should investigate the allegations and take appropriate action resulting from the investigation (BCO 31-2). However, if his presbytery refuses to act on the allegations, then at least two presbyteries may request the General Assembly to assume original jurisdiction over the man.

However, in TVP’s judgment, the issue before them was not the actions of a teaching elder, but the actions of a Session, hence BCO 34-1 was not applicable. As stated above, the conduct of worship is under the jurisdiction of the Session and TVP dealt with the matter in light of the BCO 12-5 [e].

But in any case, it is clear that TVP did act on the allegations when, at its fall stated meeting on October 12, 1999, TVP adopted two resolutions on this matter. Since there was no refusal to act the resolutions of the three Presbyteries are administratively out of order and not properly before the General Assembly.

NOTE: This report was written by TE Dominic Aquila with the concurrence of RE John White, TE Robert Ferguson and TE Ben Konopa.

## ATTACHMENT 1

Overtures adopted by the TVP at its Fall Stated Meeting on October 12, 1999, at Lookout Mountain PC:

Overture #1

Whereas, the TVP has discussed at its Spring Stated Meeting, 1999, and its Summer Stated Meeting, 1999, the decision of the Session of Cedar Springs PC to allow a woman on staff, under their authority and guidance, to speak from their pulpit on two Sunday evenings, to explain her biblical approach to Christian Education; and

<a id="ga28-p240"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=242 printed_page=240 -->

Whereas, the Session of Cedar Springs PC did not do this for the purpose of promoting women preachers in the PCA; and

Whereas, the TVP has met in executive session during its Summer Stated Meeting of 1999 to discuss this issue more fully; and

Whereas, other elders and presbyteries of the PCA have communicated to the TVP and its members their concerns about this decision of the Cedar Spring PC Session; and

Whereas, TEs Cal Boroughs, Randy Nabors, and Render Caines have met with TE John Wood, moderator of the Cedar Springs Session, to thoroughly discuss this decision;

Therefore Be It Resolved that the Sessions of St Elmo PC, New City Fellowship and Covenant (Chattanooga) PC request that the TVP meeting at its Fall Stated Meeting of 1999 find that the Session of Cedar Springs PC

- is in conformity with the standards of the PCA
- has not acted in any way injurious to the testimony of Christ or His church
- is in submission to their fellow elders, and are eager to maintain the peace and purity of the church; and

Be It Further Resolved, that this action of the TVP be communicated to the Stated Clerk of the General Assembly.

## Overture #2

Whereas, the Scriptures enjoin us to “keep living by the same standards to which we have attained” (Philip 3:16); and

Whereas, we affirm that the Bible “is the one rule to direct us how we may glorify and enjoy Him” (WSC, 2); and

Whereas, the Scriptures emphatically teach: “Let a woman quietly receive instruction with entire submissiveness. But I do not allow a woman to teach or exercise authority over a man, but to remain quiet” (I Tim. 2:11-12); and

Whereas, the Bible also definitely says, “Let the women keep silent in the churches; for they are not permitted to speak, but let them subject themselves, just as the Law also says. And if they desire to learn anything, let them ask their own husbands at home; for it is improper for a woman to speak in church” (I Cor 14:34-35J); and

Whereas, our constitutional documents take note of the care that should be taken in the public reading and preaching of the Word (WLC, 156, 158; BCO 50:1-2; 53), and

Whereas, these are not minor issues of conscience but effect our presbytery’s public witness among the PCA and even in the whole world;

Therefore Be It Resolved, that the session of Mountain View PC overtures the TVP to openly and plainly declare that it is not proper for women to preach and teach in the church’s corporate worship;

<a id="ga28-p241"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=243 printed_page=241 -->

and

Be It Further Resolved, that a copy of this overture be forwarded to the Stated Clerk of the PCA.

## CASE 99-2

## COMPLAINT OF TE AURELIANO TAN VS SOUTH TEXAS PRESBYTERY

The Complaint of TE Tan was declared administratively out of order, per *SJC Manual* 10.1(a) *SJC Manual* 19.10(b) and *BCO* 43.3, as not timely filed. The action complained against occurred at the July 30-31 meeting of STP. The Complaint, though dated and sent by Fax on August 28, was not mailed until September 4, 1999; exceeding the 30-day period specified in BCO 43-3.

Further, the Complaint was not received by the Stated Clerk of General Assembly and Presbytery until September 7, 1999 by regular U S Mail. *SJC Manual* requires that “filings shall not be timely unless the documents are received in the office of the Stated Clerk within the time fixed for such filing, except that papers shall be deemed filed on the date of mailing if certified, registered or express mail of the United States Postal Service or any private service where verifying receipt is utilized. Neither facsimiles nor E-mail will be allowed for purposes of filing.” (*SJC Manual* 19.10).

**NOTE:** This report was written by RE John B. White, Jr., with the concurrence of TE Dominic Aquilla and TE Ben Konopa

## APPEAL OF ED FITZSIMMONS VS EVANGEL PRESBYTERY

This appeal was declared not timely filed and, therefore, administratively out of order in that the appeal was not filed with the Stated Clerk of General Assembly within the required 30-day time period (BCO 42-2). “Written notice of appeal, with supporting reasons, shall be filed by the appellant with both the clerk of the lower court and the clerk of the higher court, within thirty (30) days following the meeting of the court.”

### 28-53 Protest by TE James Jones, Jr.

TE James Jones entered the following protest against the Assembly’s action on the Creation Study Committee:

We, the undersigned, hereby protest the action of the 28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America in approving the report of the Creation Study Committee.

By adopting the report with the amendment that the divergent views described therein are all equally valid expressions of the teaching of the Scriptures and the Westminster Standards on the subject of Creation, the General Assembly has bound the consciences of good men.

Further, the General Assembly has by its actions created an intolerable situation for those presbyteries which have already taken a stand on the issue of Creation and which have declared that those divergent views are in fact exceptions to the Standards.

The Assembly has effected a new standard of judgement contrary to the Bible and our Constitution to which all presbyteries and individuals must now confirm.

The net result of this action is that the Standards have been modified by an unconstitutional method, the plain and clear teaching of the Word of God has been obscured, and the discipline of the church of the Lord Jesus Christ has been eviscerated.

<a id="ga28-p246"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=248 printed_page=246 -->

Therefore, we the undersigned decry and denounce the action of the 28th General Assembly in opening the door for these divergent views to be held without exception in the Presbyterian Church in America.

Signed, TE James Jones Jr,

RE Charles Joseph, North Georgia; TE Dwight Dolby, Southwest Florida; TE Daivd W. Hall, Tennessee Vly; TE George A. Crocker, Louisiana; RE M. Dale Peacock, Louisiana; TE Daniel Foreman, Westminster; TE Jeffrey M. Black, Eastern Carolina; RE David Warren, No. Illinois; TE Bill Leuzinger, Westminster; TE Jeffrey M. Pennington, No. Illinois; RE Daniel J. Jarstfer, Calvary; TE John O. Butler, Mid-America; TE Earl Fair, Ascension; TE Charles L. Wilson, Palmetto; TE Carel Van Der Merwe, Westminster; TE G. Brent Bradley, Westminster; TE Richard Hicks, Westminster; TE Robert E. Hays, Mississippi Vly; RE Lee Atkinson, Mississippi Vly; RE Charles A. Hickey, Great Lakes; TE Scott Hill, Fellowship; TE John R. Riddle, Palmetto; TE Ted Lester, No. Georgia; TE Donald S. Stone, Philadelphia; TE Robert E. Spears, Calvary; TE Irfon Hughes, Ascension; TE Keith Graham, New Jersey; TE H. Timothy Miessler, Calvary; TE Brian Abshire, Pacific NW; RE Carlton Blanton, Calvary; RE Timothy J. Verner, No. Georgia; RE Michael W. Simpson, South Texas.

The Assembly adopted the following response to the protest: “In answer to the protest, the Assembly notes that in its judgement it has established no such standard, but has merely expressed its own opinion in the matter”.

## 28-54 Theological Examining Committee

TE Dan Doriani, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the report. He informed the Assembly that TE Dominic Aquila sustained his exam for the position of PCA News Officer. The Assembly received the report.

The Theological Examining Committee met only one time from June 1999 to June 2000 to fulfill its charge: "[T]o examine all first and second level administrative officers of committees, boards and agencies, and those acting temporarily in these positions who are being recommended for first time employment" (BCO 14.1.14).

The committee members are TE Dan Doriani (chair), TE J. Ligon Duncan III, RE Terrill Elniff, RE James "Bebo" Elkin and RE Chuck Anderson. Committee member Sam Hoyt (TE) passed away in 2000. Minutes of each meeting are filed with Stated Clerk's office. Here is our summary:

## Examination

1. On June 20, the TEC examined one administrative officer, Dominic Aquila, hired by the Administrative Committee to serve as PCA News Officer. He was examined, sustained and approved in all areas specified in BCO 14.1.14.

<a id="ga28-p247"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=249 printed_page=247 -->

Christian experience, theology, the sacraments, Bible content, church
government, church history and the history of the PCA. Dr Aquila had no
exceptions to the Westminster Confession and Catechisms. The committeee
sustained the examination

Committee business
1. On occasion in the past, the TEC has said that its duty is to examine with regard
to "theological views rather than knowledge." This year the TEC reaffirmed
decisions made by the TEC in 1993, 1994, 1995 and 1999 to follow the directives
of BCO 14 1 14 by examining in each area specified there: Christian experience,
Bible content, Theology, The Sacraments, Church Government, and Church
History. We give some emphasis to theological views, especially with questions
pertinent to the administrator's area of service, but we do cover all the areas
required by the BCO.
2. The committee pursues no agenda, but does hear the views of administrative
leaders regarding controverted issues such as the Sabbath, the timing of creation,
and the roles of women, to name a few. We have approved candidates with
differing views in some of these areas as long as they affirm the fundamentals of
biblical, Reformed theology, the Westminster Standards and remain within the
accepted positions of the PCA.
3. with ted an doriani rotating off the committee at the expiration of his three-
year term, the committee elected TE Ligon Duncan to replace him as chairman
4. the tec request that the nominating committee of ga place those in nomination
men who have expressed the ability to travel and perform the duties of the
committee and, ordinarily, those with experience in theological examination at
Presbytery and/or General Assembly level.
5. BCO 14.1.14 says "No person will begin work or move on the field without prior
examination and approval by the GA's TEC." We understand the reasons why
men do in fact begin work and move to the field before the exam, but we
regrettably report that it is not the exception, but almost the rule that men begin
their work, at least on an interim basis, before their examination. The TEC is
willing to accommodate all agencies and committees for prompt exams of their
prospective appointees. We humbly petition that agencies arrange for
examination of their appointees before they begin their work
