28-57 Committee of Commissioners on Covenant College
TE John Kinser, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the Report
(See also Appendix E, p. 426) He introduced RE Joel Belz, chairman of the board of
Trustees, who addressed the Assembly. The Assembly sang “Gracious Savior, Gentle
Shepherd” . The report was closed with prayer by RE Charles Cox

#### I. Business Referred to the Committee

2000

Response to Protest”

#### II. statement of major issues discussed

• The committee was concerned that Overture 20 maybe out of order and
should not be under consideration by the Committee. This was due to the
fact that Covenant College’s response to the protest has not yet been
officially received. The Committee drafted a motion expressing its concern
over this matter, and forwarded it to the Committee for Constitutional
Business (CCB) for its opinion. CCB determined that the Overture was in
order, and the Committee then proceeded to address it.
• the committee debated at length the response of the board of trustees of
Covenant College to 27-55 Protest #1 by TE Henry Johnson, as instructed by
the 27th General Assembly.

#### III. Recommendations:

1. That the General Assembly approve the proposed operating budget for
2000 - 2001 . adopted under the AC report
2. That the General Assembly accept the Covenant College Financial
Report, dated June 30, 1999, and prepared by Hazlett, Lewis, and Bieter,
PLLC Adopted

<a id="ga28-p250"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=252 printed_page=250 -->

3 That the General Assembly receive the Covenant College Partnership
Promise, and that a representative from the college be granted five
minutes to present the program Adapted
4 That Overture 20 be answered in the negative, and that the response of
the board of trustees of covenant college be accepted as fulfilling the
directive of the 27th General Assembly Adopted

on behalf of covenant College, its board of trustees respectfully
responds to the 28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America:
At least three distinct issues are raised by the protest received by the 27th
General Assembly taking note of “the fact that a choir from
Covenant College sang in a Roman Catholic mass.”
1) The inflammatory manner in which the information was first
made available to the assembly. However factually correct the
initial resolution may have been, not all salient facts were
included with the initial report, nor had either administrators of
the college or officers of the board been asked about such details.
The college board respectfully requests that the Assembly take
care in receiving all reports, from all kinds of parties, to insure
that, in terms of the Westminster Shorter Catechism, “our own
and our neighbor’s good name” is duly protected.
2) Exploiting every opportunity and context to speak God’s
truth. the context of the incident in question was reported by
President Frank Brock to the 27th Assembly in Louisville. While
on tour in Slovakia, Hungary and the Czech Republic, the
Chamber Choir sang ten different services/programs in various
venues Arrangements in Prague were made by PCA missionary,
Sid Anderson, in Hungary by former missionary, Jim Doep, and
in slovakia by a Slovakian, Liba Radkova, who was
recommended by PCA missionary, John Lesondak. (Liba helps
recruit students for Covenant’s biblically based management
program for executives.) In Slovakia the choir sang in two
concerts in Evangelical churches (in the Lutheran tradition) and
two concerts in Catholic churches. In the Catholic churches the
choir sang a few selections during the mass that preceded the
concerts. the second of the two was particularly significant in
that the priest dispensed with the sermon and instead invited
someone from the congregation an done of our singers to testify
to the work of god in their lives. The young adult Slovakian
spoke at length (through a translator for our benefit) about her
personal faith in Christ and it was an inspiring testimony. one of
our students then spoke (again through a translator for the benefit
of the congregation) of her commitment to christ and the work of
the Holy Spirit in her life. It was a 5:00 o'clock service on a

<a id="ga28-p251"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=253 printed_page=251 -->

Friday and the beautiful baroque church was filled to near capacity. Afterward the choir met with a large group of their young people. To the extent that the engagement was more in the nature of an opportunity to witness and a concert than of a worship service, the board of Covenant College endorses the decision of the Madrigals’ director to accept the singing assignment, and would encourage judicious exploitation of all such opportunities in the future.

3) Compromise of confessional standards of worship. The fact that a Roman Catholic Eucharist was celebrated while the Madrigal group sang could complicate the whole issue if the choir participated in Eucharist but under the direction of the choir director, the Chamber Choir intentionally did not partake of the Eucharist. The board and administration of the college believe it is wrong for any group representing the college knowingly to participate in worship practices which our Confession of Faith precludes; we are further agreed that the Roman Catholic celebration of the Eucharist clearly falls into this category. The board and administration have counseled the college family to take care that such cautions are observed in the future.

##### OVERTURE 20 From Westminster Presbytery

“Reject Covenant College’s Response to Protest”

Whereas, our high calling extends beyond the local congregation to the regional and national levels of the church (Deuteronomy 13:12-18), and

Whereas, part of this responsibility involves the maintaining of truth and righteousness in all of the agencies of the General Assembly; and

Whereas, the Covenant College Board of Trustees has acknowledged that a choir from Covenant College sang during the observance of the Roman Catholic mass; and

Whereas, the Board has also responded to a protest which was lodged during the 27th General Assembly with regard to this matter; and

Whereas, in making response to this protest, the Board has tried to make a distinction between singing in a mass and participating in it; and

Whereas, this is a distinction without a difference; and

Whereas, no competent church historian or theologian would recognize such a distinction; and

Whereas, the Roman Catholic Church itself does not regard this distinction as valid; and

Whereas, our Confessional Standards are clear, that the doctrine of transubstantiation “is repungnant, not to Scripture alone, but even to common sense, and reason; and overthroweth the nature of the sacrament, and hath been, and is, the cause of manifold superstitions; yea, of gross idolatries” (Confession of Faith, XXIX, 5); and

<a id="ga28-p252"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=254 printed_page=252 -->

Whereas, the doctrine of transubstantiation is integral ly tied with the
celebration of the mass, and

Whereas, our Confessional Standards also declare the truth that “the popish
sacrifice of the mass is most abominably injurious to Christ’s one,
only sacrifice, the alone propitiation for all the sins of his elect”
0Confession of Faith, XXIX, 2), and

Whereas, as a Presbyterian denomination, we have a solemn obligation to
maintain our historic doctrine, which is also taught in Holy Scripture
(Hebrews 7:27), and

Whereas, the singing ina roman catholic mass by a choir which is under the
auspices of our denomination has subjected the Presbyterian Church in America to ridicule and suspicion, and
W'hereas, we have an obligation to protect our church’s own good name as well
as the good name of covenant college (1 Corinthians 14:40, 1
Thessalonians 5:23); and

Whereas, the explanation given by the Covenant College Board does not
excuse the participation in this false and superstitious worship (Romans
6 :1-2);

Now Therefore Be it Resolved, that Westminster Presbytery overtures the 28th
General Assembly to reject the Board’s proposed response to the protest;
and that the 28th General Assembly direct the board of covenant college
not to permit any musical group from the college to participate in any
service where a Roman Catholic mass is held,
And Be It Further Resolved, that since this matter is broader than merely the
affairs of covenant College, that Westminster Presbytery hereby
requests that if this overture is referred to a committee of
Commissioners, that it be sent to the Bills & Overtures Committee, and
that otherwise, if the response to the protest comes directly to the floor of
the Assembly (in accord with precedent - M20GA, pp 78-79), that this
Overture be considered at the time that the proposed response to the
protest comes to the floor of the Assembly.

Adopted by Westminster Presbytery on April 8, 2000.
Attested by: /s/ Stephen D Meyerhoff, Stated Clerk

5. That the General Assembly accept the Loan Study for Covenant College
board of Trustees. (This study deals with student loans, not loans for the
college itself.) Adopted
6. that the General Assembly approve the minutes of the meetings of the
board of trustees for october 7-8, 1999 and March 16-17, 2000 Adopted
7. That the General Assembly approve the revisions to the Covenant
college board of trustees standing policies and Procedures. Adopted.
8 That the General Assembly receive as information The 1998-1999
Institutional Effectiveness Report Adopted

<a id="ga28-p253"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=255 printed_page=253 -->

9. That the General Assembly praise God for the continued advancement of
the ministry of covenant College. Further, that the Assembly thank God
for the leadership of the board of Trustees, President Frank Brock, and
the faculty and staff. Adopted
10. That the General Assembly heartily commend outgoing Chairman Joel
Belz for his faithful leadership and service to the college Adopted
11. that the General Assembly forward the response of the board of
trustees to the general synod of bible presbyterian church as a partial
response to Communication 3 Adopted

COMMUNICATION 3 from the general synod of the bible presbyterian
Church “Admonitions From the Bible Presbyterian Church to General Assembly” [See also 28-67, III, 11, p 266]

Dear Rev Taylor
enclosed please find a copy of a resolution which was passed
unanimously by the 63rd general synod of the bible presbyterian church
concerning a press release issued by your 1999 General Assembly
We admonish you to recognize the apostasy in the PCUSA and to have
no fellowship with it. Certainly we are to be gracious in our Christian conduct;
yet, it is our belief that to report in a public statement that your denomination
has a “cordial” relationship with the PCUSA, while in no way mentioning the
blatant apostasy in that group, should be cause for great concern.
also of great concern is the fact that covenant colleges en ta music team
to Eastern Europe, at which time they sang during the serving of the roman
Catholic mass. Surely, this should be troubling to you and your denomination.
Please see the enclosed resolution which deals with this situation.
Sincerely,
/s/ Brad K. Gsell, Stated Clerk

ATTACHMENT
RESOLUTION 63:3
“Cordial” Relations Between Presbyterian Church in America
and Presbyterian Church (USA)

The 27th General Assembly of the presbyterian church in
America(PCA), meeting in Louisville, KY, June 1999, issued its first news
release under the title “General Assembly Brings Presbyterian Denominations
Together in Louisville.” This is a reference to Louisville being the headquarters
of the presbyterian church (USA). The statement reports that “PCA Stated
Clerk (top administrator) Dr L. Roy Taylor [paid] a courtesy call on his
counterpart, Clifton Kirkpatrick, prior to the General Assembly.”
The only hint in the statement that any difference exists between the two
denominations is the passing mention that the PCA is largely comprised of those
coming from the “conservative wing of the old southern presbyterian Church”.

<a id="ga28-p254"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=256 printed_page=254 -->

While stating that the PCA is “conservative,” while the PCUSA is “more diverse,” it is quick to add that the PCA and the PCUSA “remain cordial.”

There is absolutely no mention that the PCUSA is apostate, having a large section of the church pushing for the ordination of homosexual clergy and elders, worshipers of the goddess Sophia, a publicly pro-abortion stance, and a new catechism which attacks the central Christian teaching that salvation is possible through Jesus Christ alone.

The 63rd General Synod of the Bible Presbyterian Church, meeting in Orchard Park, NY, August 5-10, 1999, calls upon the Presbyterian Church in America to “have no fellowship with the unfruitful works of darkness, but, rather reprove them” (Ephesians 5:11), and to “come out from among them, and be ye separate, saith the Lord, and touch not the unclean thing” (2 Corinthians 6:17).

Adopted by the 63rd General Synod of the Bible Presbyterian Church,
Meeting in Orchard Park, NY August 5-10, 1999

## 28-58 Committee of Commissioners on Mission to the World

RE John Vanderveld, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the report (See Appendix J, p. 560.)

<a id="ga28-p255"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=257 printed_page=255 -->

TE Blake Purcell introduced Andrey Zeekov, Mikhail Titov, and Vladislav Nebjidovski, visitors from the Reformed church in Russia. TE Paul Kooistra reported on the work of MTW, following which the Chairman closed in prayer for MTW and for TE Paul Kooistra.

#### I. Business Referred to the Committee

1. Minutes of Permanent Committee:
March 10-12, 1999
October 6-8, 1999
2. Report of the Permanent Committee and its recommendations.
3. Budget of MTW

#### II. Statement Of Major Issues Discussed

Proposal of Mississippi Valley Presbytery regarding St. Petersburg, Russia

#### III. Recommendations:

1. That the month of October 2001 be set aside as a month of prayer for the “10/40 Window” and world evangelization, and that God would send many more laborers to His harvest field. Adopted
2. That the General Assembly urge the churches to set aside a portion of their giving for the suffering peoples of the world; to that end be it recommended that a special offering for relief and mercy (Compassion Ministry) be taken during 2001 to be distributed by MTW. Adopted
3. That the General Assembly set aside Sunday, November 19, 2000, as a day of prayer and fasting for the persecuted church worldwide. Adopted
4. That Dr. Paul D. Kooistra, having been evaluated to have performed with excellence in the position of Coordinator of Mission to the World during the 1998-1999 program year, and having been commended by the Committee on Mission to the World for his exceptional performance in serving as both the spiritual and administrative leader of Mission to the World for the past year, be recommended to the General Assembly of the PCA for reelection as Coordinator. Adopted
5. That the proposed budget of MTW, as presented through the Administrative Committee, be approved. Adopted
6. That the minutes of the meeting of March 10-12, 1999 be accepted. Adopted
7. That the minutes of the meeting of October 6-8, 1999 be accepted. Adopted
8. That the Committee of Commissioners of MTW recommends that the General Assembly fully endorse the plans for relocation of MTW’s offices from their current site in downtown Atlanta, GA to the new proposed site. Adopted
9. Committee of Commissioners for Mission to the World recommends that Overture 11 be answered in the following manner:

<a id="ga28-p256"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=258 printed_page=256 -->

- TE Blake Purcell and Mississippi Valley Presbytery are to be commended for their initiative in beginning the work in St. Petersburg, Russia.
- Since MTW has already been given by General Assembly the power of a commission to form provisional presbyteries in foreign countries, GA hereby designates MTW to function through appropriate missionaries, national workers and representatives of Mississippi Valley Presbyteries (teaching elders) as the designated commission.
- MVP and MTW are already coordinating their efforts.
- Therefore, the Overture is answered in the negative, with full consent and cooperation of all interested parties.

Adopted

That the following statement be communicated to the Stated Clerk of the General Assembly and to the Stated Clerk of Mississippi Valley Presbytery as the opinion of CMTW regarding Overture 11 – from the Presbytery of Mississippi Valley (Docket page FM 53):

While we rejoice in any establishment of Reformed and Covenantal churches around the world, the PCA has given the mandate to MTW, as recorded in the MTW Manual:

The relationship of the Committee to the presbyteries and sessions of the denomination is defined by the duties assigned to the Committee by the General Assembly. Its role is to serve and offer coordinating facilities to these church courts. It seeks to aid these courts in the search for candidates for mission service overseas, in processing candidates, maintaining essential services for, and communication with, missionaries on the field, and in keeping the home church aware of and supportive of the ministries of such missionaries. Section 1.01 paragraph 2

The primary thrust will be in the familiar denominational pattern of developing a mission work in particular countries that is completely Presbyterian and Reformed in character, with all the missionaries on such fields being missionaries of the PCA, though some may be serving on a cooperative basis as having come from sister churches in the Reformed family of denominations. Section 1.01 paragraph 11.

It is the opinion of CMTW that the Presbytery of Mississippi Valley in accordance with BCO 8-6, has the power to authorize the evangelist to ordain and install ruling elders and deacons in the congregation which he is planting in St. Petersburg; it is also the opinion of CMTW that it would be unwise for the General Assembly to establish a commission to establish a provisional Presbytery in Russia until such time as it is possible to coordinate the establishment of a Presbytery with the developing work of MTW in Russia. Heretofore, when BCO 15-6 has been implemented, it has been initiated and coordinated by MTW. CMTW believes this brings order and avoids confusion to young and emerging churches.

<a id="ga28-p257"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=259 printed_page=257 -->

## OVERTURE 11
From the Presbytery of Mississippi Valley
“Authorize Formation of a Provisional Presbytery in Russia”

**Whereas**, TE Blake Purcell is a member of Mississippi Valley Presbytery, and

**Whereas**, Mississippi Valley Presbytery has authorized TE Blake Purcell to serve out of bounds as an evangelist in St. Petersburg, Russia, and

**Whereas**, Mississippi Valley Presbytery has entrusted to TE Blake Purcell (BCO 8-6) "the power to organize churches, and, until there is a Session in the church so organized, to instruct, examine, ordain, and install ruling elders and deacons therein, and to receive or dismiss members;"

**Therefore, Be it Resolved** that Mississippi Valley Presbytery overture the 28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America, according to BCO 15-6, to exercise its "power to commit to a commission, consisting of not less than three elders, the task of forming a provisional Presbytery in a foreign country [Russia] where there exists no compatible indigenous presbyterian and reformed church. Such a commission shall have authority to act as the Presbytery in all matters pertaining to the establishment and ordering of a national church and shall report annually to the General Assembly. The commission shall be dissolved when there are at least three national teaching elders and three organized churches under its care, and these shall then constitute a separate national church."

Adopted at the Winter Stated Meeting of The Presbytery of the Mississippi Valley on February 15, 2000. Attested by: /s/ Roger G. Collins, Stated Clerk

10. That **Personal Resolution #4** from TE Jeffrey Black be answered in the negative. The reason for this is that TE Jeffrey Black and MTW have talked and TE Black is satisfied with their response. The fund drive has been discontinued, MTW has properly disposed of all funds and the Committee of Commissioners commends MTW for acting with complete integrity and good prudence in this matter.
*Adopted*

28-64 Ad-Interim Committee on Women in the Military
TE Peter Lillback, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the
report. Upon the recommendation from the Committee, the Assembly extended the
Committee for another year.

Interim Report of the
Women In The Military Committee

our committee is in its second year of existence. It was established by the GA
in response to various overtures presented to it by the presbytery of Philadelphia. Its
first report was received as information and the GA subsequently augmented the
committee with two additional members Chaplain Steve Leonard, US Army, the first
chairman oft hew im Committee, following a hospitalization, asked Dr. Peter Lillback
to assume the chair. Dr. Lillback was subsequently elected by the Committee, and
Chaplain Leonard has continued to serve as co-chairman. the original members of the
committee are: Rev. Beryl Hubbard, Chaplain Steve Leonard, Dr. Peter Lillback, Mr.
Keith Stoeber, Rev. Charlie Morrison, Mr. Don Weyburn, Rev. Frank Barker, and
Brigadier General Bentley Rayburn, US Air Force. Additional members are Dr. Steven
Clark and Chaplain Ron Swafford, US Navy. The Moderator Tom Leopard also
appointed Rev. Tim Bayly as consultant and Rev. Richard Phillips as Alternate. This
new Committee has met twice in St. Louis at Covenant Theological Seminary. The first
meeting included, for part of the time, the OPC Committee studying this matter, and
also various Covenant Seminary professors and a few invited guests who were given the
privilege of the floor when the committee was not in executive session.
While the Committee hoped to report to this GA, it has not been able to
complete its work yet. This is due to several factors. The first reason is the delay in
meeting caused by the securing of new members, and due to health concerns of
members of the committee and members of their families. Second, there has been
significant discussion from divergent viewpoints, requiring thoughtful response and
criticism by the various committee members. The careful reflection and writing this
process requires have prevented the completion of a final written report in time forth is
GA

<a id="ga28-p260"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=262 printed_page=260 -->

The Committee is pleased to report that it has raised approximately $7,700 for
its work. Moreover, the members have been thrifty in expenditures for travel and
accommodation. the hospitality of covenant seminary has substantially defrayed the
expenses of the Committee. Thus, the Committee anticipates a surplus at the
conclusion of its work.

Issues addressed by the Committee include:
Concerns sent to it by the GA:
1. the basic questions of the legitimacy of women serving in combat roles, and
whether it is appropriate for women to serve in the military,
2. Various questions stated in the overtures to the Assembly.

Historical:
1. Is there precedent for women serving in the military in redemptive history,
political history or ecclesiastical history?
2. Have various Christian teachers throughout Christian history addressed these
questions?

Hermeneutical:
1. the nature of the historical-redemptive purpose of god revealed in scripture
versus the question of moral duty taught in Scripture;
2. the nature and extent of the general equity of the ot civil law;
3. the meaning and extent of the sufficiency of Scripture.

Biblical exegetical:
1. the issues of male and female sexuality and the inter-relationships of the
genders in the purposes of God;
2. the meaning of several texts that may directly or indirectly apply to the
questions at hand.
3 the sufficiency of the gospel to impact culture and create moral renewal

Ecclesiological:
1. the limit and extent of ecclesiastical concerns that can properly be brought
before the GA;
2. the propriety of the ga addressing the church, the state and the culture on
moral issues;
3. the spirituality of the church.
4. the liberty of the conscience and the binding of the conscience.
5. the role of church discipline in regard to the issue of women serving in the
military, and/or combat.

Ethical:
1. The inter-relatedness of the pro-life commitment of the PCA and the risk of a
female combatant's unborn child,

<a id="ga28-p261"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=263 printed_page=261 -->

2. the definition and nature of a combatant a swell as the propriety of the
distinction between offensive and defensive combat,
3. the issue of oath a sit impacts both marriage and military service.

Confessional Concerns:
1. the teaching of our standards on issues of just war, and moral duties that relate
to military service by men and women,
2. the legitimacy of a range of views on these matters within our confession.

in light of the above, the Women In The Military Committee recommends to the
GA, that it extend the Committee by one year to enable it to complete its work. It is
believed that this additional year will cost the Assembly no further expense, since
necessary funds have already been raised. The extension will enable the Committee to
meet at least once more, and hopefully to produce a thorough and satisfactory response
to the concerns of the Assembly.

Respectfully Submitted,
Is/ Dr. Peter A. Lillback, Chairman

28-66 Committee of Commissioners on Insurance, Annuities and Relief
TE William Lyle, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the report

IAR in his life.

November 5, 1999 and March 3, 2000.

statements).

#### II. statement of major issues discussed

IAR Director Jim Hughes presented an oral report and referred to various items
in the printed materials. among the items reviewed were the status of the
Property-Casualty program, the Long Term Care program, the two health
programs and the status of the other insurance programs. RE Hughes also
commented that IAR continues to pursue applying technology to their various

<a id="ga28-p262"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=264 printed_page=262 -->

functional areas (particularly the internet) in order to improve information flow
to participants. Questions followed
Trustee RE Gary Campbell reviewed the Investment Funds including
participation and performance and reported changes made in the PCA Balanced
Fund this year, particularly affecting asset allocation. He also discussed the role
of values investment forum in application of an improved moral standards
investment policy and responded to questions.
Trustee TE Robert Clarke reported regarding IAR Relief. Thirty-nine
family groups falling under the guidelines are currently receiving some form of
relief. This level is projected to increase as the target group continues to
increase in age and number.

#### III. Recommendations:

1 that we receive with notations the minutes of august 13 1999, November 5,
1999, March 3, 2000. Adopted
2. That the audit report dated December 31, 1999 by Capin Crouse LLP be
received. Adopted
3. that the General Assembly approve the use of cap in crouse LLP, to conduct
the 2000 audit. Adopted
4. That the 2001 budget be received with the understanding that it is a spending
plan and will be adjusted by the Board during the year, if necessary, to
accommodate increased enrollments in the PCA benefit plans.
Adopted under the AC Report
5. That the General Assembly urge our member churches to participate in the
annual Christmas Offering or budget regular benevolence giving to support
relief activities through the Ministerial Relief Fund. Adopted
6. That the General Assembly approve the Eighteenth and Nineteenth
Amendments to the Presbyterian Church in America Voluntary Tax-Sheltered
Annuity Plan and the Eleventh and Twelfth Amendments to the Presbyterian Church in America Money Purchase Pension Plan (see Appendix G, Two, p.
480) and that the resolutions related to these amendments be adopted and
included in the minutes of the General Assembly. [Text is at Appendix G, p.
482] Adopted
7. that the General Assembly ratify the decision of the board of trustees to revise
the statement of purpose in the iar bylaws to reflect the addition of a property
and liability insurance plan for PCA churches, committees and agencies (see
Appendix G, Three, p. 509). Adopted
the purpose of the board of trustees of the Insurance, Annuity and
relief funds of the Presbyterian Church in America is to glorify god
through serving His servants who minister in the Church by offering
financial products to provide for the health and welfare, retirement
and relief needs of the denomination's ministers, missionaries, lay
church workers and their families, and to serve the churches,
committees and agencies of the PCA and related organizations by
sponsoring and endorsing a denominational property and liability
insurance program.

<a id="ga28-p263"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=265 printed_page=263 -->

security be strongly urged to consider participating at this time in light of medicare
needs and the window of opportunity presented and further that the chairman urge our
member churches to actively participate in the Ministerial Relief Fund.

- The duties and responsibilities of the Steering Committee under the Strategic Planning Committee.

## III. Recommendations:

1. That the 28th General Assembly authorize the fair-market-value sale of the PCA Office Building located at 1852 Century Place, Atlanta, Georgia 30345. [NOTE: The verbal and hand votes at the AC meeting both indicated a unanimous agreement of the AC at-large committee members and the other Committees’ and Agencies’ representatives on the AC]. Adopted

2. That $710,000 of the proceeds of the sale be returned to Mission to the World, previously received from MTW as a conditional gift to be repaid upon the sale of the PCA Office Building located at 1852 Century Place, Atlanta, Georgia 30345. [See M27GA, pp. 237-238.] Adopted

3. That the remaining proceeds of the sale after payment of $710,000 to Mission to the World be distributed 42.04% to MTW and the balance be retained by the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation). Adopted

4. That the AC and MTW be authorized by the General Assembly to use their respective equities from the sale of the PCA Office Building to secure office facilities for the Atlanta-based Committees and Agencies. The land and building to be occupied by MTW would be deeded to and owned by Mission to the World (PCA), Inc. The land and building occupied by all the other Atlanta-based committees and agencies would be deeded to and owned by the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation). Adopted

5. That the PCA committees and agencies now occupying offices in the PCA Office Building who wish to construct and purchase office facilities in the Greater Atlanta Area be allowed to work together to purchase new property adequate to build two one-story buildings with a total of approximately 44,000 square feet and move together to the new site. Adopted

6. That the PCA General Assembly committees and agencies planning to operate in the new facilities be allowed to initiate jointly a Capital campaign to help fund this project. The capital campaign will include the help of professional consultation. A feasibility study will be made before embarking on the campaign. Adopted

7. That the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) be allowed to mortgage the new property of the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) [not the property of Mission to the World (PCA), Inc.] for a maximum of $2,000,000 to finance the property/facility to be used by AC, MNA, CE&amp;P, IAR, and/or PCAF. Adopted

8. That permission be granted that a contract(s), which could bring the best benefits of all laws and regulations (federal, state and local) to the user (the PCA ministries and corporations) and the provider (the present owners) be negotiated with the advice of real-estate, legal, and accounting professionals. Such contract could involve land contributions, leases, “lease to buy” or other instruments, which would achieve the legitimate ends of both the PCA and the current owner of any property being considered for Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) usage. As the property and related contract(s) will involve both

<a id="ga28-p265"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=267 printed_page=265 -->

the Presbyterian Church in America and Mission to The World (PCA), Inc., the
contract(s) ultimately are to be approved by the board of directors of the
Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) and the board of directors of
Mission to The World (PCA), Inc. It is understood that the PCA interest is to
own its property eventually and no contract(s) will be entered into which does
not grant the PCA corporations the option to do so. Adopted
9. That the following recommendation regarding Chief Administrative Officer
(CAO) Compensation and Guideline for 2001 be approved as represented by the

The subcommittee assumed a definition of compensation as including any income, or form of

10. That a nine-member Strategic Planning Committee for the PCA General Assembly Ministries, be appointed by the AC [BCO 14-1(12), RAO 4-2], in
order to enhance their cooperative service to the denomination as a whole, with
Frank Brock, president of covenant College, as Chairman and Roy Taylor,
stated clerk of the PCA, as Secretary. The remaining GA ministries
coordinators, presidents, and directors would make up the nine-member
Strategic Planning Committee. The Strategic Planning Committee would
nominate to the AC a broadly representative twenty-four-member Steering
Committee (with each nominated member to be approved by the AC) to give
theological, spiritual and organizational input to the planning function, to
review and approve the plans as they develop before the plans are presented to
the AC by the Steering Committee. Voting members will be R.E.s and T.E.s;
any other steering committee members will serve as advisors The AC would
then receive the plan, review it, and bring the final recommendation to the 30th
General Assembly in 2002. the implementation of this motion is contingent
upon securing the required funding. Adopted

<a id="ga28-p266"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=268 printed_page=266 -->

11. That the General Assembly reply to the Bible Presbyterian Church by sending
the following communication along with a copy of “An Address to All
Churches”:
We appreciate your zeal for the Lord. We regard our separation from
our former denomination in 1973 to have been a tragic necessity. The
Presbyterian Church in America was formed as a result of “long years of
struggle and heartache on the part of many of us to return the church to purity of
faith and practice” (Minutes of the first General Assembly, p. 27, “An Address
to All Churches”). Adopted
12. That Overture 14 from New Jersey Presbytery, “Erect Committee to Develop
funding of General Assembly,” be answered in the negative on the grounds (1)
the overture does not conform to the requirements of RAO 8-4 specifying a
method of funding for an ad hoc committee, (2) the responsibility of
recommending fees for General Assembly registration is clearly delegated to the
AC (RAO 9-1, 9-4), and (3) the AC is best equipped to consider the overall
picture of the many factors in determining the fee Adopted

##### OVERTURE 14 From New Jersey Presbytery (to AC)

“Erect committee to develop funding of General Assembly”

Whereas, the General Assembly consists of all teaching elders (TE) who are in
good standing in their Presbyteries and Ruling Elders (RE) elected by
their Sessions (BCO 14-2), and

Whereas, congregations are entitled to two RE representatives for the first 350
communing members or fraction thereof, and one additional RE for each
additional 500 communing members or fraction thereof, (BCO 14-2),
and

Whereas, at the 27th General Assembly the ratio of TE stores was over 2 to 1
(854 to 374), and it has long been the concern of the PCA that there be
more RE representation from the congregations, and

Whereas, small congregations often lack the financial ability to send one
representative let alone two or three, and

Whereas, representatives of small congregations often use creative ways to save
money and still get to General Assembly meeting, (i.e. sharing rides,
using buddy fares on planes, sharing rooms with representatives from
other congregations, staying in inexpensive second-class motels, etc.),
and

Whereas, small congregations may be able to find creative ways to send more
than one representative to General Assembly without large increases in
travel and lodging costs, (i.e. sharing rides, using buddy fares on planes,
sharing rooms, having representatives pay their own travel and meal
costs, etc.), and

Whereas, the increases in the registration fee for attending the General Assembly is making it more and more difficult for small congregations
to send representatives, while large congregations are less impacted by

<a id="ga28-p267"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=269 printed_page=267 -->

the increases on a per capita basis, and

Whereas, this dynamic may lead to an imbalance in representation at the
General Assembly, not only between TEs and REs (as already seen), but
between large and small congregations, and

Whereas, the expenses of representatives to the General Assembly is to be
defrayed by the bodies which they represent, (BCO 10-6), and

Whereas, the Administrative Committee does incur significant expense in
arranging and facilitating the meeting of the General Assembly, and

Whereas, the Administrative Committee is to set a registration fee, subject to
the approval of the General Assembly, (RAO 9-4), and

Whereas, ape rca pita division of the cost is more equitable and in the spirit of
Christian love, than a flat rate which does not take into consideration the
increased financial liability this causes per capita for small congregations
when the flat rate is increased, (i.e. the ratio of increase per ca pita is
much greater on small congregations than on large congregations), and

Whereas, the bible teaches us to take into consideration the needs of our
brethren (cf. Acts 11:29).

Therefore Be it Resolved that the 28th General Assembly of the PCA be asked
to erect a committee to develop a more equitable method of funding the
cost of the Assembly.
adopted by the presbytery of new jersey on february 19, 2000.
Attested by: /s/ James A. Smith, Stated Clerk

WHEREAS, our gracious covenant Lord has blessed our churches over the
centuries in the use of the shorter catechism and the catechism for
young children in the instruction of our youth, and

WHEREAS, in years past, Presbyterian courts have made it their concern that
the youth of the churches be faithfully catechized;

NOW THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED, that the Assembly directs the
Stated Clerk to include questions on the annual church data forms to
include spaces for statistics on catechism instruction and recitations in
the previous year, and that these statistics be included in the annual
report of the stated Clerk.

## 28-71 Responses of the Assembly to the Protest and Objection

A. The Assembly adopted the following response to the Protest to the judgment of the SJC in Case 98-9:

### Response:

Although otherwise in proper form and couched in temperate language, the last sentence of the protest concluded with the words “and call upon the General Assembly, as a court, to condemn the opinion of the SJC in this matter.” This call is a request for the General Assembly to condemn its own action in as much as a judgment of the SJC is that of the General Assembly as provided in *BCO* 15-5 and cannot be considered under our rules.

### Protest:

I hereby present a formal objection to the judgment in Case 98-9, David Baer vs. Illiana Presbytery. Although we do not have the full record of the case before us, even what we do have is sufficient to demonstrate that the Standing Judicial Commission, as the commission of the General Assembly, did not follow the *Book of Church Order*.

According to *BCO* 15-3, when a presbytery commits a judicial matter to a commission, that commission “shall try the case...and shall submit to the Presbytery a full statement of the case and the judgment rendered. The Presbytery without debate shall approve or disapprove of the judgment...If Presbytery approves, the judgment of the commission shall be final and shall be entered on the minutes of Presbytery as the action. If Presbytery disapproves, it shall hear the case as a whole, or appoint a new commission to hear the case again.”

From this paragraph of our Constitution, which reflects the historic Presbyterian understanding of commissions, it is clear that a presbytery (apart from the extraordinary employment of referral of a constitutional issue to a study committee) may not accept only part of its commission’s report on a judicial matter. Rather, the court, “without debate,” votes the report up or down. That includes the censure meted out by the commission.

The reason for this provision is obvious. The commission has had the opportunity to conduct the judicial proceedings, and thus is in the best position to weigh the testimony, as well as to hear the presentation of evidence and arguments in the orderly manner prescribed by our Constitution. However, to allow the court as a whole to impose its own sentence is not appropriate, when the court as a whole has not heard the case.

And yet, that is precisely what happened in the Baer case. The SJC panel itself admits that the Presbytery’s judicial commission convicted Mr. Baer and in August 1998 administered the censure of definite suspension. At the October 1998 stated meeting of Illiana, the Presbytery accepted the findings of its commission, but then proceeded to suspend Mr. Baer indefinitely.

It would have been one thing to have changed a sentence of definite suspension from two months to, say, six months. That, too, would have been wrong. But changing the sentence from definite suspension to indefinite suspension is particularly wrong, and that for at least two reasons. One is that the sentence is lengthened to an indeterminate

<a id="ga28-p271"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=273 printed_page=271 -->

duration, from a relatively short time span. The second reason, however, is the most germane, viz., that the change in censure in this instance changed the whole judgment. Indefinite suspension is to be administered only when the person is impenitent; while definite suspension is appropriate only when the person is repentant. When the commission ruled that Mr. Baer should be suspended for a definite period of time, that commission judged that Mr. Baer had indeed repented. However, when the Presbytery imposed a censure of indefinite suspension, the court overruled that finding of penitence by the commission, and, in essence, determined that the man had not repented.

This sort of injustice is precisely what our rules and procedures are supposed to prevent. An accused had the right to plead with a court as to why he should not be found guilty, or impenitent, and why a certain censure should not be imposed. But according to the SJC’s own record of the matter, Mr. Baer was not present at the Presbytery meeting where the court, without having heard from him, proceeded to suspend him indefinitely, thereby branding him as a man who had not yet repented.

I do not wish to impugn the motives of the SJC or of the panel which heard the appeal. However, the erroneous judgment in this case is yet another example where the SJC has adjudicated in such a way as to pervert justice. By means of this formal objection, I hereby declare my repudiation of the judgment in this judicial case, and call upon the General Assembly, as a court, to condemn the opinion of the SJC in this matter.

/s/ RE Paul English, Central Carolina

The following commissioners requested their names to be recorded in support of this protest: TE Bill Leuzinger, Westminster, and TE Frank J. Smith, Northeast.

B. The Assembly adopted the following response to the Objection to the judgement of the SJC in Case 98-10:

Response:

Said objection includes the following language in the first paragraph “sincerely hoping that the GA will deny the SJC decision and instruct Eastern Carolina that the Appeal of TE Byron Curtis is out of order and not to come before Eastern Carolina Presbytery.”

The General Assembly responds as follows: A recommendation of the SJC in response to a reference of a judicial case is a recommendation of the General Assembly as provided in BCO 15-5 (See also BCO 41-1).

Objection:

The Standing Judicial Commission agreed with the panel’s recommendation that the Complaint (sic) of TE Byron Curtis be returned (sic) to Eastern Carolina Presbytery with the instruction to hear it. I do object to the recommendation upon several grounds to be stated below, sincerely hoping that the GA will deny the SJC decision and instruct Eastern Carolina Presbytery that the Appeal of TE Byron Curtis is out of order and not to come before Eastern Carolina Presbytery.

<a id="ga28-p272"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=274 printed_page=272 -->

My objection rests upon five major grounds: (I) errors in the Statement of Facts; (II) violations of the Constitution by the SJC; (III) the SJC ignoring the action of the CCB; (IV) the SJC contradicting the express understanding and intention of the Eastern Carolina Presbytery’s reference to the CCB; and, (V) apparent untruths in their reasoning.

I. Errors in the Statement of Facts

1. As to item #4 of the Statement of Facts written by the SJC, the Presbytery of the Ascension denied the transfer of TE Byron Curtis because the theology portion of his examination was not sustained. Certainly his declared exceptions were vigorously debated but the record does not declare these to be the basis of the denial of transfer. The SJC has drawn a conclusion as to why members of the presbytery voted in the manner they did so. They may be right but it is conjecture. I know why I voted and it was not merely TE Curtis’s exceptions.

2. Between items #5 and 6 should be added that the Presbytery of the Ascension granted permission to TE Byron Curtis to labor within their bounds for a period of one year to be reassessed annually.

3. As to item #10 the SJC states, “After receiving TE Curtis’s complaint, ECP sent that complaint along with TE Black’s sustained complaint as a reference to GA.” This is in error on two points. First, Eastern Carolina Presbytery did not formally receive TE Byron Curtis’s complaint (sic). Austin Leake, Stated Clerk, read the copy of TE Curtis’s Appeal (Appeal was the term TE Curtis used in his own document, the SJC altered it to “complaint.”) and notified the presbytery that it wasn’t before us as it was merely a copy of the appeal filed with General Assembly. The minutes of January 8 &amp; 9, 1999 demonstrate this in the wording of motion #75 “by acclamation the ECP appoint TEs Jeff Black and James Routszong as its respondent to SJC regarding TE Byron Curtis’ Appeal (sic) to argue the Appeal (sic) is out of order and/or defend ECP should the SJC take up the matter.”

Second, the reference to GA was directed not to the SJC but to the CCB as the minutes of May 8, 1999 prove with motion #20 “We do move that ECP clarify its reference (Motion #55 Winter 1999 Stated Meeting) not to be concerning the complaint of TE Black nor the appeal (sic) of TE Byron Curtis but this specific question to the Committee on Constitutional Business; “Does a presbytery have the authority to require a man not to teach his errors as true in any public forum save that of the courts of the Presbyterian Church in America after that man has been received into the presbytery?”

II. Violations of the Constitution by the SJC

1. The Record of the Case is defined in BCO 32-18 as the official minutes of the trial kept by the clerk. Since the appeal of TE Byron Curtis was never before the Eastern Carolina Presbytery and never appeared on our

<a id="ga28-p273"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=275 printed_page=273 -->

docket for action there can be no “Record of the Case.” Under what definition of “the Record of the Case” is the SJC arguing this case?

2. TE Byron Curtis’s original document was styled by him as an “appeal.” As per *BCO* 42-1 since there was no judicial case in which TE Byron Curtis was a party, his “appeal” was out of order and should have been dismissed.

3. Instead of dismissing the “appeal” the SJC redefined the “appeal” of Byron Curtis to be a “complainant” and directs ECP to hear it though *BCO* 43-3 requires the complaint to file his complaint with the clerk of the court against which he is complaining “within thirty (30) days following the meeting of that court.” Hence, in violation of *BCO* 43-2, the 30 day time limitation, and their own Manual, the SJC is granting TE Byron Curtis 592 days to file his appeal turned complaint. (54 days in 1998 + 365 in 1999 + 173 in 2000; from date of ECP’s action till the GA ratifies the SJC recommendation)

4. The SJC did interpret a reference from ECP to be a judicial reference when in fact the presbytery clearly stated it was a constitutional reference to the CCB. Said interpretation of the SJC does violate *BCO* 41-1, definition of references since they interpret it to be about TE Curtis’s appeal and TE Black’s complaint of which neither were matters pending before the court. (TE Curtis’s appeal never being on the docket for consideration and TE Black’s complaint having been adjudicated more than two months earlier.)

### III. SJC ignoring the action of the CCB at General Assembly 1999 in Louisville, KY.

1. The reference from Eastern Carolina Presbytery which the SJC reasoning states, “ECP referred this matter to the SJC (ROC page 199). The SJC Officers acceded to this reference and referred this matter to the Panel for consideration,” was in fact a reference to the Committee on Constitutional Business and was answered by that committee at the 27th General Assembly. See *Minutes* 27th GA, page 149. In short, the supposed reference of ECP to the SJC is simply a fiction without any existence in fact.

### IV. SJC’s contradicting and ignoring of the express understanding and intention of ECP

1. The reference to which the SJC “acquiesces” was approved by ECP on January 22, 1999, “that ECP send the appeal of TE Byron Curtis, the complaint of TE Jeff Black, and all relevant supporting materials (ie. The Minutes of our Summer and Fall Stated Meetings, etc.) to the General Assembly as a “reference” (*BCO* 41) since the subjects they cover are “delicate or difficult,” “or on which the members of the lower court are very seriously divided,” and since these subjects “relate to questions involving the Constitution and legal procedures respecting which the

<a id="ga28-p274"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=276 printed_page=274 -->

lower court feels the need of guidance.” (BCO 41-2)” Notice that the reference explicitly states that the question is one regarding the Constitution and should therefore have been sent to the Committee on Constitutional Business.

2. TE’s James Routszong and Jeff Black, realizing the General Assembly Stated Clerk might be confused brought the following resolution to ECP which was carried.

**Motion to ECP:**

**Whereas** M#55 at ECP’s winter stated meeting sent both TE Black’s complaint and TE Byron Curtis’s appeal (sic) to the General Assembly as a reference without properly specifying the particular question being asked of the General Assembly; and,

**Whereas** there may be confusion between the two separate matters of TE Curtis’s appeal (sic) and ECP’s reference; and,

**Whereas** the reference can not have any reference to TE Black’s complaint as it had already been decided at an earlier presbytery meeting and was therefore finished usiness not pending business (BCO 41-1 “…for advice or other action on a matter pending before the lower court”); and,

**Whereas** the reference can not have any reference to TE Curtis’s appeal (sic) as that was filed with General Assembly and not ECP and therefore was not business pending before ECP (BCO 41-1 as above);

**We do move** that Eastern Carolina Presbytery clarify its reference (Motion #55 Winter 1999 Stated Meeting) not to be concerning the complaint of TE Black nor the appeal (sic) of TE Byron Curtis but this specific question to the Committee on Constitutional Business: “Does a presbytery have the authority to require a man not to teach his errors as true in any public forum save that of the courts of the Presbyterian Church in America after that man has been received into the presbytery?”

Respectfully Submitted,

TE James Routszong and TE Jeff Black

**IV. Apparent untruths in SJC reasoning.**

1. The 2nd paragraph of the reasoning says, “The Respondents for ECP subsequently argued to the SJC that what ECP had intended to do was to refer the matter as a “non-judicial” reference to the Constitutional Business Committee for advice.” There never was a hearing with the SJC panel nor full commission. As one of the two respondents I would like to know when we argued such before the SJC. In fact the motions for reference and subsequently to clarify the reference were court actions of ECP not merely the Respondents opinions.

2. Also in the 2nd paragraph the reasoning says, “related to judicial complaints already in process.” This is contradicted by the Minutes of ECP. The Curtis complaint was not in process as it was never filed with presbytery and the Black complaint had been adjudicated two months

<a id="ga28-p275"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=277 printed_page=275 -->

earlier. These two items, contrary to the SJC’s reasoning were not in
process.
3. In the 3rd paragraph the reasoning asserts, “the lower court ought to hear
cases before it in fact of law the court ought to hear cases properly
before it. The SJC has dismissed many cases on the grounds that they
were not properly before it In the McDade vs Susquehanna Valley
Presbytery the SJC dismissed the case because it was filed 32 days and
not 30 days from the action of the court. Here the SJC appears to violate
the constitutional definition of properly as regarding the filing date
/s/ TE Jeffrey M. Black, Eastern Carolina Presbytery

the following commissioner requested his name to be recorded in support of this
Objection: TE Frank J. Smith, Northeast.
