UI. RECOMMENDATIONS:
1. That OVERTURE 22 from Louisiana Presbytery be answered by the adoption
of the following Resolution:

<a id="ga28-p276"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=278 printed_page=276 -->

In light of the highly charged, novel and confusing circumstances
attending the matter arising out of tennessee valley presbytery (see SJC Case
99-1, 28-47, p. 238; see also report of CCB, 28-60 and 28-63, p. 258), and as
there is no provision specifying that a request for the Assembly to take original
th
jurisdiction under BCO 34-1 is to be heard and determined by the SJC, the 28
General Assembly declares that the resolutions of western carolina presbytery
(adopted on July 20, 1999), Calvary Presbytery (adopted on July 24, 1999),
Ascension Presbytery (adopted on July 31, 1999), and James River Presbytery
(adopted on April 11, 2000), be treated as overtures timely filed to this General Assembly, and be referred to the committee of commissioners on bills and
Overtures, for recommendation to this Assembly as to whether the Assembly
should assume original jurisdiction as so requested, and refer the case for
hearing before the SJC. Adopted

GROUNDS
the plain language of BCO 34-1, absent any other provision in the
Constitution or Rules, appears to have the Presbyteries in question petition the
assembly itself concerning the question of original jurisdiction. After that
decision is rendered, the Assembly should exercise its jurisdiction in the regular
Constitutional manner, i.e., through the SJC (cf. BCO 15-4)

##### OVERTURE 22 From Louisiana Presbytery

“Condemn the SJC Decision in the John Wood Case”

Whereas, Presbyterianism historically has allowed for one General Assembly to
condemn the opinion of another General Assembly (M 12GA, p. 140: "A
subsequent General Assembly may take a contrary position and condemn
the action of a previous Assembly."); and

Whereas, the decisions of the Standing Judicial Commission (SJC) are those of
the previous General Assembly (BCO 15-4: "and each subsequent
Assembly should declare the Standing Judicial Commission as a whole
to be is commission”), and

Whereas, the General Assembly is bound "to bear testimony against error in
doctrine ., injuriously affecting the Church.” (BCO 14-6.a ); and

Whereas, the decision oft hes jc in RE Presbyterian Church in America vs.
John Wood is evidently flawed, and

Whereas, because of the peculiar nature of current PCA judicial process, the
General Assembly as such never was able directly to review this decision
of the SJC; and

Whereas, the decision is flawed in numerous ways, especially in its
interpretation of the Constitution; and

Whereas, among the errors in Constitutional interpretation are the following:
(1) the SJC assumes that a corporate responsibility by a session means
that individual members cannot also be held responsible for the action of
the session; (2) the SJC assumes that there were no doctrinal issues

<a id="ga28-p277"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=279 printed_page=277 -->

involved, but merely the action of inviting a woman to fill the pulpit,
when there is evidence that Mr. Wood advocates female preaching; (3)
the SJC assumes that action by Tennessee Valley Presbytery to vindicate
the session of cedar springs presbyterian Church, Knoxville,
Tennessee, automatically clears Mr. Wood, (4) the SJC forcibly recused
members oft hes jc from Ascension, Calvary, and Western Carolina
Presbyteries, based on the allegation that these Presbyteries were parties
to the case, whereas in point of fact, ina case of original jurisdiction, the
"original and only parties ina case of process are the accuser and the
accused. The accuser is always the Presbyterian Church in America"
(BCO 31-3), and

Whereas, this situation of having a seriously-flawed decision in this judicial
case is troubling to the church and is potentially divisive, and

Whereas, the god who has called us to peace is not the author of confusion, and

Whereas, the Ninth Commandment requires "from the heart, sincerely, freely,
clearly, and fully, speaking the truth, and only the truth, in matters of
judgment and justice" (WLC, Q/A 144), and

Now Therefore, Be it Resolve, that Louisiana Presbytery hereby overtures the
28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to
condemn and to bear testimony against the judgment of the 27th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America regarding the judicial
case with respect to the Rev. Mr. John Wood
Adopted by Louisiana Presbytery at the 63rd Stated Meeting, Opelousas, Louisiana,
on April 15, 2000. Attested by: /s/ James A Jones, jr , Stated Clerk

MINORITY REPORT ON RECOMMENDATION 1
OF THE
COMMITTEE OF COMMISSIONERS ON BILLS AND OVERTURES

That OVERTURE 22 from Louisiana Presbytery be answered in the negative.
Grounds:
1. we are in agreement with the advice of the CCB in its response to:
Reference 1 from the presbytery of western carolina (pp. 68-70);
Reference 2 from the presbytery of the ascension (p. 70),
Constitutional Inquiry 1 from the presbytery of the ascension (p. 73),

##### Overture 21 from Louisiana Presbytery (pp. 78-79).

2. BCO 34-1 has two prerequisites which must be met before the matter of TE john
Woods/Cedar Springs PCA can properly be before the GA. The SJC has
determined that Tennessee Valley Presbytery has indeed acted. Therefore, since
the first prerequisite has not been met, the GA has no jurisdiction in this matter.
3. The Resolutions from the three Presbyteries were directed by the Presbyteries to
the GA and were considered by the GA via the SJC.
4. The GA has established the SJC as the Assembly’s normal process for handling
cases of this kind (BCO 15-4, “The General Assembly shall erect a Standing

<a id="ga28-p278"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=280 printed_page=278 -->

Judicial Commission to which it shall commit all judicial cases within its jurisdiction").

5. The GA via the SJC found these resolutions to be out of order because the grounds upon which they sought the GA to act via the SJC were wrong, namely the Tennessee Valley Presbytery had acted on the matter of TE John Wood and his Session.

6. The SJC also noted that while some members of the Tennessee Valley Presbytery protested the Presbytery’s investigation, no members of the Presbytery sought to bring that protest to the SJC via a complaint.

7. As the SJC has acted on behalf of the GA it would be quite inappropriate for the GA and the SJC to take this case up a second time. Further, it would be quite inappropriate for the GA, in effect, to investigate a TE a second time when he has been cleared of wrong doing by his Presbytery. Such a second investigation would amount to an act of double jeopardy.

8. In addition, the BCO and the SJC Manual provide for only the most limited circumstances for a case that has been considered by the SJC to then be considered by the GA. That process requires that one-third of the SJC members prepare a minority report (BCO 15-5-c). It is clearly the intention of the BCO that a case once considered by the SJC cannot be again considered by the GA.

9. Further, it would appear that for this overture or for the Bills and Overtures Committee recommendation to be adopted that it would require an amendment to the BCO to set aside BCO 15 and a motion made to suspend the RAO (Article 15 and the SJC Manual).

Committee Members Joining in this Minority Report:

TE Craig Childs, Evangel    TE Fredric Mau, Palmetto
TE Stephen Estock, Southeast Alabama TE Charles McGowan, Nashville
TE Bill Lamkin, North Florida RE Claude Roberts, South Texas

2. That OVERTURE 9 from the Presbytery of the Ascension (“Amend BCO 24-1”) be answered by referring the overture back to the Presbytery. Adopted

Grounds: The pending amendment to BCO 24-1 has now received the approval of a sufficient number of Presbyteries. A change adopted so recently by the Assembly ought not to be reviewed again until the Church has had time to practically assess its effect. There is a presumptive virtue in settled law.

##### OVERTURE 9 From the Presbytery of the Ascension

“Amend BCO 24-1”

Whereas, the 26th GA adopted a proposed amendment to BCO 24-1 (printed below); and

Whereas, the grounds cited by Central Carolina Presbytery (which brought the original overture) and by the Bills &amp; Overtures Committee were that the requirements of BCO 24-1 requiring (1) that there be at least one

<a id="ga28-p279"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=281 printed_page=279 -->

month’s prior notice of a meeting for electing officers and (2) that Session inform the congregation before election day of those eligible for election “can place undue time constraints on the Session.” (M26GA, pp. 208-209); and

Whereas, that amendment is still pending because there were not sufficient presbyteries voting in favor of it (M27GA, pp. 58-60); and

Whereas, at least 13 presbyteries have voted against this proposed amendment (M27GA, pp. 58-60); and

Whereas, the reasons for the negative votes seem to be tied to (1) the absence in the new language of a clear statement that it is the Session which must examine the candidates and (2) confusion regarding the meaning of the second paragraph of the amendment [i.e., does the “30 days” requirement mean that the names of eligible nominees must be reported to the congregation at the time of the call for the meeting (which the order of the events in 24-1 seems to imply) or does it simply speak to the required notice for the meeting while leaving the Session the discretion as to when to report the names of eligible nominees (and thus allowing the Session to not report those names prior to the meeting if it so desires)?]; and

Whereas, it seems to us that it is reasonable to require 30 days notice of a meeting for an election, but not necessarily 30 days notice of who are eligible nominees; and

Whereas, it seems reasonable to us to require that the names of eligible nominees be given to the congregation before the day of election; and

Whereas, it would seem to be good stewardship to seek to address those concerns before the proposed amendment is finally adopted, thus avoiding the need for future amendments;

Therefore Be it Resolved, that the Presbytery of the Ascension overture the 28th General Assembly to adopt the following language in lieu of the pending amendment to BCO 24-1: [Additions to the pending proposal are underlined, while deletions are struck through.]

A. Substitute the following for the first paragraph:

24-1. Every church shall elect persons to the office of ruling elder and deacon in the following manner: At such times as determined by the Session, communicant members of the congregation may submit names to the Session, keeping in mind that each prospective officer shall be an active male member who meets the qualifications set forth in 1 Timothy 3 and Titus 1. After the close of the nomination period nominees for the office of ruling elder and/or deacon shall receive instruction in the qualifications and work of the office. Each nominee shall then be examined by the Session in: [NOTE: continue with the areas of examination.]

B. Substitute for the second paragraph (i.e., “The Session shall examine...”) the following:

<a id="ga28-p280"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=282 printed_page=280 -->

If there are candidates eligible for election, the Session shall report to the congregation those eligible, giving at least 30 days prior notice of the time and place of a congregational meeting for election. The Session shall give at least 30 days prior notice of the time and place of the congregational meeting for elections. It shall also report to the congregation before election day those eligible for election.

And be it Further Resolved that, if the Committee on Constitutional Business and the Bills &amp; Overtures Committee agree that the revised language has merit, that the latter be permitted to offer a partial report at the time of voting on the adoption of the pending amendment so that commissioners may have the opportunity to adopt this revised language as a proposed amendment before voting on whether or not to finally adopt the amendment which is currently before the presbyteries.

Adopted at the January Stated Meeting of the Presbytery of the Ascension on January 29, 2000. Attested by: /s/ Frederick R. Neikirk, Stated Clerk

3. That OVERTURE 2 from Grace Presbytery (“Grant Full Constitutional Status to the Entire Directory of Worship”) be answered in the negative. Adopted

Grounds: The procedure suggested by Overture 2 seems to indicate that the Directory for Worship can be given full constitutional status by a mere vote of the General Assembly. However, establishing “full constitutional status and weight” would have to be accomplished according to the proper precedent-setting procedure employed with respect to Chapters 56-58 (cf. M8GA (1980), p. 118; M9GA (1981), pp. 137-142).

##### OVERTURE 2 From Grace Presbytery

“Grant Full Constitutional Status to the Entire Directory of Worship”

Whereas, it has always been the practice of Presbyterian denominations around the world to include some form of a directory for the public worship of God within their constitutional standards; and

Whereas, the public worship of God is a principal, and the most visible, act performed by the Church of the Lord Jesus Christ; and

Whereas, the Bible enjoins all Christians to worship in a fitting and orderly way (1 Corinthians 14:40); and

Whereas, the matter of public worship is one of considerable debate and increasing tension within the Presbyterian Church in America; and

Whereas, the current Directory for Worship of the Presbyterian Church in America is only “an approved guide” and “does not have the force of law and is not to be considered obligatory” in most of its parts;

Therefore, be it resolved that Grace Presbytery overture the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to rescind the current prefatory

<a id="ga28-p281"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=283 printed_page=281 -->

statement to the Directory For Worship, and in so doing grant the
Directory for Worship full constitutional status and weight.

Adopted this 11th day of May, in the year of our Lord, nineteen hundred, ninety-
nine, by the presbytery of grace in its ninety-fourth stated meeting, at
Hazlehurst, Mississippi. Attested by: /s/ E. C. Case, Stated Clerk

5. That OVERTURE 12 from Potomac Presbytery (“Prohibit Women from
Preaching in Public Worship”) be answered in the affirmative as amended

Add a new clause at BCO 12.5d., after the words “Bible study groups;”,
as follows: to encourage the women in the church to hold meetings and give
instruction appropriate to their calling (e.g. Titus 2:3-5), while prohibiting
women from expounding ©f and preaching the word of god a san ordinance of
public worship (cf. BCO 4-4; 53-2), as this is clearly forbidden in Scripture (1
Tim. 2:11-12). Adopted as amended

Grounds: The overture is very narrowly drafted, applicable by virtue of the
parenthetical only to the expounding and preaching of the word of god a san
ordinance of public worship (i.e., the sermon) in the local church. Making this
restriction explicit will protect and proclaim the well-grounded biblical
consensus that exists in the PCA on this matter.

##### OVERTURE 12 From Potomac Presbytery

“Prohibit Women from Preaching in Public Worship”

Whereas, questions have been raised across the church concerning the
legitimate role of women in the PCA, and whether the constitution of
the PCA is sufficiently explicit to guard against unbiblical practices with
respect to the ministry of women in our churches;

Therefore, Potomac Presbytery respectfully overtures the 28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to proceed to the
amendment of Book of Church Order to the following effect: add a new
clause at BCO 12.5d., after the words “Bible study groups;”, as follows:
to encourage the women in the church to hold meetings and
give instruction appropriate to their calling (e.g. Titus 2:3-5),
while prohibiting women from expounding or preaching the
word of god a san ordinance of public worship (cf. BCO 4-4;
53-2), as this is clearly forbidden in Scripture (1 Tim. 2:11-12);

Adopted by Potomac Presbytery at the 41st Stated Meeting, February 12, 2000.
Attested by: /s/ Richard R. Larson, Stated Clerk

S. That OVERTURE 10 from the presbytery of mississippi valley (“Restrict
Preaching or Exhorting to Qualified Men Only”) be answered in the negative

<a id="ga28-p282"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=284 printed_page=282 -->

(See also Majority Rationale, p. 80 and Minority Reports, p 87, 89) Adopted

Grounds: The overture asks the Assembly to replace the language in question
If the request is taken literally, it can only be acceded to if the views argued in
the minority report of the CCB are correct, and the DFW in this section is not a
part of the Constitution. But in that case, there is little point in the adjustment,
since the affected document is counted as what amounts to an in thesi statement
of a previous Assembly. If the request is taken loosely, as a request for the
Assembly to initiate the Constitutional process for amending the BCO, it can
only be acceded to if the views argued by the majority of the CCB are correct,
and the section is part of the Constitution. But in that case, the matter is better
addressed by Overture 12.

##### OVERTURE 10 from the presbytery of mississippi valley

“Restrict Preaching or Exhorting to Qualified Men Only”

Whereas, the Westminster Confession of Faith, Chapter XXI, paragraph 5
discusses the elements of corporate worship, and “sound preaching. . .” is
an element of corporate worship, and

Whereas, god ordained men to bethe spiritual leaders of israel (Ex. 18: 17-26),
and

Whereas, the priesthood of israel was composed only of the sons of aaron (Ex.
28: 1-4), and

Whereas, the apostles of Jesus, used of god to establish the new covenant
Church for our Lord, were all men (Mt. 10: 1-4), and

Whereas, the Bible forbids women to teach or exercise authority over men (I
Tim. 2: 11-15), and

Whereas, Larger Catechism, question 158 states: “.. .the word of god is to be
preached only by such as are sufficiently gifted and also duly approved
and called to that office,” and

Whereas, God forbids ordained office to women (I Tim. 3: 2,12, Titus 1: 6), and

Whereas, the entire bible teaches the principle of covenant heads hip of men
over women (I Cor. 11: 3), a principle which is usurped when women
exhort or instruct men in the church,
Let it Therefore Be Resolved that Mississippi Valley Presbytery overture the
28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to replace
Book of Church Order paragraph 53-6, which currently reads: “No
persons hall be invited to preach in any of the churches under our care
without the consent of the Session, ” with the following: “Only qualified
men with the consent of the sessions hall be invited to preach/exhort in
any of the churches under our care. ”

adopted at the winter state d meeting of the presbytery of mississippi valley on
February 15, 2000. Attested by: /s/R oger G. Collins, Stated Clerk

<a id="ga28-p283"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=285 printed_page=283 -->

6. That OVERTURE 15 from New Jersey Presbytery (“Revise BCO 56-5 Regarding ‘Our Holy Religion’”) be answered by referring the matter back to New Jersey Presbytery to perfect the form (e.g., RAO 10-11) and resubmit if they so desire.
Adopted

##### OVERTURE 15 From New Jersey Presbytery

“Revise BCO 56-5 Regarding ‘Our Holy Religion.’”

Whereas, the first paragraph of *The Directory for the Worship of God*, states that “BCO 56, 57 and 58 have been given full constitutional authority by the Eleventh General Assembly after being submitted to the Presbyteries and receiving the necessary two-thirds (2/3) approval of the Presbyteries,” and

Whereas, BCO 56-5 states, regarding the questions a minister is to ask when doing an infant baptism, “The minister shall then propose the following questions…”, and

Whereas, the word “shall” requires the minister to use the specific wording of the questions as recorded in BCO 56-5, and

Whereas, in question three the term “our holy religion” is used, and

Whereas, in modern English the term “religion” carries with it the idea of a man-made belief system in that there are numerous world religions, and

Whereas, Colossians 3:23 specifically uses the term “religion” in a negative sense referring to man-made religion, and

Whereas, the BCO 56-5 use of the term “our” also adds to the connection to Colossians 3:23 about religions as being of human origin, and

Whereas, the intent of BCO 56-5 is that of encouraging the teaching of the true doctrines given to the Church by the Lord Jesus as revealed in the Bible,

Therefore, be it so ordered that the BCO be revised by the following action: Delete the phrase “our holy religion” and add the phrase “the holy Bible”, in BCO 56-5, so that question three will read, in part, “…that you will teach (him) the doctrines of the holy Bible, and…”

Not adopted by the Presbytery of New Jersey. [RAO 10-10]
Attested by: /s/ James A. Smith, Stated Clerk

NOTE: Presbytery did, however, vote to suggest to the Assembly regarding OVERTURE 15 and 16 that the Assembly erect a committee to deal with language and style in the BCO.

7. That OVERTURE 16 from New Jersey Presbytery (“Revise BCO 56-5 Regarding ‘Means of God’s Appointment’”) be answered by referring the matter back to New Jersey Presbytery to perfect the form (e.g., RAO 10-11) and resubmit if they so desire.
Adopted

<a id="ga28-p284"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=286 printed_page=284 -->

##### OVERTURE 16 From New Jersey Presbytery

“Revise BCO 56-5 Regarding “Means of God’s Appointment”

Whereas, the first paragraph of the directory for the worship of God, states
that “BCO 56, 57 and 58 have been given full constitutional authority by
the Eleventh General Assembly after being submitted to the Presbyteries
and receiving the necessary two-thirds (2/3) approval of the
Presbyteries,” and

Whereas, BCO 56-5 states, regarding the questions a minister is to ask when
doing an infant baptism, “The minister shall then propose the following
questions. . .”, and

Whereas, the word “shall” requires the minister to use the specific wording of
the questions as recorded in BCO 56-5, and

Whereas, in BCO 56-5, question three contains the archaic phrase “o f God’s
appointment”,

Therefore, be it so ordered that the BCO be revised by the following action:
Delete the phrase “o f God’s appointment” and add the phrase “appointed
by God” in BCO
56-5, so that question three will read, in part, . and that you will strive,
by all the means appointed by God, to bring (him) up in the nurture and
admonition of the Lord?”

not adopted by the presbytery of new Jersey. [RAO 10-10]
Attested by: /s/ James A. Smith, Stated Clerk

8. That OVERTURE 17 from Philadelphia Presbytery (“Add Vow for Elders
when Receiving Members”) be answered in the negative. Adopted

Grounds: such avow is already affirmed by the elders of the church (cf. BCO
21-5.7. -5 .8 ; 24-5.4).

##### overture 17 From Philadelphia Presbytery

“Add Vow for Elders when Receiving Members”

Whereas, elders are called upon to shepherd the flocks that the Chief Shepherd
has placed under their care (1 Peter 5:1-5), and

Whereas, new church members come under the shepherding care and authority
of the elders of the church that they join, and

Whereas, new church members are called upon to assent their submission to
“the government and discipline of the church” represented by the elder-
shepherds of the church (BCO 57-5, Question 5), and

Whereas, church membership represents not merely the commitment of the
member to the authority of the church, but the commitment of the church
to the care of new members;

<a id="ga28-p285"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=287 printed_page=285 -->

Therefore, we propose that the elders of the church express their commitment
to shepherd their new members by adding this Question Six under BCO
57-5:
6. To the elders of the church (standing): Do you, as elders and
shepherds of this congregation, commit yourselves to provide
spiritual care, nurture, and discipline to these who have submitted
themselves to your God-given authority, recognizing that they are
now among the sheep for whom you will give an account to the Chief
Shepherd?

Adopted November 13, 1999, by the presbytery of philadelphia for presentation
to the 28th General Assembly, 2000, in Tampa, Florida
Attested by: /s/ Frank D Moser, Stated Clerk

9. That OVERTURE 18 from Ellisville PCA, Ellisville, MS (“Amend BCO 13-1
to Allow Additional Ruling Elders as Presbytery Commissioners”) be answered
in the negative Adopted

Grounds: the problem of limited ruling elder participation does not appear to be a function of the standards established in the BCO, and thus is not remedied
by the proposed expansion.

##### OVERTURE 18 From Ellisville PCA, Ellisville, MS

“Amend BCO 13-1 to Allow Additional Ruling Elders as Presbytery
Commissioners”

Whereas, the formation of the Presbyterian Church in America was the result of
the Lord, in His Providence, convicting elders of the need to remain true
to the Biblical principles and teachings upon which our faith is based,
and

Whereas, our elder fathers in the faith were the first to point out and take a
stand against errors being made and taught in the old Southern church,
and

Whereas, ruling elders have played an integral part in the organization and
growth of the Presbyterian Church in America, serving diligently in all
courts of the church, and

Whereas, the parity of the elders hi pis compromised when the composition of
church courts contain substantially more teaching elder commissioners
than ruling elder commissioners, and

Whereas, ruling elders serve the church generally, when called thereunto (BCO
8-3; Acts 20:28f), as well as the local congregation, and

Whereas, the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America
recognized the need for more ruling elder involvement and participation
in said court by amending the Book of Church Order to increase the

<a id="ga28-p286"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=288 printed_page=286 -->

number of ruling elder commissioners from each church from one ( 1) to
two (2), with additional commissioners for larger congregations, and

Whereas, there is a similar need for more ruling elder involvement and
participation in the church presbyteries.

Now Therefore, Be it Resolved, that the undersigned Session overtures the
General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to amend the
Book of Church Order, 13-1, to increase the number of ruling elder
commissioners to presbytery meetings from each church from one ( 1) to
two (2), with additional commissioners for larger congregations, and

Be it Further Resolved, that the Book of Church Order 13-1 be amended by
deleting the present paragraphs and substituting the following, to wit:
the presbytery consists of all the teaching elders and churches within its
bounds that have been accepted by the Presbytery. When the Presbytery
meets as a court it shall comprise all teaching elders and ruling elders as
elected by their Session. Each congregation is entitled to two ruling
elder representatives for the first 350 communing members or fraction
thereof, and one additional ruling elder for each additional 500
communing members or fraction thereof.

Adopted by the Session of the undersigned Session at a called meeting on the 5th
day of December, 1999.
Attested by: Is/ Charles Probst, clerk of Session, Ellisville Presbyterian Church

NOTE: This Overture was rejected by Grace Presbytery (RAO 10-10)

10. That OVERTURE 6 from Cherokee Presbyterian Church (“Amend BCO 16-4
to Allow that Exceptions May Not Be Taught”) be answered in the negative.
Adopted

grounds the powers of the presbytery with respect to this issue are well
defined in the Constitution (e.g., BCO 13-9.d, -9,f.) and have been carefully and
consistently construed by the General Assembly in judicial cases (e.g., M14GA,
pp. 125-126, M18GA, p. 205, M20GA, pp. 163-164). Further, the proposed
amendment introduces, without definition, terminology unknown to our
Constitution, and thus liable to misinterpretation.

##### OVERTURE 6 From Cherokee Presbyterian Church

“Amend BCO 16-4 to Allow that Exceptions May Not Be Taught”

the session of the cherokee presbyterian church respectfully requests that the
North Georgia Presbytery nevertheless forward for consideration by the PCA ’s
General Assembly in June, 2000, the following overture (to be accompanied by
notice of the Presbytery’s negative vote on the overture at its fall meeting):
We hereby request the 2000 General Assembly of the PCA
proceed with the process to add to the BCO, Chapter 16 on Church

<a id="ga28-p287"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=289 printed_page=287 -->

Orders the following Paragraph 16-4:

“When the process of examination reveals any exceptions to the
constitutional standards that the relevant court judges, if taught, to be
harmful to the peace and well-being of the church (though not serious
enough to disqualify the candidate for office), it may by a majority
vote direct the candidate for office not to teach the exception.”

Rationale: this clarifies by making explicit to all what is understood by
many to bean implicit power of the courts of the church.
thank you for your consideration of this request.

Letter dated January 7, 2000 and signed by Rev. Ted Lester, Moderator, for the
session of cherokee presbyterian church of Woodstock, Georgia. NOTE:
North Georgia Presbytery had previously rendered a negative vote upon its
earlier consideration. at the last meeting of the presbytery of north Georgia, on
January 15, 2000, the Committee on Bills and Overtures in their report,
instructed the clerk to forward this overture to the office of the stated Clerk,
Presbyterian Church in America, for handling.

11. That OVERTURE 13 from Susquehanna Valley Presbytery (“Re-Ordination of
Former PCA Ministers”) be answered in the negative Adopted

Grounds: The procedure, if desired, is already available to the Presbytery via
the “extraordinary” clause of BCO 21-4. NOTE: the premise of the first
“Whereas” is not well founded. A presbytery may do as Potomac Presbytery in
such cases, e.g., adopt a resolution as follows: “That, upon review as required by
BCO 13-2 and 34-10, and finding no culpable dereliction, nor evident lack of
acceptance by the church, the following Teaching Elders be continued on the
roll w/o call: — — ----------. This continuance is approved with the
understanding that it shall be reviewed annually, and with the understanding that
each candidate shall have the responsibility to demonstrate that a continued
absence of call is not the result of “breach of his covenant engagement”, or “lack
of acceptance to the church” (cf. b pp ,.A.2.i.).”

##### OVERTURE 13 From Susquehanna Valley Presbytery

“Re-ordination of former PCA Ministers”

whereas the Book of Church Order rightly calls fora presbytery to divest a
minister if he continues on the rolls without call for over three years
(BCO 34-10, 13-2), and

Whereas, this procedure is rarely followed, and

Whereas, this leads to men not being as closely connected to sessions as is good
for them or as is good for the work of the church, and

<a id="ga28-p288"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=290 printed_page=288 -->

Whereas, this often puts pressure on presbyteries to approve “calls” which are questionable, and

Whereas, one reason for this failure of presbyteries to act is that both the man and his presbytery are hoping a call will come and if it does after he is divested he would have to go through the entire ordination process again,

Therefore, the Presbytery of Susquehanna Valley hereby overtures the General Assembly to add a new section 5 as follows to chapter 21 of the BCO and renumber the following sections:

NEW 21-5: Modified procedures may be used for the re-ordination of a former PCA minister who receives a call to a particular church after he was divested without censure at his request (38-2) or was divested of his office without censure against his will (34-10). The presbytery in which he is being called shall carefully investigate the circumstances under which he was divested. If the presbytery decides that the reason he was divested was simply a lack of call, it may proceed to give him the trials for ordination outlined in 21-4 without going through the normal candidate, licensure, and internship procedures. At its discretion, the presbytery may accept his previous ordination in lieu of examining him in the Greek, and Hebrew languages, church history, and the history of the PCA.

Adopted by the Presbytery of Susquehanna Valley on February 18, 2000.

Attested by: /s/ David F. Rundle, Stated Clerk

12. That OVERTURE 4 from James River Presbytery (“Clarify Use of Professional in Cases of Process”) be answered in the affirmative with the following substitute.

That BCO 32-19 be amended by deleting the current language (indicated by strikeouts) and replacing it with the following new language.

32-19. No professional counsel shall be permitted as such to appear and plead in cases of process in any court; but an accused person may, if he desires it, be represented before the Session by any communing member of the same particular church, or before any other court, by any member of that court. A member of the court so employed shall not be allowed to sit in judgment in the case.

32-19. The accused shall be entitled to be represented before the courts of this church by counsel, who shall have the right to be heard by oral and/or written argument. Any such counsel shall be a communing member of the Presbyterian Church in America. Any member of a court so engaged shall not be allowed to sit in judgment of the case. No person shall accept any fee or other remuneration for any service rendered as counsel, but reimbursement may be made to cover any personal expenses incurred or to be incurred by such counsel in the course of representation.

Adopted

<a id="ga28-p289"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=291 printed_page=289 -->

Grounds: The overture identifies important defects in the provision as it stands,
i.e., the possibility of discontinuity of counsel in the appeals process, and the
uncertainty of the church with respect to the meaning of “professional counsel”.
The substitute language provides improved economy, consistency, and logical
progression in the proposed amendment, while making plain the otherwise
implied rights of counsel, and broadening the membership criteria for counsel.

##### OVERTURE 4 From James River Presbytery

“Clarify use of professional counsel in cases of Process”

Whereas, BCO 32-19 forbids the use of professional counsel “as such” in cases
of process but permits an accused to be represented before the session of
the church of the accused by a member of that church, and before any
other court by a member of that court; and

Whereas, the phrase “as such” is subject to different interpretations; and

Whereas, the limitation on representation before a court higher than the Session
may destroy the continuity of representation of the accused and
otherwise prejudice his defense,

Therefore, Be it Resolved that the James River Presbytery respectfully
overtures the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America
to clarify, amend, and restate BCO 32-19 so that it will read in its
entirety as follows:
32-19 An accused person may be counseled and
represented before the Session or any other court by any
communing member of the same particular church of which
the accused is a member, or by any member o for
commissioner to a court before which the accused appears,
provided, however, that the individual serving as counsel
may not serve for a fee or other remuneration
Notwithstanding the foregoing, payments may be made to
reimburse or cover any direct expenses incurred or to be
incurred by such counsel in the course of representation. If
such counsel is a member o for commissioner to the court
before which the accused appears, he may not sit in judgment
in the case.
adopted at the spring stated meeting of james river presbytery on april 8,
2000. Attested by: /s/ Eugene H. Friedline, Stated Clerk

13. That OVERTURE 7 and COMMUNICATION 1 from North Florida
Presbytery (“Decry contentious practices of the “Presbyterian and Reformed
News”) be answered in the negative. Adopted

<a id="ga28-p290"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=292 printed_page=290 -->

Grounds: The Overture would improperly have the Assembly conclude as
grounds for the requested action censurable offenses without due process, or
would restrain improperly and unwisely dissemination of information in the
Church. Further the Overture appears to unwisely call into question the supreme
value of an independent press to a church whose authority is grounded in the
willing compliance of an informed conscience and whose testimony to the truth
is, humanly speaking, enabled by full and free debate. The overture appears to
neglect entirely the essential place such an independent press has had in the
history of the conservative movements in both northern and southern
Presbyterian Churches, and the similar language of condemnation employed by
those who found the views then expressed disagreeable. The wisest and most
profitable response of pres byte rs to publications with which they disagree is to
publish their own views, setting forth the truth, correcting errors, and evidencing
both formally and materially the virtues that are rightly lauded in the Overture.

Minority Report on OVERTURE 7 and COMMUNICATION 1

That Overture 7 and Communication 1 from the presbytery of north florida be
answered as follows:

The Presbyterian Church in America affirms that the only official publications
authorized to record the story of the PCA are those sanctioned by the General Assembly. All other publications represent only the views and opinions of
individuals within the denomination. Christian forbearance requires that these
publications and the people responsible for them should be treated with the
respect due to any minority view within the church. Moreover, we affirm the
great value of an independent press in assuring full and free debate within the
church and the role such publications have played in the history of the
conservative movements in both the Northern and Southern Presbyterian
Churches.
The issues raised by the Overture from North Florida are serious,
especially the allegations of possible violations of scripture and the subordinate
standards However, the General Assembly is not the proper forum in which to
investigate these reports. Consequently, the Assembly encourages the Presbytery
of north florida to bring the charges to the courts of original jurisdiction to
investigate and take whatever action is deemed appropriate.

TE Craig Childs TE Stephen Estock TE Paul Frick
TE Bill Lamkin RE Bill McClay TE Charles McGowan

##### OVERTURE 7 from presbytery of north florida

“Decry contentious practices of the ‘Presbyterian and Reformed News’”

<a id="ga28-p291"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=293 printed_page=291 -->

Whereas, the Presbyterian and Reformed News, in an apparent disregard for the wisdom of Proverbs 6:16-19, [There are six things which the Lord hates, Yes, seven which are an abomination to Him: haughty eyes, a lying tongue, and hands that shed innocent blood, a heart that devises wicked plans, feet that run rapidly to evil, a false witness who utters lies, and one who spreads strife among brothers, (NASB)] has printed stories and information that has created much confusion and fomented division within the PCA, and

Whereas, the Presbyterian and Reformed News has printed statements even though the person or persons have asked not to be quoted (Spring 1998 issue, p. 5), and

Whereas, the Presbyterian and Reformed News has printed statements about a member of North Florida Presbytery that called into question his reputation, integrity and Christian character (New Bulletin #27-1, June 14-15, 1999), and

Whereas, the Presbyterian and Reformed News has printed summaries of the minutes of North Florida Presbytery,

Therefore Be it Resolved, that the Presbytery of North Florida overtures the Presbytery of the Northeast to instruct the Editor(s) of the Presbyterian and Reformed News that Christian charity demands that they more seriously consider and humbly respect the requests of Christian brothers and Presbyteries. Ephesians 4:3 [I, therefore, the prisoner of the Lord, entreat you to walk in a manner worthy of the calling with which you have been called, with all humility and gentleness, with patience, showing forbearance to one another in love, being diligent to preserve the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace. (NASB)]. Moreover, in the light of wisdom of the Westminster Larger Catechism 144-145:

Q. 144. What are the duties required in the ninth commandment?

A. The duties required in the ninth commandment are, the preserving and promoting of truth between man and man, and the good name of our neighbor, as well as our own; appearing and standing for the truth; and from the heart, sincerely, freely, clearly, and fully, speaking the truth, and only the truth, in matters of judgment and justice, and in all other things whatsoever; a charitable esteem of our neighbors; loving desiring, and rejoicing in their good name; sorrowing for, and covering of their infirmities; freely acknowledging of their gifts and graces, defending their innocence; a ready receiving of a good report, and unwillingness to admit of an evil report, concerning them; discouraging tale-bearers, flatterers, and slanderers; love and care of our own good name, defending it when need requireth; keeping of lawful promises; studying and practicing of whatsoever things are true, honest, lovely, and of good report.

<a id="ga28-p292"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=294 printed_page=292 -->

Q. 145. What are the sins forbidden in the ninth commandment?

A. The sins forbidden in the ninth commandment are, all prejudicing the truth, and the good name of our neighbors, as well as our own, especially in public judicature; giving false evidence, suborning false witnesses, wittingly appearing and pleading for an evil cause, out-facing and overbearing the truth; passing unjust sentence, calling evil good, and good evil; rewarding the wicked according to the work of the righteous, and the righteous according to the work of the wicked; forgery, concealing the truth, undue silence in a just cause, and holding our peace when iniquity calleth for either a reproof from ourselves, or complaint to others; speaking the truth unseasonably, or maliciously to a wrong end, or perverting it to a wrong meaning, or in doubtful or equivocal expressions; to the prejudice of truth or justice; speaking untruth, lying, slandering, backbiting, talebearing, whispering, scoffing, reviling, rash, harsh, and partial censuring; misconstructing intentions, words, and actions; flattering, vain-glorious boasting; thinking or speaking too highly or too meanly of ourselves or others; denying the gifts and graces of God; aggravating smaller faults; hiding, excusing, or extenuating of sins, when called to a free confession; unnecessary discovering of infirmities; raising false rumors, receiving and countenancing evil reports, and stopping our ears against just defense; evil suspicion; envying or grieving at the deserved credit of any, endeavoring or desiring to impair it, rejoicing in their disgrace and infamy; scornful contempt, fond admiration; breach of lawful promises; neglecting such things as are of good report, and practicing, or not avoiding ourselves, or not hindering what we can in others, such things as procure an ill name.

The Presbyterian and Reformed News should more diligently strive to protect the good name and reputations of all people, even those who do not share the views of the Editor(s) and should promote the Biblical principles of mutual accountability, truthfulness and unity. Such a humble and gracious attitude is in accordance with, and even required by, the fifth membership vow: (BCO 57-5 – Do you submit yourselves to the government and discipline of the Church, and promise to study its purity and peace?) as well as the sixth ordination vow (BCO 21-5 and 24-5 – Do you promise to be zealous and faithful in maintaining the truth of the Gospel and the purity and peace of the Church, whatever persecution or opposition may arise unto you on that account? – Do you promise to strive for the purity, peace, unity and edification of the Church?), and

Be it Further Resolved, that North Florida Presbytery decries the Presbyterian and Reformed News and states to all churches that we stand with and for

<a id="ga28-p293"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=295 printed_page=293 -->

our brother and fellow Teaching Elder and take exception to statements
questioning a brother’s character made about our brothers in this
Presbytery, and

Be it Further Resolved, that the North Florida Presbytery instruct the Stated
clerk of our presbytery to communicate with the Editor(s) of the
Presbyterian and Reformed News that we do not want our minutes or
summaries of the minutes of north florida presbytery printed in the
Presbyterian and Reformed News, and

Be it Further Resolved, that the presbytery of north florida forwards this
overture to the 28th General Assembly as a communication to show our
disfavor, dissatisfaction, and disapproval of much of the current
contentious and divisive practices and faultfinding spirit of the
Presbyterian and Reformed News, and

Be it Further Resolved, that North Florida Presbytery overtures the General Assembly to decry much of the current contentious and divisive practices
and faultfinding spirit of the presbyterian and reformed News, and
Be it Finally Resolved, that the General Assembly instructs its Stated Clerk to
include in his next letter to all teaching elders and clerks of Sessions, a
statement about the view of the General Assembly regarding the
Presbyterian and Reformed News.

approved by the presbytery of north florida this day: January 14, 2000.
Attested by: /s / Randy L. Wilding, Stated Clerk

COMMUNICATION 1 from presbytery of north florida
“Decry contentious practices of the ‘Presbyterian and Reformed News’”
[See text at OVERTURE 7]

14. That OVERTURE 24 from Southeast Alabama Presbytery (“Improper
dissemination of information regarding judicial cases and information
Detrimental to Reputations”) be answered in the negative. Adopted

Grounds: The Assembly has no power to do as requested in the first “resolve,”
and it would be unwise to do so if it did, since ecclesiastical discipline is purely
moral or spiritual in its object, and “can derive no force whatever, but from its
own justice, the approbation of an impartial public, and the countenance and
blessing of the great head of the Church” (BCO Preface, II. Preliminary
Principles #8, emphasis added). Even with divinely appointed institutions, when
those institutions are directed by redeemed sinners, experience teaches that
justice is more likely to be achieved in public than in private. So too a little
reflection reveals that the impartial public must be an informed public if its
approbation is to be of any moral value. Among Presbyterians, at least, an
independent press has been one of the essential elements in procuring and
preserving these first two great means of effective, Christ-honoring discipline,

<a id="ga28-p294"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=296 printed_page=294 -->

and the Assembly should do nothing that would have a chilling effect on the vitality of such an essential agency. There are more appropriate means available to those who would seek to bring balance to the editorial advocacy, or correct abuses by publications which serve the Church in this role.

##### OVERTURE 24 From Southeast Alabama Presbytery

“Improper Dissemination of Information Regarding Judicial Cases and Information Detrimental to Reputations”

Whereas, the Lord, through the Apostle Paul, urges believers to live “with all humility and gentleness, with patience, showing forbearance to one another in love, being diligent to preserve the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace” (Ephesians 4:2-3 – NASB); and

Whereas, the authors of the Westminster Larger Catechism explain that the Ninth Commandment requires of us “the preserving and promoting of truth between man and man, and the good name of our neighbor, as well as our own;…a charitable esteem of our neighbors; loving, desiring, and rejoicing in their good name; sorrowing for, and covering of their infirmities;…a ready receiving of a good report, and unwillingness to admit of an evil report, concerning them;” (WLC 144); and

Whereas, the Book of Church Order (PCA) in 27-1 says, “Discipline is the exercise of authority given the Church by the Lord Jesus to instruct and guide its members and to promote it’s purity and welfare”; and

Whereas, the Book of Church Order (PCA) 27-5 says, “Scripture law is the basis of all discipline because it is the revelation of God’s Holy will. Proper disciplinary principles are set forth in the Scriptures and must be followed. They are:

- **a.** Instruction in the Word;
- **b.** Individual’s responsibility to admonish one another;
- **c.** If the admonition is rejected, then the calling of one or more witnesses;
- **d.** If rejection persists, then the Church must act through her court unto admonition, suspension, excommunication and deposition.

Steps (a) through (d) must be followed in proper order for the exercise of discipline”; and

Whereas, the Presbyterian and Reformed News which is published primarily by members of the PCA has printed articles about judicial cases which are in process and therefore it circumvents the constitutionally ordained church courts, and may prejudice the court by the information it disseminates;

Therefore Be it Resolved, that Southeast Alabama Presbytery overture the General Assembly to instruct all members of the Presbyterian Church in America not to publish information about a judicial case which is in process; and

<a id="ga28-p295"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=297 printed_page=295 -->

Be it Further Resolved, that General Assembly instruct all members of the
Presbyterian Church in America to follow the procedures set down in the
Book of Church Order when there is a grievance and to refrain from
improperly disseminating information detrimental to the reputation of
others, and
Be it Finally Resolved, that the General Assembly instruct its Stated Clerk to
include in his next pastoral letter to all churches in the PCA a copy of
this overture.
adopted unanimously by the presbytery of southeast Alabama, April 28, 2000,
at Millbrook, Alabama. Attested by: Is/ Henry Lewis Smith, Stated Clerk

15. That OVERTURE 5 from Louisiana Presbytery (“Amend BCO 38-4 by
Deletion”) be answered in the negative. Adopted

Grounds: The third and fourth “Whereas” clauses misconstrue the proper
interpretation of the provision in question. Rightly construed, no remedy is
needed. It cannot be “reasonably inferred that administrative removal from the
membership roll may NOT occur under this provision if the member were
regularly worshipping and participating in another church other than the one in
which he took his membership vows” because the sentence is qualified by the
opening sentence: “When a member of a particular church has willfully
neglected the church fora period of one year, or has made it known that he has
no intention of fulfilling the church vows. . . Clearly mere participation at
another church does not exempt one from consideration under this standard. The
provision is further qualified in the second paragraph by the phrase “will not
fulfill his membership obligations” (emphasis added) which obligations are
summarized in the parenthetical in the first paragraph, i.e., “(BCO 57-5, nos. 3-
5)” . Again, clearly mere participation in another church does not satisfy this
standard.
On the other hand, the sentence in question (“in this or any other branch of the
visible Church”) is important to keep in the provision because it makes clear that
if a person is willing to fulfill the vows of membership (not just attend or
participate) in another church, the Session should not remove the person from
the roll in an act of “pastoral discipline”, but dismiss the person to that branch of
the visible Church.

##### OVERTURE 5 From Louisiana Presbytery

“Amend BCO 38-4 by Deletion”

Whereas, BCO 38-4 creates a method for administrative removal of a member
from the church of the lord jesus christ without formal judicial process
under certain specified circumstances.

Whereas, the first sentence of the second paragraph of BCO 38-4 reads thusly:
“If after diligently pursuing such pastoral discipline, and after further
inquiry and due delay, the session is of the judgment that the member

<a id="ga28-p296"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=298 printed_page=296 -->

will not fulfill his membership obligations in this or any other branch
of the visible church (cf. BCO 2-2), then the Session shall erase his
name from the roll.”

Whereas, the highlighted words create serious constitutional problems for a
Session faithfully attempting to exercise its duties under BCO 38-4,
inasmuch as it might be reasonably inferred that administrative removal
from the membership roll may NOT occur under this provision if the
member were regularly worshipping and participating in another church
other than the one in which he took his membership vows.

Whereas, the highlighted words may be reasonably interpreted to require a
Session to exercise formal discipline rather than administrative removal,
if a member ignored his membership vows and began attending another
congregation.

Whereas, it is the intent of scripture and our constitutional standards that
decisions pertaining to the election of officers and well-being of
particular congregations should be decided by those members who are
faithful to their membership vows.

Whereas, the Session has the constitutional authority to receive, remove and
dismiss members. BCO 12-5(a).

Whereas, the Session should reasonably and equitably have the authority under
BCO 38-4 without instituting formal process to administratively remove
a member who has failed to keep his membership vows, regardless of
whether the member is attending another congregation.

Therefore, Be it Resolved that Louisiana Presbytery respectfully moves that
the first sentence of the second paragraph of BCO 38-4 be amended to
delete the highlighted language above so that the sentence will read
thusly: “If after diligently pursuing such pastoral discipline, and after
further inquiry and due delay, the session is of the judgment that the
member will not fulfill his membership obligations, then the Session
shall erase his name from the roll.” Presbytery respectfully requests that
the 28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America concur
in this proposed amendment and that there be the initiation of
constitutional amendment procedures in accordance with our Book of Church Order.

Adopted by Louisiana Presbytery on October 16, 1999.
Attested by: /s/ George Thompson, Stated Clerk

16. That OVERTURE 27 from Gulf Coast Presbytery (“Amend RAO 14-7-3 by
Adding Another Sentence”) be answered in the negative. Adopted

Grounds: A rule change adopted so recently by the Assembly ought not to be
reviewed again until the Church has had time to practically assess its effect.
There is a presumptive virtue in settled law.

<a id="ga28-p297"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=299 printed_page=297 -->

##### OVERTURE 27 from Gulf Coast Presbytery

“Amend RAO 14-7-3 by Adding Another Sentence”

WHEREAS, the purpose of the committee on review of presbytery records of
the General Assembly is stated in RAO 14, and

WHEREAS, the change in RAO 14-7-3 by last year’s General Assembly moves
the committee on review of presbytery records away from its stated
purpose by making it determine the theology and practice of the PCA,

THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED that RAO 14-7-3 be amended to read as
follows:
1. minutes approved with exception of substance which shall be presented
to the General Assembly, which presentation include citation of any
relevant Scriptural, Constitutional and /or RAO references and provide
the Committee’s rationale for finding the exception of substance. The
citation(s) and rationale are only to be seen as advice bv the General Assembly through her committee to help the Stated Clerk and the
Presbytery cited to frame a satisfactory response to the citation.
[Changes to current RAO underlined ]

Reason: the committee on review of the presbytery records should not be
in the business of framing doctrine and practice for the PCA. The
committee’s work is to approve minutes and if exceptions of substance are
found, to bring to General Assembly to vote on them. All references should
be a help to a stated clerk and a presbytery to frame a satisfactory response
to the next General Assembly. It should be up to the Assembly on motion
from the floor to deal with the proper recourse of an egregious exception.
An example would be a man coming for ordination denied the Virgin Birth
and was still approved for ordination by a Presbytery. it should be up to the
assembly to order are examination of them an and if his view was not
changed not to admit him.

Adopted at the 81st stated meeting of gulf coast Presbytery.
Attested by: /s/ Robert S. Homick, Stated Clerk

Note: in light of RAO 10-6, this item was not properly before this (27th)
General Assembly and will be taken up by the 28th General Assembly unless
this Assembly suspends RAO 10-6 [ SeeM 27G A, p. 67 and p. 154 ]

17. That OVERTURE 1 from Heartland Presbytery (“Declare Full Preterism a
Heresy”) be answered by the following:

While grateful for the concern expressed in the Overture, the 28th General Assembly finds our Constitutional Standards sufficient testimony to the PCA’s
understanding of the doctrines of scripture with respect to the matter raised in
the Overture (see WCF 8, 32, and 33). Were this Assembly, in the abstract, to

<a id="ga28-p298"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=300 printed_page=298 -->

declare either more or less than the express statements of our Constitution, it
could not in that declaration either add to, nor take away from, what is
constitutional with respect to these doctrines and duties, nor could such a
declaration infringe upon the right and responsibility of sessions and
Presbyteries to interpret and apply the Constitution as they see best, subject
always to the procedures of review and control, complaint and appeal. (Cf.
M22GA (1994), pp. 233-234.) Adopted

##### OVERTURE 1 From Heartland Presbytery

“Declare Full Preterism a Heresy”

At its 32nd Stated Meeting on August 21, 1999, Heartland Presbytery
took the following action:
99-79. Overture to General Assembly to adopt presbytery’s position
paper on full preterism It was moved and seconded that General Assembly be overtured to adopt Heartland Presbytery’s position paper
on full preterism. Adopted.
I am attaching the resolution and the supporting position paper which
were the basis for the above excerpted action

Heartland Presbytery respectfully overtures the Presbyterian Church in America General Assembly to adopt this position paper as the General Assembly position on full preterism.
Attested by: Is/ Lawrence N. Lunceford, Stated Clerk

whereas full pr et eris mi sade ni aloft he doctrine of the future, visible,
physical, second coming of our lord and savior jesus Christ, and
whereas full pr et eris mi sade ni aloft he biblical doctrine that the just
and the unjust await a resurrection of their present mortal bodies
from the dust, and
whereas full pr et eris mi sade ni aloft he biblical doctrine of aye t future
final judgment, and
whereas these three doctrines are fundamentals of the reformed system
of doctrine contained in the Westminster Confession of Faith and
the Larger and Shorter Catechisms, and

Whereas the Apostles’ Creed, the Nicene Creed, and the Athanasian
creed all declare these doctrines to be fundamentals of the
Christian Faith, and

Whereas many serious doctrinal deviations can and have logically
flowed from these denials, and
whereas the implications of these doctrinal deviations affect the whole
system of doctrine in such away that it is virtually impossible to
teach other portions of the reformed doctrinal system without
also evidencing full preteristic beliefs, and

Whereas the apostle Paul says that this error has the potential to

<a id="ga28-p299"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=301 printed_page=299 -->

“overthrow the faith of some” and “to spread like cancer” (2 Timothy 2:17-18),

Therefore be it resolved that we declare full preterism to be a heresy which is incompatible with being a teaching elder in Heartland Presbytery, and

Furthermore be it resolved that teaching elders in Heartland Presbytery who hold to the teaching of full preterism will be subject to suspension from office, deposition from office or excommunication, and

Furthermore be it resolved that we instruct all the sessions of Presbytery of this action; and that ruling elders who hold to the teachings of full preterism be ineligible as officers of churches in presbytery, and

Furthermore be it resolved that the Candidates and Credentials Committee be instructed to examine candidates for licensure, ordination or transfer on these issues.

Heartland Presbytery Position Paper on Full Preterism
Approved by presbytery action #99-71, 8/20/99

I. Definition of Full Preterism

The terms “full preterism,” “consistent preterism,” “hyper-preterism,” “radical preterism,” and “Hymenaenism” have all been used with varying degrees of positive or negative connotations to define the same system of thought. A simple definition of a full preterist is a person who believes that all the prophecies of the Scripture have been fulfilled. Full preterism has at least the following characteristics:

1. A belief that the Second Coming of Christ has already occurred and no further bodily appearing is expected,
2. A belief that the resurrection has already happened and that therefore there will be no future physical resurrection of our present mortal bodies;
3. A belief that the final judgment of all men has already taken place (in 70 AD), the time when the separation of the goats and sheep (Matthew 25) took place, and that there is to be no future day of judgment for all mankind.

Full preterism is truly a unique system of eschatology with a unique hermeneutical approach to many passages. Full preterists are not to be confused with those who teach a preteristic (fulfilled in the past) interpretation of some passages. All orthodox theologians have taken a preteristic interpretation of many prophetic passages (primarily those related to Christ’s first coming, but also to passages prophesying Christ’s judgment in 70 AD). In contrast to orthodoxy, full preterism teaches that all prophecy has been fulfilled. This paper is not an exposition of the system, but an elucidation of some of the key heretical teachings.

II. Brief demonstration of the heretical and far reaching implications of full preterism, contrasting Biblical statements with full preterist statements

Full Preterists agree that the impact of this doctrine touches far more than just eschatology. Full Preterist John Noe says, “Indeed, a correct view of this consummated reality truly does matter. The consequences of misunderstanding or saying that this resurrection hasn’t happened yet are devastating.”1 Conversely the apostle Paul assures us that to believe that the resurrection is past is devastating since it has the potential to “overthrow the faith” and “spread

1 Noe, Your Resurrection Body and Life , p. 43

<a id="ga28-p300"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=302 printed_page=300 -->

like cancer” (2 Tim. 2:17-18). The following quotes demonstrate the reality of this in several
areas of doctrine. 2

A. Christology
1. Nature of Christ’s resurrection body
a) Scripture which is contradicted
‘“Behold My hands and My feet, that i ti si Myself. Handle Me and see, for a spirit
does not have flesh and bones as you see 1 have." When He had said this, He showed them His
hands and His feet. But while they still did not believe for joy, and marveled, He said to them,
“Have you any food here?” so they gave him apiece of a broiled f is handsome honeycomb
And He took it and ate in their presence.” (Luke 24:39-43)
“Therefore, being a prophet, and knowing that God had sworn with an oath to him that
of the fruit of his body, according to the flesh, He would raise up the Christ to sit on his throne.”
(Acts 2:30)
Jesus answered and said to them, “Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it
up.” Then the Jews said, “It has taken forty-six years to build this temple, and will You raise it
up in three days?” but he was speaking of the temple of his body. ” (John 2:19-21)
“But if the Spirit of Him who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you, He who raised
Christ from the dead will also give life to your mortal bodies through His Spirit who dwells in
you.” (Rom. 8:11)
“For we are members ofHis body. ofHis flesh and ofHis bones.” (Eph. 5:30)

b) Full Preterist quotes
“Christ was resurrected not so much by the act of reanimating His earthly body, but by
escaping Death and Hades .. Christ’s reanimation ofH is earthly form wasn’t the resurrection
itself, it was the byproduct - and proof - ofHis resurrection. And even Christ was not raised to
be ‘in the flesh’.”3
“He was raised into the same body He used before He died. This was done to prove to
the disciples that He had kept His word... He now appeared to His followers at need, having the
same properties as the angels.”4

2. Nature of Christ’s mediatorial reign

- **a)** Scripture which is contradicted 1 Corinthians 15:20-28, 50-58 with Rev. 20:7-15
- **b)** Full Preterist quotes

“Almost all full preterists believe the millennium occurred during the period from AD
30-70, and that Christ returned in AD 70 at the end of that millennial period ”5
“The 40-year transition period was a period of ‘already, but not yet.’ When Christ
returned at AD 70 He finished crushing the enemies of His Father’s reign, took the kingdom
away from them, gave it back to the Father to whom it belonged, then sat down at His right side
to rule eternally with Him. ”6

B.< Anthropology
1. A low view of our present bodies

2 This paper limits discussion to the principle areas of doctrinal disagreement, though many other areas
are affected as well.
3 Daniel E. Harden, Overcoming Sproul’s Resurrection Objections: The First-Century fulfillment of
the par o us ia of christ and the resurrection of the dead (Kingdom Publications: Bradford, pa 1999),
p. 32
4 Daniel E. Harden, Overcoming Sproul's Resurrection Objections, p. 36
5 Ed Stevens. Stevens Response to Gentry, (Kingdom Publications: Bradford, PA, 1997), p. 3.
6 Ed Stevens. Stevens Response to Gentry, (Kingdom Publications: Bradford, pa 1997), p. 46.

<a id="ga28-p301"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=303 printed_page=301 -->

a) Scripture which is contradicted
“Do you not know that your bodies are members of Christ? Shall I then take the
members of Christ and make them members of a harlot? Certainly not!” (1 Cor. 6:15)
“Or do you not know that your body is the temple of the Holy Spirit who is in you,
whom you have from God, and you are not your own? For you were bought at a price;
therefore glorify God in your body and in your spirit, which are God’s." (1 Cor. 6:19-20)
“Not only that, but we also who have the first fruits of the Spirit, even we ourselves
groan within ourselves, eagerly waiting for the adoption, the redemption of our body ” (Rom.
8:23)
“I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God, that you present your bodies
a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable to God, which is your reasonable service.” (Rom. 12:1)
“For no one ever hated his own flesh, but nourishes and cherishes it, just as the Lord
does the church.” (Eph. 5:29)

b) Full Preterist quotes
“We are not interested in this old body surviving ”?
“Our emotional attachment to them [our bodies] will be no different than our
attachment to those body parts we cut off and discarded last week - hair, fingernails, etc. ”8
“ . when the shell is discarded, a new and better form can be inhabited.”9
“Look carefully at what the resurrection of the body is called in Rom. 8:23b. ‘...also
we ourselves groan within ourselves, eagerly expecting adoption, the redemption of our body.’
The Greek word for ‘redemption’ is APOLUTRO. It means ‘to be set free from.’ Here, Paul is
referring to eagerly awaiting to be set free from our bodies! Sounds like a new creation to
me ” 10

2. A defective view of Adam and Eve’s pre-fall bodies
a) Scripture which is contradicted
“Because you have heeded the voice of your wife, and have eaten from the tree of
which I commanded you, saying, ‘You shall not eat of it’: ... to dust you shall return” (Gen.
3:17-19)
“Therefore, just as through one man sin entered the world, and death through sin, and
thus death spread to all men, because all sinned” (Rom. 5:12)
“Nevertheless death reigned from Adam to Moses...” (Rom. 5:14)
“For if by the one man's offense death reigned through the one... ” (Rom. 5:17)

b) Full Preterist quotes
“This infers that Adam and Eve would have physically died even if they hadn’t sinned
and been cast out of the garden.” 11
‘“ For it is appointed unto men once to die, but after this to face the judgment’ (Heb
9:27). That [physical life] wasn’t lost in Adam, therefore, it’s not restored in Christ, nor will it
be.” 12
“Physical death of the old physical body remains the natural consequence of being
created human.”13

7 John L. Bray, the rapture of christians (John L. Bray Ministry, Inc.: Lakeland, FL, 1998), p. 24.

Feb. 12, 1999.
11 John Noe, Your Resurrection Body and Life , p. 23

<a id="ga28-p302"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=304 printed_page=302 -->

3. A defective view of our resurrection bodies
a) Scripture which is contradicted
“And after my skin is destroyed, this I know, that in mv flesh I shall see God...” (Job
19:26)
“And many of those who sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake, some to everlasting
life, some to shame and everlasting contempt .” (Dan. 12:2)
“Your dead shall live; together with my dead body they shall arise. Awake and sing,

dead " (Isaiah 26:19)
“ . . the graves were opened; and many bodies of the saints who had fallen asleep were
raised; and coming out of the graves after his resurrection, they went into the holy city and
appeared to many.” (Matthew 27:52)
“Do not marvel at this; for the hour is coming in which all who are in the graves will
hear His voice and come forth...” (John 5:28)
“But if the Spirit of Him who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you, He who raised
Christ from the dead will also give life to your mortal bodies through His Spirit who dwells in
you.” (Rom. 8:11)
“The body is sown in corruption, it is raise in incorruption. it is sown in dishonor, it is
raised in glory. it is sown in weakness, it is raised in power. it is sown a natural body, it is
raised a spiritual body.” (1 Cor. 15:42-44)
“Now may the God of peace Himself sanctify you completely; and may your whole
spirit, soul, and body be preserved blameless at the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ.” (1 Thess
5:23)
“For if we have been united together in the likeness of His death, certainly we also shall
be in the likeness of his resurrection " (Rom. 6:5; compare with Luke 24:39-43 and 1
Corinthians 15:12-23)

b) Full Preterist quotes1
“It is not the physical body which is raptured. It is the Christian himselfwho is
raptured as he leaves his body behind and takes on a new body forever.” 14
“Paul calls the resurrection body, which God ‘gives’ a believer in Heaven, a ‘spiritual
body’ (1 Cor. 15:38,44), not a ‘resurrected (physical) body.’”
“That’s the discontinuity between Jesus’ resurrection and ours He was the only one
promised His same earthly body back, the one that was nailed to the cross. It was changed into
a resurrected body that was similar yet quite different from His natural body All Jesus’
followers, however, receive a new spiritual body that is like Jesus’ resurrected body That’s a
continuity. But it is not an old resurrected physical body.” 15
Commenting on 1 Corinthians 15, Harden says, “It does not say that we shall be raised
in exactly the same manner as Christ In fact, what is implicit here is that we shall not be raised
in the same way.”16 (Emphasis his.)
“This [Luke 20:34-36] shows that after the resurrection at A D. 70 in the afterlife, the
state of man is like that of the angels. 17 Angels are spiritual creatures, membersof the
heavenly realm. Can they be seen, touched, handled? Not under normal conditions.” 18
“Sproul’s fallacy here is in thinking that the term body must of necessity be
physical." 19
“His work of resurrection has nothing to do with reconstituting decomposed or alive
human bodies at some unscnptural ‘end of time.’ This tradition of men, like others before, has
made the word of God of little or no effect . ”20

14 John L. Bray, the rapture of Christians, p. 32
15 Noe, Your Resurrection Body and Life , p. 71
16 Daniel E. Harden, Overcoming Sprout s Resurrection Objections, p. 30.
17 It is important to remember that Origen was condemned by the ancient church for using this
language
18 Daniel E. Harden, Overcoming Sprout 's Resurrection Objections, p. 42.
19 Daniel E. Harden, Overcoming Sproul 's Resurrection Objections, p 49
