C. Cosmology
1. Death and decay not result of the fall but result of Creation
a) Scripture which is contradicted
See Genesis 3:14-19 for curse on cattle, every beast of the field, etc. But especially
note the following: “Then to Adam He said, “Because you have heeded the voice of your wife,
and have eaten from the tree of which I commanded you, saying, ‘You shall not eat of it’:
Cursed is the ground for your sake, In toil you shall eat of it All the days of your life. Both
thorns and thistles it shall bring forth for you, And you shall eat the herb of the field. In the
sweat of your face you shall eat bread Till you return to the ground, For out of it you were
taken; For dust you are, And to dust you shall return"’ (Gen. 3:17-19)
“Therefore, just as through one man sin entered the world, and death through sin, and
thus death spread to all men, because all sinned” (Rom. 5:12)
“For if by the one man's offense death reigned through the one....” (Rom. 5:17)
“For the earnest expectation of the creation eagerly waits for the revealing of the sons
of God. For the creation was subjected to futility, not willingly, but because of Him who
subjected it in hope; because the creation itself also will be delivered from the bondage of
corruption into the glorious liberty of the children of God For we know that the whole creation
groans and labors with birth pangs together until now. Not only that, but we also who have the
first fruits of the Spirit, even we ourselves groan within ourselves, eagerly waiting for the
adoption, the redemption of our body” (Rom. 8:19-23)

b) Full Preterist quotes
.. physical death was part of creation from the beginning .”21
“That [physical life] wasn’t lost in Adam, therefore it’s not restored in Christ, nor will it
be.”22
“Physical death of the old physical body remains the natural consequence of being
created human ”23
“The question is whether physical death was the ‘Death’ God judicially threatened on
the day of Adam’s disobedience, or whether physical death was the natural consequence of
being human and made from dust.”24

2. The eternality of this present world (including death and suffering)
a) Scripture which is contradicted
“For He must reign till He has put all enemies under His feet. The last enemy that will
be destroyed is death.” (1 Cor. 15:25-26)
“And God will wipe away every tear from their eyes, there shall be no more death, nor
sorrow, nor crying. There shall be no more pain, for the former things have passed away.” (Rev.
21:4)
“But the day of the Lord will come as a thief in the night, in which the heavens will
pass away with a great noise, and the elements will melt with fervent heat; both the earth and
the works that are in it will be burned up Therefore, since all these things will be dissolved,
what manner of persons ought you to be in holy conduct and godliness, looking for and
hastening the coming of the day of God, because of which the heavens will be dissolved, being
on fire, and the elements will melt with fervent heat? Nevertheless we, according to His
promise, look for new heavens and a new earth in which righteousness dwells Therefore,
beloved, looking forward to these things, be diligent to be found by him in peace, without spot
and blameless.” (2 Pet. 3:10-14)

20. Noe, Your Resurrection Body and Life , p. 74.
21 Noe, Your Resurrection Body and Life , p. 23
22 Noe, Your Resurrection Body and Life , p. 43
23 Noe, Your Resurrection Body and Life , p. 71
24 Ed Stevens. Stevens Response to Gentry, (Kingdom Publications: Bradford, PA 1997), p. 25

<a id="ga28-p304"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=306 printed_page=304 -->

b) Full Preterist quotes
the world, the universe, the kingdom, the Church age, biblically have no end....
Therefore, they have no last day, last hour, last minute, last second, or last anything in which to
place a resurrection. ”25
“Comparatively, just as sin didn’t cease to exist after Jesus put it away, physical death
was not eliminated by its final defeat. Jesus never meant that believers would not continue to
die physically. ‘For it is appointed unto men once to die, but after this to face the judgment’
(Heb 9:27). That [physical life] wasn’t lost in Adam, therefore, it’s not restored in Christ, nor
will it be. ”26
“I believe there is a long future ahead of us on this planet. . . Millions or billions of
generations (or even eternity) may be more like it. ”27

3. No renovated new earth to come back to. Those who leave earth
will only be in heaven
a) Scripture which is contradicted
“For the promise that he would bethe heir of the world was not to abraham or to his
seed through the law, but through the righteousness of faith” (Rom. 4:13)
“By faith Abraham... sojourned in the land of promise as ina foreign country... not
having received the promises, but having seen them afar off we reassured of them, embraced
them, and confessed that they were strangers and pilgrims on the earth... And all these, having
obtained a good testimony through faith, did not receive the promise, God having provided
something better for us, that they should not be made perfect apart from us.” (Heb.
11:8,9,13,39,40)
“Blessed are the meek, For they shall inherit the earth.” (Matt. 5:5)
“For the earnest expectation of the creation eagerly waits for the revealing of the sons
of God. For the creation was subjected to futility, not willingly, but because of Him who
subjected it in hope; because the creation itself also will be delivered from the bondage of
corruption into the glorious liberty of the children of God. For we know that the whole creation
groans and labors with birth pangs together until now. Not only that, but we also who have the
first fruits of the Spirit, even we ourselves groan within ourselves, eagerly waiting for the
adoption, the redemption of our body.” (Rom. 8:19-23)

b) Full Preterist quotes
“The Scriptures state we ‘shall dwell in the house of the Lord forever’ (Ps 23:6b).
That’s in Heaven, net on a renovated New Earth 2.”28

D. Pneumatology
1. Biblical Statements that are contradicted
“In Him you also trusted, after you heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation;
in whom also, having believed, you were sealed with the Holy Spirit of promise, who is the
guarantee of our inheritance until the redemption of the purchased possession, to the praise of
His glory.” (Eph. 1:13-14)
“And do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God, by whom you were sealed for the day of
redemption.” (Eph. 4:30)

25 Noe, Your Resurrection Body and Life , p. 37
26 Noe, Your Resurrection Body and Life , p. 43
27 ed Stevens. Stevens Response to Gentry, (Kingdom Publications: Bradford, PA 1997), p. 47
28 Noe, Your Resurrection Body and Life , p. 63

<a id="ga28-p305"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=307 printed_page=305 -->

2. Full Preterist Quotes
“If the transition period for Pneumatology is over, the transition period of eschatology
must also be over.”29
“What I find interesting in Gentry’s30 statements here, is that he seems to leave himself
hopelessly vulnerable to the charismatics’ arguments for a continuation of the miraculous sign
gifts, when he says that we today still ‘have the outpoured Holy Spirit and His gift of teachers.’
Full preterists don’t believe that.”31 [Note: Ed Steven’s is responding to Gentry’s article that
called full preterism heresy and outside the Orthodox faith. Gentry is a partial preterist like
Sproul]
“If Gentry does not take a preterist approach to these passages [Acts 2:17fF; 1 Cor. 1:5-
8; 1 Cor. 13:8ff], he leaves himself defenseless against the charismatic view. But if he does
take a preterist approach here, his statement that we still ‘have the outpoured Holy Spirit and
His gift of teachers’ seems inconsistent and contradictory. ”32
“The ‘earnest of the Spirit,’ therefore, is tied to the outworking of God’s promise to
ISRAEL. This is not an age-long operation, but the focus is on ‘the last days’ of historic
Judaism. The purpose of the Spirit, in its manifold gifts and operations, was to accomplish the
change that was decisively determined in Christ’s death and resurrection. Paul said that God
has wrought us for ‘this very thing.’ The change belonged to Paul's time. The supernatural
gifts of the Spirit belong to his time. That from which the change was being made belonged to
his time. The consummation of the change belonged to his time (1 Cor. 10:11; Rom. 13:11,12;
Heb. 6:1-6) ”33

HI. Creedal Issues
A. Ancient Creeds
1. Apostles’ Creed
“The third day he rose again from the dead. He ascended into heaven, and sitteth on the
right hand of God the Father Almighty; from thence he shall come to judge the quick and the
dead. I believe in the Holy Ghost... the resurrection of the body... ”

2. Nicene Creed
“We believe ... And he shall come again, with glory, to judge both the quick and the
dead... And we look for the resurrection of the dead, and the life of the world to come. Amen.”

3. Athanasian Creed
“Whosoever will be saved: before all things, it is necessary that he hold the Catholic
Faith: Which Faith except every one do keep whole and undefiled: without doubt he shall perish
everlastingly. And the Catholic Faith is this... [body of text] From whence he shall come to
judge the quick and the dead. At whose coming all men shall rise again with their bodies; And
shall give account for their own works. And they that have done good shall go into life
everlasting; and they that have done evil, into everlasting fire. This is the Catholic Faith: which
except a man believe faithfully, he cannot be saved.”

B. The Westminster Confession of Faith
1. Full preterists who deny that Romans 8:23 teaches “the redemption of our body” but
rather insist that it means “escape from our body” would be out of accord at the
following place in the Confession: WCF 32:1.

29 Ed Stevens. Stevens Response to Gentry, (Kingdom Publications: Bradford, PA, 1997), p. 20
30 Dr. Kenneth Gentry (a partial preterist) was one of the first to call Full Preterism a heresy.
31 Ed Stevens. Stevens Response to Gentry. (Kingdom Publications: Bradford, PA 1997), p. 19.
32 Ed Stevens. Stevens Response to Gentry, (Kingdom Publications: Bradford, PA 1997), p. 19.
33 Max R. King, The Cross and the Parousia of Christ, (Parkman Road Church of Christ, 4705
Parkman Road, Warren, Ohio., 1987), p. 596.

<a id="ga28-p306"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=308 printed_page=306 -->

2 Full preterists who deny that our mortal bodies will rise from the grave, and that these
bodies are “the self-same bodies, and none other” than that which we now possess
would be out of accord at the following places in the Confession: WCF 32:2; 32:3.
3 Full preterists who deny that Christ's body that came out of the grave was and continues
to be His resurrection body are out of accord with the following places in the
Confession W CFiA.
4 Full preterists who deny that Christ's body is of the same nature as our resurrection
bodies are out of accord with the following place m the Confession 32:3
5 Full preterists who deny that there is still to come a physical, bodily, Second Coming of
our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ are out of accord with the following places in the
Confession : WCF 33:1,2,3.
6 Full preterists who deny that there is a future final day of judgment of the righteous and
of the unrighteous are out of accord with the following places in the Confession : WCF
33:1,2,3.
7 Full preterists who deny that the Holy Spirit continues to give spiritual gifts, or that the
Holy Spirit continues to be our “earnest,” or who deny that we are “sealed to the day of
redemption” by the Holy Spirit are out of accord with the following places in the
Confession and should be noted as holding either scruples, notations or exceptions:
WCF 12:1; 18:2; 26:1.
8 Full preterists who deny that Satan and his demons continued to be present adversaries
to the souls of the righteous after 70 AD are out of accord with the following places in
the Confession and should be noted as holding either scruples, notations or exceptions:
WCF5:6; 17:3; 21:1.

C. The Westminster Larger Catechism
The Full Preterist position is in contradiction to at least the following sections of the
WLC. 28,52,53,54, 56,85,86,87,121,145,158,167,176,191,195.

D. The Westminster Shorter Catechism
The full Preterist position is in contradiction to at least the following portions of
the WSC: 28,102.
A bibliography is available to those who request it.

Adopted at the 32nd Stated Meting of Hearland Presbytery on August 21,1999.
Attested by: Is/ Lawrence N. Lunceford, Stated Clerk

18. That Overture 25 from Korean Southeastern Presbytery (“Support North
Korean Refugees”) be answered in the affirmative as amended
Adopted as amended

That the 28th General Assembly:
1 set aside a special time of prayer during this General Assembly for
those living in North Korea and for those fleeing that country
asking that the way be made clear for the proclamation of the
Gospel and that they be enabled to “lead a tranquil and quiet life in
all godliness and dignity.” (1 Tim. 2:2b)
2 Give permission to post a sign-up sheet for all commissioners who
would like to continue supporting these people in prayer.
3. Give permission to send a letter to each local church requesting
them to support these people by special prayer

<a id="ga28-p307"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=309 printed_page=307 -->

##### OVERTURE 25 From Korean Southeastern Presbytery

“Support North Korean Refugees”

The Korean Southeastern Presbytery overtures the 28th General Assembly to:

1. Write an official letter to the Chairman of the United Nations requesting that the U.N. provide protection for the refugees escaping from North Korea and that refugee camps be established to provide shelter for them.

2. Write an official letter to the President of The United States requesting that the United States provide protection for the refugees escaping from North Korea and that refugee camps be established to provide shelter for them.

3. Set aside a special time of prayer during this General Assembly for the refugees who are escaping from North Korea.

4. Give permission to post a sign-up sheet for all commissioners who would like to continue supporting these refugees in prayer.

5. Give permission to send a letter to each local church requesting them to support these refugees by special prayer.

Adopted by the Korean Southeastern Presbytery at its 29th Stated Meeting on April 3, 2000. Attested by: /s/ Dan Dongkyo Lee, Stated Clerk

### Names of those that signed up to pray:

Francis Hare, James R. Simoneau, L. Roy Taylor, J. Robert Fiol, David V. Massey, Marshall Joiner, Jim Knight, Philip ‘Blevins, William D. Johnson, David Miner, Bill Cranford, Zack Eswine, James MacGregor, Bill Leuzinger, Dwight Dolby, Mark Thompson, Ken Scheffter, David Kertland, John Hunt, and George F. Ganey III.

### Names of those who signed up to receive ongoing/follow-up information on developments in Korea:

Bill Johnson, Marshall Joiner, Zack Eswine, James MacGregor, Bill Leuzinger, Dwight Dolby, Ty Keys, Tom Schonthaler, Ken Scheffter, and David Kertland.

19. That Communication 7, relayed to us verbally by the Stated Clerk, asking that RE Kim Sang-Chul from Seoul, South Korea, be permitted to address the Assembly for up to 10 minutes on matters relating to Overture 25, be answered in the affirmative.
Adopted

20. That PERSONAL RESOLUTION #1 from RE Robert H. Miller be answered in the negative.
Adopted

**Grounds**: The Assembly’s views are adequately expressed and properly communicated in its communication, and it would be impractical to ask the Stated Clerk to seek to speak further on behalf of the Assembly.

PERSONAL RESOLUTION #1 from RE Robert H Miller
That the Stated Clerk be directed to request an audience with those
government officials who have not responded to the Assembly’s previous
communication on the abortion issue in order to convey personally the
Assembly’s grave concerns.

The following commissioner requested that his negative vote on the
Assembly’s response to this Resolution be recorded: RE Lyle Lagasse, Rocky
Mountain Presbytery.

21. That PERSONAL RESOLUTION #2 from TE Jerry Mead be answered in the
negative Adopted

Grounds: Such treaties and legislation are not properly subjects for the
Assembly’s consideration (W CF 31-5). Further, if it were the Assembly’s
business, the language of the convention is not before the assembly and thus
any statement would be made in ignorance. The Assembly has declared its
understanding, through its Constitution and various declarations, of the Bible’s
teaching on the particular moral issues noted in the personal resolution. The
assembly encourages all members of the PCA to continue in their God-given
responsibilities as active and discerning citizens.

PERSONAL RESOLUTION #2 from TE Jerry C Mead
a convention of the elimination of discrimination against women is
recommended by a committee of the united nations to its members. This
convention proposes the legalization of prostitution, a swell as the recognition of
homosexual practice as normal, marriage of sames ex couples, and other pro
abortion legislation.
If ratified, by the U. S. Senate, this will have the force of international
law and become the law of the land. It will also come under regular U.N.
compliance review to enforce the provisions in the U. S
I move the twenty-eighth General Assembly of the PCA goon record as
opposed to this convention, that the Stated Clerk be instructed to communicate
this opposition to the president of the senate and the senate majority leader
and / or members of the PCA communicate this opposition to their senators.

22. That PERSONAL RESOLUTION #3 from TE Charles Wilson be answered in
the negative. Adopted

Grounds:
1. the scope of the requested issues to be studied is sufficiently vague and
broad as to make the study impractical, unreasonable, and unlikely to be
fruitful.

<a id="ga28-p309"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=311 printed_page=309 -->

2 Systems are in place to allow the Assembly and the Denomination to
deal with the issues noted in the resolution (e.g., the Creation Study
Committee, the Bills and Overtures process, etc ).
3. The peace and purity of the PCA will be maintained as the Holy Spirit
continues to apply the truth of ephesians 4:2-6 to our hearts

PERSONAL RESOLUTION #3 from TE Charles Wilson.

Whereas, there are many unsettling issues in the PCA such as the Creation
issue, the role of women in the Church, and the requested division of
Westminster Presbytery on theological grounds, and

Whereas, this is obvious unrest across the PCA is regarding the bound of unity
within the Westminster Confession and Catechisms, and

Whereas, this unrest is divisive in character, thus hampering the Church from
effectively carrying out the Great Commission,

Now therefore, be it resolved, that the General Assembly recognize the
seriousness of this situation, and that the Assembly elect a committee, or
authorize the Moderator to appoint a committee, that is broadly
representative of the varied viewpoints. This committee is to
1 make serious study of the diversity of opinion in the PCA in the
light of our Constitution, such as issues regarding Scripture and
Hermeneutics, subscription to our Standards by church officers,
church polity, worship, biblical discipline, and mission
2 Propose a method, satisfactory to all parties, as to how the present
difference may be laid to rest
3. Report to the twenty-ninth General Assembly.
4. Be supported by voluntary contributions.

23. That PERSONAL RESOLUTION #6 from RE Robert H. Miller be answered
in the negative. Adopted

Grounds: It would be premature for the Assembly to take action on this issue
before the Study Committee on Women in the Military has completed its work

PERSONAL RESOLUTION #6 from RE Robert Miller.

Whereas, the Ad-Interim Committee on Women in the Military has asked to
delay its report to the Assembly for yet another year, and

Whereas, the General Assembly, in accepting the Committee’s
recommendation, the PCA has for a fifth consecutive year remained
resolutely silent about the biblical un righteousness o fm an’s enlistment
of women as military warrior, and

Whereas, continued resolute silence conveys implicitly the Assembly’s
positive approval for “women warrior” policies that are continually
being implemented in plain official view and within the pastoral scope of
PCA ministers, chaplains and lay leadership, and

<a id="ga28-p310"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=312 printed_page=310 -->

Whereas, the consciences of men and women in the church, and the nation at large, deserve to be informed positively by divinely revealed Truth about the creatively ordained purposes of God in fashioning manhood and womanhood with respect to the duty of guardian and protector; and

Whereas, the commonly and historically understood broad teaching and specific directions of the entire Word of God affirm without exception, (as has been concluded now by four reliable evangelical denominations), that woman was not made to be the warrior, guardian, protector of man; and

Whereas, several particular churches, under their Presbytery’s warrant, have resolved authoritatively that woman is not to be drafted or to be recruited as a military combatant; and

Whereas, certain PCA Sessions and Presbyteries having thus spoken without contradiction by a higher court, the church has indeed spoken authoritatively against the posting of woman as military warrior; and

Whereas, the PRJC has asked for certain guidance regarding the biblical view of using woman for combat, consistent with the minister’s duty to proclaim the Kingdom of God;

Now therefore, be it resolved, that the twenty-eighth General Assembly hereby advises the PRJC that its chaplains have denominational license to counsel women against enlisting or remaining active in combatant categories of military armed forces, and, pending receipt and acceptance of a report from the Assembly’s Ad Interim Committee on Women in the Military, the General Assembly reserves judgement on the general propriety of women in the military and appropriate actions.

24. That, pursuant to the Assembly’s referral of the Overtures from Western Carolina, Calvary, Ascension and James River Presbyteries, the Committee recommends the following:

A. That the Overtures from Western Carolina, Calvary, and Ascension Presbyteries be answered in the affirmative, the Assembly taking original jurisdiction of Dr. John Wood with respect to the allegations set forth in the Overtures; that the matter be referred to the SJC to consider after the manner of BCO 31-2, and other applicable provisions of the rules of discipline pertaining to the process against a minister, and that representatives of the petitioning presbyteries be permitted to represent their allegations before the SJC.

B. That the Overture #23 from James River Presbytery be answered in reference to item A above.

C. That the SJC be directed to draft procedures for the handling of future matters under BCO 34-1 to be proposed to the 29th GA. Adopted

RESOLUTION ADOPTED BY WESTERN CAROLINA PRESBYTERY
on July 20, 1999

Whereas, the Word of God is clear that only men are to lead in public worship

<a id="ga28-p311"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=313 printed_page=311 -->

(1 Corinthians 14:34; 1 Timothy 2-3); and

Whereas, this understanding of God’s will has been upheld by the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America (M25GA, p. 162: “The subordinate standards are clear that the preaching of the Word in the public worship on the Lord’s Day is to be conducted by elders, under the oversight of the session”; M26GA, p. 252, with regard to the taking exception to the minutes of Southern Florida Presbytery for having a woman lead the congregation in the opening prayer and another woman who read passages of scripture prior to the sermon: “The 26th General Assembly refers Southern Florida Presbytery to I Corinthians 14:34; I Timothy 2:11-15; Westminster Larger Catechism, Questions 155-159”), and

Whereas, female preaching is a violation of the PCA Constitution, as evident by the following:

1. Larger Catechism, Q/A 158: “By whom is the word of God to be preached?” “The word of God is to be preached only by such as are sufficiently gifted, and also duly approved and called to that office.”

2. BCO 7-2: “…In accord with Scripture, these offices [elder and deacon] are open to men only.”

3. BCO 19-1: “To preserve the purity of the preaching of the Gospel, no man is permitted to preach in the pulpits of the Presbyterian Church in America on a regular basis without proper licensure from the Presbytery having jurisdiction where he will preach. A ruling elder, a candidate for the ministry, a minister from some other denomination, or some other man may be licensed for the purpose of regularly providing the preaching of the Word upon his giving satisfaction to the Presbytery of his gifts and passing the licensure examination. (See also BCO 22-5 and 22-6)”; and

Whereas, the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) was founded in 1973, in part, in reaction against the elevation of women to positions of authority in the Presbyterian Church in the United States (PCUS); and

Whereas, the PCA position on this matter was because of a principal [sic] commitment to the Bible as the inerrant and infallible Word of God, which speaks with such clarity to the issue; and

Whereas, many people who founded the PCA paid a dear price, emotionally, spiritually, and financially (sometimes even sacrificing church buildings to a liberal ecclesiastical establishment), in order to establish a new denomination that would be free of the threat of female preaching; and

Whereas, the PCA has broken fraternal relations with the Christian Reformed Church of North America (CRCNA) and has declined to enter into fraternal relations with the Evangelical Presbyterian Church (EPC) because of the commitment of those churches to the ordination of women to ruling and teaching office; and

<a id="ga28-p312"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=314 printed_page=312 -->

Whereas, the toleration of women preaching in the crcna was a major
contributing factor to that denomination ordaining women ministers, and

Whereas, historically, Presbyterian denominations have apostatized on the
matter of ordaining women as ministers after becoming accustomed to
women filling the pulpit; and

Whereas, concern over reports that women have been preaching in the PCA led
Western Carolina Presbytery to overture the 27th General Assembly to
advise sessions and presbyteries that this practice should not be allowed,
and

Whereas, in answering the overture, the General Assembly affirmed that
female preaching is a violation of the teaching of Scripture, and

Whereas, while answering the overture in the negative, the General Assembly
did so because of a desire that this matter be dealt within the lower
courts (thereby inviting judicial process); and

Whereas, it has been widely reported that Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church,
Knoxville, Tennessee, has had women preachers to fill its pulpit; and

Whereas, on August 16 and 23, 1998, a woman led in worship, preached,
giving instruction and exhortation in the Sunday evening worship service
at Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church, and

Whereas, the senior pastor of cedar springs presbyterian Church, John Wood,
has publicly defended the practice of women preaching, teaching and
exhorting, and

Whereas, this has become a public scandal throughout the church; and

Whereas, this has been an occasion of offense and of alarm by many in the
denomination, and

Whereas, pastor Wood has confirmed in private conversation with teaching
elders Joseph A. Pipa, Jr., and Morton H. Smith on July 17, 1999, that he
believes women may preach in our pulpits; and

Whereas, at its March 1999 stated meeting, Western Carolina Presbytery asked
Tennessee Valley Presbytery to investigate this matter; and

Whereas, Tennessee Valley Presbytery has, since the reception of that letter
from Western Carolina Presbytery, had two stated meetings, and

Whereas, Tennessee Valley Presbytery has taken no action in the matter, and

Whereas, Tennessee Valley Presbytery has not even formally communicated
with Western Carolina Presbytery regarding its concern, and

Whereas, we are fast approaching a one-year statute of limitations on matters
of scandal (BCO 32-20), and

Whereas, the Book of Church Order, reflecting the teaching of holy scripture
regarding mutual accountability, provides that “if the presbytery [having
original jurisdiction over a minister] refuses to act in doctrinal cases or
cases of public scandal and two other presbyteries request the General Assembly to assume original jurisdiction (to first receive and initially
hear and determine), the General Assembly shall do so” (BCO 34-1); and

Whereas, scripture instructs the children of god that they must be concerned
about sin in the camp (Deuteronomy 13:12-18; Joshua 7); and

<a id="ga28-p313"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=315 printed_page=313 -->

Whereas, the New Testament tells us that we are part of a body, and that what one member does affects all the members (1 Corinthians 12); and

Whereas, for us not to be concerned about erring brethren is evidence of our lack of love (Psalm 141:5; Galatians 6:1); and

Whereas, this issue, if not dealt with swiftly and decisively, will lead the PCA down the same path as other denominations which have gone into liberalism;

Now, therefore, be it resolved, that Western Carolina Presbytery hereby invokes the provision of BCO 34-1 by requesting the General Assembly to assume original jurisdiction over John Wood, a ministerial member of Tennessee Valley Presbytery, in order to answer the following charges:

1. That he has violated the Biblical teaching on worship (as understood by the PCA), which is to be led by men under the Headship of Christ, in having a woman fill the pulpit, preach, give instruction and exhort the congregation from the Word of God, which is specifically forbidden by LC 158, which answers the question, “By whom is the word of God to be preached?” by saying, “The Word of God is to be preached only by such as are sufficiently gifted, and also duly approved and called to that office.” (See also: BCO 7-2; 19-1; 22-5; 22-6.)

2. That Dr. Wood has violated the clear teaching of the Word of God, which says that women are to keep silence in the church, and are not to exercise authority over men (1 Corinthians 14:34; I Timothy 2:8-15; 4:13), in violation of the Second Commandment as interpreted by our Standards. (Larger Catechism Q/A 108: “What are the duties required in the second commandment?” “The duties required in the second commandment are, the receiving, observing, and keeping pure and entire, all such religious worship and ordinances as God has instituted in his word...” Larger Catechism Q/A 109: “What are the sins forbidden in the second commandment?” “The sins forbidden in the second commandment are all devising, counseling, commanding, using, and any wise approving any religious worship not instituted by God himself...”)

## RESOLUTION PRESENTED TO CALVARY PRESBYTERY
on July 24, 1999

Whereas, the Word of God is clear that only men are to lead in public worship (I Corinthians 14:34; I Timothy 2-3); and

Whereas, this understanding of God’s will has been upheld by the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America (M25GA, p. 162: “The subordinate standards are clear that the preaching of the Word in the public worship on the Lord’s Day is to be conducted by elders, under the oversight of the session”; M26GA, p. 252, with regard to the taking exception to the minutes of Southern Florida Presbytery for having a

<a id="ga28-p314"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=316 printed_page=314 -->

woman lead the congregation in the opening prayer and another woman who read passages of Scripture prior to the sermon: “The 26th General Assembly refers Southern Florida Presbytery to I Corinthians 14:34; I Timothy 2: 11-15; Westminster Larger Catechism, Questions 155-159”); and

Whereas, female preaching is a violation of the PCA Constitution, as evident by the following:

1. Larger Catechism, Q/A 158: “By whom is the Word of God to be preached?” “The Word of God is to be preached only by such as are sufficiently gifted, and also duly approved and called to that office.”

2. BCO 7-2: “... In accord with Scripture, these offices [elder and deacon] are open to men only.”

3. BCO 19-1: “To preserve the purity of the preaching of the Gospel, no man is permitted to preach in the pulpits of the Presbyterian Church in America on a regular basis without proper licensure from the Presbytery having jurisdiction where he will preach. A ruling elder, a candidate for the ministry, a minister from some other denomination, or some other man may be licensed for the purpose of regularly providing the preaching of the Word upon his giving satisfaction to the Presbytery of his gifts and passing the licensure examination. (See also BCO 22-5 and 22-6.)”;

Whereas, The Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) was founded in 1973, in part, in reaction against the elevation of women to positions of authority in the Presbyterian Church in the United States (PCUS); and

Whereas, the PCA position on this matter was because of a principal [sic] commitment to the Bible as the inerrant and infallible Word of God, which speaks with such clarity to the issue; and

Whereas, many people who founded the PCA paid a dear price, emotionally, spiritually, and financially (sometimes even sacrificing church buildings to a liberal ecclesiastical establishment), in order to establish a new denomination that would be free of the threat of female preaching; and

Whereas, the PCA has broken fraternal relations with the Christian Reformed Church of North America (CRCNA) and has declined to enter into fraternal relations the Evangelical Presbyterian Church (EPC) because of the commitment of those churches to the ordination of women to ruling and teaching office; and

Whereas, the toleration of women preaching in the CRCNA was a major contributing factor to that denomination ordaining women ministers; and

Whereas, historically, Presbyterian denominations have apostatized on the matter of ordaining women as ministers after becoming accustomed to women filling the pulpit; and

Whereas, concern over reports that women have been preaching in the PCA led Western Carolina Presbytery to overture the 27th General Assembly to

<a id="ga28-p315"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=317 printed_page=315 -->

advise sessions and presbyteries that this practice should not be allowed,
and

Whereas, in answering the overture, the General Assembly affirmed that
female preaching is a violation of the teaching of Scripture, and

Whereas, while answering the overture in the negative, the General Assembly
did so because of a desire that this matter be dealt within the lower
courts (thereby inviting judicial process); and

Whereas, it has been widely reported that Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church,
Knoxville, Tennessee, has had women preachers to fill its pulpit, and

Whereas, on August 16 and 23, 1998, a woman led in worship, preached,
giving instruction and exhortation in the Sunday evening worship service
at Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church, and

Whereas, the senior pastor of cedar springs presbyterian Church, John Wood,
has publicly defended the practice of women preaching, teaching and
exhorting, and

Whereas, this has become a public scandal throughout the church, and

Whereas, this has been an occasion of offense and of alarm by many in the
denomination, and

Whereas, pastor Wood has confirmed in private conversation on July 17, 1999,
with Drs. Morton Smith and Joseph Pipa that he believes women may
preach in our pulpits, and

Whereas, at its March 1999 stated meeting, Western Carolina Presbytery asked
Tennessee Valley Presbytery to investigate this matter, and

Whereas, Tennessee Valley Presbytery has, since the reception of that letter
from Western Carolina Presbytery, had two stated meetings; and

Whereas, Tennessee Valley Presbytery has taken no action in the matter, and

Whereas, Tennessee Valley Presbytery has not even formally communicated
with Western Carolina Presbytery regarding its concern, and

Whereas, we are fast approaching a one-year statute of limitations on matters
of scandal (BCO 32-20), and

Whereas, the Book of Church Order, reflecting the teaching of holy scripture
regarding mutual accountability, provides that “if the presbytery [having
original jurisdiction over a minister] refuses to act in doctrinal cases or
cases of public scandal and two other presbyteries request the General Assembly to assume original jurisdiction (to first receive and initially
hear and determine), the General Assembly shall do so” (BCO 34-1), and

Whereas, scripture instructs the children of god that they must be concerned
about sin in the camp (Deuteronomy 13:12-18, Joshua 7), and

Whereas, the new testament tells us that we are part of a body, and that what
one member does affects all the members (1 Corinthians 12), and

Whereas, for us not to be concerned about erring brethren is evidence of our
lack of love (Psalm 141:5; Galatians 6:1); and

Whereas, this issue, if not dealt with swiftly and decisively, will lead the PCA
down the same path as other denominations which have gone into
liberalism;

<a id="ga28-p316"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=318 printed_page=316 -->

Now therefore, be it resolved, that Calvary Presbytery hereby invokes the
provision of BCO 34-1 by requesting the General Assembly to assume
original jurisdiction over John Wood, a ministerial member of tennessee
Valley Presbytery, in order to answer the following charges:
1 That he has violated the Biblical teaching on worship (as
understood by the PCA), which is to be led by men under the
heads hip of Christ, in having a woman fill the pulpit, preach, give
instruction and exhort the congregation from the word of God,
which is specifically forbidden by LC 158, which answers the
question, “By whom is the word of god to be preached?” by
saying, “The word of god is to be preached only by such as are
sufficiently gifted, and also duly approved and called to that
office.” (See also: BCO 7-2; 19-1; 22-5; 22-6.)
2 that dr wood has violated the clear teaching of the word of God,
which says that women are to keep silence in the church, and are
not to exercise authority over men (1 Corinthians 14:34; I Timothy
2:8-15, 4:13), in violation of the second commandment as
interpreted by our Standards (Larger Catechism Q/A 108: “What
are the duties required in the second commandment?” “The duties
required in the second commandment are, the receiving, observing,
and keeping pure and entire, all such religious worship and
ordinances as God has instituted in his w ord...” Larger Catechism
Q/A 109: “What are the sins forbidden in the second
commandment?” “The sins forbidden in the second commandment
are all devising, counseling, commanding, using, and any wise
approving any religious worship not instituted by God him self.. ”)

OVERTURE FROM THE PRESBYTERY OF THE ASCENSION
On July 31, 2000

The Presbytery of the Ascension, meeting in Butler, PA, July 31, 1999,
overtures the General Assembly of the PCA as follows:

Whereas, the Word of God is clear that only men are to lead in public worship
(1 Corinthians 14:34, 1 Timothy 2-3); and

Whereas, this understanding of God’s will has been upheld by the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America (M25GA, p. 162: ‘The
subordinate standards are clear that the preaching of the word in the
public worship on the Lord’s day is to be conducted by elders, under the
oversight of the session”; M26GA, p 252, with regard to the taking
exception to the minutes of southern florida presbytery for having a
woman lead the congregation in the opening prayer and another woman
who read passages of scripture prior to the sermon: “The 26th General Assembly refers Southern Florida Presbytery to I Corinthians 14:34, I

<a id="ga28-p317"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=319 printed_page=317 -->

Timothy 2:11-15; Westminster Larger Catechism, Questions 155-159”); and

Whereas, female preaching is a violation of the PCA Constitution, as evident by the following:

1. Larger Catechism, Q/A 158: “By whom is the word of God to be preached?” “The word of God is to be preached only by such as are sufficiently gifted, and also duly approved and called to that office.”
2. BCO 7-2: “...In accord with Scripture, these offices [elder and deacon] are open to men only.”
3. BCO 19-1: “To preserve the purity of the preaching of the Gospel, no man is permitted to preach in the pulpits of the Presbyterian Church in America on a regular basis without proper licensure from the Presbytery having jurisdiction where he will preach. A ruling elder, a candidate for the ministry, a minister from some other denomination, or some other man may be licensed for the purpose of regularly providing the preaching of the Word upon his giving satisfaction to the Presbytery of his gifts and passing the licensure examination. (See also BCO 22-5 and 22-6)”; and

Whereas, the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) was founded in 1973, in part, in reaction against the elevation of women to positions of authority in the Presbyterian Church in the United States (PCUS); and

Whereas, the PCA position on this matter was because of a principal [sic] commitment to the Bible as the inerrant and infallible Word of God, which speaks with such clarity to the issue; and

Whereas, many people who founded the PCA paid a dear price, emotionally, spiritually, and financially (sometimes even sacrificing church buildings to a liberal ecclesiastical establishment), in order to establish a new denomination that would be free of the threat of female preaching; and

Whereas, the PCA has broken fraternal relations with the Christian Reformed Church of North America (CRCNA) and has declined to enter into fraternal relations with the Evangelical Presbyterian Church (EPC) because of the commitment of those churches to the ordination of women to ruling and teaching office; and

Whereas, the toleration of women preaching in the CRCNA was a major contributing factor to that denomination ordaining women ministers; and

Whereas, historically, Presbyterian denominations have apostatized on the matter of ordaining women as ministers after becoming accustomed to women filling the pulpit; and

Whereas, concern over reports that women have been preaching in the PCA led Western Carolina Presbytery to overture the 27th General Assembly to advise sessions and presbyteries that this practice should not be allowed; and

Whereas, in answering the overture, the General Assembly affirmed that female preaching is a violation of the teaching of Scripture; and

<a id="ga28-p318"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=320 printed_page=318 -->

Whereas, while answering the overture in the negative, the General Assembly did so because of a desire that this matter be dealt with in the lower courts (thereby inviting judicial process); and

Whereas, it has been widely reported that Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church, Knoxville, Tennessee, has had women preachers to fill its pulpit; and

Whereas, on August 16 and 23, 1998, a woman led in worship, preached, giving instruction and exhortation in the Sunday evening worship service; and

Whereas, the Senior Pastor of Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church, John Wood, has publicly defended the practice of women preaching, teaching and exhorting; and

Whereas, this has become a public scandal throughout the church; and

Whereas, this has been an occasion of offense and of alarm by many in the denomination; and

Whereas, at its March 1999 stated meeting, Western Carolina Presbytery asked Tennessee Valley Presbytery to investigate this matter; and

Whereas, Tennessee Valley Presbytery has, since the reception of that letter from Western Carolina Presbytery, had two stated meetings; and

Whereas, Tennessee Valley Presbytery has taken no action in the matter; and

Whereas, we are fast approaching a one-year statute of limitations on matters of scandal (BCO 32-20); and

Whereas, the Book of Church Order, reflecting the teaching of Holy Scripture, provides that “if the presbytery [having original jurisdiction over a minister] refuses to act in doctrinal cases or cases of public scandal and two other presbyteries request the General Assembly to assume original jurisdiction (to first receive and initially hear and determine), the General Assembly shall do so” (BCO 34-1); and

Whereas, Scripture instructs the children of God that they must be concerned about sin in the camp (Deuteronomy 13:12-18; Joshua 7); and

Whereas, the New Testament tells us that we are part of a body, and that what one member does affects all the members (1 Corinthians 12); and

Whereas, for us not to be concerned about erring brethren is evidence of our lack of love (Psalm 141:5; Galatians 6:1); and

Whereas, this issue, if not dealt with swiftly and decisively, will lead the PCA down the same path as other denominations which have gone into liberalism;

Now therefore, be it resolved, that the Presbytery of the Ascension hereby invokes the provision of BCO 34-1 by requesting the General Assembly to assume original jurisdiction over John Wood, a ministerial member of Tennessee Valley Presbytery, in order to address the concerns originally brought by Western Carolina Presbytery to the 27th General Assembly:

1. That he has violated the Biblical teaching of worship as understood by the Presbyterian Church in America, which is to be led by men under the Headship of Christ, in having a woman fill the pulpit, preach, give instruction and exhort the congregation from the Word

<a id="ga28-p319"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=321 printed_page=319 -->

of God, which is specifically forbidden by LC 158, which answers
the question, “By whom is the word of God to be preached9” by
saying, “The word of god is to be preached only by such as are
sufficiently gifted, and also duly approved and called to that office.”
(See also: BCO 7-2, 19-1; 22-5; 22-6.)
2 That Dr. wood has violated the clear teaching of the word of God,
which says that women are to keep silence in the church, and are not
to exercise authority over men (1 Corinthians 14:34, 1 Timothy 2:8-
15, 4:13), in violation of the second commandment as interpreted by
our Standards. (Larger Catechism Q/A 108: “What are the duties
required in the second commandment?” “The duties required in the
second commandment are, the receiving, observing, and keeping
pure and entire, all such religious worship and ordinances as God has
instituted in his word ...” Ixtrger Catechism Q/A 109: “What are the
sins forbidden in the second commandment?” “The sins forbidden in
the second commandment are all devising, counseling, commanding,
using, and any wise approving any religious worship not instituted by
God himself. . .”)
3. That he has countenanced these activities which have taken place on
the Sabbath, in violation of the fourth commandment (Larger
Catechism, Q/A 118: “Why is the charge of keeping the sabbath
m or especially directed to governors of families and other
superiors?” “The charge of keeping the sabbath ism or especially
directed to governors of families and other superiors, because they
are bound not only to keep it themselves, but to see that it be
observed by all those that are under their charge. .”; Ixtrger
Catechism, Q/A 119: “What are the sins forbidden in the fourth
commandment?” “The sins forbidden in the fourth commandment
[include] .. all profaning the day by . doing that which is in itself
sinful...”);
4. That he has broken his ordination vow about being subject to the
brethren in the Lord, by going contrary to the constitution of the
church, and the position taken by the Assembly against preaching by
women, in violation of the fifth commandment (Larger Catechism,
Q/A 128: “What are the sins of inferiors against their superiors?”
“The sins of inferiors against their superiors a re.. rebellion
against...their lawful counsels [and] comm ands...”).

adopted by the presbytery of the ascension a tits july stated meeting on july
31, 1999. Attest: /s/ Frederick R. Neikirk, Stated Clerk

##### OVERTURE 23 From James River Presbytery (to SJC and CCB)

“Request Assembly to Assume Original Jurisdiction”

<a id="ga28-p320"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=322 printed_page=320 -->

Whereas, due to the public nature of the letter from teaching elder john wood
and the session of cedar springs presbyterian church dated october 25,
1999 to the Tennessee Valley Presbytery, it is our concern that this
overture be handled in a timely and God-honoring manner.

Whereas, the Scriptures clearly teach that a woman is not to teach or exercise
authority over a man, but is to remain silent (1 Timothy 2:12); and

Whereas, the PCA Book of Church Order should be interpreted consistently
rather than in a way that is contrary to the Scriptures; and

Whereas, to permit a woman to preach or teach in the context of worship would
be a gross violation of 1 Timothy 2:12; and

Whereas, the session of cedar springs presbyterian church has made it known
that a woman has been invited to be the plenary speaker for the 2001
World Missions Conference,

Therefore Be it Resolved, that James River Presbytery overtures the General Assembly as Western Carolina, Calvary and Ascension Presbyteries
have done in objecting to the Cedar Springs Presbyterian Church’s use of
female Preachers in their Worship service, and request the 28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to assume original
jurisdiction in this matter in accordance with the provisions of BCO 14-6
and BCO 34-1.

adopted at the spring stated meeting of james river presbytery on april 11,
2000. Attested by: /s/ Eugene H. Friedline, Stated Clerk

25. that the minutes of louisiana presbytery .
a Be approved without exception: December 12, 1998; April 17, 1999;
October 16,1999; January 15, 2000;

- **b.** be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: None.
- **c.** be approved with exceptions of substance stated below:

Exception: General: no directory of presbytery churches, roll of
presbytery or roll of candidates and licentiates of the presbytery churches
is included,
d That the following responses to the 27th GA be found satisfactory:
Exception: January 16,1996: no minutes of executive session
Response: there was no executive session during the meeting of
January 16, 1996. During the meeting the Presbytery arose as a
Presbytery, was seated as a Corporation, elected officers, arose as a
Corporation and was again seated as a Presbytery. The business

<a id="ga28-p329"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=331 printed_page=329 -->

transacted in that meeting is recorded within the minutes of Presbytery, not as separate minutes.

Exception: October 19, 1996: Expiration of license to preach. Then licentiate did not apply for renewal. Procedures for renewal not followed.

Response: Presbytery met only two times in Stated Meetings in 1996: in January and again in October. In the interval between the meetings TE Ron Davis of Bethel Presbyterian Church in Lake Charles died. Licentiate RE Ben Thompson, a member of Bethel Presbyterian Church whose license was still valid, was asked by the Session of Bethel Presbyterian Church to fill the pulpit. Before the Stated meeting in October RE Thompson’s license expired. When the Presbytery discovered the oversight in October 1996, the motion was made, citing BCO 19-2, to waive re-examination in this extraordinary case and to renew his license. RE Thompson had been preaching regularly under the supervision of the Bethel Session.

General: Session records not reviewed in 1996.

Response: The Session Records Committee apparently did fail to examine minutes in 1996 due to the departure of the committee members from Presbytery. Subsequently, session records have been reviewed by the committee.

That as no response to the 27th GA was received this exception should be submitted to the 29th GA:

General: No standing rules submitted per RAO 14-4-c&amp;e (second year).

Adopted

29. That the Minutes of Missouri Presbytery:

- **a.** Be approved without exception: January 19, 1999;
- **b.** Be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: May 20, 1999; July 20, 1999;
- **c.** Be approved with exceptions of substance stated below:

Exception: May 20, 1999: Presbytery did not record why it considers a work a valid Christian ministry. *BCO* 8-7; 13-5; 20-1.
Exception: May 20, 1999: The report of the commission to install teaching elders contains no record of the following:
1) The pronouncement of installation (*BCO* 21-7);
2) The charge to the congregation (*BCO* 21-7);
3) That the TEs answered the constitutional questions in the affirmative (*BCO* 21-9);
4) That the congregation or session were asked the constitutional questions (*BCO* 21-10);
Exception: July 20, 1999: The Standing Rules mandate a meeting in October, yet no minutes for that meeting were submitted;
Exception: General: The Standing Rules are not complete. (RAO 14.4.c.2).

d. That the following responses to the 27th GA exception be found satisfactory:
Exception: April 21, 1998: No record of candidate questions put to transferring TE. *BCO* 13-6.
Response: While presbytery did not specifically ask Rev. Amadeo Torres the questions put to candidates at the time of their ordination (*BCO* 21-5), the minutes of the Missouri Presbytery’s Stated Meeting of 21 April 1998 clearly indicate that Rev. Torres signed his ministerial obligation which includes his written assent to all the ordination questions. Presbytery considered Rev. Torres’ subscription to the Ministerial Obligation as fulfilling the requirement of *BCO* 13-6. However, to avoid confusion in the future, Missouri Presbytery will require a verbal response from each ordained minister seeking admission to the presbytery.
Exception: General: Allowed an unlicensed man to preach in a PCA pulpit for more than a year. *BCO* 19-1,20-2,22-5,22-6.
Response: Our Candidates and Credentials Committee attempted to work with Westminster PC, Booneville, for about a year. At first the committee attempted to bring the retired Methodist minister that was filling the pulpit through the trials for ordination. When it was clear that he would not be able to pass the theological exams, they spoke with the

<a id="ga28-p331"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=333 printed_page=331 -->

elders of this small church (about 25 members) about their options. All
of this took about a year and a half the matter has finally been resolved
this past month when the church voted to pullout of the PCA.
Adopted
30. that the minutes of nashville Presbytery:
a Be approved without exception: None.
b be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery:None,
c. be approved with exceptions of substance stated below:
Exception: the minutes of 1999 were submitted too late to be reviewed
by the committee.
d That the following responses to the 27th GA exception be found
satisfactory with the advice to Nashville Presbytery to insert the
phrase “BCO 40-1,2,3 and” between “according to “ and “rules
adopted” in sentence one section 10-1 of Presbytery’s Standing
Rules, so that the sentence will read: “Sessions shall keep their
records according to BCO 40-1,2,3 and rules adopted by the
Presbytery...”
Exception: General: Churches not reporting to presbytery. 40-1,2,3.
Response: in the past two years all of our churches submitted their
sessional records for review (reported). In 1998 Nashville Presbytery
only met three times and not all the churches reported in that calendar
year. We will take greater care to have our churches adhere to a schedule
that meets the requirements of the BCO.
Exception: General: Standing rules on session records should comply
with BCO 40-1,2,3.
Response: nashville presbytery cannot detect the lack of compliance in
our Standing Rules with BCO 40-1,2,3, nor has this been pointed out
before. the sections o fours rs which appear to be germane to the
general exception, together with our adopted list of minutes rules, are
excerpted below:
3-1 Book of Church Order. Chapter 13 of the Book of Church Order, THE PRESBYTERY, shall govern Presbytery in all matters to
which this chapter speaks.
CHAPTER TEN: SESSIONAL RECORDS
10-1 RULES: Sessions shall keep their records according to rules
adopted by the Presbytery, securing from the stated clerk a copy of these
rules inserting the list of rules on the inside of the front cover of their
Sessional Records Book. a copy of the sheet listing the items by which
the annual check of sessional records is made, shall also be inserted in
the inside of the front cover of each sessional records Book. Sessions
are to inform themselves about the contents of both of these papers and to
seek to observe them in keeping Sessional Records Books,
10-2 EXAMINATION Sessional records shall be presented for
examination according to the schedule set by the Stated Clerk.
comments of approval or criticism concerning each set of records shall

<a id="ga28-p332"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=334 printed_page=332 -->

be noted on the correction sheet for the help of those preparing the minutes.

**Guidelines for keeping Session Minutes consistent with the Book of Church Order and Rules of Assembly Operation.**

1. All Minutes should be typed or written legibly.
2. All Minutes are to be kept in one volume for permanent record.
3. The name of the church is to appear on the cover of the volume.
4. Pages are to be numbered consecutively.
5. No blank pages are to be left between records of meetings.
6. The minutes of the annual congregational meeting and all other called meetings are to be included in the book.
7. Every meeting is to be recorded as to date, place, time, name of those present, absent, and excused. The minutes are to show that the meeting was opened and closed with prayer and each meeting should be recorded so as to show an accurate record of the proceedings. **Only motions passed need to be recorded.**
8. Minutes should show the purpose of the meeting stated or called. If called, the exact call of the meeting must be stated, with evidence that only business was conducted.
9. The names of the elders and viewpoints expressed by them need not be recorded.
10. All meetings are to be attested by the clerk and the moderator and the minutes of each meeting are to be approved at that meeting or at subsequent meeting as read or corrected.
11. Joint meetings of the Session and Deacons are to be recorded.
12. Congregational meetings of the Session and Deacons are to be recorded.
13. The minutes should show that a quorum was present at any given meeting.
14. Records are to be examined as to whether the proceedings have been correctly recorded in the light of the Book of Church Order and the above guide of the Nashville Presbytery.
15. A copy of the NP Standing Rules and a copy of this sheet of guidelines should be included with the minutes.

Please show us how our rules fail to comply. The Nashville Presbytery is currently studying and changing its structure. This process will result in changes to the Standing Rules. We will take care to adopt rules that fully comply with the BCO.

**Exception: General:** No record of ordinands being asked if their views had changed since licensure BCO 21-4

**Response:** Nashville Presbytery asks ordinands about changes in views as a regular part of their examination. The clerk has not recorded responses which reflect no change of views, but he will in the future.

**Exception: General:** Commissions minutes and reports not included. BCO 15-1,2.

<a id="ga28-p333"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=335 printed_page=333 -->

Response: Nashville Presbytery will take greater care to see that all commission reports and minutes are included with the minutes.
Adopted

- **a.** Be approved without exception: February 20, 1999; September 18, 1999;

32. That the Minutes of New River Presbytery:

- **a.** Be approved without exception: None.
- **b.** Be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: January 23, 1999; July 9-10, 1999; October 9, 1999; November 12-13, 1999;
- **c.** Be approved with exceptions of substance stated below:

Exception: March 12-13, 1999 (p. 3): Items 75-17.f., 75-17.g., and 75-17.i, refer to appendices which are not attached.
d. That the response to the 27th GA exception be found not satisfactory for the following reasons:
1. The WCF and Catechisms teach explicitly that the sacraments as a means of grace must be administered only by one called to do so — “a minister of the Word lawfully ordained.” WCF 27.4, cf. Heb 5.4.
2. WCF 21.7 affirms that the Lord’s Day is the Christian Sabbath as part of Covenant theology, the system of Reformed doctrine.
3. In response to the reason submitted to the committee:
A. To teach these things at all is to disturb the peace and purity of the Church.
B. The courts of the Church have declined to define the “fundamentals of the system of doctrine” and they have never limited them to the results of past judicial decisions.
C. That General Assembly has determined that only Teaching Elders can administer the sacraments. (M5GA pp38-39; 197).

Exception: Man licensed who “holds exception in his view of the Sabbath (he does not believe that the Sabbath Day and Lord’s Day are the same), the law (he is not absolutely sure that the Decalogue is the New Testament Ethic), and on who may administer the Sacraments (he cannot see that the administration of the sacraments is the sole prerogative of teaching elders; he believes that ruling elders and others who have demonstrated knowledge, understanding, and maturity concerning the sacraments should be allowed to administer them.)” (WCF 21-7, 27-4, 19-2).

Response: That New River Presbytery resubmits its initial response and requests that the General Assembly accept it as satisfactory for the following reasons:

<a id="ga28-p334"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=336 printed_page=334 -->

Response: “Terry Roush has rescinded his exception concerning the
Decalogue as the New Testament ethic and expressed a view consistent
with the Reformed Faith. The Presbytery finds his other two exceptions
allowable and therefore his license remains in effect.”
Reasons:
1. When the General Assembly acting at the 25th General Assembly,
approved the original exception to our minutes, the Committee on Review
of presbytery records had failed to report to the assembly that in the same
motion in which the Presbytery approved the three original exceptions, it
also approved the following statement: ‘and that he be instructed not to
preach or teach these exceptions in such a way to disturb the peace and
purity of the church.’ (Minutes of NRP 7/13/96, p5, Item 67-18.)
2. Since the two remaining exceptions have not been found through
required judicial and constitutional process to be considered as
‘fundamentals of the system of doctrine’ and thus are allowable
exceptions, the Presbytery has the right to grant these exceptions, as
outlined in BCO 39-3 ( 1), to wit:
“(1) A higher court, reviewing a lower court, should limit itself to
the issues raised by the parties to the case in the original (lower)
court. Further, the higher court should resolve such issues by
applying the constitution of the church, as previously established
through the constitutional process.”
Adopted

that the minutes of north georgia Presbytery:
a Be approved without exception: None.
b. be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: January
16, 1999;
• c. be approved with exceptions of substance stated below:
Exception: January 16, 1999(page 6&7): No evidence that the TE
was examined on Sacraments and Church Government. BCO 13-6.
Exception: January 16, 1999 (p. 8): AD HOC Committee, A TE
cannot by himself ask a Shepherding Committee to visit It requires
request of a session.
Exception: April 20, 1999 (p. 5, #3): No evidence that TEs were
examined in Theology BCO 21-4-a-4.
Exception: May 25, 1999 (called meeting, p. 2): Each area of
examination should be indicated BCO 13-6.

<a id="ga28-p335"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=337 printed_page=335 -->

Exception: July 17, 1999 (p.4, #2): No indication that candidates were
endorsed by their sessions BCO 18-2
d That the response to the 26th GA that was received and submitted to
the 28th ga was found to be satisfactory:
Exception: January 18, 1997, Licentiate being examined for ordination
was not examined in sacraments nor asked if his views have changed since
licensure. BCO 21-4
Response: Presbytery acknowledges the error and will endeavor to guard
against it occurrence in the future.
That the response to the 25th GA exceptions are found to be
satisfactory:
[Response is from the clerk, not from presbytery ]
Exception: May 11,1996: No quorum for called meeting. No ruling elder
present BCO 13-4.
Response: The exception was holding called meetings without the
presence of ruling elders. Our response to this is that it did not actually
happen. the clerk reported the names of the churches represented, which
were, in fact, represented by ruling elders. He simply neglected to record
their names, not realizing the oversight since the church was listed as being
represented. Presbytery regrets the omission.
Exception: November 27, 1996: No quorum for called meeting. No
ruling elder present. BCO 13-4.
Response: The exception was holding called meetings without the
presence of ruling elders our response to th isis that it did not actually
happen The omission is there, but so were the elders. The Clerk
reported the names of the churches represented, which were, in fact,
represented by ruling elders He simply neglected to record their names,
not realizing the oversight since the church was listed as being
represented. Presbytery regrets the omission.
Exception: January 17,1998: Licentiate being examined for ordination
was not examined in sacraments nor asked if his views have changed
since licensure. BCO 21-4
Response: Presbytery has instructed its Credentials Committee to
prepare a checklist of those areas required by the Book of Church Order
and that this checklist be utilized for Presbytery’s examination of each
licentiate.
Exception: April 21, 1998: Presbytery erred in allowing a congregation
to dissolve a pastoral relationship. BCO 13-9-c.
Response: presbytery respectfully suggests that a careful reading of the
section of the minutes will disclose that the dissolution was
accomplished by pres byte ry upon adoption of the appropriate presbytery
Committee report.
Exception: April 21, 1998: Covenant questions should be addressed to
the session, not the congregation in assistant pastor installation. BCO 21-
10 .

<a id="ga28-p336"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=338 printed_page=336 -->

Response: Presbytery acknowledges the error, and in the future will
equip each ordination and/or installation with a checklist of procedures
to insure that the Book of Church Order requirements are met.
Exception: April 21, 1998: Presbytery cannot restore from
excommunication. This can only be done by session BCO 37-9-b.
Response: Presbytery calls attention to the fact that the individual who
was restored was a former Teaching Elder who had been
excommunicated by Presbytery and that the restoration was “through”
the session of park view church
Exception: July 18, 1998: no record of commissions provided. BCO
15-1
Response: Presbytery acknowledges the error and will diligently
endeavor to require that commissions be presented and recorded at the
time ordination and/or installations is approved.
Exception: July 18,1998: No call presented, discussed or attached to be
approved. BCO 20-1.
Response: Presbytery acknowledges that the approved call was not
attached as it should have been and will exercise care to insure that this
error does not occur in the future.
Exception: October 20, 1998: Commission to receive a church did not
have a quorum. BCO 15-2.
Response: Presbytery acknowledges the error and will increase future
commissions to three TE’s and three RE’s to insure a quorum of two
T E ’s andre ’s.
Exception: October 20, 1998: no record of call tote and no record of
installation commission. BCO 15-1,2.
Response: Presbytery submits that the TE accepted a position with
MTW and installation was not required.
Exception: General: no record that presbytery voted on any of the
exceptions taken to sessional records or that any session has responded
to exceptions taken to its records. BCO 40-1,3.
Response: record of adoption of the report of the committee to
examine sessional records omitted by error. The exceptions noted were
of such nature that response on the part of sessions was not required.
BCO 40-3. Adopted

<a id="ga28-p337"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=339 printed_page=337 -->

36. That the Minutes of Northeast Presbytery:

- **a.** Be approved without exception: October 30, 1999; December 15, 1999;
- **b.** Be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: January 16, 1999; May 22, 1999; August 14, 1999; September 17-18, 1999; November 15, 1999;
- **c.** Be approved with exceptions of substance: None.
- **d.** That the response to the 27th GA exception be found satisfactory:

Exception: November 21, 1998: Presbytery erred in allowing commissions to take up matters at their own discretion. *BCO* 15-1.
Response: RECOMMEND that the Presbytery rescind the action of the November, 1998 Pro re nata meeting and that the regional committees be instructed that they no longer have the authority to act as regional commissions except as specifically authorized by Presbytery for a particular matter.
RECOMMEND that Presbytery direct the Stated Clerk to communicate this action to the General Assembly and to thank the Assembly for their oversight in this matter.

Adopted

37. That the Minutes of Northern California Presbytery:

- **a.** Be approved without exception: July 24, 1999; October 1-2, 1999;
- **b.** Be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: None.
- **c.** Be approved with exceptions of substance stated below:

Exception: March 12-13, 1999 (p.2, Item 7): Minutes of called meeting January 23, 1999 are not included. (RAO 14-3).
d. That the response to the 27th GA exception be found satisfactory:
Exception: February 11, 1998: Presbytery erred in receiving a candidate who is not a member of the PCA. *BCO* 18-2.
Response: Presbytery does not agree with the exception as stated. Simply put, the Presbytery never received the candidate in question, as stated in the exception. The Presbytery did not ever conceive of the possibility of waiving the requirement of *BCO* 18-2 that a candidate coming under care must be a member of a PCA church. The Presbytery assumed that by requiring an endorsement from a PCA body before a candidate could be received that such a candidate would be required (1) to be a communicant member of a PCA congregation for six months, and (2) to have a satisfactory endorsement presented from the governing body of that congregation.

The Presbytery now realizes that in light of the wording of *BCO* 18-8 (“An applicant coming as a candidate from another denomination must present testimonials of his standing in that body and must become a member of a congregation in the Presbyterian in America, he shall THEN FULFILL the requirements of applicants listed under *BCO* 18-2…”) we should not have even proceeded with and sustained the first part of the examination for bringing a man under care.

<a id="ga28-p338"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=340 printed_page=338 -->

The Presbytery will not repeat such action in the future. We will not initiate any examinations of candidates for coming under care who are not first members of a PCA congregation.

Adopted

38. That the Minutes of Northern Illinois Presbytery:

- **a.** Be approved without exception: None.
- **b.** Be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: January 15, 1999; April 12, 1999; July 27, 1999; August 20, 1999; October 22, 1999;
- **c.** Be approved with exceptions of substance: None.
- **d.** That the response to the 27th GA exception be found satisfactory.

Exception: October 23, 1998: No mention of men coming under care meeting the 6-month requirement. BCO 18-2.
Response: The Presbytery of Northern Illinois regrets its error of omitting mention in its October 23, 1998, minutes of the fact that the men coming under care met the 6-month requirement of BCO 18-2. We have taken procedural steps that should prevent a recurrence of this omission.

Adopted

Acknowledging our aforementioned error, Siouxlands Presbytery assures General Assembly of our intention to pursue the organization of future mission churches with great diligence.”

IV. GENERAL RECOMMENDATIONS

That the 28th General Assembly instruct the Presbyteries to give diligence in the following matters:

a. Record the establishment of commissions and their quorums and include commission reports in Presbytery records (RAO 14-3.e.4).

b. Record specific parts of examinations and trials for licensure, candidacy, internship, transfer and ordination (RAO 14-3.e.5).

<a id="ga28-p344"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=346 printed_page=344 -->

c. Ensure that a proper call must be written and in the hands of the Presbytery prior to being acted upon by the Presbytery, and if the call comes from a source other than a Church, Presbytery, or the General Assembly, always make a record of the reasons why it considers the work to be a valid Christian ministry with testimony to his liberty to proclaim and practice fully and freely the whole counsel of God, as contained in the Scriptures and understood in the WCF (BCO 8-7; 20-1).

d. State clearly the reasons for invoking the extraordinary clause in waving portions of an exam and/or trial (BCO 19-2).

e. Encourage their churches to submit Session records for annual review and to comply with recording Sessions record reports. (BCO 12-7).

f. Terms of calls should be included in the minutes or copies of the calls as attachments to the minutes.

g. Include the docket in the minutes or as attachments.

h. Ensure that TEs laboring out of bounds “make report to Presbytery at least once each year” (BCO 8-4).

i. Ensure that Committees overseeing interns report on every intern at each stated meeting of the Presbytery and that these reports become part of the minutes of Presbytery (BCO 19-12).

j. Record exceptions taken by TE’s when examined.

k. Send, with their Minutes, any appendices or attachments that are relevant to the contents of the Minutes.

Adopted

28-74 Approval of Minutes

28-75 Report of the Committee on Thanks

Resolution of the Committee on Thanks
“This is the message we have heard from him and declare to you: God is light;
in him there is no darkness at all. If we claim to have fellowship with him yet walk in
the darkness, we lie and do not live by the truth But if we walk in the light, ashe is in
the light, we have fellowship with one another, and the blood of Jesus, his Son, purifies
us from all sin.”( l John 1:5-7)
“How great is the love the Father has lavished on us, that we should be called
the children of God! And that is what we are! The reason the world does not know us is
that is did not know him.”(1 John 3:1)
“Dear friends, do not believe every spirit, but test the spirits to see whether they
are from God, because many false prophets have gone out into the world This is how
you can recognize the spirit of God: Every spirit that acknowledges that Jesus Christ
has come in the flesh is from God, but every spirit that does not acknowledge Jesus is
not from God. this is the spirit of the antichrist, which you have heard is coming and
even now is already in the world. You, dear children, are from God and have overcome
them, because the one who is in you is greater than the one who is in the world.”(1 John
4:1-4)
We, the 28th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America, having
been challenged to “Consecrate the Church and Confront the Culture,” thank God for
His faithfulness to His church and for His provision of his Word upon which these dual
challenges have been based
We also offer thanks for His servants, through whom this challenge has been
articulated and lived out . . .
. . . those working on and with both the General Assembly Arrangements
committee and the arrangements committee of the southwest florida

<a id="ga28-p346"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=348 printed_page=346 -->

presbytery whose advance preparations made for the ease of assembly
work and the provision of meaningful women’s and children’s
ministries,
the city of tampa whose citizens in the form of the staffs at the tampa
Convention Center and the local hotels have also made us comfortable as
we received these challenges,
those planning, leading, and participating in our times of public worship,
particularly those preaching the Word and sharing their musical gifts,
whose service provided an opportunity for us to earnestly seek our
Father’s grace and provision to respond to these challenges,
. those guiding us from the platform— our retiring Moderator, RE Tom
Leopard, our Moderator TE Morton Smith and his substitutes, TE Paul
Settle and TE Frank Barker, our Stated Clerk TE Roy Taylor and his
staff, the permanent and temporary staff of the Stated Clerk’s office, the
parliamentarians, timekeeper, the recording clerks, floor clerks, the
audio-visual staff, and our organist and pianist— whose collective service
moved us from the call to order to adjournment with obvious concern for
fair and gracious progress through the docket and kept reminding us of
these challenges,
. . the staffs of the permanent commit te sand agencies whose service on our
behalf has been reported and we celebrate those who labored as members
of our committees of commissioners
. . and those exhibitors whose service provided us resources to respond to these
challenges.
As we leave Tampa and consider the work before us at the 29th General Assembly in Dallas, we ask our God to prepare us to take these challenges home and
that we would be “Consecrating the Church and Confronting the Culture” in our local
communities— that our great God and Father would be praised and honored above all.
/s/ TE James Phillis, Western Carolina Presbytery
/s/ RE Brad Bradley, North Texas Presbytery
Adopted
