<a id="ga28-p349"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=351 printed_page=349 -->

# REPORT OF THE STATED CLERK to the 28th GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA JUNE 2000

## Clerk's Activities

Since the last General Assembly, I have had the privilege of ministering the Word in a number of churches, and presbyteries, reporting on the ministry of the Office of the Stated Clerk and Administrative Committee. In addition to the normal duties of the Stated Clerk (“RAO” 3-2; 3-3) I worked with the RUM sub-committee of MNA helping to draft a proposal for RUM’s becoming a permanent committee and working out the necessary changes to our constitutional and secondary documents to effect that change. Additionally, I met with the MNA Worship sub-committee in helping to prepare worship guidelines for MNA church planters.

## Interchurch Relations

IRC chairman R. Irfon Hughes and I attended the annual North American Presbyterian and Reformed Churches meeting. The PCA Stated Clerk was elected as chairman for 1999-2000. NAPARC is asking member Churches respective committees to outline their distinctives from other NAPARC Churches and similarities with other NAPARC Churches to be discussed at the Fall, 2000 meeting with a view toward possible Church union. The Free Church of Scotland is sending an Ecclesiastical Observer to our Assembly and requests that we send an observer to their Assembly. Dr. Duncan Rankin is in the U.K. on sabbatical and can attend the Free Church Assembly. I have also received an inquiry from the Presbyterian Church of Ireland regarding the possible exchange of Ecclesiastical Observers. I have informed the IRC.

## Correspondence

- The only response I have received from United States Federal Government officials regarding the Assembly’s communication on the abortion issue has been from Senator Trent Lott, Majority Leader of the U.S. Senate. The President, Vice-President (president of the Senate), Chief Justice of the Supreme Court, and Speaker of the House of Representatives have not replied.

- I have received letters of greeting from The Reformed Presbyterian Church in North America, and the Reformed Church in the United States indicating that they will not be sending Fraternal Delegates this year.

- I have received two communications from the Christian and Missionary Alliance requesting that I, in the name of the PCA, join with them in filing an *amicus curiae* brief in the *Newport, Oregon Church of the Nazarene v. Hensley* case and to contribute PCA funds to the case. I declined to do so (“RAO” 3-2, t.) on the ground that I am not authorized to make such commitments without the action of the

<a id="ga28-p350"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=352 printed_page=350 -->

General Assembly. The case involved a pastor's seeking unemployment compensation after being discharged from the church, unemployment taxes for ministers, and the autonomy of churches.

## Law Suit

In October 1999, a suit was filed in the Commonwealth of Virginia, *Andrew S. Robinson v. the Presbyterian Church in America*. (See Board of Directors Report, p. 357 ff.)

## Corrections

- Previous Assembly Minutes. See Part IV, page 613.
- Book of Church Order. Some 1997 revisions of the BCO contain a section 6-5 regarding a minimum voting age. That proposed amendment was not approved by the 1997 General Assembly and therefore is not part of the BCO. That error was corrected in 1998 but some people who have not gotten newer editions are unaware of that.
- Westminster Standards. We will publish the Westminster Standards again soon. Several matters have come to our attention;
- The following words were inadvertently omitted from the 1996 edition of the Westminster Larger Catechism, Answer 96 following “or” and preceding “inexcusable”: upon their continuance in the estate and way of sin, to leave them. So the section should read, “or upon their continuance in the estate and way of sin, to leave them inexcusable.”
- Footnote 11 in WSC Answer 4, incorrectly cites Psalm 117:5 as the proof text. It should be Psalm 147:5. There is no Psalm 117:5
- Our present reading of WCF XXII.3 is taken from the 1936 version of the Orthodox Presbyterian Church, not the original British version. The original British version’s final sentence was, “Yet, it is a sin to refuse an oath touching anything that is good and just, being imposed by lawful authority.” The original British version’s final sentence was deleted in the 1936 version of the Orthodox Presbyterian Church, copied by the PCA. That fact is not mentioned in “Preface” as presently printed. That fact will be mentioned in the new printing of the “Preface.”
- The present version of WCF XXIV.4 regarding consanguinity (marrying one’s wife’s kindred) is not a change made in the American version of 1789. It was a change made in the OPC version of 1936. The “Preface” incorrectly mentions it as a change made in the American version of 1789. That error will be corrected in the “Preface” of the new printing.

## Advice of the Committee on Constitutional Business to the Clerk

I have asked the advice of the CCB on several matters. The CCB met and offered the following advice in accordance with “RAO” 7-2 (1):

**Issue 1.** - I have not brought to the Assembly communications from individuals who have other means of access through the lower courts of the PCA (“RAO” 10-1; 10-2).

**Advice 1** — The Committee concurs with the conclusion of the Stated Clerk in regard to his response to communications from individuals. It further notes that

<a id="ga28-p351"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=353 printed_page=351 -->

language in “RAO” 10-2 clarifying the circumstances under which communications may (or may not) be received from individuals would be helpful.

Issue 2. I have advised churches contemplating a situation in which Presbytery bounds may change that when a church becomes a member of a different presbytery, the teaching elders and ruling elders of the church would need to be examined and approved by the new presbytery.

Advice 2 – It is the advice of the Committee that churches must be members of the Presbytery within whose geographical bounds they reside unless the General Assembly makes explicit provisions to the contrary (e.g., boundary counties (*Digest* vol. 1, p. 214), Korean Language Presbyteries (*Digest* vol. 1, p. 219)). In any case, in which a church becomes a member of a different presbytery, the teaching elders and ruling elders of the church would need to be examined and approved by the new presbytery.

Issue 3. My procedure in the case of resignations or deaths of General Assembly Committee members has been and is to inform the alternate that he is to fill the remainder of the term of the member who died or resigned. I informed the Nominating Committee of that action. I base my procedure on,

- *BCO* 14-1-12, “Persons who have served for a full term, or for at least two years of a partial term, on one of the Assembly’s permanent committees or agencies shall not be eligible for re-election to an Assembly committee until one year has elapsed. (Exceptions may be permitted in agency bylaws approved by the Assembly).

- The citation of *BCO* 14-1-12 in RAO 4-5.

- The term “at least two years” would make no sense, if an alternate could only serve until the next Assembly when filling a vacancy.

Advice 3 – It is the advice of the Committee that there is no clear definition in the *BCO*, “RAO”, or *Robert’s Rules* as to whether an alternate committee member filling a vacancy serves only until the next meeting of the General Assembly or until the end of the un-expired term. In light of that lack of clarity, we advise the Stated Clerk that he should continue the apparently long-standing precedent of having the alternate serve out the un-expired term.

Issue 4. I have received an Overture from Westminster Presbytery. It deals with Covenant College, yet the Presbytery requests that it be sent to the Bills and Overtures Committee. It is my intention to refer this Overture to the Committee of Commissioners on Covenant College in accordance with “*RAO*” 3-2, g.; 10-5 because it deals directly with a matter related to Covenant College. At the time of the receipt of communications someone from Westminster Presbytery could move that the matter be sent to the Bills and Overtures Committee, and let the Assembly decide. Moreover, the matter would come up through the Report of the Committee of Commissioners on Covenant College

<a id="ga28-p352"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=354 printed_page=352 -->

and would fulfill the Presbytery’s request “that this overture be considered at the
time the proposed response to the protest comes to the floor of the Assembly.”
Advice 4 - it is the advice of the committee that “RAO” 13-1 states, “the
committee of commissioners on covenant colleges hall handle the
matters, touching Covenant College.” As the “RAO” may be suspended only
by vote of the General Assembly (“RAO” XVIII), a request of a presbytery fora
particular referral may not supersede the “RAO” provision. Thus, the matter
should be referred to the committee of commissioners on covenant college
unless the General Assembly votes to suspend this provision of the “RAO” .

New Churches Added or Lost since the 27th General Assembly
This information is recorded at 28-10, page 48 and 49.

Presbytery Votes on BCO Amendments
Thirty-nine (39) Presbyteries must approve a proposed amendment to the BCO
(.BCO 26-2). [Text and action at 28-12, p 53 ]

Statistics
the following statistics give us some idea regarding the condition of the PCA
insofar a scan be determined from the data reported a majority of churches submitted
annual reports. Some churches, however, did not report at all and some churches
submitted partial reports. Note that in addition to the 5,620 adult professions of faith
reported in 1999, that 4,307 covenant children made profession of faith a swell fora
total of 9,927 professions of faith reported in 1999. The total reported membership
increase was 9,149, a 3.16% annual growth rate. Increases were recorded in most
categories with the exceptions being, the number of mission churches (-21), the number
of ministerial candidates (-15), and the amount contributed to General Assembly
Ministries (down $646,206). Major increases reported were: Total Contributions
(+$54,975,217), Presbytery Causes (+$1,140,657), Congregational Benevolences
($23,472,024), Total Benevolences (+$24,049,012), and Total Disbursements
($36,574,800). some of the major congregational contribution increases are due to
more complete reporting of data. the PCA continues to be one of the fastest growing
denominations in north america an done of the most generous, for which we thank
God.

Report of the Administrative Committee to the 28th
General Assembly of the
Presbyterian Church in America
June, 2000

I. Meetings
October 1, 1999
March 17, 2000
the committee also meets during the week of the assembly

II. Summary of the Actions of the Board of Directors
1. approved the articles of amendment of mission to the world (PCA),
Inc., “ARTICLE VII., LIQUIDATION, Section 2. Liquidation.”
2. Noted that the annual corporate filings for RPCES corporations have
been accomplished in a timely manner
3. Noted that the annual corporate filings have been accomplished in a
timely manner in all states where the corporation is registered to conduct
business. [Note: The Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation)
is currently registered as a foreign corporation in Georgia, Missouri and
M ississippi]
4. approved a motion that the officers of the Presbyterian Church in America, (A Corporation) be authorized to give a letter of intent to any
property owner after negotiating a fair-market price for the proposed
office sites with the proviso that this letter of intent requires no escrow
funds or financial obligations at this point in time. If these proposals
were acted upon favorably by the General Assembly, the Presbyterian Church in America, (A Corporation) would act in good faith in
accordance with the letter of intent. (See “III. Office Building” below).
5. received a report that a lawsuit was filed in the commonwealth of
Virginia against the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation)
and Mission to the World, Inc. and is still pending at this time.
Approved a motion that the information regarding the lawsuit be
received as information and that the staff be directed to seek a Biblical
means of resolving the suit within the church

III. PCA Office Building
The 26th General Assembly (1998) directed the AC, MTW and the then-existing
Capital Building Fund Committee to seek an “amicable solution” to the
concerns about the suitability of the present PCA office Building. A Building
Management Committee (BMC) was formed in accordance with the original
proposal for building operations concomitant with the purchase of the building.

<a id="ga28-p359"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=361 printed_page=359 -->

The BMC reports to the AC. All Committees and Agencies that have offices in the PCA Office Building are represented on the BMC and participate in the decision-making process regarding the operation of the building. The formation and functioning of the BMC was the “amicable” aspect. By the providence of God, supplying funds through God’s people, we were able to pay off the mortgage on the present building at the end of 1998. In so doing, we saved $90,000 in interest and were able to lower the rent for PCA Committees and Agencies. Concerns regarding the long-term suitability of the present Office Building were considered by the BMC since the last General Assembly. These concerns are basically: (1) Employees hired in recent years are not able to afford to purchase suitable housing within reasonable commuting time and distance from the building, (2) Because of housing costs and commuting time and distance, it has been increasingly difficult to hire new employees, (3) Employee productivity is hampered due to the inadequacies of the HVAC system in providing a pleasant work environment (particularly on the second floor) and due to commuting time and distance. The BMC gave its blessing to MTW’s investigating office sites for MTW in other cities as well as Atlanta. MTW engaged the Staubach Company as consultants to evaluate a number of cities. Staubach recommended that the Greater Atlanta Area is the best location. The BMC decided that all Atlanta-based Committees and Agencies seek to remain together in a common location, engaged the Staubach Company as consultants to evaluate an number of sites, and directed Staubach to concentrate the search in the northeast quadrant of metro Atlanta in the Sugarloaf Parkway vicinity of Interstate Highway 85, approximately seventeen miles northeast of the present building. The Staubach Company presented a short list of sites to the BMC. The BMC approved a motion that the decision be made by the AC for recommendation to the General Assembly. The AC unanimously approved a recommendation and enabling motions that the General Assembly authorize the sale of the present building and build-to-suit new office facilities. (See “Recommendations” XI-1-8 below). We believe this is the “solution” aspect of the “amicable solution.” There have been concerns within the PCA about the original purchase, financing, and operation of the PCA Office Building. We do not want to put the denomination through renewed stress on this issue. We believe that the build-to-suit option for more suitable facilities is a wise solution for our office building needs for the next couple of decades. We think that office relocation is financially feasible and wise stewardship. (See attachment for additional information).

IV. Strategic Planning

The Administrative Committee is unique in that all General Assembly Committees and Agencies are represented on the Committee (BCO 14-1-12) and “the Administrative Committee shall function as a service committee to the General Assembly and the denomination” (RAO 4-2). There has been no unified, coordinated strategic planning for General Assembly Ministries that was ever actually implemented. A cooperative climate of ministry exists among the

<a id="ga28-p360"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=362 printed_page=360 -->

Committees and Agencies. We recommend that strategic planning be done under the aegis of the AC involving a broadly representative twenty-four-person Strategic Planning Steering Committee (approved by the AC¹) and the nine coordinators working under the Steering Committee, with the Steering Committee reporting to the AC and the AC bringing a final recommendation to the 30ᵗʰ General Assembly. The required funding would be secured outside the AC budget. (See “Recommendations” XI-10 below).

V. News Office &amp; PCANews.Com

The General Assembly has delegated the operation of the PCA News Office to the AC. Heretofore we have not had a full-time PCA News Officer or PCA News Service at present. Functions of the News Office have been carried out on a part-time basis by Bob Fiol during the year and Brian Kinney during the Assembly week. After “The PCA Messenger” was discontinued in 1994, the General Assembly delegated to the AC the responsibility of developing the PCA News Office. We have not begun another magazine heretofore because of fiscal exigencies. We believe we can now effectively resume the accurate and timely publication of the story of the PCA by means of an Internet magazine, “PCANews.com,” which we intend to launch at the General Assembly. PCANews.com will include many items of interest including, PCA news (updated six days per week, Monday through Saturday), feature articles, news of special interest to pastors, missions updates, educational ministries, outreach ministries, columnists, editorials and letters, debates, the marketplace (classified ads and job opportunities), and a number of helpful resources such as prayer requests, devotional materials, ministry materials for families, youth, and children as well as book reviews. Dr. Dominic Aquila will be serving as the new PCA News Officer and Editor of PCANews.com, subject to his approval by the Theological Examining Committee.

VI. Financial Information

There is good news on the financial front. The Lord blessed the AC financially in 1999. The fiscal year started with a Fund Balance Deficit of $42,460 and an indebtedness of $125,000 carried over from 1998. The undesignated contributions increased in ‘99 over ’98 by approximately $100,000. For this, we are very grateful to our gracious Lord and to our PCA constituency for their very kind support. Also, cost savings measures were employed wherever reasonable, resulting in a net operating surplus for 1999 of $72,612. As of this writing, our

¹ The AC approved a motion that the following list of names be approved as comprising the Strategic Planning Steering Committee:
Ruling Elders: Harry Pinner, Richard Hostetter, Sam Duncan, Mike Wilson, Joel Belz, Mark Belz, Dick Ellingsworth, Bob Massengill, Jack Williamson, Tom Harris. Alternates: Jim Seneff, John White, John Spanier, and James Bebo Elkin.
Teaching Elders: Will Barker, Joseph Wheat, Tim Keller, Dave Clelland, Samuel Park, Frank Barker, Ligon Duncan, Ric Cannada, Fred Harrell, Mike Campbell. Alternates: Rob Rayburn, Wilson Benton, Dan Doriani, and Wayne Herring.
Women: Kay James, Paige Benton, Susan Hunt and Barbara Thompson.

<a id="ga28-p361"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=363 printed_page=361 -->

debt of $125,000 has been reduced to $15,000 and our Fund Balance is again a surplus. We are glad for God’s favor to us in this way and ask you to pray that this positive financial trend would continue.

## VII. Development

In 1999, the AC has been involved in a serious effort to inform the churches of our ongoing ministry and of the related financial costs. In our fourth-quarter development efforts AC members and staff called or visited churches. Individualized requests were made to PCA churches. We have had encouraging responses. Some churches that had discontinued supporting the AC have renewed their support. Some churches that have never supported the AC have begun to support the AC for the first time. Some churches have increased their support. Some churches have committed to support the AC beyond the level of Partnership Shares. By God’s blessing, we have enjoyed some improvement. However, we are not yet where we should be. Contributions which should be over $900,000 were $660,000. Statistically, those churches contributing all or a portion of the Partnership Share to AC was only 47.8% (683 churches out of 1430.) Of this number those giving at the 75% or more level was only 27.4% or 393 churches. 747 churches (52.2%) made no contribution to the AC in 1999. Lord willing, we will assertively continue our efforts to inform our PCA constituency of the AC ministry and to encourage all of our churches to support generously this work.

## VIII. Conflict Resolution

As we continue to seek to become a Church holy before God, the manner in which we respond to sin and conflicts in the body, and gently shepherd those caught in sin, will reflect our commitment to the authority of God’s Word and the spirit of love which should define all of our actions and relationships. We believe biblical peacemaking (conciliation) is one of God’s highest priorities; therefore, it must also be one of our highest priorities.

The BCO reflects our commitment to following God’s procedure for reclaiming those going astray. Our desire is to provide guidance through the steps of biblical peacemaking required as pre-conditions to judicial process in cases of personal offense and for use whenever possible in cases of general offense.

The Administrative Committee (October 99) adopted, in concept, as a broad statement of principle and general guidance “Biblical Conflict Resolution” (see attachment) to be incorporated later, when perfected and approved, as an appendix in the BCO (for information only, not part of the constitution). Further, that the Stated Clerk’s Office pursue with Peacemaker Ministries® a proposal to develop a plan to train designated representative from the PCA so that we may indeed become a Church holy before God.

<a id="ga28-p362"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=364 printed_page=362 -->

IX. Significant Actions Taken
1. Received progress reports concerning the PCA Historical Center.
2. approved recommendations for the setting of the docket of the General Assembly.
3. Directed the AC Staff to pursue with Peacemakers Ministries® a
proposal to develop a plan to train designated representatives from the
PCA so that we may indeed become a more holy Church under God.
4. adopted a recommendation to the General Assembly as a response
(including a copy of “An address to all churches of jesus christ
throughout the World from the First General Assembly”) to a
communication (Communication 3, “Admonitions from the Bible
Presbyterian Church”) from the Bible Presbyterian Church (See
“Recommendations,” X I-11 below.

<a id="ga28-p363"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=365 printed_page=363 -->

5. Adopted a policy, that all PCA churches and Presbyteries who wish to have their official primary websites listed as links to the PCA website may do so by notifying the Office of the Stated Clerk.

6. Approved speakers for General Assembly services to be listed in the docket.

7. Approved a recommendation that **Overture 14** from New Jersey Presbytery, “Erect Committee to Develop Funding of General Assembly,” be answered in the negative on the grounds 1) the overture does not conform to the requirements of RAO 8-4 specifying a method of funding for an *ad hoc* committee, (2) the responsibility of recommending fees for General Assembly registration is clearly delegated to the AC (RAO 9-1, 9-4), and (3) the AC is best equipped to consider the overall picture of the many factors in determining the fee. See Recommendations XI-12 below.

8. Approved a recommendation that the Committees and Agencies who will participate in the new office construction project notify the Building Management Committee regarding their intentions by June 1, 2000.

9. Approved a recommendation that the officers of the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) be authorized to give a letter of intent to any property owner after negotiating a fair-market price for the proposed office sites with the proviso that this letter of intent requires no escrow funds or financial obligations at this point in time. If these proposals are acted upon favorably by the General Assembly, the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) would act in good faith in accordance with the letter of intent.

10. Approved a recommendation that the AC Staff be permitted to begin marketing the present PCA Office Building, 1852 Century Place, Atlanta, Georgia, with the understanding that no final action [sale and/or closing] would occur without the approval and authorization of the General Assembly in June 2000.

11. Evaluated the Stated Clerk as required by RAO 3-3.

12. The AC commended the MTW coordinator, staff, and committee for their connectional and brotherly spirit and generosity in the matter of enabling and funding of the acquisition of property and construction of the new offices.

13. Approved a recommendation that the entire staff of the AC [Roy Taylor, John Robertson, Bob Fiol and Laurel De Bert in particular, but also Peggy Little, Monica Johnston, Amy Park, Susan Cullen and Mary Bradley Bayne] be commended for their extraordinary efforts, negotiations and arrangements on behalf of the Administrative Committee in the accomplishments of its duties.

14. Appointed the following members to the PCA Historical Center Subcommittee, with gratitude to the Lord: David Calhoun, Mrs. Shirley Duncan, Miss Lannae Graham, Edward Harris, Donald J. MacNair,

<a id="ga28-p364"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=366 printed_page=364 -->

Fredric Mau, John Spencer, (Melton Duncan, alternate); ex-officio
members: John Robertson and L. Roy Taylor.
15. Adopted standards for General Assembly Commissioner registration
refunds.
16. Approved new exhibitors at the General Assembly.
17. approved procedures for approval of advertisers on PCANews.com

#### X. Recommendations

1 That the 28th General Assembly authorize the fair-market-value sale of the PCA
Office Building located at 1852 Century Place, Atlanta, Georgia 30345.
[NOTE: the verbal and hand votes at the AC meeting both indicated a
unanimous agreement of the AC at-large committee members and the other
Committees' and Agencies ’ representatives on the AC],
2. That $710,000 of the proceeds of the sale be returned to mission to the World,
previously received from MTW as a conditional gift to be repaid upon the sale
of the PCA office building located at 1852 Century Place, Atlanta, Georgia
30345. [SeeM 27G ^, pp. 237-238 ]
3. that the remaining proceeds of the sale after payment of $710,000 to Mission to
the World be distributed among the PCA Committees and Agencies having
offices in the PCA Office Building. such distribution of remaining proceeds is
to be made in accordance with the square footage of offices pace used by each
respective Committee or Agency in 1999, namely 8.72% to AC; 17.60% to
CE&P; 6.10% to IAR; 21.21% to MNA, 42.04% to MTW; and 4.33% to PCAF.
4. That the PCA Committees and Agencies presently occupying the PCA Office
Building be authorized by the General Assembly to use their respective equities
from the sale of the PCA office building to secure office facilities for the
Atlanta-based Committees and Agencies. The land and building to be occupied
by MTW would be deeded to and owned by Mission to the World (PCA), Inc.
The land and building occupied by all the other Atlanta-based committees and
agencies would be deeded to and owned by the Presbyterian Church in America
(A Corporation).
5. That the PCA Committees and Agencies now occupying offices in the PCA
Office Building who wish to construct and purchase office facilities in the
Greater Atlanta Area be allowed to work together to purchase new property
adequate to build two one-story buildings with a total of approximately 44,000
square feet and move together to the new site.
6. That the PCA General Assembly Ministries planning to operate in the new
facilities be allowed to initiate jointly a Capital Campaign to help fund this
project. the capital campaign will include the help of professional consultation
A feasibility study will be made before embarking on the campaign to assure its
success.
7. That the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) be allowed to
mortgage the new property of the Presbyterian Church in America (A
Corporation) (not the property of mission to the world (PCA), Inc.) for a

<a id="ga28-p365"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=367 printed_page=365 -->

maximum of $2,000,000 in order to finance the property/facility to be used
potentially by AC, MNA, CE&P, IAR, and/or PCAF.
8. That permission be granted that a contract(s), which could bring the best
benefits of all laws and regulations (federal, state and local) to the user (the PCA
ministries and corporations) and the provider (the present owners) be negotiated
with the advice of real-estate, legal, and accounting professionals. Such contract
could involve land contributions, leases, “lease to buy” or other instruments,
which would achieve the legitimate ends of both the PCA and the current owner
of any property being considered for PCA usage. As the property and related
contract(s) will involve both the Presbyterian Church in America and Mission to
The World (PCA), Inc., the contract(s) ultimately are to be approved by the
board of directors of the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) and
the board of directors of mission to the world (PCA), Inc. It is understood
that the PCA interest is to own its property eventually and no contract(s) will be
entered into which does not grant the PCA corporations the option to do so.
9. That the following recommendation regarding CEO Compensation and
Guideline for 2001 be approved as represented by the schedule and notes below.
The increase over 2000 is calculated at 3% (three percent).

10. That a nine-member Strategic Planning Committee for the PCA General Assembly Ministries, in the context of their service to the denomination as a
whole, be appointed by the AC [BCO 14-1(12), RAO 4-2] with Frank Brock,

plans as they develop before the plans are presented to the AC by the Steering

former denomination in 1973 to have been a tragic necessity. The Presbyterian Church in America was formed as a result of “long years of struggle and

picture of the many factors in determining the fee.

ATTACHMENT 1
BUILDING OPTIONS &amp; COSTS
Prepared by Roy Taylor and John Robertson

The Building Management Committee has been considering several options regarding the best stewardship of financial and building resources for the PCA in light of the age of the present PCA Office Building (18 years old), the condition of the building and its equipment, the likelihood of major expenditures in the next five years, the location of the building, residual value of the building, and how these factors relate to ministry of the Committees and Agencies operating out of the building. Since the 1999, General Assembly the BMC gave their support to MTW’s seeking a new location for MTW’s offices. MTW used the Staubach Company’s consultative services to consider a number of cities. Staubach’s study concluded that MTW should locate in the northeast quadrant of the Greater Atlanta Area (the Sugarloaf Parkway region near I-85N) rather than the present location. The other committees and agencies then decided to try to remain together at a common site. The BMC asked the Staubach Co. to locate suitable sites in the Greater Atlanta Area. About a dozen sites were located in the northeast quadrant. The short-list search was concentrated in the general area of Sugarloaf Parkway in Gwinnett County near I-85N at the request of the BMC. The Staubach Co. completed its short-list report January 25, 2000. The BMC met February 8 to act on the report and then passed the following motion:

While it is not necessary that we (the Atlanta-based PCA committees and agencies) locate to a common area, we are a denomination and we believe it is best that we stay together; therefore we, the Building Management Committee, request the General Assembly, through its Board of Directors and/or Administrative Committee take whatever steps necessary to secure a suitable location for the PCA denominational offices that will house the Atlanta-based committees and agencies in one common office location.

There have been concerns within the PCA about the purchase, financing, and operation of the PCA Office Building. We do not want to put the denomination through renewed stress on this issue. We believe the recommendation we will present will be a wise solution for our office building needs for the next couple of decades. We believe that we have received sufficient information to move forward with making a decision. Following our usual procedure of the AC staff making studied recommendations to the committee for action; we present to the AC the following evaluation of the options available and a recommendation. We evaluate these options according to four factors:

1. Cost Factor - The financial outlay required and means of financing for each option.
2. Quality of Life Factor - This includes such items as commute time for employees, commute costs, and costs of housing within a reasonable commute to the office site. Housing costs near the present building are high. Small “starter” homes are in the $200,000 range. Town homes are in the $300,000-

<a id="ga28-p369"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=371 printed_page=369 -->

$350,000 range Suburban home prices are lower and homes are more
affordable for our people. Therefore, some employees commute two hours or
more per day
3 Staff Recruitment Factor - How the office location relates to staff recruitment,
particularly highly qualified mid-level management personnel within the PCA
salary structure. th equality of life factors greatly influence staff recruitment
Recent staff recruitment efforts for positions paying $24,000 to $60,000 have
taken over six months due, in large part, to the present location of the PCA
Office Building
4. Ministry Productivity Factor - the inadequacy of the Heating, Ventilation,
and Air Conditioning system (HVAC) in providing a useable workplace, a
pleasant work environment, th equality of life factor, and the staff recruitment
factor all contribute to ministry productivity. Our present office situation
adversely affects ministry productivity, particularly for Committees located on
the second floor due to the inadequate HVAC.

The present rental rate for PCA committees and agencies is $14 00/SF through
2001. MTW has prepaid its rent through 2001. In 2002, the rental rate would change.

Option One: Maintain Status Quo
• Action - Do nothing until required to do so by equipment failure (HVAC and/or
electrical, etc.).
• Costs - $500,000 to $1,800,000. This would far exceed BMC reserves. A
mortgage would have to be secured to cover costs. Rental costs would need to be in
the $ 1 1 00-to-$16.00/SF range to pay off an 8% mortgage over ten years.
• Evaluation - We do not recommend this option.
1. Though this may be the cheapest option, the lowest cost ($500,000) far exceeds
the BMC reserves. This is an unrealistically optimistic option that is unlikely to
occur. A mortgage would have to be secured to pay the costs and debt
retirement would have to be added to the rent. Increased rent could approach
the build-to-suit option costs.
2. th equality of life factor would not be addressed.
3. The staff recruitment difficulty would remain the same.
4. A major reason for leaving the present building is that ministry productivity is
hampered for committees on the second floor (MNA & MTW). Due to the
inadequacy of th eh v ac system, it is not presently possible sufficiently to cool
the second floor in the summer and heat it in the winter.

Option Two: refurbishing of present building
• Action - Refurbish the present PCA Office Building.
• Costs - (Attachment A). Increase rental costs to $16.00/SF to pay off an 8%
mortgage over ten years in an estimated amount of $2,000,000.00 and cover
operating costs.
• Evaluation - We do not recommend this option.

<a id="ga28-p370"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=372 printed_page=370 -->

1. A mortgage would have to be secured. Rental increases to pay off the mortgage would be similar to the build-to-suit option costs.
2. The quality of life factor would not be addressed.
3. The staff recruitment difficulty would remain the same.
4. There would be a disruption of ministries by moving offices within the building or moving them outside the building while refurbishing is being done, and then moving the offices to their new positions within the building after refurbishing is completed. Ministry productivity would be hampered.

## Option Three: Leasing

- Action - Sell the present building, distribute equity to committees and agencies. Each committee and agency separately or together could lease office space on the open market.
- Costs - $15.50 to $22.50/SF leasing cost.
- Evaluation - We do not recommend this option.

1. About $8,100,000 would be spent over ten years without gaining any equity. Leasing Costs would equal or exceed the build-to-suit option.
2. The location of the building would need to be farther northeast to deal with the quality of life factor.
3. A location in Gwinnett County would be more favorable to improve staff recruitment.
4. The location of the building nearer the pool of present and future personnel would improve ministry productivity due to improved quality of life and better personnel recruitment.

## Option Four: Buy an Existing Building

- Sell the present building and buy another existing building.
- Costs - This is the most difficult of the options to pin down to a SF figure due to the many unknown factors. A reasonable estimate is in the range as mid-level refurbishing of present building ($23.39/SF rent and mortgage, $2,709,722, $64.52/SF construction)
- Evaluation - We do not recommend this option. Finding a suitable used building would be a needle-in-a-haystack discovery.

1. A used building may not be as cost effective to operate over the next decade as a new building would be. We would not likely receive the $1,500,000 initial capital infusion for buying an existing structure, if it were not at least 17 miles NE of our present location. There would likely be reconfiguration costs. The reasonably estimated costs would exceed the costs of the build-to-suit option.
2. If the building were not located significantly outside the perimeter (I-285), the quality of life problems would not have been solved.
3. A location in Gwinnett County would be more favorable to improve staff recruitment possibilities.
4. The location of the building nearer the pool of present and future personnel would improve ministry productivity due to improved quality of life and better personnel recruitment.

<a id="ga28-p371"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=373 printed_page=371 -->

Option Five: Build to Suit
• Action - Sell the existing building, use equity to build to suit in the Sugarloaf
Parkway area, MTW makes initial financial input of $1,500,000, a capital campaign
of approximately $ 1,000,000 may be conducted, and a mortgage secured for
$1,050,000 to a maximum of $1,600,000. The Presbyterian Church in America (A
Corporation) would own and operate a 24,000 sf building adjacent to a 20,000 SF
building that Mission to the World (PCA), Inc. would own and operate in a campus
arrangement.
• Costs - total costs of construction and occupancy two one-story buildings (20,000
and 24,000 SF) about $6,140,000 less proceeds from sale of present building (Low
$2,171,400, Medium $2,590,000, High $3,158,400). Net costs = $3,968,600,
$3,550,000, or $2,981,600 depending on proceeds of sale of present building
Operating costs of $5.50/SF plus mortgage payment of $8.98 to $9.10/SF over a
seven, ten, or seventeen year period, depending on amount and length of mortgage
@ 8% = $14.48 to $14.60/SF or rounded up to $15.00/SF
• Evaluation - We strongly recommend this option
1 We can do this near the present rent rate ($14.00/SF). it isles s expensive to
build two one-story buildings than one two-story building We would be
gaining equity in an asset over the years.
2 An office location in the Sugarloaf Parkway area near I-85N will deal
successfully with th equality of life issues.
3. An office location in the Sugarloaf Parkway area near I-85N will give the PCA
Committees and Agencies improved opportunities to recruit employees,
particularly mid-level management personnel.
4 because th equality of life issues and staff recruitment issues would have been
improved, ministry productivity would increase Moreover, the build to suit
option provides the least disruptions to PCA ministries. Offices built to suit the
PCA Committees and Agencies would be designed to fit the unique usage
factors of the PCA Ministries.

Recommendation
We strongly recommend the Build-to-Suit Option for the reasons stated above
Having all the Atlanta-based committees and agencies together on a two-building
campus would demonstrate unity and provide convenience for people coming to the
offices The Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) and Mission to the World
(PCA), inc would have oversight of their own office properties the PCA was begun
based on voluntary cooperation rather than coercion. We (the AC staff) believe that it
would be good that all committees and agencies choose to move together to the same
campus.

<a id="ga28-p372"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=374 printed_page=372 -->

ATTACHMENT 2
BIBLICAL CONFLICT RESOLUTION
[WORKING DRAFT)

Preface
As we continue to seek to become a church holy before God, the manner in which
we respond to sin and conflicts in the body, and gently shepherd those caught in sin, will
reflect our commitment to the authority of God’s Word and the spirit of love which should
define all of our actions and relationships. Biblical peacemaking is one of God’s highest
priorities (Matt. 5:23-24, Rom. 12:18; Gal.6:l); therefore, it must be one of our highest
priorities. the Book of Church Order (BCO) reflects our commitment to following God’s
procedure for reclaiming those going astray. It says, “Scriptural law is the basis of all
discipline because it is the revelation of God’s Holy will Proper disciplinary principles are
set forth in the Scriptures and must be followed” (BCO 27-5). It also says, “An injured
party shall not become a prosecutor of personal offenses without having tried the means of
reconciliation and of reclaiming the offender, required by Christ” (BCO 31-5). The purpose
of this appendix is to provide guidance through the steps of biblical peacemaking required
as pre-conditions to judicial process in cases of personal offense (BCO 31-5), and for use
whenever possible in cases of general offense (BCO 31-7).

Each presbytery should endeavor to have several elders trained in the methods of
“Christian conciliation” (including mediation and arbitration), and available to serve as
Christian conciliators in cases that could and should be resolved privately before judicial
process is initiated. We would do well to follow the wisdom of those who have considered
the importance of this matter For example, Elder Edmond Clowney states, “Discipline. ..is
not first an exercise of negative judgment, a matter of church courts and censures. It begins
with the care of friends with whom we strive to follow Christ” (Clowney, E.P., The Church,
IVP, 1995).

The Biblical Steps of Discipline as they Relate to Biblical Peacemaking

BCO 27-5 outlines the proper principles for the exercise of church discipline. This
BCO section emphasizes that the steps indicated, (a) through (d), must be followed in
proper order. The steps are:

- **a)** Instruction in the Word;
- **b)** Individuals’ responsibility to admonish one another (Matt 18:15; Gal. 6:1);
- **c)** If the admonition is rejected, then the calling of one or more witnesses (Matt.

18:16);
d) If rejection persists, then the church must act through her court unto
admonition, suspension, excommunication and deposition.
This appendix addresses the first three steps, (a) through (c), by providing an expanded
discussion of the implications raised by Scriptural direction for a complete process at each
step Step (d), formal church discipline, is addressed by Part II of the Book of Church Order, The Rules of Discipline.

<a id="ga28-p373"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=375 printed_page=373 -->

1. The first step - Instruction in the Word.
Many Christians do not fully understand the emphasis God places on living at peace
and in unity with others ( Eph. 4: 3). Preaching and teaching should regularly address this
emphasis and the purposes for church discipline as set forth in BCO 27-3 and BCO 27-4
Attention should be drawn to the fact that the main procedural passage related to church
discipline, Matthew 18:15-20, is set forth in the context of two powerful parables teaching
the extent and depth of God’s love in reclaiming those who have strayed (Matt 18 :10-14),
and the vast measure of His forgiveness and the expansive forgiveness He expects from His
children toward one another (Matt. 18:21-35). The extent of instruction on reconciliation
provided to church members in “new member classes” and regular preaching from the
pulpit will have a significant bearing on the extent to which a church member understands
the benefits of church discipline and has consented to ecclesiastical jurisdiction (which is
helpful for the church if it is to avoid legal liability for the proper exercise of church
discipline).

Teaching God’s Word concerning sin and conflicts that result from it, and how
Christians should respond biblically to conflict, will equip church members to become
peacemakers themselves. Peacemaking has always been one of a Christian's most
important ministries. as we are reminded in 2 Corinthians 5:18, God “reconciled us to
himself through Christ and gave us the ministry of reconciliation ” One of the most
powerful ways that we can encourage reconciliation with God is to model reconciliation
among people. When others see us resolve our differences in a loving and biblical manner,
they are inclined to give more weight to what we say about the Lord (John 13:34-35; 17:20-
23). On the other hand, when they see Christians embroiled in disputes, they tend to write
God’s people off as hypocrites and dismiss the claims of Christ (Rom 2:21-24; 1 Cor 6:1-
8 ).

the bible sets forth a process for resolving personal and substantive disputes ina
constructive manner. This process is sometimes referred to as “Christian conciliation” and
it may be used to resolve everything from minor personal differences to church divisions or
lawsuits involving church members. As indicated in passages such as Proverbs 19:11,
Matthew 5:23-25 and 18:15-20, 1 Corinthians 6:1-8, and Galatians 6:1, conciliation
involves three basic steps: (1) when Christians are involved in a conflict that is too serious
to overlook, the first thing that they should do is meet together privately and in person to
try to resolve their differences; (2) if this effort is unsuccessful, they should ask one or more
other Christians to meet with them and help them to seek reconciliation and a voluntary
settlement of their differences (a process sometimes referred to as mediation); and (3) if
they cannot arrive at a voluntary settlement, they should ask one or more other Christians to
hear both sides of the matter and render a biblically based decision that both sides are
obligated to accept (a process sometimes referred to as arbitration).

There are many benefits to resolving disputes through Christian conciliation. It
prevents a public quarrel that would dishonor the Lord Jesus Christ and diminish the
credibility and witness of His church. Conciliation also allows Christians to demonstrate
their faith in Christ and their confidence in His teachings (John 13:34; 14:15; 17:20-23). In
addition, conciliation encourages forgiveness and promotes reconciliation, which can help

<a id="ga28-p374"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=376 printed_page=374 -->

to preserve valuable relationships and strengthen the church (Eph. 4:29-32; Col. 3:12-17).
Conciliation also helps people to identify and deal with the root causes of conflict, which
may include such things as pride, selfishness, fear, vengeance, greed, bitterness, or
unforgiveness (see Matt 7:3-5). This allows people to make changes in their lives so that
they will enjoy more peaceful relationships in the future (Eph 4:1-3, 22-24).

Christian conciliation is especially beneficial for people who sincerely want to do
what is right and are open to learning where they have been wrong (Prov. 15:31-32).
Conciliators can help them to identify improper attitudes or unwise practices, to understand
more fully the effects of their decision and actions, and to make improvements in their lives
that will help them to honor and serve the Lord more effectively in the future (1 Peter 2:12).
Teaching and Ruling elders are encouraged to learn all they can about Christian
conciliation and develop local ministries that will equip members under their care to
respond to conflicts in a manner that reflects the power of Christ working in their lives. The
more church members understand these concepts and benefits, the more likely they will be
to apply biblical principles when conflicts occur.

2. The second step - An individual's responsibility to admonish another (Matt
18:15, Gal. 6:1). scripture warns against making a premature judgment about a matter (
Matt. 7:1-5). By first going personally and in private to those who have offended, we
provide the opportunity for clarification of misunderstandings and avoid premature
judgment This step includes the three responses of personal peacemaking as follows:

Overlook an offense: An individual may overlook an offense of another if it is minor in
nature aid it has not significantly dishonored God, damaged a personal relationship, or hurt
other people (including the offender) “A man’s wisdom gives him patience; it is to his
glory to overlook an offense” (Prov 19:11).

Discussion: When personal wrongs are too serious to overlook, the parties are required to
seek to resolve them privately through loving confrontation and confession “If your
brother has something against you ...go and be reconciled” (Matt. 5:23-24). “If your brother
sins against you, go and show him his fault, just between the two of you” (Matt. 18:15).

Negotiation: When a substantive issue related to money, property, or other material issues
or rights divides Christians, a process of biblical negotiation should be followed that will
meet the interests of all those involved “Each of you should look not only to your own
interests, but also to the interests of others” (Phil. 2:4).

Privately responding to conflict early, before it escalates, through the means of
these three forms of personal peacemaking will often result in reconciliation and no need
for further peacemaking attention. Successful personal peacemaking is usually based on at
least one parties’ willingness to accept responsibility for how they have contributed to the
conflict and by being willing to humble themselves for the greater goal of God’s glory
through “making every effort” (Eph. 4:3) to keep the unity of the spirit.

3. The third step - If private admonition is rejected, then calling of one or more
witnesses (Matt 18:16; 1 Cor. 6:1-9). involving others is a serious escalation of a conflict

<a id="ga28-p375"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=377 printed_page=375 -->

and should be considered only after the elements of step two have been fully exhausted
Step three includes the three assisted peacemaking responses as follows:

Mediation: If a dispute cannot be resolved through personal peacemaking, the parties
should ask one or more other Christians to meet with them to help the parties communicate
more effectively and explore possible solutions. The mediators may give advice but have
no power to impose solutions. They may later act as witnesses in formal church
disciplinary proceedings should mediation fail due to hardness of heart or the unrepentant
sin of one or both parties. “If he will not listen, take one or two others along, so that every
matter may be established by the testimony of two or three witnesses.” (Matt. 18:16).

Arbitration: when two parties to a conflict cannot comet oa voluntary agreement on a
substantive issue, one or more arbitrators may be appointed to listen to the arguments and
evidence of each side and render a binding decision. “If you have disputes about such
matters, appoint as judges even men of little account in the church” (1 Cor. 6:1-8).

Church Discipline. Formal church discipline, pursuant to the procedures of BCO Part II, is
reserved for those cases where the person who professes to be a Christian refuses to be
reconciled, repent of personal sin, and do what is right. Church leaders bearing
ecclesiastical jurisdiction over such persons should formally intervene to promote justice,
repentance, and forgiveness. “If he refuses to listen, tell it to the church” (Matt. 18:17-20).

Application of the Principles
Judicial cases that come to the attention of church courts frequently begin as
personal disputes between two or more individuals. Typically, when one side feels they are
not getting what they want, they bring charges of sin against the other side. In this manner,
sessions and presbyteries are drawn into conflicts that would better be resolved at a personal
level through Christian conciliation. of course, when conflicted parties refuse to be
reconciled, the accountability afforded by formal church discipline should be used to further
the goals of purity in the church, the rebuke of offenses, the removal of scandal, and the
spiritual good of offenders (1 Cor. 5:5). Every effort should be made, however, to resolve
the conflict through repentance, confession, forgiveness and reconciliation before formal
charges are entertained by a church court. The intent of the relevant BCO provisions is to
encourage Christian unity before the formal provisions of church discipline are brought to
bear on a situation. of course, not every situation will lend itself to Christian conciliation.
However, every effort should be made to utilize private confrontation, mediation, and
arbitration in order to reserve the use of formal church discipline, and the time of church
courts, for those matters related to the purity of the church and the keeping and reclaiming
of disobedient sinners (BCO 27-3). many of these end swill be realized through the
processes of Christian conciliation and, therefore, should be viewed as prerequisite
procedures to the formal disciplinary process.

<a id="ga28-p376"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=378 printed_page=376 -->

Failure to Follow the Steps
Churches unwilling or unable to instruct their members completely in the full
breadth of the steps of conciliation and discipline encourage resort to unbiblical, worldly
responses to conflict The escape responses of denial, flight, and even suicide can be
expected if church members are not positively directed to the biblical processes of Christian
conciliation. Others, when not given the hope of having matters responded to within the
body of Christ, will resort to civil litigation, verbal and physical attack, and possibly even
murder to remove an opponent where there is no avenue for a just resolution.

Moving a case too quickly to formal church discipline can build barriers that could
otherwise be dealt with in mediation or arbitration. Every church and presbytery is
encouraged to equip elders and mature members with the skills of Christian conciliation,
and to exhaust conciliation processes and remedies before moving to formal church
discipline.

Use of Conciliation Clauses in Membership Documents
One significant way church members can be educated and prepared for biblical
conflict resolution is through the use of a conciliation clause in the membership covenant or
other agreement signed by new members when they join the church The recommended
language for this clause is set forth below. It should be noted that signing this clause cannot
be made a condition for membership, but may be encouraged as a voluntary biblical
commitment It should be clearly offered as an optional statement for the resolution of any
disputes that may arise.

Christian Conciliation
Members are encouraged but not required to make this additional commitment.

if i ever have a dispute with the church that cannot be resolved through it sown
internal procedures, I agree to resolve the dispute according to biblical principles (such as
those set forth in Matthew 5:23-25 and 18:15-20, and 1 Corinthians 6:1-8) by submitting
the matter to mediation and, if necessary, arbitration, according to the Bylaws of this church
and the rules of procedure of the institute for christian Conciliation. I understand that
arbitration is a legally binding process, and that judgment upon an arbitration award may be
entered in any court otherwise having jurisdiction.

Dated th is day of ________ . 19___ .

Name (Printed) Signature

Name (Printed) Signature

Parent’s or Guardian’s Name (for minors) Parent’s or Guardian’s Signature

<a id="ga28-p379"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=379 printed_page=null -->

this agreement is subject to arbitration pursuant to the (s tate !
arbitration act . title # # . chapter # . [S tate ] code annotated .

[The Institute for Christian Conciliation is a division of Peacemaker Ministries. For a copy
of the rules of Procedure, see www.HisPeace.org or call (800) 711-7118. Other
conciliation clauses for use in employment, construction, and service contracts are also
available]

Amend Church Bylaws to Allow Continuing Church Jurisdiction
The following clause is recommended for inclusion in local church bylaws to allow
the continuing ecclesiastical jurisdiction over a member who may otherwise simply flee
from the church to avoid biblical discipline:

Members may be removed from membership at their own request by informing the
Session of their intention to withdraw and the reasons therefore. If a member
requests to withdraw because of specific problems or disappointments with the
church, the Session shall attempt to resolve those matters so that the member may
remain in the church and enjoy greater fruitfulness and personal spiritual growth. If
the Session is unable to resolve those matters, it shall offer to assist the member in
locating a church of like faith and practice that can respond more effectively to his
gifts and needs If it appears to the Session that a member has requested removal
merely to avoid church discipline, that request shall not be given effect until the
disciplinary process has been properly concluded.

Use of this clause will also help the church avoid serious legal liability for following
through with all of the steps of church discipline. Members should be informed of its
provisions and carefully taught the Scriptural basis for continuing shepherding and pastoral
care especially when caught in sin (see also BCO 38-3 and 38-4).

Conclusion
The courts of the church are encouraged to employ the means of Christian
conciliation whenever possible. That is, churches and presbyteries are encouraged to defer
judicial action until all other remedies have been exhausted Peace, unity, and the mission
of the church can be furthered through careful and complete attention to every step of the
process as set forth in BCO 27-5 as understood through the expanded guidance concerning
Christian conciliation as set forth above.

Ill

SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT
ADMINISTRATIVE COMMITTEE TO THE
28™ GENERAL ASSEMBLY
JUNE 2000

Significant action:
1 that the administrative committee accept and approve the minutes of the audit
Review Committee, the Audit Report, and their recommendations.

2. That the Administrative Committee receive the interim Statements for AC and
for the Building, dated May, 31,2000, as information with gratitude to God and
for those He has used as His instruments.

Additional Recommendations:

19. That the 28th General Assembly accept the invitation from Central Carolina
Presbytery and approve meeting in the Winston-Salem or Charlotte, North
Carolina, area for the 31st General Assembly in June 2003, subject to suitable
facilities being available

20. That the 28th General Assembly accept the invitation from Pittsburgh Presbytery
and approve meeting in the Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, area for the 32nd General Assembly in June 2004, subject to suitable facilities being available.

21 That the housing allowance for TE J. Robert Fiol [$36,656] and for TE L. Roy
Taylor [$24,480] be approved.

22. that the General Assembly approve of Capin, Crouse and Company as auditors
for the Committee on Mission to the World for the calendar year ending
December 31, 2000.

ATTACHMENT 3

Report of the
PCA Historical Center to the
Twenty-Eighth General Assembly of the
Presbyterian Church in America

Our Mission: Since its establishment in 1985, the PCA Historical Center has sought to
preserve and promote the record of what god has done and is doing through the people
of the PCA. We do this by:
• preserving the official records of the PCA, its agencies and committees.
• collecting the personal papers of key individuals in the PCA.
• promoting the writing of church histories b your local congregations.
• working to develop in the people a deeper appreciation of the value of our legacy

Facilities Expansion: in october of 1999 construction began on the expansion and
renovation of the J. Oliver Buswell, Jr. Library, where the Historical Center is located
In preparing to occupy our new facilities, proposals have been placed before potential
donors, seeking funding for high-density moveable shelving, furnishings and
equipment. By God’s grace, we prayerfully anticipate being able to properly equip this
new facility, in order to better serve the people of the PCA for the next 25 years of her
history, and beyond.

Website: An initial Internet presence for the Historical Center was posted just prior to
the last General Assembly. Since that time work has been underway to expand the
resources posted to our site, with the goal of having on-line all of the findings aids (i.e.,
box content lists) for the collections housed in the Center Work is also in process to
post to the Internet our first exhibit, focusing on the history of presbyterian missions in
Dehra Dun, India. through the joining and receiving of 1982, the PCA participates in
a rich inheritance and a tradition of work ind eh ra dun that reaches back to 1834 In
ihat year the Reformed Presbyterian Church sent J.R Campbell, and later David Herron
and others RPC workers were in later years joined by missionaries from the Bible
Presbyterian Church, an association which may have prefigured the subsequent merger
of those two denominations. Now the PCA sustains this tradition in supporting a
number of faithful men and women who continue to labor in this field. From David
Herron to David Fiol, the work there spans an amazing 166 years in proclamation of the
gospel.

Publications: a number of new brochures are being written, addressing a variety of
topics of concern to patrons of the historical Center. Also in the works is the first
catalog of the holdings of the Center. our intention is to have many of these materials

<a id="ga28-p380"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=382 printed_page=380 -->

published and ready for distribution when the Historical Center opens its new facility in September, 2000.

**Records Processing:** In the last year work has progressed in processing the papers of Drs. C. Gregg Singer, Robert G. Rayburn and Donald MacNair, along with the records of the Stated Clerk’s office and Mission to North America. Notable collections received include the papers of William Chisholm, RPCES missionary to Korea, and a very unique set of seven scrapbooks donated by the Rev. Henry G. Welbon, a few months prior to his death on December 11, 1999. These scrapbooks document the history of the Modernist controversy of the 1930s and the birth of both the Orthodox Presbyterian Church and the Bible Presbyterian Church.

A related effort to records processing has been the migration of finding aids and other indexes to electronic format, in preparation for posting to our web page. Over 500 pages of these documents have been entered into table form and converted to HTML. One benefit of this conversion will be a new level of search capacity, thus increasing our ability to access these valuable collections.

**Aiken Taylor Award:** No award was presented in 1999, as no papers were received in submission for the Award. For our part, we must work to promote awareness of the contest and the prestige of the Award. Also under consideration is the size of monetary prize attached to the Award, which may need to be increased, in order to solicit greater interest.

**Staff:** The staff of the Historical Center currently consists of a part-time director, working 20 hours per week. Mrs. Florence Graham, who has worked here as a volunteer almost since the inception of the Historical Center, continues in her faithful service. Due to the difficulties posed by the construction of the Buswell Library, efforts to enlist Covenant Seminary student volunteers has been put on hold until the completion of construction.

**Historical Center Subcommittee:** The Historical Center Subcommittee provides oversight of the operations of the Center. Members of the Subcommittee are:

Dr. David Calhoun
Dr. Donald J. MacNair
Mrs. Shirley Duncan
Dr. Frederic Mau
Miss Lannae Graham
Mr. John Spencer
Mr. Edward S. Harris

Dr. L. Roy Taylor and TE John Robertson are ex-officio members of this committee.

Respectfully submitted,

/s/ Wayne Sparkman
Director, PCA Historical Center

(1) The PARTNERSHIP SHARE is that portion of the approved expense budget
which is dependent on contributions from the PCA churches and individuals
When the contributions are less than the amount of the PARTNERSHIP
SHARE, the committees, agencies and institutions cannot fulfill the programs
and services which the General Assembly approved at the June 2000 meeting

(2) Institutions are on a July 1 through June 30 Fiscal Year.

(3) The PCA Office building budget is not included in the PARTNERSHIP SHARE.
Gifts for the Office Building Fund are over and above the PARTNERSHIP
share and benefit all of the committees and agencies in Atlanta.

(4) The Mission to the World PARTNERSHIP SHARE includes the consolidated
portion of them tw budget identified as unrestricted or general Fund. It does not
include any items which are considered Restricted or "donor designated" funds,
including, but not limited to, such items as: missionary support, program ministry
and project budgets, etc.

ATTACHMENT 5

ADMINISTRATIVE COMMITTEE
2001 PROPOSED BUDGET

BUDGET PHILOSOPHY
the budget is built primarily on the job description of the stated clerk in the
RAO, which determines the services that are to be provided by the office of the stated
Clerk to the churches, presbyteries, committees and agencies, and to the General Assembly.

GENERAL COMMENTS
many of the activities and responsibilities of the administrative committee are
directly affected by the size and growth of the PCA, which in turn are reflected in
annual budget increases for many line items. The economic inflation rate also affects
most budget items and is applied to most line items, except as noted in the Economic
Assumptions shown below.

The budgets are presented in a format to comply with the standards for not-forprofit organizations adopted by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB).
the fa sb standards provide a definition of “supporting activities” which they call
“management and general” Therefore, compensation for the Stated Clerk and his staff
is allocated according to the estimated time spent by each person in “program,”
administration, and fiind raising areas

2001 ECONOMIC ASSUMPTIONS
A Stated Clerk/Administration
2.3 % PCA growth rate
3 .3 % Atlanta —Consumer Price Index (CPI) and inflation rate
2.7% National —Consumer Price Index (CPI) and inflation rate
10 0 % Health Insurance premiums (per IAR, Atlanta is 5.1%)
5.1 % Transportation
The Full Time Equivalent (FTE) employees budgeted at the beginning and
end of the year will be 11.75.

NOTE: Since the expenditures were held down tightly in 1999 to recover from 1998,
the 2001 budget was not always built off of the 1999 actuals plus the CPI, etc.

B. PCA Office Building
0% Rent remains at $14 per square foot for 2001
3.3 % Consumer Price Index (CPI) and inflation rate
The Full Time Equivalent (FTE) employees budgeted at the beginning and
end of the year will be 0 .5

<a id="ga28-p387"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=389 printed_page=387 -->

CHRISTIAN EDUCATION and PUBLICATIONS
2001 Proposed Budget

Philosophy:
CE/P's ministry program is driven by the Strategic Faith Plan. The plan defines CE/P's
mission, goals, strategies and programs. The proposed budget is a "program-based"
budget and represents the resources needed to adequately fund the planned activities,
which have been approved and/or directed by the Committee and the General Assembly.

Summary:
The proposed 2001 expense budget represents a total increase of $283,631 or 11.68%
from the 2000 budget. CE/P's contribution need only increases by $117,719 or 7.51%.
These increases are detailed and explained below.

Assumptions:
3% PCA growth rate
3% Occupancy cost (rent)
3% Consumer Price Index/Inflation Rate
5% Postal rates
10% Health insurance premiums increase
10% Costs for travel (transportation, food & housing)
5% Workman's Compensation Insurance

Both the approved budget for 2000 and the proposed budget for 2001 are based
on 24.55 full time equivalent employees. Currently, CE/P is not fully staffed so
no increase is anticipated.

Notes:

• Other Revenues (line 2) for 2001 are anticipated as follows:
Bookstore Sales/Postage $745,000
WIC Conference Fees 33,000
Subscriptions and Advertising 88,000
Video Library Memberships 50,000
Reimbursements (travel, postage, etc.) 39,000
Seminar/Conference Fees 70,000

• Training, Conferences and Consulting (page 2, line 3) is anticipated to increase
due to the increased demand for CE/P’s Regional Teacher Trainers. This
increased demand necessitates CE/P identifying and training five new
trainer/consultants Also, CE/P is currently planning to cosponsor a conference on
mercy ministries with MNA. This multi-day conference is anticipated to have
between 400 and 600 in attendance. Revenues generated by the various training
events will fund approximately $40,000 of the $68,063 increase.

<a id="ga28-p388"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=390 printed_page=388 -->

• Likewise, the Women in the Church (page 2, line 4) is anticipated to increase due
to increased size and scope of the annual training meeting for women in
leadership. This ultimately necessitates increasing the part-time administrative
assistant to a full-time position.
• CE/P continues to help local churches that request assistance in developing Men's
Ministries (page 2, line 5) in a limited way. The decrease in the budget reflects a
more realistic expectation that CE/P's next step in this ministry is employ an
individual on a part-time basis to further develop a PCA-wide men's ministry
network.
• The decrease in Multi-cultural ministries (page 2, line 6) reflects CE/P is no
longer seeking a staff person to replace TE Dwight Linton. CE/P believes it would
be more effective to contract with various consultants who have expertise working
with different ethnic groups.
• The increase in Youth Ministries (page 2, line 7) is due to the continued
development of a network of regional youth trainers, additional participants in the
internship program and CE/P’s sponsorship of the presbyterian youth association
conferences. The Designated Reserve (page 2, line 17) represents the balance of
the WIC Love Gift which was given for CE/P's Youth Ministries department.
• CE/P has maintained a Children's Ministries department (page 2, line 8) in
proposed budgets for several years. As it appears CE/P will have a director on
board by September 2000, management has refined the anticipated cost of staffing
the department with a teaching elder and part-time staff assistant.
• Publications and Curriculum (page 2, line 9) includes the periodicals Equip for
ministry and the bulletin supplement and a portion oft hew ic resource Letter. It
also includes the costs associated with developing and producing other annual
materials for Stewardship season, PCA week of prayer and christian education
Sunday as well as other video series and training manuals. The increase represents
the cost of the additional issues that are being provided to the increasing number of
conference attendees.
• The Bookstore (page 2, line 10) represents the largest portion of the proposed
budget increase and the majority of the increase is purchasing additional
merchandise for resale. though increased sales revenues will fund most of this
increase, additional costs will be incurred as the Bookstore e-commerce web site
will need to be enhanced and integrated with the existing inventory management
system. Additional personnel will be needed to manage this endeavor. Additional
advertising and promotion is anticipated as the Bookstore desires to partner with
'www.pcanews.com'.
• Depreciation (page 2, line 14) and Capital Expenditures (line 18) increases
proportionally as CE/P seeks to utilize technology to improve services to local
churches while minimizing the need for additional personnel.
• Management and General (page 2, line 13) is anticipated to increase
commensurate with the anticipated growth of the PCA and increased operating
costs.

<a id="ga28-p391"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=391 printed_page=null -->

The increase in Fund Raising (page 2, line 15) is due to the CE/P committee’s
decision to engage the services of marketing Partners, Inc. to develop a
comprehensive marketing and fund raising plan for CE/P .
Though CE/P has temporarily been relieved of the responsibility of funding a
portion of the PCA foundation (page 2, line 16 and page 1, line 31), CE/P’s
management earnestly desires to participate in this program once again if funds are
available.
The Coordinator, his assistant and related expenses are allocated to the various
expense categories as follows: Training 25%, Fund Raising 10%, Administration
10%, Bookstore 15%, WIC 10%, Children 5%, Youth 5%, Equip for Ministry
20 % .
Additional expenses associated with the WIC Leadership Training meeting and the
Mercy Ministry conference. In previous years the WIC meeting was expensed as
committee meetings because it was limited to PresWICs. no wit is a broader
based training event so its expenses are represented in honorariums, travel and
meeting facility.

COVENANT COLLEGE

GENERAL NOTES TO ACCOMPANY 2000-2001 PROPOSED BUDGET

The college uses the NACUBO (National association of college and university
Business Officers) definitions of revenue and expense categories. This insures that the
college will be able to directly compare various ratios with other colleges and assess our
effectiveness in accordance with our assessment systems. While the categories do not
exactly parallel the definitions used by the Accounting and Financial Reporting Guide
for Christian Ministries, there is some similarity. NACUBO categories including
Instructional, Academic Support, Library, Student Services, Public Service and Student
Aid could be broadly considered “Program Services ” maintenance of plant and
Institutional Support could be considered “Supporting Activities.”
Several major accounting changes were made in 1996-1997 in accordance with
changes in standards published by the Financial Accounting Standards Board. Included
in these are two that have significantly changed the figures shown from previous years
and made comparisons with those years unproductive. Therefore, the proposed budget
spreadsheets show only years 1996-1997 and following.
First, depreciation is now an operating expense rather than an expense only in
the plant fund. the requirement to fund depreciation comes from a variety of directions
Funding depreciation is a good thing for an organization as it provides funds to repair
and replace facilities as they age without additional fund raising. Generally accepted
accounting principles also require depreciation to be accounted for as an operating
expense and these rules must be followed to obtain an external audit in good standing.
Finally, the accrediting body in which Covenant participates requires a balanced bottom
line for continued accreditation, including depreciation. For these three reasons, the
college must actually fund the depreciation expense from increased revenues. Due to
this change, tuition will rise 3% more than otherwise would be the case for the next
several years. These tuition increases will fund depreciation, allow Covenant to show a
net positive bottom line and reduce pressure on future capital campaigns by providing
funds for facility and equipment replacement and repair. This expense is shown on the
expense spreadsheet divided between maintenance of plant Depreciation, representing
the depreciation costs of the facilities used for educational purposes, and Auxiliary
Depreciation, representing the depreciation costs of the residence halls, food service and
for-profit facilities.
Second, since capital gifts are shown as revenue in the year they are used for
construction, but the construction expense only appears over time through depreciation,
capital projects tend to positively overstate the operating results of the college. In order
to show the results more clearly, the operating net results for the college are shown on
the expense budget at the bottom of the page on the line titled “Increase (Decrease) in
Undesignated Net Assets.” this line wills how a negative figure until the completion of
funding the depreciation expense through the tuition increases mentioned in the
previous paragraph.
