COVENANT THEOLOGICAL SEMINARY
NEW BUDGET HIGHLIGHTS
FY2000-01

I. Revenue:
A. Tuition and Fees:
1. Tuition in most programs will increase from $242 per credit hour to
$250. This 3 .3% increase is less than last year (4.3%) and the lowest
since at least 1990. Because we discount any hours over 14 in a
semester, the annual tuition fora student taking a full 30 hours would
be $7,332. Tuition will pay about 42.2% of the cost to educate a
seminarian. This is down from 42.5% this year and 45% last year.
2. we are projecting enrollment in the fall of 2000 to bethe same as
this year’s projection, at 379 full time equivalents. This is five more
than this Fall’s actual result.

B. Endowment: Earnings used after management expenses are budgeted to
be 7.2%. This is up from 5.16% actually experienced for FY98-99 and
the 6.85% budgeted for FY99-00. We have not budgeted for new gifts
which will come in during the course of the new budget year as a result of
the Capital Campaign because they are so difficult to predict. Ultimately,
therefore, we should use less than 7.2% of earnings from funds which will
actually be in hand. Endowment income in all areas (unrestricted,
apartments, and student aid) will increase by $105,600, versus $396,000
for this year.

C. Gifts & Grants: The Gifts & Grants goal (PCA Partnership Share) will
increase from $1,730,000 to $1,760,000. this is an increase of 1.73% and
follows an increase of $100,000 or 6.13% for this year. This gifts &
grants goal is an increasingly smaller percentage of the total operating
budget compared to other typical seminaries. We are projecting Restricted
gifts of $215,000 for next year, up from $200,000 for this year

D. Student Aid: this source of income will decrease to $219,400 from
$224,100. These funds come from Endowment earnings. I believe that
my projection for this year was a little high.

E. Other Income: we expect this source of income to increase to $152,550
in the new year from $122,550 in the current year. most of this increase
is from higher interest income due to funds held for the Youth In Ministry
Institute. The interest income is unrestricted.

F. Auxiliary Enterprises: Rents for the apartments will see smaller
increases this year, as shown below. This follows a 3.38% increase for

<a id="ga28-p396"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=398 printed_page=396 -->

this year Single student housing will be increasing about 3.0%. The
additional income from all rents will be about $14,300.

99-00 Rent 00-01 Rent Increase
2-Bedroom $520 $535 2.88%
3-Bedroom $650 $665 2.31%

II. Expenditures:
A. General:
1. the budget assumes overall salary increases of 3% for staff and
faculty.
2. Medical Insurance premiums will change July 1, 2000. Preliminary
quotes reflect a 8-15% increase, which has been the experience of
other local employers. I have budgeted for 15%, but hope to achieve
a lower increase.
3. General expenses (where appropriate) were increased by 3% based
on inflation projections in the Kiplinger Letter.
4. In the past, expenses for the Counseling Department were included in
the Instruction budget. To track this better, I have pulled those
expenses out of the instruction department and created a separate
budget for Instruction - Counseling.

B. Educational & General:
1. President & Trustees: the dean of Administration’s position is
moved out of the President’s Office.
2 Instruction - General Budget:. A full year’s salary and benefits are
reflected for a NT professor added in January 2000. Cuts were made
in student wages, supplies and equipment.
3 Instruction - Doctor of Ministry: Cuts were made to wages -
other, mailing and travel.
4. Instruction - Master of Theology: No changes
5 Instruction - Evening Program: Budgeted for 18 3-hour classes at
$725 per credit hour.
6 Instruction - Extension Program: Reflects normal inflation.
, 7. Instruction - Counseling: This new budget area is carved out of
Instruction - General Budget, for easier monitoring.
8 Instruction - Francis Schaeffer Institute: Increases were made to
student wages, printing and advertising.
9. Instruction - Church Planting: Only inflationary increases
10. Registrar's Office: One half-time position is made full time, with
benefits.
11. Library: Provision is made for an additional full time staff person.
12. Student Services: The Dean becomes eligible for retirement
benefits this year.

<a id="ga28-p397"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=399 printed_page=397 -->

13. Family Nurture: Wage additions were made for more hours for
childcare workers.
14 Student Aid:

- **a.** Administration - Normal increases.
- **b.** Scholarships - Student Aid use is calculated as a percentage of tuition. In the budget the aid is calculated at 32.12% of tuition (not including fees).

15. Development: overall decrease due to a shifting of salaries.
16. Public Relations: The increase is due to absorbing a publication
(Leader To Leader) from the Capital Campaign, which is ending.
17. Gift & Estate Planning: Some staff wages and benefits are
absorbed from the Capital Campaign.
18. Admissions: Normal inflationary increases.
19. Electronic Media Production: A small increase in student labor is
included.
20. Business Office: Cuts were made in supplies, travel and repairs.
21. Computer Services: This budget area was new in 1999-00.
increases are for repairs and maintenance of hardware and software.
22. Physical Plant: Increases include more student labor for
housekeeping, due to new construction, and a full time maintenance
person.

C. Auxiliary Enterprises:
1. O perations: Some student labor is shifted to Physical Plant.
2. Student Apartments: less student labor due to the addition of a
full-time maintenance worker in Physical Plant. half of this person’s
salary will be covered from the endowed maintenance reserve for the
apartments.
3. Tape of the Month Club: reflects a shifting of salaries

D. Transfers:
1. Educational & General:
a. Money is annually transferred to the Plant Fund so that we can
replace the President's auto every four years. His used vehicle (a
mini van) is then used by the Seminary transportation department
for a few more years to provide for regular vehicle rotations.
Also, $32,250 is transferred to Renewal and Debt to amortize the
expenses associated with the Administrative Software Project.
This project will be fully amortized in December, 2004.

2 Auxiliary Enterprises:
a. $36,000 is set aside as reserves for future apartment repairs.

E. Contingency: The provision is for $1,500.

INSURANCE, ANNUITIES AND RELIEF

PROPOSED BUDGET

statement of purpose
the purpose of the board of trustees of Insurance, annuity and relief funds of the
Presbyterian Church in America is to glorify God through serving His servants who
minister in the Church by offering financial products to provide for the health and
welfare, retirement and relief needs of the denomination's ministers, missionaries, lay
church workers and their families, and to serve the churches, committees and agencies
of the PCA and related organizations by sponsoring and endorsing a denominational
property and liability insurance program.

• This budget reflects the costs incurred to administer the trust funds for
Insurance, Retirement, and Relief. This budget does not reflect the
financial activity in those trust funds. (Complete financial activity in the
trust funds may be found in the IAR Annual Report, which includes
audited financial statements.)

• The 2001 budget reflects an increase of 9.79% over the 2000 budget.
1999 actuals are unaudited.

• the increase in the retirement portion of support and revenue is a more
accurate representation of the allocation of expenses between supporting
activities.

• the total number of staff budgeted for in 2001 is ten FTE. Currently, all
of these positions are filled.

• an overall net increase of 22.7% in salaries and benefits is assumed in
2001. Budgeted positions include a 4% cost of living increase and a
10% health insurance increase.

• an overall net increase of 96.4% in marketing is assumed in 2001 to
allow for technology advances: development of our web site, interactive
links between participant accounts and retirement planning software, and
other enhancements to IAR services.

MISSION TO NORTH AMERICA
PROPOSED BUDGET

The MNA Mission:

In obedience to and in dependence upon our sovereign God, the purpose of MNA is to
enable PCA churches and presbyteries to start and develop healthy, reproducing
churches in North America by providing vision communication, resource development,
strategic planning, training, support services, and special ministries, so that a
movement of multiplying reformed churches is initiated and sustained.

• This budget reflects our continuing emphasis on regionalization - assisting and working
with presbyteries to develop church planting in local areas

• While the total revenue and expenses will increase 6.8% compared to the 2000 budget, the
portion included in the “partnership share” will increase only 5%.

• An overall net increase of 5% in salaries and benefits is assumed That is an aggregate of
cost of living, merit increases and health insurance costs

• The total number of multicultural missionaries budgeted for is 4 Between 59 and 77
projects are budgeted to receive direct support in church planting, multicultural church
planting and urban and mercy ministries

• The total number of full-time equivalent staff budgeted for in the 2000 budget was 32-1/2.
In 2001 it is 30 1/2. This decrease is due to eliminating two positions previously budgeted
but never filled in campus ministries Twenty-eight and a half of these positions are
currently filled The unfilled positions are church relations, and an assistant coordinator for
church planting.

• The cost being charged by the Administrative Committee for office space is assumed to
remain at the actual 2000 rate of $ 15 per square foot.

• This budget reflects a 54.9% increase in Chaplain Ministries. That is due to the fact that the
Armed Services are offering more opportunities than are currently being filled More
emphasis will be placed on recruiting additional chaplains.

• The 31.7% decrease in the campus budget is due to two main factors The first is the
decrease in personnel (see above paragraph). The second factor is the transfer of the
International Campus Ministers to the oversight of the affiliated campus committees rather
than the GA-MNA Committee.

• The complete 2001 budget for Reformed University Ministries (MNA Campus Ministries
and the affiliated presbytery campus committees) is provided for information as an
attachment of MNA’s 2000 GA report

MISSION TO THE WORLD
MTW DOCKET MEMO

TO: Finance/Management Subcommittee
FROM: Roger R Kooistra
DATE: February 18, 2000

subject : Mission to the World Proposed Budget for Year 2001 * *

The proposed Mission to the World Consolidated Operating Budget for the calendar
year 2001 is presented on the following pages. The consolidation includes career
missionaries and projects, MTW/'IMPACT, the Home Office, the Medical Insurance
Fund (MIF) and the Endowment Fund

The Presbyterian Church in America Administrative Committee has scheduled the
review of budgets to take place march 18, 2000.

RECOMMENDATION: That the Proposed Consolidated 2001 Budget for Mission to
the World, including MTW/IMPACT. the Medical Insurance Fund and the Endowment
Fund be approved for the calendar year 2001 in the amount of $34.237.027 for expenses
and the MTW Capital Budget be approved for the calendar year 2001 in the amount of
$90.000 for presentation to the PCA Administrative Committee.

XI. PROPOSED CONSOLIDATED 2001 BUDGET

I Methodology and Background:
The 2001 budget is proposed from an analysis of key factors that influence the income
and expenses of mission to the world operating ina global context with a rapidly
changing global economy. we reviewed the results of 1999 and extended these
indicators into 2000 and 2001.
The year 1999 saw the US dollar remain relatively strong through out the year For
mission work this can sometimes be helpful Our overseas missionary expenses in US
dollars ended the year at approximately ninety five percent of budget without any major
change to program content we also saw a consistent and growing us economy with a
good growth in the value of the stock market again in 1999. The economic environment
was positive and this was reflected in consistent growth in contributions from churches
and individuals to our missionaries and programs We continued to see additional gifts

<a id="ga28-p407"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=409 printed_page=407 -->

and trusts coming into the endowment fund The total income for the year was just
under one hundred fifteen percent of budget.

remembering that it is all by the grace of God, the solid US economy, a strong dollar, a
growing base of home churches, and a stable force of missionaries all contributed to a
very positive year for Mission to the World.

II. Future Directions for 2001:

There are several major goals that influence the 2001 budget. The Global Church
Planting Center (GCPC) has started to appear in various forms under the direction of
missionary Bill Goodman in Mexico City. During this year, the GCPC staff will
conduct three Train the Trainer workshops in Africa, Asia and Europe. In addition,
center staff will assist in the assessment, training and deployment of new missionaries.
an evaluation of the leadership development needs of MTW missionaries will be
conducted and a pilot program to meet these needs initiated. the purpose of the gc pc
is to actively support and involve our missionaries and national pastors in proven
methods and best practices in promoting church-planting movements.

The goal to open at least three new fields in 2001 has been budgeted. The planning and
selection of potential fields will occur in 2000 and the selection, recruitment and
itinerating for the missionaries in 2001.

Functional realignment and expanded staffing were completed in 1999 for our peoplecentered efforts in the home office. Plans in the home office are to strengthen recruiting
and ongoing support of the missionary staff because the world is undergoing such rapid
change. Increases in home office staff in 2000 and 2001 are for short-term programs
that are projected to expand, the GCPC, the international university ministries, and
church relations. Most of
these increases have been covered through special funds already raised for the specific
ministry.

Plans for information technology in 2001 focus mainly on possibly replacing the
recruiting system, better utilization of global communications (the Internet) and the
integration and sharing of data between the donor services system, the human resource
information system, the contact management/recruiting system and the short-term
project management system.

Major development efforts will continue to focus on raising endowment funds. The
endowment income will go to reduce the administrative assessment and to fund major
new programs. Our Partner Relations staff has also set goals to continue to strengthen
relationships with churches who are the major revenue source for MTW and a growing
factor in funding the home office.

<a id="ga28-p408"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=410 printed_page=408 -->

III Projection Base for 2001:
projections have been made regarding the number of missionaries, home office staff,
annual income and annual expenses. In making these projections the following
assumptions have been used:

We anticipate that continued efforts to recruit missionaries in 2000 will show additional
results during 2001. the number of career missionaries is projected to grow to 530,
two-year missionaries to 150, internship missionaries to 250 and two-week missionaries
to 6000

We will have a small increase in the home office staff in 2001 for the ministry
initiatives that have recently been started and growth in our short-term missions
programs.

We have anticipated that the US dollar will continue to be strong and about as stable as
in 1999 We expect other global economic factors to be unstable, with higher inflation
than here in the US, but somewhat offset by currency values.

Income (excluding endowment and gift annuities) and expenses have grown from $7.8
million in 1985 to $32.5 million in 1999, with projected growth to $34.1 million in
2001. Income projections have assumed a continuing strong support for missionaries
from churches and individuals ina reasonably stable us economy and in a gradually
growing PCA denomination. We have projected the support requirements of
missionaries, adjusted for inflation, as a basis for future income. For expenses, the
combined effect of salary adjustments, growth and currency value, using historic trends
indicates an increase of approximately 6 percent for 2000 and 5 to 6 percent for 2001

Missionary support accounts with deficit balances continue to remain low. Total
deficits for all missionaries have gone from $400,000 in 1994 down to only $18,740 in
1999 indicating the strong support of MTW ’s ministry partners.

Partnership share giving for the home office grew from $300,000 in 1994 to $1,117,000
in 1999 and is projected to grow to $1,300,000 in 2001. We have assumed that good
church relations and increased services to churches will allow a gradual annual increase
in partnership share giving.

project and field income is calculated as apart of the support requirements of
missionaries and we expect a 4 to 5 percent growth in 2001.

Investment income projections assume that interest rates will remain stable near their
current levels. In 2001, we will see some endowment earnings available as needed in
the general fund and the GCPC to cover operational costs. In addition, the GCPC has
set aside funds for the next three years operations.

<a id="ga28-p409"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=411 printed_page=409 -->

The 2001 proposed budget for impact is base dona summer program of 6000
students, an internship program of 250 persons and a two-year missionary staff of 150
missionaries. The following major factors form the basis for projecting the income and
expenses for IMPACT:

A Office Staff—The cost in the home office reflect several changes and new
staff added during 1999 and 2000 One additional full-time equivalent
position is added for 2001.

B. Expanded programs in most areas will generate both higher income and
expenses that are being designed to stay in balance

The medical insurance fund (MIF) had an average expense year in 1999. We expect
that costs will increase slightly faster than inflation. The operating capital reserve
showed some improvement in 1999 but will still produce very little investment income.

With controlled or specially funded costs in the home office, a slight increase in
missionaries and increases in partnership share giving, we project that the fixed monthly
administrative assessment charge per missionary will remain unchanged for a eighth
straight year.

Major programs that will influence expenses in 2001 are our continued growth of
church-planting movements worldwide with training and mentoring, the international
university ministry, the two major capital campaigns for Ukraine and Italy, the
continued growth of the summer two-week program, and short-term medical ministry

There are three tables showing the consolidated income and expenses budget proposed
for 2001. The first table presents a historical perspective showing 1999, 2000 approved
at General Assembly and 2001 information. The second shows a functional breakdown
between program, administration and fund raising. The third table shows changes in
budget from 2000 to 2001.

In addition to the income and expense budget, the capital expense budget is presented
on the last table in the amount of $90,000. It assumes any major facility changes will
be handled as a separate budget. The major items to be purchased relate to information
technology, improved telecommunication and some office reconfigurations to maximize
space utilization for staff growth.

<a id="ga28-p410"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=412 printed_page=410 -->

<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=414 printed_page=412 -->

PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA FOUNDATION, INC.
2001 PROPOSED BUDGET SUMMARY

GENERAL COMMENTS:

In preparing the PCA Foundation Proposed 2001 Budget, we tried to keep expense
increases to a minimum where possible However, the budget anticipates the
continuation of recent initiatives and implementation of new initiatives and, therefore,
shows some increases in support/revenue and in expense from 1999 Actual and 2000
Budget levels.

NOTES TO NATURAL EXPENSE STATEMENT

SUPPORT & REVENUE

UNDESIGNATED EARNINGS (line 1) - These are earnings on funds held by the PCA
Foundation, mainly from Advise & Consult Funds and the PCAF Endowment, which
help underwrite the Foundation’s operating expenses. The 2001 budget of $350,000
represents a 51% increase over 1999 actual and a 19% increase over the 2000 amount
budgeted these increases are primarily in anticipation of the continued growth of
A&C Funds.

C & A SUPPORT (line 2) - General Assembly mandated support from the four
remaining Committees and Agencies (Covenant College, Covenant Theological
Seminary, Mission to North America, and Mission to the World) was eliminated in
2000. Their mandatory support in 1999 was $15,000 each, fora total of $60,000,
having been reduced from $30,000 each in 1998, $34,000 each in 1997 (after a $10,000
refund to each), and $44,000 each in 1996.

FEES (line 3) - Administrative fees charged on funds held for long term
administration such as Charitable Remainder Trusts, Endowments, Annuities,
Designated Funds, etc. The 2001 budgeted increase over 1999 actual is warranted due
to an expected increase in the number of charitable remainder trusts and endowments.

CONTRIBUTIONS (line 6) - Gifts made to help underwrite the Foundation
Operating Budget. the budgeted amount of $150,000 is due primarily to a matching
challenge contribution and the corresponding matches fora period of 10 years. This
will help provide the resources necessary for the PCAF to operate without mandatory
funding from the Agencies and Committees beginning in 2000.

<a id="ga28-p413"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=415 printed_page=413 -->

OPERATIONS EXPENSES (Notes generally relate to notable comparisons of 2001
budget to 2000 budget)

STAFF WAGES & BENEFITS (line 11) - reflects salary increases of 5% and the
hiring in 1999 of a full time employee

# RIDGE HAVEN CONFERENCE CENTER
## 2001 PROPOSED BUDGET AND BUDGET COMPARISON STATEMENT NOTES

Line 2, Camps and Conference Facilities - In 1998 we changed our method of calculating our cost and income of facilities from a customer-room-rate estimate that ranged from about $25 to $50 to an actual cost-per-room or cost-per-building determined from actual laundry, housekeeping, and utility costs for the room or building.

Line 5, Resident Leases - This category includes income from lot leases, lot lease interest, lot lease maintenance fees (the administrative amount we charge for keeping all lot information on file and current), water hookups, water usage fees and road maintenance fees. The amount projected for 2001 is the actual amount expected without lot sales or water hookup fees of which we have no guarantee or expectation of income.

Line 6, Other Contributions - This category includes contributions from WIC groups, other groups, and individuals toward the daily operations of Ridge Haven. This category includes contributions from WIC groups, other groups, and individuals toward the daily operations of Ridge Haven.

Line 7, Non-medical Reimbursements - This category includes amounts paid to Ridge Haven by individuals or groups for the use of Ridge Haven postage, office copier, vehicle parts, fuel, pay phone, etc.

Lines 9 &amp; 41, Meals - The large variation between the actuals for 1999 and both the 1999 and 2000 budgets is due to a change in the way we were providing Food Service. Prior to 1999, our Food Service was provided by a Food Service Contractor. When the provider moved to a different part of the State and closed the local business, we changed Food Service to an "in-house" position. The change has brought improvements in many categories including the financial one.

Line 13, Sale of Assets - This category was added in 1997 and reflects the liquidation of material items no longer needed. From year-to-year the certainty of income from the sale of assets is unforeseeable.

Line 17, Other Salaries, wages and Benefits - The Ridge Haven staff consists of a total of 12 year around employees. The Administrator, Administrative Assistant, Camps Director/Resident Manager, Maintenance Superintendent, and Food Service Director are salaried, full-time employees. The Maintenance Assistant and Office Assistant are full-time, hourly paid employees. The Accounting Manager, Food Service Assistant

<a id="ga28-p417"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=419 printed_page=417 -->

Director, and two House Keeping staff are all year around part time hourly paid
employees. The amount provided in the budget reflects 1) the addition of two positions,
Resident Manager and Conference Director, 2) increased compensation for the
Administrator, Administrative Assistant, Maintenance Superintendent, Maintenance
Assistant, and Food Service Director to bring them up to the average compensation
level for employees in comparable positions in other area camps and conference
ministries, and 3) a 3% cost-of-living increase for all other employees.

Line 19. Counselor and Camp Support Staff - The budgeted figure for the last three
years provides for an amount needed to allow us to remain competitive in compensating
counselors and support staff and adding additional counselors and support staff for
larger camps and a junior counselor program.

Line 21. Workers Comp. - The amount reflects the corresponding higher cost due to
increased compensation and added staff identified in the line 17 note.

Line 22. Payroll Taxes - The amount reflects the corresponding higher cost due to
increased compensation and added staff identified in the line 17 note.

Line 23. Other Fees - This category includes North Carolina Fees (usually a small
amount), Miscellaneous fees (also usually a small amount), Solid Waste Fees, and Pool
Fees (the amounts we pay for county inspection at the beginning of each season).

Line 26. Camps and Conferences Expenses - Previous to 1998 this category included
only such items as supplies and equipment. In 1998 we also included on-site and offsite fees (rafting costs, etc) and bus charters for these trips which had been previously
put in line 23, Other Fees.

Line 34. Equipment/Furnishings - We continue to need additional equipment and
furnishings for several of our buildings. These items are purchased as the money
becomes available.

Line 35. Grounds - We have several erosion and landscape projects we are seeking to
implement. The amount indicated will allow us to do these as the money comes in.

Line 38. Maintenance and Tools - Ridge Haven owns and operates a large and varied
inventory of maintenance equipment and tools including a tractor, truck snow plow
attachment, air compressor, tire-changer, bush hog, etc. The needed funds will allow us
to replace and add needed equipment and tools.

Line 40. Vehicles - The amount in this category allows us not only to maintain our
vehicles but also to replace the vehicles beyond cost effective repair and to add needed
newer ones.

<a id="ga28-p418"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=420 printed_page=418 -->

Line 45. Facilities Renovation - All 21 of the ridge haven buildings/facilities need
renovations from time-to-time that are not otherwise provided for in the day-to-day
maintenance category. The amount provided in this category reflects renovations for 9
different buildings that could use immediate attention. Professionally estimated costs
for the proposed renovations were used to arrive at the stated number.

Line 46. Water Systems - The amount reflects needed and anticipated expenses for
major upgrades and improvements already under way including the addition of anew
commercial-grade well (required by State standards) and combining our two existing
systems into one system for better monitoring, improved safety, and increased
maintenance efficiency.

<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=421 printed_page=419 -->

28th GENERAL ASSEMBLY REPORT
CHRISTIAN EDUCATION AND PUBLICATIONS

The calendar year 1999-2000 has been a busy one for CE/P. The staff has visited more
than two-thirds of the presbyteries, at least 100 churches and hosted a number of
training events locally and around the country. The attempt to challenge the church to
think about this “moment of opportunity and challenge” to reach the millennial
generation and to pass onto the next generation the truths of god and the christian faith
has been a major focus of the committee and staff. The September 1999 Mercy
Ministry Conference sponsored and hosted by CE/P and its WIC department was an
encouragement to many of our churches. We had 4,000 participants at the conference
and continue to receives tories from local churches of the follow-up ministries that are
growing out of that conference.

We along with Mission to North America are planning a denomination-wide conference
on Mercy Ministries in March 2001 for men and women who either are personally
involved or want to be involved. We are especially targeting deacons for this
conference. It too will be held in Atlanta with outstanding PCA men and women
participating in and leading the conference. The stage was set at the September
conference and the challenge for follow-up has come forth from that event.

Along with our present regional trainers and youth regional trainers, CE/P is in the
process of adding wic regional trainers to our resources to better serve the churches in
their development of women’s ministries. We will start with six regional trainers that
will give us nearly thirty regional people working with CE/P to help local churches in
their educational and training ministries.

CE/P realized a number of years ago that regional trainers were necessary to keep up
with the growth of the church and to covert he churches and regions more effectively
and efficiently. Those trainers are equipped by the CE/P staff to assist them in their
ministry to the PCA. They are highly qualified and gifted men and women. Feedback
from churches utilizing our resources and training personnel has been most positive and
encouraging.

Internet Services:
As personal computers and online services proliferate the PCA, CE/P is committed to
making more resources available via the Internet. Thus far, in 2000, CE/P has
redesigned and enhanced its web site so that it provides information on upcoming
training events, leadership resources, curriculum, books and videos. Additionally, the
Bookstore now operates an e-commerce web site so the PCA members can browse the
catalog and initiate an online purchase. CE/P will continue to explore new
opportunities and provide more electronic ministry resources. As funds become
available, CE/P will begin to offer resource libraries and interactive training.

<a id="ga28-p422"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=424 printed_page=422 -->

## Youth Ministry Department:

This past year CE/P said good-bye to Rev. Will LaRose who served as the first CE/P National Director of Youth Ministry. Will has returned to ministry in the local church. CE/P is grateful for the good work Will did in laying the foundation for denominational youth work in the PCA. Through that ministry, CE/P has built a national network of youth pastors, established a group of six regional youth trainers, established a resource file of youth pastor resumes, and helped place youth pastors in local church ministries. CE/P has also developed a youth pastor’s intern-training program with several interns participating. During this time, CE/P has developed an official relationship with Presbyterian Youth in America (PYA, a leadership conference ministry for PCA young people) by absorbing them into CE/P’s overall ministry. We are grateful for the good labors of Will LaRose and delighted he serves as one of the regional trainers.

In October, the Lord brought Rev. Dean Conkel, PCA teaching elder, to accept a call to CE/P to replace Will as National Director of Youth Ministry. Dean comes to CE/P Lakemont Presbyterian Church in Augusta, GA, where he served as youth pastor. Making contacts, expanding the youth ministry network within the PCA and expanding the intern-training program are top priorities for this ministry. It is part of CE/P’s effort to reach this generation of youth and enfold them into the church.

## Training Department:

When the PCA developed its organizational structure and decided that in keeping with the great commission of our Lord that Christian Education was to be one of the program agencies, one of the main assignments from the start was that of training. Equipping saints for the work of ministry has been a theme over the years. Much of CE/Ps effort and resources has been poured into equipping the equippers in local churches and presbyteries. Training is the top priority for CE/P in all of its programs. We are facilitators and enablers to church in the area that some would call human resources. Solidly biblical and reformed materials have also been made available in conjunction with this training ministry.

This past year CE/P has seen a record number of seminars conducted by the staff, the regional teacher trainers, and youth regional trainers. This ministry is making a significant impact in the lives of teachers, leaders, and officers throughout the PCA. These are the people who serve on the front lines to teach and lead children, youth, and adults to greater depths of spiritual maturity. What CE/P is seeing and hearing from these who serve in various positions in the local church is that they are rededicating themselves to grow and commit themselves to others in the body of Christ. If you are looking to provide training for your leaders and teachers, contact CE/P to see where and when you can include them in a CE/P seminar.

The regional trainers in the general area of Christian education, youth ministries, Women in the Church ministries, and soon to be children’s ministries, have touched

<a id="ga28-p423"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=425 printed_page=423 -->

hundreds of churches during the past several years in training, consulting, and assisting local churches and presbyteries. A major part of CE/P's activities in this area has been its attempt to interface with presbytery Christian education and WIC ministries. An annual meeting with presbytery Christian education chairmen is held in Atlanta to discuss strategy, networking, and cooperation in this process.

## Children's Ministry:

For the second straight year CE/P has hosted a conference in Atlanta for children's workers in our PCA churches. Due to the positive response of the first conference in '99, CE/P had to open the second conference to even a larger number. There is overwhelming agreement among these key leaders of children's ministry about the need for more communication, training, and contact among themselves. They are looking to CE/P to provide that kind of service.

Growing out of these meetings, one aspect of CE/P's Strategic Plan is to add a staff person who will serve as the CE/P's National Director of Children's Ministry. The Lord is opening the way as monies are designated for that position and agreement has been reached with an outstanding children's pastor to move into this position—first, as a consultant and then full-time in 2001. This is so critical to the strategy of CE/P in its focus on reaching the millennial generation, CE/P asks that the General Assembly pray for this important endeavor that will have a significant bearing upon the future of our church.

There are a growing number of men and women who are being called to full-time positions as directors of children's ministries. Similarly to youth ministry, we are seeing more and more people being challenged to see as a special children's ministry calling within the church. In today's highly specialized and generational diverse age, children's ministry is a challenging opportunity to work within our covenantal framework to assist churches in their responsibility to minister to families and individuals. More and more churches are contacting us in their search for personnel to fill these positions.

## Resources:

CE/P continues to develop material resources for PCA churches. Through our partnership with the Orthodox Presbyterian Church in the joint venture of Great Commission Publications, more than 60% of our churches are using the best Sunday school curriculum within our theological framework. Plus, through that partnership we are ministering to churches in other denominations.

The Bookstore continues to grow and provide excellent service. During this past year and as indicated above, the staff has been able to get the Bookstore on line, thus expanding CE/P's resource availability. CE/P is committed to providing and recommending books that are important in the teaching and training process.

The Video Library continues to expand the number of tapes it offers. Some interesting statistics: there are 3,000-4,000 tapes available including over 700 tape series. Three

<a id="ga28-p424"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=426 printed_page=424 -->

hundred and fifty churches are enrolled with our library. This ministry continues to receive good reviews from member churches.

## CE/P Publications:

The *Equip for Ministry* magazine is highly regarded by those who subscribe. Leadership in PCA churches write and tell how much they appreciate receiving it. We now print 10,000 copies with each issue.

The CE/P *Bulletin Supplement* serves over three hundred churches and the *WIC Resource Letter* is mailed quarterly to each local PCA church. The Resource Letter also contains a master copy of the WIC Newsletter that is used by local churches as a bulletin insert. Plans are underway to develop both a children’s and youth newsletter to enable better communication and networking throughout the church.

## Conclusion:

CE/P is seeking to realize more and more the great moment of opportunity that the Lord has set before us as part of His universal body. The committee and staff work regularly to understand the times in which we live and what God would have us to do. We believe the PCA has been raised up by God for these times and with the challenge and need to ground people in the faith and equip them to reach out into the world with a Christ-like concern. CE/P desires to help our churches make a real difference in people’s lives in living, understanding, and bearing witness to the truth within the Gospel framework.

## Recommendations:

The Committee for Christian Education and Publications makes the following recommendations to the 28th General Assembly:

1. That on behalf of the PCA General Assembly, CE/P express its gratitude for the service of the following committee members: TE George Mitchell, TE Paul Settle, RE Barron Caulfield, and RE Ron Martin.

2. That the proposed budget for 2001 as amended by #7 and #13 be approved and forwarded to the Administrative Committee.

3. That the minutes of September 16-17, 1999 and February 24-25, 2000 be approved.

4. That on behalf of the CE/P’s WIC and MNA Committees, the General Assembly expresses our thanks to the churches that participated in the 1999 WIC Love Gift. ($117,400.00 was contributed to MNA project.)

5. That churches be encouraged to join in the support of the 2001 WIC Love Gift directed to Ridge Haven Conference Center.

<a id="ga28-p425"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=427 printed_page=425 -->

6 That the General Assembly express appreciation to the PCA churches that
participated in and supported the September 1999 Mercy Ministries Conference.

7. that the following women who have served well as members of CE/P’s
Women’s Advisory Subcommittee over the past several years: Georgia Settle, Pam
Benton, and Carol Arnold, be thanked for their diligence and service.

8 That churches be urged to participate in the joint Mercy Ministry Conference
sponsored by CE/P and MNA in March 2001.

9. That local churches be encouraged to participate in both the regional and
denominational conferences coordinated and jointly sponsored by CE/P (on worship
and music).

10. That the General Assembly express thanks to Pioneer Clubs for its co-operative
ministry to our participating churches and encourage churches not participating to
consider using this program as apart of their ministry to children.

11. That the General Assembly express thanks to the Great Commission
Publications staff, especially Tom Patete and Mark Lowrey, for their cooperation and
team work with CE/P and the PCA churches.

12. That following the annual evaluation, the CE/P Committee unanimously
recommends to the 28th General Assembly that TE Charles H. Dunahoo be elected to
serve as coordinator of christian education and publications for the coming year.

<a id="ga28-p426"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=428 printed_page=426 -->

The 1998-99 Institutional Effectiveness Report
Covenant College Office of Planning

Introduction
The Institutional Effectiveness Report is divided into six major sections: Academic
Affairs, Student Affairs; Planning and Research; Financial Affairs; Advancement, and an
update on the 1995 - 2000 Five-vear P laa Each section contains information which
provides evidence of the health of the institution. some of that evidence is in the form of
student outcomes, some as inputs, and some as results of various performance indicators.

The Academic Affairs section includes information about students, academic
assessment, faculty, and academic support functions. The student information deals with
both student outcomes and student profiles. both types of information give evidence of
the type of student attracted to covenant college and the results of their academic
experience. faculty information provides input evidence of the college’s efforts to
provide a quality educational opportunity for the students. Academic support information
demonstrates additional input evidence of an atmosphere conducive to learning.

the student affairs section presents evidence of services provided to our students by
the Student Development Department. This information includes both input and outcome
evidence.

the planning and research section presents the activities and finding of the planning
and research functions of the college as they impact input and change decisions.

the financial affairs section of the report furnishes evidence of institutional financial
stability and how resources were used to provide students with learning opportunities. All
financial aid information is included in this section.

The section on Advancement includes all financial development and new student
recruitment information. While this evidence tends to be input in nature, it also
demonstrates the commitment of donors and new students to the institution.

The sixth, and final, section of the report outlines the progress of the college to meet the
goals of the 1995-2000 Five-vear Plan and any new opportunities that the college has
chosen to explore.

Section 1: Academic Affairs
institutions of higher education often boast concerning the characteristics of their
incoming students as a means of expressing th equality of the academic program. While
there is some merit to the concept that good students attract good students, such reporting
does not express the effectiveness of the institution in educating those students. The first
portion of section 1 will provide a profile of the students, their entry-level academic
skills, enrollment, retention and completion. However, there al measure of the college’s
effectiveness is seen in the outcome information found later in this section.

Student Profile
The Fall 1999 student body is made up of 57% female and 43% male. This continues a
trend toward matriculation of more women students than men. The denominational mix
of our students is still strongly PCA with 61% of our students coming from our own
denomination. New students totaled 258, 2 less than last year. However, improved
retention accounted for an increase in traditional students. details of enrollment, SAT
scores, retention and graduation follow.

average growth of a little
over 5% per year since the
inception of the Quest and
Master of Education
programs. This year’s
growth is 7.3%.

Academic Preparedness

SAT Scores
The following graph shows the average
SAT scores of Covenant’s entering firsttime freshmen over the last five years as
compared with the national average. For
the first time in 5 years, Covenant’s SAT
score average has dropped. The average
score for this year’s entering freshmen
was 1163 compared to last year’s 1167.
However, that remains 14.5% higher
than the national average.

Secondary Marks of Entering Freshmen
As demonstrated by the graph below, a larger percentage of our freshmen are
reporting grades of mostly a ’s than ever before.

<a id="ga28-p431"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=433 printed_page=431 -->

Student Retention
covenant was again ranked in the top tier of regional liberal arts colleges in the south
according to the 2000 issue of the U. S. News and World Report Best Colleges. The
average freshmen retention level for those recognized as the Best Regional Liberal Arts
Colleges in the South (33 schools) was 72.8%. Covenant’s freshmen retention rate was
80%.

Only one college in the first tier (Fisk University) had abetter rate of retention than
Covenant College.

Retention rates are affected by many different factors. One factor reported earlier on
page 4 was the SAT scores. As shown on that page, SAT scores have dropped this year
at Covenant and across the nation, but among the top tier schools mentioned above,
only one college has entering freshmen with sores in a higher percentile ranking than
Covenant.

retention continues to be of concern. While our rates compare favorably to colleges
our size, we desire to see an improvement. A new plan was introduced this year which
might reflect improvement in next year’s results.

Graduation Rate
This statistic measures how many students in any given freshman class graduate within
150% of the planned time. Since Covenant offers baccalaureate degrees based on a
four-year curriculum, the graduation rate is based on a six-year period. To be
considered in the graduation rate, students who came to Covenant as first-time freshmen
in the fall of 1993 (n=168) must have graduated from covenant by may of 1999.

one issue of concern over the years has been that not enough students who start at
Covenant graduate from Covenant. The table below shows that this statistic has been
steadily climbing and has now reached 57%.

<a id="ga28-p432"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=434 printed_page=432 -->

![img-0.jpeg](img-0.jpeg)

## Academic Assessment

A careful plan of academic assessment has been implemented at Covenant College to serve two purposes:

1. To give a sense of the performance of various aspects of the academic community for the purpose of praise to God for His work in our midst; and
2. To provide evidence for reflective practitioners to use in decision-making processes to improve the quality of service provided to our Lord within the call of a learning community.

A caution regarding the limits of evidence needs to be considered as assessment is reviewed. Although the data gathered here provide a picture of institutional effectiveness, it is not a complete picture. We do not pretend to be objective nor do we believe that specification of many component parts provides a sense of the total work of the academic program.

Nonetheless, it would be foolish to think that the following information has nothing to say to us and should not be used as we evaluate our effectiveness. The following pages document many things for which the college community may be thankful and some areas where we are eager to seek improvement with God’s help.

## Course Evaluations

All professors distributed course evaluations in a variety of courses in the fall and spring semesters. Mean ratings were close to a four on a five-point scale, indicating a high degree of student satisfaction and effectiveness of the academic courses. Of special note from the descriptive study are: the higher than normal number of sophomore students responding (indicating more advanced courses being used for assessment and likely less core), the average grade expected by students is slightly higher than a B, and about half of the courses were required for the major. The correlation study indicates coefficients of practical significance around a cluster of pedagogical effectiveness and the general effectiveness of the course. Assessments and assignments also had strong degrees of interaction.

<a id="ga28-p433"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=435 printed_page=433 -->

Recommendations
1. Generally instruction in courses is perceived as very strong. Attention needs to be
given to maintaining the general strength of pedagogy.
2. the strong cluster involving assignments underscores the necessity of paying
close attention to the relevance of assignments to the course of study. This is a
current strength which needs to be maintained.
3. one of the distinctives of the college courses is the expertise of the faculty. The
highest rating of 4.5 for both semesters needs to be maintained.
4. Tests should be constantly updated and reviewed as to their relevance to the
course.

Departmental Assessment
During the 1995 SACS self-study, each department was to construct three objectives and
prescribe indicators for each assessment to be reviewed on an annual basis. During the
1998-99 school year all departments reviewed the 1995 documents and made the
necessary modifications. most of the departments were th enable to implement an initial
assessment during this year. This data is being used to determine if the expected
outcomes have been met and if any changes should be made. For the departments which
were unable to collect the necessary data, the process implementation will begin in 1999-
2000 .

Curriculum Review
The five-year rotation of reviewing departmental curriculum was suspended for one year. In the
1999-2000 school year, the curriculum committee and the department of academic affairs hope to
em ploy external reviewers for this purpose. departments would be placed ina rotation (sim ilar to
the current curriculum committee review ) and com plete a mini self-study with a visit from
regional professionals with expertise in relevant areas.

Final Exams
Final exams were reviewed for the 1998 fall semester for pedagogical soundness and an
application of the general array of knowledge advocated by the taxonomy of
Educational Objectives: Cognitive, by Benjamin Bloom. Nearly every professor
submitted all of the final exams that were administered. the dean of faculty reviewed the
tests and determined that virtually all of the exams were thoughtfully constructed and
implemented an adequate array of questions. No significant recommendations arose from
this assessment; therefore, although the dean of faculty retains the right to collect samples
of final exams, the process was temporarily suspended (spring 1999) as a matter of
efficiency.

Faculty Mentoring
anew initiative in faculty mentoring of new instructors and professors was implemented
for the three new hires of the 1999-2000 school year. Each mentor was given an
orientation, a list of proposed activities and topics, and a small allowance ($300).
Mentors were encouraged to perform a classroom observation, take part in a social
outing, and commit to regular meetings for prayer and support.

<a id="ga28-p434"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=436 printed_page=434 -->

to assess the effectiveness of the program, a brief email questionnaire was distributed to
the mentors and their charges, with the following general responses:
1. What were some positive experiences in the program9
• Class observations
• Office visits
• Meals together
• $300 budget (two responses)
• Got to know another family and the area (two responses)
• Answers to practical questions
• Advice (from a mentor)
2. What were some problems with the program9
• None (four responses)
• difficulty making connections because of extenuating circumstances (two
responses)
3. what general advice do you have for the future of the program?
• Start it earlier (it began after the school year)
• a clearer communication of governance
• Maintain the present course for a few years

Recommendation
The program will be maintained for the 1999-2000 school year and it will begin prior to
faculty retreat

Faith Statements
as apart of the application process at covenant College, prospective students respond to
a demanding application form. One question on the form provides an opportunity for
students to express a personal statement of faith. During assessment day, juniors are
given the opportunity to rewrite that statement, and a team of trained rate rs evaluates the
statements for any changes.

The question asks: Please tell us about your personal walk with the Lord. Ideally, your
testimony should communicate a personal faith that is alive and active, descriptive of who
you are uniquely in Christ. Show how God has used your family background, your
experiences at Covenant, and individual characteristics to make you unique and uniquely
His.

a group of second-year graduate students were trained in rating change in statements of
faith according to a scale of 1 to 5 from strong regression of faith to strong progression of
faith respectively across three areas of faith application: 1) with God; 2) with others; and
3) with the creation. Inter-rater reliability was established and a mean of progression was
documented in all three areas.
1. with God = 4.4 Std. Dev. = .6
2. with others = 4.0 Std. Dev. = .8
3. with creation = 3.7 Std. Dev. = .8

<a id="ga28-p435"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=437 printed_page=435 -->

Growth was indicated in all three areas with significant overlap within confidence levels.
the nature of the question likely resulted in the higher ratings for relationship with god
than others or creation It is positive to note that when students give testimony to their
faith they include these other areas, and that should be seen as a tribute to the college

Math Placement Value Added:
Similarly, a math placement testis given at the beginning of the freshman year and is re
administered to juniors during assessment day the test has a possibles core of 60 points
The following results were tabulated for this year Data was collected from 45 students
who took both tests One subject was not considered because he/she took a course at
another institution the portion of students who took at least one math course at
Covenant (n=20) had a mean improvement of 2.75 points, and the portion of students
who did not take a math course (n=24) had a mean decrease of 2 21 points.

The increase for students participating in a math course indicated an increased
proficiency, and the decrease for non-participants indicated what could be considered a
natural diminishing of skills due to non-use

Major Field Achievement Tests (MFATS)
On assessment day in April, students from ten of the majors take the major fields
Achievement Tests. The scores are reported by discipline according to mean percentile
rank. The college has a generally strong showing considering that an average college
would rank at the 50th percentile in all areas. it is notable that eight of the ten disciplines
had a mean score at or above the 60th percentile The two departments with lower scores
had small sample sizes and the results would be considered too small to be generalizable.
Given the large fluctuation in sample size from year to year, a study was conducted to
determine a 6-year average percentile ranking. Our student average was in the 59th
percentile. This means only 41% of the college graduates scored better than our average
during that time.

Each department receives a detailed report and will incorporate this year’s findings into
next year’s departmental assessments. The percentile rankings with sub-scores and
assessment criteria follow.

Senior Integration Papers (SIPs)
every department on campus provides aversion of a senior integration paper the
specific assignment varies from major to major but is to be rated on a four-item, eightpoint scale. the purpose of the paper is to provide a cap stone experience for the student
and an assessment for the department. Students are to select a topic or project and to
approach its study ina scholarly manner from a christian world and life view.
professors rate the performance of the students relative to written expression, oral
expression, content of the discipline, and the integration of faith.

Seventy-three sets of data from nine departments were reported to the office of
academic affairs. In general, students scored consistently high on all four scales with
little difference between. considering the standard errors of the means, all of the total
means overlap significantly at the 95% confidence level (+/- 1.96 x SeM). The
integration of faith rating remains slightly lower, perhaps due to raised expectations in
this area. The mean scores were:

<a id="ga28-p438"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=440 printed_page=438 -->

Mean SD SeM
Written expression 6.4 13 .14
Oral communication 6.5 1.2 .13
Integration of faith 6.0 1.3 .13
content of discipline 6.4 1.2 .12

the means of individual departments roughly followed those above. The only
statistically significant variation was with the business department that had a mean for
the integration of faith category of 2.5 However, the sample of 2 is likely not
generalizable to the department.

the correlation study indicated a strong interaction between and among all of the items
All combinations were statistically significant and practically significant (r> .5 creating
and r2 > .25 variation of the first). Most notable was the high correlation between
written expression and the content of the discipline, and between written expression and
oral expression. those who wrote better also presented a stronger understanding of the
discipline and communicated more effectively with their speech.

Recommendations
1 Although we experienced an increase in reporting (from 73 to 98) more careful
follow-up to insure a full complement of scores is needed.
2. an investigation of the slightly lower faith integration scores is in order to see if
the difference is a rater reliability problem or if it is an area for more significant
attention in the programs of the college.
3. Instruction at the departmental level needs continued emphasis, especially in light
of the high correlation noted above.

Student Contact Program A new program was initiated to make purposeful
connection between freshmen and professors. The student contact program grouped six
freshmen with a willing faculty member who was to make purposeful contact each
month through individual conversations, electronic connections, and small group
gatherings. Modest stipends and allowances were given to the faculty ($300 each).
Faculty were oriented in the early fall and a general assessment meeting was held on
December 9, 1998. At that point, less than 60 percent of the students selected were
engaged in the program. (See graph below.) Although some purposeful contacts were
made and monitored, it was obvious that the program was falling short of its intended
outcomes. this general assessment of the new initiative added momentum to the
decision to organize anew freshman experience through the self in society core course.
details of that program will be included in next year’s report.

Quest
Quest has continued to grow. This year a new location was started in Rome, GA, with
34 students enrolled. Quest graduated a record 133 students in May, 1999. The college
revenue budget was built on the assumption that Quest would contribute $220,000. In
fact, the Quest revenue contribution was $378,000. Quest had 341 tuition payments for
the 1998-1999 academic year, which also represents a record number of students. The
new quest budget goal of $295,000, which is accompanied by new overhead costs
relating to staff and faculty additions, will require another record enrollment year.

quest developed three areas of assessment with specific intended outcomes:

1. Students will report at least one year past their graduation a positive conceptual and
practical impact of the quest curriculum on their work performance. They will also
report on whether a particular course had a positive impact on their world and life view.

Results: For the fourth consecutive year scores for the survey were high. evaluations of
the ten Quest modules resulted in positive impact scores ranging from 90% to 100%.
Concepts affecting their career/personal development resulted in positive scores ranging
from 95% to 98%. The two modules rated the highest by students were “Group and
Organization Behavior” and “Business Communications” at 96%. The lowest rated
modules were “Readings In Organization Management” and “Personnel Management”
at 89%. “Philosophy of Culture” was ranked by 93% of the students a shaving a positive
impact on their world and life view, with 92% reporting this impact for our old and new
testament courses. both of these scores were slightly higher than the scores of last
year’s survey.

<a id="ga28-p440"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=442 printed_page=440 -->

2. Students will indicate that their Quest educational experience has provided a
positive contribution in their career/personal life in areas related to: Decision making,
problem-solving, analytical ability; relational skills, and self-image.

Results: The Summer 1998 survey for the nine career/personal development categories
again resulted in very positive feedback all categories had positive scores of either
96% or 94%.

3. independent review of six quest research Projects. Dr. Tom Gavin, who holds
the dec os imo chair of excellence at the university of tennessee at Chattanooga,
reviewed twelve randomly selected research projects.

Results: Dr. Gavin reported that the Research model and student work within that
model was satisfactory in terms of goals and quality.

Curriculum/Module Revisions
In 1998 three courses in the quest program were revised ora rein the process of being
revised.
• Module 5 - Strategic Management was revised by Dean Cleavenger.
• Module 7 - Creative Problem Solving was also revised by Dino Smith.
• the biblical literature and doctrine module is in the early stages of revision by
ray clark of the bible department

master of education program
the faculty of the master of education program held their annual assessment meeting
on August 26, 1998, and reviewed the following six stated outcomes for the program:

1. A biblical world-view is applied to theoretical and research
understandings in education.
2. Educational theory is applied to professional practice.
3. School administrators and supervisors are equipped to assume leadership
in schools.
4. classroom teachers are equipped to bring about greater integration of
curricula within schools.
5. Teachers and administrators are equipped to facilitate needed change
within their schools.
6. teachers and administrators are equipped to describe the role of school
in society.

Each stated outcome was measured and the results evaluated. The department felt all six
outcomes were being met at an acceptable level and no major changes were
recommended.

<a id="ga28-p441"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=443 printed_page=441 -->

Faculty Profile
the faculty of covenant college provides the major learning input opportunities for our
students. a college should be known and evaluated by th equality of education offered
and the learning outcomes of the students who go through that process. Therefore, an
examination of the faculty teaching at covenant will provide important evidence of
quality opportunities.

an important part of the student’s education aimed at meeting these goals is the core
curriculum. As stated in the Academic Bulletin, “the core curriculum is based on the
faculty ’s belief that a liberal arts education should be broad and inclusive, introducing
students to ideas and values of continuing concern and providing them with historical
and spiritual perspectives on the complex problems of our society.”

covenant demonstrates the importance of the core curriculum in the educational
experience by ensuring that the same professors who teach in the major curriculum staff
these courses. The following analysis includes the entire core as listed in the catalog,
except for courses that are sometimes taken for other than core requirements, such as
the humanities electives and lab science requirements.

This past year 30 faculty members taught in the CORE curriculum, which represents
56% of the full-time teaching faculty involved in the CORE. of those 30, 3 were
faculty with administrative duties and 76.7% hold earned doctorates or the highest
degree in their field. Adjunct faculty taught only 13 .3% of the total credit hours in the
CORE

the length of a contract offered to a faculty member at covenant college is dependent
on quality of teaching, the longevity of service, and a scholarly presentation which
demonstrates integration of the discipline and the christian faith. the board of trustees
approves a five-year contract (tenure) when faculty have demonstrated a high level of
quality over a period of time. following is a table showing the current status of faculty

The earned doctorate degree is held by 83.7% of the full-time teaching faculty. Once
again, this is an all-time high for Covenant College.

longevity of highly qualified faculty speaks well for the academic opportunities

Academic Support
The academic administration division provides resources to support the academic
activities of the college. While these academic activities are inputs to the educational
experience, they demonstrate the level and quality of academic opportunities available
at Covenant College and play an important role in student outcomes.

Women In The Church Lecture Series
Dr. James Skillen, director of the center for public justice in Washington, served as
the guest lecturer for the third annual WIC Lecture Series. He taught a one credit course
entitled “Public Christianity”, which examined the role of the christian in government,
politics and public justice. Dr. Skillen also gave two Chapel presentations entitled
“Fruits That Will Last”, and “Side by Side: Men and Women Glorifying Their
Creator.”

<a id="ga28-p443"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=445 printed_page=443 -->

Diversity Scholarship Recipient Program (DSR)
In the 1998-99 academic year, the DSR Program provided $349,049 in financial
assistance for 69 students, these disbursements combined Diversity Scholarship monies
and Community Foundation monies. of the 69 students this year, ten graduated, while
four have been awarded a diversity scholarship fora fifth year of study. of the
remaining 55 students, 11 did not reapply for the scholarship or became ineligible for a
variety of reasons. The remaining 44 students returned this fall. This fall there were
twenty-two (22) new awards and one reinstated award to a former DSR student,
bringing the total of d sr students to 71

The DSR Program initiated the first annual Diversity Lecture Program, which was
entitled “Possibilities for the 21st Century: Racial Reconciliation through Community,
Church and Friendship.” The two primary speakers, dan hayes of atlanta community
Ministries, and tom fritz of inter cultural Resources, CCFC shared from their own lifeand-work experiences practical ways in which reconciliation between ethnic groups has
occurred and can potentially occur in a number of settings such as the church, the
community, and the workplace. Sixty-six individuals attended the Saturday sessions.
several utc upward bound students were in attendance along with a number of people
connected with New City Fellowship

Technical Services/Instructional Technology
Two administrative changes have resulted in better technology services for the
academic community. In July 1998, jeff barr assumed the role of director of
instructional technology and in october of the same year, Marjorie Crocker became
the Chief Information Officer.

In the 1998-99 school year ninety-three (93) machines were maintained for student use.
The Macintosh lab on campus was replaced with a Windows NT lab. In addition, ten
(10) faculty and nineteen (19) staff received new or upgraded machines and an
additional sixteen (16) faculty received software upgrades.

Major changes were made in the servers used throughout the college The DEC Alpha
2100 e-mail server, WYCLIFFE, was replaced with a Dell PowerEdge dual 333 mHz
RAID 5 27GB hard drive with 256 MB RAM and the software upgraded to the latest
version of microsoft exchange server (5.5sp2) Additional server changes included a
new Dell PowerEdge server, LUTHER, dual 350 mHz, RAID 5 27GB hard drive with
128 MB RAM for faculty network storage space and instructional technology software,
a new Dell PowerEdge server, EDWARDS, dual 333 mHz, RAID 5 27GB hard drive
with 128 MB RAM to improve service to the Records and Admissions Offices and a
new Dell PowerEdge server, WHITEFIELD, 333 mHz, RAID 5 12GB hard drive with
128 MB RAM to serve the development department.

technology services also assisted the library with the implementation of the gay lord
Polaris system, an access 7 database for the purchasing department, the conversion of all
giving records and donor/alumni/church address information on the development

<a id="ga28-p444"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=446 printed_page=444 -->

department’s new Blackbaud Raiser’s Edge system, and the conversion of the accounting office to a Windows version of Blackbaud’s Student Billing.

The telephone department of technology services ran all of the fiber, cat.3 (voice) and cat.5 (data) lines necessary for the addition of Maclellan Hall, had additional local trunks installed to meet the growing demand for local lines due to the increase in the number of students, and completed all necessary programming for the telephone in-house. Additional upgraded from coax wiring to cat.5 increased the reliability and speed of the network in the affected staff offices and permitted connection for the Bagpipe and Tartan offices to the campus computer network.

This year, the Covenant College website (http://www.covenant.edu) underwent a major overhaul. A new “look and feel” was implemented to better complement other promotional materials produced by the school. Perhaps the most popular new feature was the searchable alumni database.

## Experiential Studies

Thirty-three (33) Covenant students participated in semester-long off-campus study programs for the 1998-1999 academic year. Fourteen of the students participated in study abroad experiences designed by other institutions, while Dr. Nicholas Barker and Dr. Joseph Clumpner led student groups to Slovakia and China, respectively. The Slovak program represents a joint venture with Mission To The World, with missionaries John and Kathy Lesondak formally assisting the program.

Sixty-eight (68) Covenant students spent their spring break on short-term missions projects. Five staff members, one former staff member, one faculty member, and one faculty spouse led the groups to Scotland, Ireland, New York City, Jamaica, and Peru.

## Library

A library committee was formed Fall 1998 to visit the libraries of other colleges in the region, in order to make comparisons with the Kresge Library. The committee, consisting of library personnel, faculty, staff, and students visited University of the South, Berry College, Trevecca College, Belmont College, and Middle Tennessee State University. A number of needs were identified as a result of this visit, with corresponding suggestions made to the faculty sub-committee of the three-year planning committee regarding space, aesthetics, technology, materials, staff, perception, and location centrality. The Library ordered a new materials detection and security system, along with additional compact shelving for better space utilization. The Library also received from Georgia’s Private Academic Libraries a $14,270 grant. It will be used for re-wiring the first floor of the Library and wiring of the second floor with a portion of the award going toward the related campus-wide upgrade.

This year the library has cataloged 1,033 new titles, comprising 1,352 printed volumes in all classification areas and non-book items totaling 99 volume equivalents. Withdrawn from the collection were 237 items. The budget for library materials was divided into

<a id="ga28-p445"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=447 printed_page=445 -->

60% for periodical subscriptions, database subscriptions and microfilm, and 40% for
books and non-book media.

The interlibrary loan services increased 43% over last year processing 2,896 transactions
through OCLC affiliated libraries. In addition to loans made through OCLC affiliated
libraries, a substantial number of periodical articles we resupplied through full-text
document delivery services such as DIALOG, JSTOR, and many databases through the
GALILEO system. the increasing availability of whole books via full-text file transfer
through the internet would seem to offset the demand for works supplied by means of
librarians, yet more books are being sent out from our library to supply the requests of
libraries elsewhere, and our patrons continue to request books and articles not available
here.

For the third consecutive year, we sent our standard survey to SIP students to gather
information about satisfaction with library services. The survey showed a high
satisfaction (81% and above) for access to adequate learning resources, catalogs,
indexes, bibliographies, outside materials indexes, bibliographies; interlibrary loan
service; organization of collections, hours of operation, and items easy to find The two
lowest satisfaction categories were collections and databases (73%) and facilities for
print/non-print materials (63%). Interlibrary loan services results improved from 61% to
81% over the past year, while collections and databases improved from 39% to 73% in
this year’s survey.

Audio-Visuals Department
During the Fall 1998 semester, the sound system in the Great Hall was upgraded
specifically to accommodate the Madrigal Dinner. areas of the great hall that had not
previously been serviced with PA - verandah and fireplace areas - are now serviced

The sound system in Sanderson 215 was upgraded during the summer, 1999. The
upgrade was precipitated by the “death” of the sound board and concerns from the
chow iii course regarding th equality of sound for playing stereo CD’s. This was a
timely upgrade a sit is tied with installation of a “smart cart” and video projector in S-
215 there will now be capability of providing stereo sound and video projection of
almost any signal.

The workload for AV Services has steadily increased over the past few years. An
additional work study position was approved for the 1999-2000 academic year, in part
to help meet the demand for anticipated faculty AV support in the Sanderson and Mills
smart classrooms.

Section 2: Student Development

The residence life environment must promote and enhance student learning Students
regularly assess whether or not living on campus is conducive to study and growth The

1998 and 1999 Student Satisfaction Surveys (SSS) illustrate our students’ view of

than both national colleges and CCCU norms.

higher” (98) and “higher” (98) in importance and “higher” in satisfaction
than both national colleges and CCCU norms.
• 97 - 99 residence hall regulations “higher (97)” and “equal to or higher”
(98) in importance and “higher” in satisfaction than both national colleges and
CCCU norms.

higher” (98) in importance than national norms and “higher” in satisfaction
than both national colleges and CCCU norms.

“higher” in satisfaction than both national colleges and CCCU norms.

Grants received to benefit the college’s freshmen year experience, a biblical
understanding of family and scholarship
In May 1998, the college received a grant from the U.S. Department of Education’s Fund for
the Improvement of Post-secondary Education (FIPSE). For the first year of a two-year award,
the office of student development facilitated a review of the “Freshmen Year Experience.” Two
areas targeted for review included academic advising and the early warning system

The results are as follows:
1 The Covenant College FIPSE team visited Eastern College to participate in the
“Enhancing Student success through First-year Program Reform.”
2 the act survey for academic advising was incorporated into the assessment of
the college.
3 New students will experience a revised Self in Society (SIS) freshmen course.
Changes to the course include:
• A revised SIS syllabus;
• SIS instructors trained to advise more effectively,
• Classes were scheduled during orientation week to emphasize the
academic purpose and priorities of an academic culture,
• More sections were added to the class to improve our faculty/new-
student ratio,
• Section instructors will continue to advise their advisees after orientation
their students thereby increasing contact and information flow, and
• Coordination between the SIS syllabus and the Career Development
Center was implemented
4. members of the covenant college f ip se team attended the council of
Independent College’s Conference on developing and Academic culture.

<a id="ga28-p447"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=449 printed_page=447 -->

5. members of the faculty planning Committee, Chaplain’s Office, faculty and
office of student development attended the trinity international University’s
conference on spiritual formation with a specific charge of reviewing the first-
year experience at Covenant.
6. allocation off ip se funds for:
• Site Visit to Eastern College
• survey of academic advising
• Operating Supplies
• Faculty Development Workshop in May 1999
• Faculty breakfasts for SIS
• Spiritual Formation Conference
• Fees for CIC conference Creating Cultures in Learning
