THE REPORT OF THE COMMITTEE ON MISSION TO NORTH AMERICA TO THE 28TH GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

#### I. INTRODUCTION

Advancing the Gospel into the 21st Century

"The harvest is plentiful but the workers are few. Ask the Lord of the harvest, therefore, to send out workers into His harvest field."
Matthew 9:37-38

As we find ourselves moving into the 21st century, our Lord's command to pray for harvest workers is perhaps more relevant than ever before in the history of North America. The greatest calling of all is the call to prayer, for it is only the Lord Who can send out the laborers, and only His Spirit Who can work in the hearts of the lost to transform them. We look forward with great anticipation to see what the Lord purposes for us as His people as we wait on Him. At the heart of our advance must be our commitment to plant churches that are Gospel-centered and Gospel-driven.

The greatest challenge, as we then "put feet to our prayers," is in identifying more men who, with their families, will follow the Lord's call to serve as church planters, campus ministers, chaplains and revitalizing pastors. Churches and presbyteries are taking more and more initiative in planting churches and beginning campus ministries. Everywhere, church planters are sought to respond to the opportunities that surround us in a culture increasingly devoid of Gospel influence.

Churches and presbyteries are increasingly taking up the call to reach our campuses with the Gospel. The armed services offer the PCA the opportunity to fill many more chaplain positions than we have thus far been able to fill. Let us join together as a denomination in praying for more workers in these crucial positions, and let us give every encouragement and training opportunity for those who have the gifts and experience to answer these calls, even as we pray that the Lord will send them forth.

We remind the General Assembly that with every passing year North America is increasingly a "foreign" mission field. California today is a multicultural state, for over 50% of its population is of ethnic diversity. By the year 2050, it is estimated that Hispanics will become the predominant people group in the United States. Let us unite

<a id="ga28-p517"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=519 printed_page=517 -->

in special prayer for the Lord's provision to call committed and equipped ministers of the Gospel who can effectively reach the many ethnic groups and cultures that increase in number rapidly throughout North America.

In this entire endeavor, evangelism must be at the core. Last October 19-21, GA/MNA organized an Evangelism Convocation, held at Briarwood Presbyterian Church in Birmingham, Alabama. The keynote speakers were TE Jim Kennedy, TE Ligon Duncan, TE Paul Kooistra, TE Bryan Chapell and TE Tim Keller. Breakout sessions were led by Frank Barker, Stephen Beck, Rick Downs, Charles Dunahoo, Preston Graham, Fred Harrell, Barry Henning, Archie Parrish, Randy Pope, Mark Roessler, Kennedy Smartt and John Smed. Around 400 teaching elders, ruling elders and lay people participated in this gathering. Let us pray that the Lord will continue to bear fruit from the teaching and fellowship experienced in this gathering.

Yes, the fields are still "white unto harvest" and the "laborers are few," but by God's grace we will call church planters to labor among us to reap a harvest of souls. We will call campus ministers to labor on the college campuses. We will call chaplains and urban and mercy ministry workers to proclaim the riches of Christ crucified. We will support the leaders in churches where renewal is so needed. By God's grace, we pray that the Gospel will be advanced in ever-greater measure through the PCA!

1999 was a year of transition and challenge for the GA/MNA staff. We believe that the Lord has provided a strong staff team to lead the PCA in the next stages of reaching North America with the Gospel. We ask the prayers of the General Assembly, that the GA/MNA staff may be faithful stewards of all that is entrusted to them, and most of all may be led by God's Spirit in providing the leadership and coordination needed toward the advancement of the Lord's Kingdom through the PCA. While more detail for each ministry is given in section II, we summarize here a few highlights from each:

Church Planting: We continue to see growth in "grassroots" participation in church planting, as churches and presbyteries increasingly sponsor church planting. The cities continue to be a place in which the Lord is working - through the PCA - to build His Church. At the same time, there is growth in reaching every kind of community - large and small, growing and non-growing, in every region of North America.

Multicultural Ministries: We rejoice in the emergence of solid movements in church planting among the Korean, Haitian, Brazilian and African American communities. In addition, an increasing number of PCA church plants are truly multicultural - bringing together, by the power of the Gospel, people of diverse cultural and ethnic backgrounds into particular churches.

Campus Ministries: We rejoice in the rapid growth of the number of campuses served by Reformed University Ministries. With ministries on 48 campuses already, an additional 9-10 are in the planning stages to begin in the fall of 2000.

<a id="ga28-p518"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=520 printed_page=518 -->

Urban and Mercy Ministries: Through the 1998 WIC Love Gift for GA/MNA Multicultural Ministries, and the 1999 PCA Women in the Church Conference, we believe that 1999 was a year of great advancement in urban and mercy ministries for the PCA. Ongoing follow-up activities are planned to assist churches in initiating and enhancing these ministries.

Church Vitality: Through the various conferences and schools of prayer, the Lord is at work in growing a base of PCA people who pray and watch for revival, while working for more vital churches. Recognition of the need for renewal in PCA churches continues to grow.

Chaplain Ministries: The armed services continue to request the commissioning of many additional chaplains from the PCA. Thank the Lord for the appreciation the military has for the work of PCA ministers of the Gospel. It is a cause for great rejoicing that PCA teaching elders would be sought after by a military in such a secular society.

We remind the 28th General Assembly of the GA/MNA Vision Statement, with its emphasis on establishing reproducing churches and presbyteries:

Joyfully depending on our sovereign God, the persistent prayers of God's people, a practice of biblical stewardship of time, talent and treasure, we will work in cooperation with the other PCA denominational committees and agencies to fill the United States and Canada with reproducing churches and presbyteries.

- These will be churches that are Christ-centered, spiritually healthy, worshipping, evangelizing, theologically sound, culturally relevant, Presbyterian churches of all sizes - small, medium and large.

- These will be churches and presbyteries that are faithful messengers, dependent on the Holy Spirit to bring the life-changing power of the Gospel of Jesus Christ to those living in rural, suburban and urban areas, transforming every arena of life and culture through preaching grace, doing justice and showing mercy.

- These will be churches and presbyteries that incorporate the varied ethnic, cultural, socioeconomic and language groups throughout the region by cultivating reconciliation and planting churches.

- These will be churches and presbyteries that serve the whole church of Jesus Christ through propagation of campus ministries, chaplain ministries, and mercy ministries, preaching the Gospel and spreading rich biblical teaching throughout North America.

Our concluding challenge is that we seek the Lord together and work together in reaching North America for Christ. For this to occur, the Lord will have to send forth

<a id="ga28-p519"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=521 printed_page=519 -->

laborers who are obedient to the Great Commission. May we renew our commitment to become a Gospel centered, Gospel driven denomination to the glory of God and the advancement of His Kingdom.

## II. MINISTRIES

### A. Church Planting

GA/MNA Church Planting takes its direction from the key themes reflected in the introduction to this report: our Lord's calling is to be His servants in advancing the Gospel into the 21st century in North America, through church planting. We respond to this calling, recognizing that the harvest is plentiful but the laborers few. Perhaps as never before, the mission field of North America is ready for harvest.

Even as the challenge of the harvest grows greater, so does the vision of the Presbyterian Church in America to respond to this opportunity for the advancement of the Gospel. In ever greater numbers, churches and presbyteries are increasing their commitment to church planting. Resources are being committed to the advancement of the Church in greater measure.

Many communities considered sufficiently "churched" even in the recent past are now ripe for the Gospel. The great challenge is the same as that of our Lord's time: where are the laborers? As churches and presbyteries grow in their vision for church planting, this is the single greatest need that must be addressed. GA/MNA Church Planting offers to the churches and presbyteries of the PCA services to assist them in selecting, placing and equipping church planters.

For the PCA to truly become a church planting movement, the following eight key result areas are essential. GA/MNA focuses its resources in building these services:

- Concerted Prayer
- Recruiting Church Planters
- Assessing Potential Church Planters
- Training Church Planters
- Assisting Church Planters in Fund Raising
- Placing Church Planters on the Field
- Coaching Church Planters in the Process
- Building a Denominational Team of Church Planting Leadership

Concerted Prayer: Prayer is the foundation of all Christian endeavors. It is through prayer that God works to advance His Kingdom. It is significant that our Lord's command is to pray that He will send the laborers. We call upon the General Assembly to join in focused, concerted prayer for the advancement of the Kingdom through church planting. Only if the Spirit of God is at work through our efforts will our efforts be effective in advancing the Kingdom.

<a id="ga28-p520"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=522 printed_page=520 -->

By God’s power, lives are transformed by the Gospel as we wait upon Him in prayer. GA/MNA communication pieces, *Multiply* and the web site (www.pcanet.org/MNA), give a high priority to prayer by suggesting weekly prayer requests on a regional basis for the advancement of the church. The *Intercessor* for all regions may be accessed on the web site, offering greater opportunity for intercession for ministry across North America.

**Recruiting Church Planters:** Record numbers of assessed and qualified church planters have been presented to the GA/MNA Committee for approval during the last two regular meetings of the committee. We are seeing more men available to start churches than ever before. At the same time, the need for church planters continually grows, as churches and presbyteries develop church planting movements. *As churches increase their Partnership Share/Askings giving, more resources will be devoted to recruiting church planters.* In particular, GA/MNA is searching for an additional staff member whose focus of ministry will be church planter recruiting.

**Bi-Vocational Church Planting:** Historically the PCA has used, almost exclusively, church planters who serve full-time in church planting. In order to increase the number of men who can enter the harvest fields, it is our goal to encourage, support, recruit, train, and place bi-vocational church planters. There is strong biblical and historic precedent for “tentmakers” who earn their living in the marketplace, even as they labor also in the Gospel. Today’s technological climate particularly offers the possibility of employment that pays well but is flexible enough to provide the church planter the opportunity to earn most or all of his living in the marketplace.

**Assessing Potential Church Planters:** For more than 15 years, GA/MNA has worked to develop the church planter assessment process into its current format. The primary focus is on spiritual competencies, the church planter and his family’s calling to this task, his heart commitment to evangelism and other biblically defined qualities. *There is ever greater variety in the leadership styles of the men qualified by GA/MNA assessment for church planting, and there is more and more variety also in the kinds of communities to which men are going to plant churches – large and small populations, growing and non-growing, and in all regions of North America.* Every community and region is considered a potential place for people to be reached with the Gospel, through church planters who are uniquely equipped and called to reach those communities.

**Training Church Planters:** GA/MNA offers four different types of church planter training, each addressing different needs. *All PCA church planters, whether or not they participated in the GA/MNA assessment process, and regardless of their funding sources or sources of oversight, are invited to take advantage of these training resources.*

1.  The National Training Conference — Offered August 7-10 in the Atlanta area for all PCA church planters, their wives, leaders in church planting churches,

<a id="ga28-p521"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=523 printed_page=521 -->

presbytery GA/MNA chairmen and committee members, and church planting network leadership. This training is “curriculum driven” which means that the various seminars and workshops are designed to respond to the different phases of the life of a church plant (start-up phase, ministry building phase, organization phase, and the reproduction phase). There will also be training seminars for presbytery GA/MNA chairmen to better equip them to provide proactive leadership in their presbyteries.

2. Regional Training Conferences — The curriculum is complementary to the national training. However, by taking advantage of the smaller numbers than attend the national conference, and by addressing issues unique to each region, these events provide a more personal level of interaction and individualized coaching.

3. Boot camp — A workshop oriented training experience over several days, designed to provide the environment and coaching to assist the church planter in the development of working plans such as Philosophy of Ministry, and Church Planting Strategy for his new church start. This training focuses in greater detail on the initial stages of the church plant.

4. Coaching Retreats — These retreats are designed with the individualized needs of the church planter in mind. The group is small, and the ratio of church planters to coaches as small as possible (2 to 1). The coaches are experienced church planters who are skilled in the process of assisting the church planter in working through the unique needs of his particular church planting experience.

Church planting apprenticeships are a further means of training many additional church planters. Men who are new seminary graduates, or who have a need to especially sharpen particular skills for church planting, work under the mentorship of an experienced church planter for 1-3 years. Following this structured training experience, the apprentice then goes out to plant a church, often in a community close to that in which he experienced his apprenticeship. Through apprenticeships, many more men are being trained as church planters.

Assisting Church Planters in Fund Raising: We do not know of a situation where a church planter was not placed on the field because of a lack of funds. However, GA/MNA’s role in the funding process is changing. Following the universal trends in benevolent giving today, churches and individuals committed to church planting prefer to support church planters they know personally or they prefer to use their financial support to bring church plants to particular kinds of communities.

GA/MNA continues to seek to develop funding for church plants in all kinds of communities. In most cases, because of the personal relationships church planters have, GA/MNA staff time is used most effectively in assisting the church planter in his fund raising efforts. Why is this strategy, which looks a lot like the traditional missionary approach to funding, more effective? First, churches and individuals are willing to give more generously when there is a very personal tie with the church planter. Second, there is something very healthy about the process of a church planter casting his vision

<a id="ga28-p522"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=524 printed_page=522 -->

to potential partners and building the relationship with them that will serve to sustain
him not only with funding, but certainly also with prayer, as he attempts the dijficidt
task of church planting.

the timothy fund for church planting apprentices is a special fund from which
support is committed to church planting apprentices. Begun in 1998, the fund has now
received total contributions of $385,000 from churches, individuals and foundations.
This fund provides a significant supplemental funding for many apprentices.

Placing Church Planters on the Field: The placement process begins with a church
planter committing to a set of biblical values, which God has put on his heart. These
values form the foundation of a vision that will shape the church. After the vision is
solidified and documented, the location that will best facilitate this vision is then
determined, by the mutual agreement and sense of the lord ’s call to the church planter,
and the presbytery, in consultation with the GA/'MNA church planting staff. GA/MNA
staff then offers assistance to the presbytery and the church planter in the many practical
steps and administrative details that are necessary to begin the work.

Coaching Church Planters in the Process: In addition to the coaching retreats
mentioned above, GA/'MNA offers the church planter direct consultation by staff, and
assistance in establishing mentoring relationships fo reach church plcmter. Regional
coordinators, movement leaders and church planting network leaders also play a major
role in the coaching process, or in identifying others who may assist the church planter.

building a denominational team of church planting Leadership: as the vision for
church planting continues to grow among particular churches, presbyteries and among
church planting networks (which include a wide variety of formal and informal
structures), GA/MNA fulfills a growing role of serving, coordinating and taking the
initiative to develop the resources necessary for the vision for an advancing church to
be fulfilled. GA/MNA brings this leadership through several avenues:

Regional Coordinators: We continue to build the regional church planting team We
have added a region for the Rocky Mountains, divided the Southeast Region into two
territories to gain more impact, and plan to add new regional coordinators in the future.
All regional coordinators serve on a limited part-time or volunteer basis.

Presbytery GA/MNA Chairmen: Currently, GA/MNA works closely with many
presbytery GA/MNA chairmen as they provide church planting leadership in their
presbyteries. A future goal is to increase the training, resources and coordination of
activity available to presbytery chairmen.

Church Planting Networks: GA/MNA is active in the process of encouraging the
development of church planting networks. a church planting network is simply a group
of churches who combine time, talent and treasure to plant churches more effectively
together than they could do separately. Some are highly structured and defined, while

<a id="ga28-p523"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=525 printed_page=523 -->

others are more informal. GA/MNA encourages all such efforts that enhance the ability
of particular churches and pres by teri es to fulfill their calling to build church planting
movements.

B. Multicultural Ministries

state of the task

as we reflect on events of 1999, there are clear signs that PCA commitment to
communicating the Gospel among ethnic groups that are rapidly growing in North
America continues to increase. Around the year 2056 the growth of ethnic population
groups will be such that over 50% of the total population will be non-white. The PCA,

<a id="ga28-p525"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=527 printed_page=525 -->

historically motivated and recognized for its Matthew 28 Great Commission zeal and
efforts, now has an unprecedented cross-cultural evangelistic opportunity and challenge
right at its own front door.
The growing interest in multicultural ministries and church planting is observed in part
in the following ways:

• Church and presbytery leaders communicate frequently with GA/MNA personnel
seeking help in evangelizing ethnic groups as part of the changing demographics
surrounding their churches;

• an increasing number of second-generation leaders from various ethnic
backgrounds are entering reformed seminaries and non-traditional presbytery
sponsored leadership training programs,

• Presbyteries are designing and implementing non-traditional ways to train and
credential Teaching Elders, including ethnic leaders who are emerging from within
their boundaries, without lowering the standards established by the PCA General Assembly.

• Articles in Multiply and Intercessor have highlighted multicultural church planting
and have generated much interest and response.

• There is an increasing interest by some presbyteries, churches, and church planters
in starting churches that are intentional in reaching the ethnic populations in their
geographical areas.

the purpose of GA/M NA’s multicultural thrust is to encourage and initiate reformed
church planting among ethnic people that results in Gospel movements that saturate
these people groups.

The term multicultural is used in two ways. (1) to describe the general work of ethnic specific church planting through which congregations a reformed that are largely
homogeneous and non-white, (2) To describe churches whose leaders and members are
blended together from a variety of ethnic backgrounds worshipping and serving God.

Advances in 1999

• Veteran MTW missionary/church planter TIE Tim McKeown was called by
GA/MNA to begin work as GA/MNA’s Multicultural Ministries Coordinator on
October 1st Since TE McKeown’s cross-cultural experience is with Hispanic
peoples, we believe the Lord has provided leadership to assist the PCA in advancing
the Gospel among the many Hispanic groups now represented in North America.

• Ethnic and multicultural church planters continue to be assessed, placed and
coached.

<a id="ga28-p526"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=528 printed_page=526 -->

- We are actively preparing for the assessment of new ethnic church planters by trained leaders from within their own ethnic contexts. This approach allows for more effective assessment that takes into account the uniqueness of each culture. Training of Haitian leaders and preparing of materials in Creole has been done anticipating a Haitian Assessment Center in 2000. Hispanic materials have also been completed and utilized on a trial basis. Significant progress has also been made in preparing for an African American assessment.

- In addition to the major Korean first-generation participation in the PCA, three other ethnic movements are growing: the Brazilian, with half a dozen churches planting more churches, under the leadership of TE Valter Moura; the African American, with a strong apprenticeship program being led by TE Louis Wilson; and the Haitian, with four churches or missions, and a number of leaders in seminary being led by TE Dony St. Germain. In addition, under the leadership of TE Randy Nabors, the network of churches involved in urban and mercy ministries continues to grow.

- The most significant advance in terms of addressing the need for more ethnic church planters is the focus on training ethnic leaders through the use of apprenticeship programs. Louis Wilson is now in his third year of providing a strong model in the African-American community, with one apprentice having been sent to begin a new PCA African-American church plant in the Chicago suburbs. Dony St. Germain’s approach with Haitian leaders is preparing church planters to begin church plants in key cities like Chicago with plans for church planting in New York City and Montreal in the near future.

## Korean Ministries

Since the 27th General Assembly, we have seen substantial progress in ministry among the Korean churches of the PCA. We divide our report into the two distinct groups represented by Koreans in North America.

**Reaching "First Generation" Koreans** (those who were born in Korea, and for whom Korean is their primary language for worship, as well as for everyday discourse within their communities):

There are over 170 PCA Korean language churches that are members of seven Korean language presbyteries. TE Henry Koh, GA/MNA Korean Ministries Coordinator, serves these churches with a particular focus on encouraging their participation in the fellowship and financial support of the PCA and in working with the Korean pastors in their commitment to the reformed faith. The booklet *Reformation and Revival in PCA* by Michael F. Ross of Trinity Presbyterian Church, Jackson, MS, has been translated into Korean Language and distributed to each Korean pastor.

<a id="ga28-p527"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=529 printed_page=527 -->

There are about 300 Korean pastors in the PCA but only about 10 percent of them
participate in the pension program through Insurance, Annuity and Relief (IAR). all of
the brochures in the i ara rein the process of being translated into korean Language.
at present translation of “Tax Sheltered Annuity,” ‘Long Term Disability,” and
“Employee Benefits” have been completed. Also the revision and change in the Book of Church Order (BCO) is being updated into Korean Language BCO.

the annual conference of the coalition of korean churches i np came tat korean
presbyterian church of houston from february 14-16, 2000. the purpose of the
Coalition is to maintain Christian fellowship and close relationships among Korean
churches in the PCA and to discuss and carry out ministry activities in cooperation with
one another.

pastor jon g yo on lee of seoul presbyterian church was the main speaker of the event.
new president and officers of the coalition w er echos enduring this annual event. The
Korean Women in the Church (WIC) also conducted their own seminars, elected a new
president and discussed their annual project. wi cis also planning the publication of a
korean translation of the book, "Leadership for Women in the Church," by Susan Hunt.

Reaching "Second Generation" Koreans (those of korean family background who
were bom in the United States, a nd whose primary language is English):

Organized by TE Henry Koh, the first GA/MNA Korean English Ministry Pastors'
Conference was held January 31 through February 2, 2000, at Korean Central
Presbyterian Church, Vienna, VA, in the Washington, DC area. The conference theme
was Reaching the Next Generation through Church Planting. Participation in the
conference was strong than anticipated, with over 110 attending. tapes et soft he
second PCA Convocation on Revival and Reformation were distributed to participants,
paid for by offerings during the conference.

Through the conference, a number of potential second-generation church planters were
identified. the prayers of the General Assembly are requested as men train further for
this crucial ministry, wit hits great challenge of reaching people with the Gospel, even
as they experience great transition in their own lives and the life of their families and
churches. a variety of approaches to church planting are emerging, as first and second
generation church planters take up this challenge. As the first and second generations
work together, the gospel will be advanced through the planting of churches that are
able to reach and retain second and succeeding generations of koreans living in north
America.

C. Thanksgiving Ministries — Urban and Mercv

“He defended the cause of the poor and needy, and so all went well. Is that not what it
means to know m e? ” declares the Lord. Jeremiah 22:16

<a id="ga28-p528"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=530 printed_page=528 -->

The PCA urban and mercy ministry movement is a growing movement. The Spirit is at work, convincing us of His call to show the mercies of our Lord, even as we preach the Gospel in some of the most challenging communities in North America. And the Lord is blessing, bringing response to the Gospel by the power of His Spirit, along with addressing personal and community needs at a significant level.

**Urban and Mercy Women in Leadership Awards:** On a one-time basis, a portion of the 1998 WIC Love Gift was used to give awards to women who provide leadership in urban and mercy ministries in the PCA. Nominated by their peers, these are women who demonstrate personal commitment and sacrifice as they lead mercy ministries or encourage PCA members to become involved in these ministries.

Eleven recipients of the awards were announced at the 1999 PCA Women in the Church Conference, whose theme was mercy ministries. The recipients of these awards were:

Mariam Bell – Justice Fellowship; McLean, VA
Barbara Cole – Grace Maternity Home; Richardson, TX
Penny Nelson Freeman – Harvest USA; Philadelphia, PA
Barbara Horn – Park Cities Presbyterian Church; Dallas, TX
Joyce Horton – Women’s Prison Ministry; Clinton, MI
Jennifer Mahaffey – New City Fellowship of North County; Ferguson, MO
Paige Overton Pitts – New Hope Academy; Franklin, TN
Yvonne Dodd Sawyer – Hope for New York; Miami, FL
Amy Sherman – Charlottesville Abundant Life Ministries; Charlottesville, VA
Susan Tibbels – New Song Fellowship; Baltimore, MD
Pat Wheeler – Bethany Christian Services; Greenville, SC

It is our prayer that this one-time recognition will have the following ongoing effects:

- A number of women who now live in limited circumstances have been identified and encouraged to develop further their gifts in mercy ministry leadership.
- Encouragement of further direct involvement between women in established PCA churches and women who are in need of ministry.
- Recognition of leaders in mercy ministries; some award recipients are women of long-standing in PCA leadership roles – others are relatively new to the PCA.

GA/MNA Urban and Mercy Ministries continues its ongoing efforts from the past:

- Building the network of communication and resources to encourage urban and mercy ministries, and making them visible to the church and the watching world.
- The annual Christian Community Development Association (CCDA) meeting is a gathering point for PCA-affiliated urban and mercy ministries. In 1999, at least 150 from PCA-affiliated ministries attended the CCDA conference in Chicago. This

<a id="ga28-p529"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=531 printed_page=529 -->

conference remains the best single place to draw from the expertise, experience and
biblical perspective of others engaged in such ministries.

• At each General Assembly, GA/MNA sponsors a dinner, at which time the work of
one or more urban and mercy ministries is presented. The dinner is open by
advance reservation to all who wish to attend.

• a directory of PCA-affiliated urban and mercy ministries is available from
GA/MNA on request we invite the churches of the PCA to visit our w eh site
h w . pcanet. orx/MNA/ and order a copy of the GA/MNA Urban and Mercy
Ministries Directory. Since the people and organizations listed in the directory
welcome opportunities to encourage and provide consultation in forming new
ministries, this directory is a valuable resource for any PCA church who is involved
in urban and mercy ministries, or who wishes to begin such ministries in their
community. PCA churches and presbyteries are encouraged to avail themselves of
the great wealth of experience that already exists in the PCA, as they seek to
develop or increase ministry in their own communities.

• Leadership development: The Thanksgiving Offering continues to be a resource for
encouraging the development of urban and mercy leadership in the PCA. Primarily,
the funds are used to sponsor internships for new staff members, seminary students
or college students working in urban and mercy ministries. by the end of February,
more than $46,000 was received in response to the 1999 Thanksgiving Offering
appeal.

Beginning with the 11th General Assembly in 1983, Bethany Christian Services has
been endorsed by the PCA, upon GA/M NA’s annual review and recommendation. We
note with gratitude the services of bethany to thousands of christian families
throughout its 56 years of service, and commend Bethany to the PCA for continued
involvement and support. their report is found as attachment C.

D. church vitality

GA/MNA Church Vitality has as its mission to watch and pray for revival as we work
and pray for more vital churches who advance the Gospel into the 21s* century.

Watching and Praving for Revival
after more than sixty schools of prayer throughout the PCA, The Kingdom Campaign
strategy, based on Acts 1:14-15, was developed to help the Church build and use its
super weapon, Kingdom-focused prayer. Presbytery-level Leadership Training has
been developed, with six events planned for 2000, rolling out to all presbyteries in the
next five years

<a id="ga28-p530"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=532 printed_page=530 -->

Along with this, Kingdom Intercessor Training (KIT) is designed to teach local church
bodies and their neighbors how to grow in Kingdom intercession Apprentice training
has begun, to raise up the next generation of leader/teachers for KITs.

key covenant fellowships a redesigned to help participants in their study of revival, as
they continue to watch and pray for it. A text containing discussion guides, called The
spirit of Revival, was published in February 2000 which encourages pastors to build a
Fellowship. A Leader’s Guide is also available.

In September 1999, the second annual Convocation on Revival and Reformation was
conducted at Trinity Presbyterian Church, Jackson, MS. Over 100 pastors and ruling
elders gathered to study reformation in today’s Church. Participants reported a definite
anointing on the meeting and fellowship. James Montgomery Boice is the plenary
speaker for the third annual Convocation, scheduled for October 31—November 2,
2000 .

Working and Praying for More Vital Churches
The Sixth Annual Embers to a Flame Conference at Briarwood Presbyterian Church in
Birmingham, AL, provided training, ideas and encouragement to more than 120 pastors
and ruling elders this event continues to bethe main source of revitalization teaching
for churches on a statistical plateau or in decline. Arrangements for second-level
Embers training were made at this year’s conference.

Development work on The Barnabas Institute continues, with plans for the initial
training to beheld in spring of 2001 The Institute will help pastors and their wives
learn more about their sense of calling to revitalization work.

days of prayer and fasting in presbyteries, churches and other groups continue to be
held. Materials for a suggested format are in development and available upon request.

Pastors-at-Large and Consulting Services are other ministries available to churches and
pastors, in order to provide counsel, advice, encouragement and further training in
church vitality.

E. Campus Ministries

Reformed University Ministries

reformed university ministries of mission to north america has a goal of building the
church now and for the future. This is accomplished by: “Reaching students for Christ
and equipping students to serve.” that phrases peaks eloquently of our commitment to
our covenant children and our obedience to the Great Commission. College years are a
time of great opportunity for reaching students with the Gospel. So many testify in later
life that their college years were the time when they seriously considered God’s truth.

<a id="ga28-p531"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=533 printed_page=531 -->

Reformed University Fellowship (RUF), as the ministry is known on campus, offers the truth of God’s Word to students who are searching. By working within the context of the church, the Body of Christ, we follow His leadership as He builds His Kingdom on earth. Students are instructed in Evangelism and Missions, Growth in Grace, Fellowship and Service, and a Biblical World-and-Life View. We are strengthening the church by ministering to covenant children and by evangelizing the lost on campuses across this nation. An ordained PCA minister leads each RUF while actively working to accomplish goals in these four major areas.

International Students Christian Fellowship (ISCF) has been renamed RUF – International (RUFI) and has been placed under the oversight of Presbytery Committees on Campus Ministries. Reformed University Ministries is reaching out to “all nations,” sharing the Gospel of Christ and training Christian internationals to serve Him all over the globe. The 350 students involved in RUFI are from at least 40 different countries. Most of these countries are restricted or closed, prohibiting missionaries to serve there. Many of the students return to their own countries to build the Church at home. Our missionaries are privileged to introduce internationals to Christ. One of their goals is to help Christian internationals in their struggle to live for Christ in their own cultures.

Campus interns allow a much broader outreach on the campuses. In this learning and ministering position, young men and women, all recent college graduates, work directly with a campus minister, receiving on-the-job-training in evangelism, small group leadership, and one-to-one ministry. A planned program of biblical and theological training accompanies this practical service. After their internship with Reformed University Ministries, interns disperse into the world mission field, carrying to their future vocations and churches their deepened understanding of God’s Word and experience in His service. Truly, campus interns are learning to be “an instrument for noble purposes, made holy, useful to the Master and prepared to do any good work” (II Timothy 2:21 NIV).

Weekly large group meetings, small groups, and one-to-one staff/student sessions provide the structure for campus ministry. Each is essential in ministering to college students. The weekly large group meeting teaches the Word in truth by Bible exposition, examination, and discussion of Scripture. Small groups focus on study, prayer, and fellowship. One-to-one sessions between students and staff members offer in-depth discipling of Christians, evangelistic encounters, and the building of trust-confidence relationships. Each of these three parts supports and strengthens the others. One of the most distinctive things about the ministry of RUF is its emphasis upon the development of a biblical world-and-life view. Only as we see through God’s perspective can we understand the truth of any situation. Only as students learn to think biblically will it make a real difference in the Church and the world.

Summer Conference provided solid Bible exposition and teaching for equipping students to better understand and live the Christian life; provided teaching, training and

<a id="ga28-p532"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=534 printed_page=532 -->

equipping in skills related to reaching others for Christ; and provided fellowship and
fun in beautiful surroundings among Christians from over 38 college and university
campuses it was held over a period of two weeks in may of 1999, and was extremely
well attended. As many as 600 students and about 120 staff were able to leave the
routine of books and schedules to reflect on life, deal with questions, and be taught from
God’s Word. the theme of the conference was “Justification,” and the TE Fred Harrell
and the TE Scotty Smith expounded the Scriptures. Students were eager to receive
God’s word because of the love of god and the fellowship among other Christians,
many new friendships were made. These friendships were used by God to bring
students closer to Himself. In 2000, Summer Conference will return to a one-week
format The conference, which will address the topic of Sanctification, will be held
May 15-20. Our speaker will be the TE George Robertson from Covenant Presbyterian
Church in St Louis, Missouri

conferences provide activities and a relaxing atmosphere a swell as a concentrated time
for biblical teaching that is new to many students. This teaching is done through
elective workshops which the student may choose (two per day), small group study,
prayer, and large group teaching every evening. One campus minister rejoiced that,
"Many students are understanding the Gospel for the first lime and many Christians
are being encouraged and strengthened in their walk with God. ” In addition to
Summer Conference, regional fall, winter, and spring conferences are held throughout
the year

In 1999, there were many weeks of training for the field staff this in-depth training is
a distinctive of the ministry and provides philosophical, practical, and reflective
instruction to RUF campus ministers, interns, and staff. a week of non-staff training
was held in Jackson, Mississippi, in August for those interested in learning how
Reformed University Ministries does its work, whether church staff, missionary or lay
person This training, recently named foundations of campus Ministry, will be held
August 7-10 this summer at Simpsonwood Conference Center in Atlanta, Georgia.

1999 was a year of record growth as seven new campus ministries were started. These
new ministries are: Appalachian State University, Lehigh University, Louisiana State
University, Rice University, university of Georgia, Middle Tennessee State University
and Florida Southern College New campus ministers were placed at Florida State
University, Mercer University, Southern Methodist University, Texas A&M University,
and university of Missouri, and fill-in campus workers we replaced at university of
memphis and university of north carolina at Asheville. This immense increase in
ministry opportunity has greatly challenged our Reformed University Ministries-
GA/MNA staff. Restructuring and changes in personnel have occurred throughout
1999. the beginning of 2000 finds us in possibly our best position in history as far as
human resources and the great evidence of God’s material blessing to Reformed.
University Ministries. Reformed University Ministries continues to grow, with as many
as ten new campus ministries anticipated in the fall of the year 2000.

<a id="ga28-p533"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=535 printed_page=533 -->

What is the reason for this growth and excitement? One campus minister told us, “God’s Word is alive and active. The Gospel is powerful. That is why RUF is growing . . . RUF seeks to be faithful to God’s Word and to biblically minister to students.”

RUFI now has campus ministers at Clemson University, the University of North Carolina at Charlotte and the University of Texas. RUFI held the first annual International Fall Conference for the Carolinas which proved to be a major opportunity both to strengthen Christians through fellowship and teaching and to present clearly the claims of Christ to non-Christians in the international community.

Only God could bring together students and ministers from so many different walks of life to accomplish His purposes. Each person that is touched by Reformed University Ministries will, in turn, touch many other people in the course of his or her life. One campus intern noted that, “A true measure of success for RUF . . . may only be seen in the light of eternity.” How true! And how glorious!

Refer to Attachment for a listing of presbyteries and their affiliated committees, campus staff and location of ministries; and the complete 2001 budget for Reformed University Ministries (GA/MNA Campus Ministries and the affiliated presbytery campus committees) provided for information.

## F. Chaplain Ministries

### The Hope of the Gospel

On 8 November 1999 the devotional booklet, Tabletalk, published by Ligonier Ministries, used Isaiah 61:1 as the Scripture reference for the devotional entitled The Hope of the Gospel.

The Spirit of the Lord God is upon me, because the LORD has anointed me to preach Good Tidings to the poor; He has sent me to heal the broken hearted, to proclaim liberty to the captives and the opening of the prison to those who are bound. Is. 61:1

“The portrait of salvation that is presented in this chapter (Is.61) is filled with the imagery of restoration, of life being brought out of death, of that which has been broken down being built up again. The hope of the Christian message is that God brings life out of death and healing out of suffering. Jesus Christ died on the cross for the sins of His people and conquered the grave by His own power so that we might live again. Some times our situations seem so dark and impossible that we can’t believe anything can change them. We look into our own hearts, and we see only darkness. We look at our broken marriages, our lost friendships, our shattered lives, and we wonder whether anyone can put the pieces back together again. It is easy to become lost in a sea of despair and think that even God cannot bring healing to such broken lives. But Isaiah proclaims He can. The Good Tidings that Jesus Christ preaches to the poor in spirit, to the broken hearted are that He can set the prisoners free from their captivity. He has the power to rebuild the ruins, ‘to give them beauty for ashes, the oil of joy for mourning, the garment of praise for the spirit of heaviness.’ . . . He has the power to bring life to a heart that is dead in sin. He can restore lives that have been torn apart by drugs, alcohol, and abuse. He can mend marriages that have been broken apart by unfaithfulness and bitterness. Christianity is not simply a set of rules that we live by; it is a change wrought by the Spirit of God in the hearts of sinners. It is about forgiveness and reconciliation, not only

<a id="ga28-p534"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=536 printed_page=534 -->

between individuals and God but between sisters and brothers, husbands and wives, children and parents, and sworn enemies."

This Scripture and devotion is a vivid reminder of the ministry in which our chaplains are involved. Quarterly reports from our chaplains and personal conversations with them indicate that much of their ministry is involved with people who are experiencing broken marriages, shattered lives, lost friendships and situations that seem dark and impossible. Quite often the chaplain is sought out as a last resort, which leads to an opportunity to share the Good News of the Gospel! Consequently, my motivation to support our chaplains and their families, oversee their ministry, recruit additional chaplains, and inform our denominations on how the chaplains are used as God’s instruments in communicating The Hope of the Gospel is enhanced.

Active Duty and Reserve Military Chaplains, Veteran Administration, Hospital, Prison, Hospice, Police Force and Retirement Center Chaplains work and live in an environment and among a group of people who, because of their unique circumstances, quite often recognize and respond to The Hope of the Gospel. During 1999 between 400 and 500 people to whom our chaplains ministered came to faith in Jesus Christ because of The Hope of the Gospel. Christians, often in need of a pastor, were discipled, encouraged and taught; broken hearts, broken marriages, lost friendships, and shattered lives were mended; lives that had been torn apart by drugs, alcohol, and abuse were restored because of their response to Jesus Christ, the Savior. He has the power to rebuild the ruins, "to give them beauty for ashes, the oil of joy for mourning, the garment of praise for the spirit of heaviness, that they might be called trees of righteousness, the planting of the Lord, that He might be glorified." Is. 61:3

The Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) is privileged to send Chaplain as missionaries to Federal, State, and City governments. Windows of opportunity to present The Hope of the Gospel are abundant. For sure, there seem to be many more non-believers in our government institutions than there are believers; however, a few, through the ministry of our chaplains, are experiencing salvation through Jesus Christ, for which we give praise to God!

Mission, Goals and Strategy

Our Savior began His earthly ministry by declaring: "The Spirit of the Lord is upon Me because He has anointed Me to preach Good News...". (Luke 4:18)

The primary mission of GA/MNA Chaplain Ministries, on behalf of our the PCA, is to recruit, send, and oversee chaplains who will be used as God’s instruments in presenting The Hope of the Gospel to personnel (and their family members) working in our government institutions, hospitals, retirement centers and other organizations employing chaplains. We attempt to recruit and send chaplains who are "called", gifted and willing to engage the military, Veteran Administration, State and Federal Prisoners and hospital patients with the Gospel, "knowing that every sphere of influence is to bow the knee to the Lordship of King Jesus." (Act 17: 16-34; 19: 17-41.)

<a id="ga28-p535"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=537 printed_page=535 -->

To assist PCA Chaplains in their respective ministries, the Coordinator and Associate
Coordinators consider their primary tasks to be: pastoral calls on chaplains and their
family members, recruiting chaplains and chaplain candidates, increasing awareness of
chaplains and their ministries within our denomination, attendance at chiefs of
Chaplains conferences, publicity, enlisting prayer support for chaplains, and providing
professional administration.

Chaplain/Chaplain Family Visitation

The primary purpose for visiting and conducting pastoral calls on our chaplains and
their families is:
• To encourage and enable ministry;

• To evaluate;

• to confirm and enhance the sense of "connectionalism" with the chaplains
presbytery and denomination,

• To confirm, verbally and non-verbally, to the institution to which the chaplain is
assigned, that he is sent by his denomination, and thus, responsible to teach The
hope of the gospel as taught and believed by his Church.
Most military active duty, Veteran’s Administration and Prison Chaplains were visited
by the Coordinator and/or one of the associate coordinators during 1999. Also, a
significant number of reserve and national guard chaplains were visited. In nearly all
cases, feedback from chaplains has been positive.

Recruiting

During 1999 approximately 30 seminary students made inquiry concerning the Chaplain
Candidate Program. Between 15 and 20 a rein the process of working on their
application. Some have completed the process and have been commissioned. In addition
we are presently working with a few applicants who desire reserve or active duty in the
military. Twelve men were accessioned to military active duty in 1999

Church Sponsorship

the primary purpose of enlisting church/congregational sponsorship is to:
• Enlist prayer support for chaplains and family members;
• Enlist financial support for GA/MNA Chaplain Ministries,
• increase the concept of "connectionalism" between the chaplain and his presbytery
and denomination.

<a id="ga28-p536"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=538 printed_page=536 -->

Web Site (www.pcanel.org MNA)

the intended purpose of the website is to provide information and increase awareness
of chaplain ministries within the membership of the PCA, enhance recruitment and
encourage prayer and financial support. Eventually included on the Web Site will be
application forms and the Guardian (prayer calendar)

Financial Status

We are very thankful to the Lord that through His gracious provision, the benevolent
giving exceeded the amount that was expended during 1999. The primary reasons for
this are:
• increase in the number of congregations including chaplain ministries in their
annual budget, (The goal of enlisting 300 Churches to include this ministry in their
budget continues),
• Associate Directors flying on military aircraft when possible,
• In most cases congregations that have included Chaplain Ministries in their
Missions Conferences have paid travel expenses for the speaker,
• Increase in giving by the chaplains themselves. Most have paid their dues and
several have exceeded the requirement

Policy Changes:

Beginning immediately Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) applicants applying for
military active duty, VA, prison and hospital chaplaincy will be required:
• To enlist three PCA congregations as sponsors who will commit to pray for him and
his family and include Chaplain Ministries in their annual budget for $300,
• The Chaplain will commit to writing quarterly updates with specific prayer requests
to sponsoring congregations;
• the chaplain will agree to provide a prayer card with a family picture to prayer
supporters.

refer to attachment for the chaplain Roster.

G. ga /M na stewardship

As we look to the Lord to advance the Gospel into the 21st century, the Lord calls us to
commit our time, talent and treasure to that advancement. GA/MNA seeks to exercise a
careful stewardship of all the resources the PCA entrusts to her, and at the same time to
encourage the churches and members of the PCA to invest their resources in building
the Kingdom. GA/MNA ministries offer the opportunity for the Lord's people to use
their resources in this way. The Apostle Paul asks, “How will they hear without a
preacher9 How will they preach unless they are sent?” (Romans 10:14-15). As
churches and individuals give of their time, talent and treasure, they fulfill this crucial
role of sending the Lord's servants into the harvest field.

<a id="ga28-p537"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=539 printed_page=537 -->

communications :
• multiply offers quarterly communication of what the lord is doing through
various ministries of the PCA that advance the gospel weekly content for
intercessory prayer with a regional focus is included in each issue.
• The GA/MNA web site (www.pcanet.org/MNA/) is in the process of being
substantially upgraded, and will be built over time into a major resource for all
kinds of information and referral sources for ministries related to building the
church. The intercessor content for all regions is included on the web site

partnerships hare /A s kings giving :
In 1999, church giving to GA/MNA increased 9% over 1998. GA/MNA expresses
gratitude to God for this increase, and encourages the churches of the PCA to make this
giving to all committees and agencies a high priority. The Lord is at work through the
PCA in advancing His Kingdom. In order for the PCA to grow stronger over the years,
the member churches must be committed to denominational support, just as a particular
church can be strong financially only if her members give faithfully. GA/MNA is
seeking to build endowment funds, in order to provide greater resources for annual
operations and program support.

church planting project funding :
• All church planters are supported by gifts designated for their particular
projects. GA/MNA invites churches and individuals to consider joining as
partners with a church planter, enlisting him as a missionary sent and
supported by their church.
• Ethnic or multicultural church planters and movement leaders require a
special priority for project support. Often the churches they lead in planting
take longer to reach self-support, yet these men often lack the strong networks
of family and friends in the PCA who will commit to their support.
• GA/MNA seeks to establish endowment funds for the support of multicultural
church planting, in order to increase the number of church planters who can
respond to this call.
• The Timothy Fund for Church Planting Apprentices is a special fund to
supplement the support of men who have been graduated from seminary, but
who need further training before they begin to lead a church plant. Thus far,
gifts totaling $385,000 have been contributed to this fund, substantially
assisting many apprentices.

church vitality and chaplain ministries : These two ministries are supported almost
entirely by designated gifts, since Partnership Share/Askings giving is insufficient to
sustain them. We are grateful to the Lord that both ministries received full support at
their current operating level in 1999. However, these ministries fulfill a vital role, and
the potential for expansion of ministry is great, as additional resources are provided

estate design and endowment services : GA/MNA utilizes and recommends the
services of the PCA foundation in assisting individuals in their stewardship In

<a id="ga28-p538"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=540 printed_page=538 -->

addition to the regular PCA Foundation programs, GA/MNA Advise and Consult Funds
are also available, managed by the PCA Foundation.

five million fund for church buildings : Providing interest-free loans of up to
$80,000, this fund continues to be a helpful source for churches as they put together
funding packages for their building programs. Though the funds are limited, they often
make a difference in the church's ability to qualify for a loan, or they provide additional
working cash that is very necessary. This fund is supported by the return payments of
the churches to whom loans are made, a swell as by donations.

8 The following motion was approved by the 27th General Assembly: That the
General Assembly refer the matter of the coalition of korean presbyterian churches
of the PCA seeking recognition to bring fraternal greeting to the PCA General Assembly to the GA/MNA Permanent Committee (in consultation with the
administrative committee and the committee on review of presbytery Records) to
clarify the relationship of the coalition of korean presbyterian churches to the General Assembly and to report its findings to the 28th General Assembly

The GA/MNA permanent committee recommends that the 28th General Assembly adopt the following responses:
• the purpose of the coalition of korean presbyterian churches of the PCA,
as stated in its Bylaws, is “to maintain Christian fellowship and close
relationships among Korean churches in the PCA and to discuss and carry
out ministry activities in cooperation.”
• The 28th General Assembly commends the korean language churches of the
PCA for their labors in the Gospel.
• inasmuch as the coalition is not a court of the church, the Assembly
encourages the Coalition to bring to the GA/MNA Permanent Committee,
through TE Henry Koh as GA/MNA Korean Ministries Coordinator, any
desires the coalition may have for communication with the General Assembly.

9. The 27th General Assembly instructed the GA/MNA Permanent Committee to
study the issue of women speaking and teaching in public , and present clear
guidelines to the 28th General Assembly of their application of i timothy 2: 11-12 and
other scripture when selecting women for such ministry.

<a id="ga28-p541"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=543 printed_page=541 -->

The GA/MNA permanent committee recommends approval of the following response:

This instruction to the GA/MNA Committee was issued in response to a specific
instance of a woman teaching in an GA/MNA conference in February 1999 For
this reason, and because GA/MNA does not possess the authority to select or
assign people to other types of speaking and teaching ministries (except
assigning teaching elders as church planters), we understand the parameters of
our response to be limited to the issue of choosing women for speaking and
teaching in seminars, conferences and worship services conducted under the
auspices of GA/MNA.

GA/MNA conducts two kinds of activities a tits conferences and seminars that
are addressed by this motion. One activity is worship. The Scriptures, as
interpreted by the subordinate standards, give sufficient standards for the
ordering of public worship. the preaching of the word in worship is to be
protected, and that protection is assured as the preaching of the word is
conducted by elders. GA/MNA adheres to this standard in the worship services
that it conducts

The other activity is seminars designed to provide church planters and other
leaders with greater insights and skills for their ministry. While GA/MNA is
careful to assure that worthy material is presented in these seminars, the context
is not that of a worship service, and therefore the subject matter does not require
the same degree of protection as the content of worship

the primary difference between the preaching of the word in worship and the
subject matter of seminars has a tits heart the issue of authority. The content of
seminars is not intended to carry the weight to which the description “teach or
have authority over” (I Timothy 2:11-12) would apply. the context of this
passage is the Apostle’s presentation of instructions for the proper conduct of
public worship and not that of the more informal seminar which is generally
more subjective, informal and based on personal experience than is the
preaching of the word in worship

In most cases, teaching and ruling elders present the content in the seminar
context. However, from time to time, GA/MNA may ask that godly men and
women give presentations even though they are not elders, but who in God’s
providence may have insights and experience which may prove helpful and
instructive to those who lead in ministry.

10. that overture 3 from westminster presbytery requesting to divide
Westminster Presbytery into two Presbyteries, be answered in the affirmative.

11. That the General Assembly note that the entire 2 0 0 0 budget of ga /M na
campus ministries , a swell as that of the affiliated campus ministries, is provided to

<a id="ga28-p542"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=544 printed_page=542 -->

the General Assembly for its information; and further, that the Assembly note with
thanksgiving the opportunities and challenges represented by the entire 2001 budget.

12. The 24th and 25th General Assemblies received overtures from at least four
presbyteries requesting the separation of reformed university ministries from
mission to north america for the purpose of establishing reformed university
ministries as a permanent committee of the PCA.

Included in the General Assembly’s responses to these overtures is this motion,
approved by the 24th General Assembly:
The General Assembly believes that constituting Reformed University
Ministries as a new permanent committee may have significant advantages and
disadvantages for Reformed University Ministries, GA/MNA and for the General Assembly of the PCA. Further, the change proposed has never been formally
considered by GA/MNA or by General Assembly.
Therefore, the General Assembly recognizes that GA/MNA Committee has
appointed a study committee to study the relationship of reformed university
Ministries and GA/MNA, and awaits the report of its findings and recommendations to
the 25th General Assembly, by normal process, through the GA/MNA Committee.

For the following reasons, the GA/MNA Committee believes it is expedient that the
GA/MNA committee be released from responsibility for the oversight of reformed
University Ministries:
■ the rapid growth of reformed university ministries has been nurtured by the
protection and wisdom of GA/MNA during its critical formative years.
Reformed University Ministries will continue to mature as it assumes full
responsibility under the Lord for its future.
* The size, scope and budget of reformed university ministries have grown to a
place of parity with that of GA/MNA, which legitimizes a move from a
subordinate ministry relationship to a full ministry partnership.
■ Reformed University Ministries’ distinctive mission, philosophy of ministry,
and leadership have matured to the place where further expansion (both national
and international), require that Reformed University Ministries fully accept the
privileges and responsibilities of a permanent program committee, accountable
to the PCA General Assembly.
■ The 24th and 25th General Assemblies received overtures from PCA Presbyteries
urging that Reformed University Ministries be permitted to become a permanent
program committee of the Presbyterian Church in America. This
recommendation affirms the (grassroots) wisdom that was brought forward
from the churches and local campus subcommittees.
• the primary ministry of GA/MNA is the planting of churches; while campus
ministry is related, it is not an integral part of the church planting process. In
particular, the GA/MNA Committee has far more expertise in governing church
planting than it does in governing campus ministries. Leaders who have the
strongest interest in the latter generally are elected to the GA/MNA campus

<a id="ga28-p543"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=545 printed_page=543 -->

ministries subcommittee rather than the GA/MNA Committee. Functions that
are related, such as campus ministers moving into church planting, could be
facilitated just as easily if the two ministries were separate.
■ In most cases, the presbyteries have separated the supervision of church planting
and campus ministries, so that a campus subcommittee reports to the presbytery
through presbytery GA/MNA, but the presbytery GA/MNA committee is little
involved with the leadership of the campus ministries
■ much of the management of the two ministries is already separated internally,
such as accounting and audits, office space, staff roles. Current Reformed
University Ministries staff and subcommittee prefer to separate all aspects of
management.
■ in the perception of many even in the PCA membership, and certainly among
non-PCA students and others, there is little or no awareness of a connection
between the two ministries.
■ the financial support of reformed university ministries is already separate
from GA/MNA, except for the $100,000 that GA/MNA contributes annually to
Reformed University Ministries from Partnership Share receipts. This
commitment brings a limitation to GA/MNA fund raising, in that GA/MNA
must provide support, but cannot address the Reformed University Ministries
constituency to raise this support.
■ the field management structures of church planting and campus ministries are
quite different. Church planters are placed and become self-supporting, not
maintaining any formal relationship with GA/MNA on a long-term basis,
whereas campus ministries remain permanently in an affiliated status This
means that the two organizations have different styles of operation, both
internally and externally.
■ The above factor also means that whereas GA/MNA may well fulfill its mission
on a long-term basis without seeing very much organizational growth, Reformed
University Ministries will grow ever larger as an organization as more campus
ministries begin. If current growth trends continue, the Reformed University
Ministries administrative staff will eventually be larger than GA/MNA staff.
■ The campus ministries subcommittee already has a defined role in selecting the
campus ministries coordinator such that the GA/MNA Coordinator cannot
unilaterally make his own choice of the person to fill this position, as he is
authorized by the GA/MNA Committee to do with all other staff.
■ For public relations and fund raising purposes, Reformed University Ministries
would be able to project a stronger image if it were separate from GA/MNA.

in light of the overtures received by the 24th and 25th General Assemblies, and the
considerations listed above, that reformed university ministries be separated from
the ga /M na committee and be established as a permanent program committee of the
General Assembly effective at the 29th General Assembly (2001), after due
constitutional process.

<a id="ga28-p544"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=546 printed_page=544 -->

13. That the General Assembly re-elect RE Bentley Rayburn and TE James
hutchens to another term on the presbyterian and reformed joint commission on
chaplains and military personnel (PRJC) as members of the class of 2004

14 That the General Assembly adopt the 2001 ga /M na budget and commend it
to the churches for their support.

Respectfully Submitted,
Committee on Mission to North America

SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT
OF THE COMMITTEE ON MISSION TO NORTH AMERICA
TO THE
28 th GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE PCA

The Committee on Mission to North America submits the following recommendation as
a supplement to the report of the committee on mission to north america to the 28th
General Assembly:

RECOMMENDATIONS:

15 that overture 19 from northeast presbytery requesting to form three new
Presbyteries (Southern New England, New York State and Northern New England) out
of the existing northeast presbytery (thereby dissolving the existing Northeast
Presbytery), be answered in the affirmative.

ATTACHMENT C

BETHANY CHRISTIAN SERVICES
Report to the 28th General Assembly

Throughout His life on earth, Jesus proclaimed and lived the expectation of our heavenly father. His message was very clear:

“Love the Lord your God with all your heart, mind and soul and love your neighbor as yourself”.

What a challenge ... to us as a church and to us as individual Christians. With His guidance and grace, we can achieve a small beginning. Bethany is privileged to be one vehicle through which the church as a unified body and as individual Christians can fulfill our commitment to exemplify that love.

Bethany’s mission statement commits us to

“Manifest the love and compassion of Jesus Christ by protecting and enhancing the lives of children and families.”

As Christians, we are the continued embodiment of Christ’s love. When we care for others, Jesus is caring for them. When we proclaim His saving grace through our actions and words, we are sharing with others that their ultimate comfort and answer to life’s challenges is coming to Him for rest.

Bethany’s primary focus is on children and families. Our fervent commitment is to the protection of life prior to and following birth.

This past year the Lord gave us the privilege of ministering with you from 67 offices located throughout the United States and within 14 foreign countries. Through Bethany’s efforts, 1,478 children joined their adoptive families. Over 11,000 young people living with unplanned pregnancies received counseling. An additional 10,000+ received Christian guidance and counseling through our crisis pregnancy hotline (Bethany Lifeline). Each person whose life was touched through Bethany received the clear message that life is precious, that the child developing within the womb is a human life and that the lives of the children whom we parent are loved by the Lord.

Among the 1,478 children who joined their adoptive families were many whose lives were punctuated with unusual challenges and needs. Well over 500 were children who languished in foreign orphanages prior to Bethany’s intervention. Over 200 were children who endured the terror of abuse at the hands of their birth family. Three hundred seventy-five (375) were children of color. All were children who are precious to our heavenly father. Their needs and in some cases their pain grieved Him. It was

<a id="ga28-p550"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=552 printed_page=550 -->

Bethany’s privilege to be a vessel through which the lives of these children were
transformed.

bethany is indeed an extension of the christian body. We don’t simply represent you.
wear e you ! you are bethany ! Our ministry is a partnership. Together we fulfill
our mission of love.

this partnership of love is expressed in multiple and unique ways. members of your
congregation serve as foster parents. Others adopt. Still others serve on our governing
boards or as volunteers. You join us in prayer for those we serve and for those who
serve. And you participate with your financial support . . $1,350,000 last year. Thank
you for loving and caring with us.

As we enter this new year and this new millennium, Bethany will be confronted with
many challenges and opportunities. We approach those opportunities with excitement
and confidence. We will continue to develop additional branch office locations in order
to more comprehensively protect the lives of children and families and to proclaim his
love to them. We have just opened an office in Traverse City, Michigan and within the
year expect offices to begin in Orlando, Florida and Phoenix, Arizona. Likewise, the
extension of christian mercy through our international program will continue to expand
in locations and in numbers of people served. We will strengthen our Christ centered
love and message to them. we will continue to proclaim the importance of and will
work to strengthen the Christian family.

Life is fast nowadays. Change happens quickly. Bethany is anticipating and is
prepared to meet those changes. We also, however, live within the assurance of the
Lord’s unchangeable love. And, we live His unchangeable expectation that we:

“Love the Lord our God with all our heart, our soul, and our mind and
that we love our neighbors as ourselves. ”

With you, we eagerly look forward to practicing that love ... in Jesus’ name.

Respectfully Submitted,

Glenn DeMots
Bethany Christian Services

<a id="ga28-p551"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=553 printed_page=551 -->

ATTACHMENT D

REFORMED UNIVERSITY MINISTRIES

The Committee on Mission to North America provides support services through Reformed University Ministries–GA/MNA to presbyteries whose campus ministries are affiliated with Reformed University Ministries–GA/MNA. The presbyteries receiving services make a contribution toward their cost. Presbyteries and their affiliated committees are completely responsible for the funding of ministries within their area and for determining the budget for such ministries. Reformed University Ministries–GA/MNA receives and disburses funds only as directed by the presbyteries and their affiliated committees.

From January 1, 1999 to December 31, 1999, Reformed University Ministries–GA/MNA received $3,035,080 and disbursed $2,822,728 as directed by presbyteries and their affiliated committees. The funds are received for particular ministries, which are the responsibility of a presbytery as noted below. The responsible body receives an audit report of its funds. The following list gives the presbyteries and their affiliated committees, campus staff, and location of ministries receiving support services–accounting through Reformed University Ministries–GA/MNA, and other ministries and staff affiliated with Reformed University Ministries–GA/MNA.
