## REPORT FROM THE COMMITTEE ON MISSION TO THE WORLD TO THE TWENTY-EIGHTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
June 2000

It is not necessary to argue that our world is changing at a very rapid rate. Some years ago the futurist, Alvin Toffler, predicted in his best seller, *Future Shock*, that the speed with which the future was approaching would overwhelm humankind. He was overly pessimistic on two fronts. First, the momentum of change did not pick up speed as fast as Toffler thought it would. Secondly, humans have proved more resilient to change than he expected. Nevertheless, the change around our world seems to have overcome some inertia and is now accelerating. No place is this change more evident than in the world of missions. In fact, change seems to be the operative word whether one is talking to missionaries on the field or reading the published mission literature. There is strong evidence that the center of missions is shifting south and east, leadership is changing to indigenous leaders, and even who is considered a missionary today is changing. Some are moved around the world in connection with their occupation and function as missionaries in their new location. Many respond to a short-term challenge of one-to-two years, and fulfill a specific task.

All of this needs to be seen in the context of the fact that human beings resist change. God has made us with a need for permanence first in our relationship with Him, and then with our relationship with others. Permanence is important both in establishing culture and in establishing family foundations. Because of our need for permanence, change always comes at a cost. Nevertheless, the axiom that if we don’t change we die is as true today as it ever was.

We emphasize this to reflect on the fact that at Mission to the World we have seen a great deal of change over the last few years. For instance, our relief work has grown from a rather small ministry to a quite substantial mission emphasis. This has come at the insistence of the church at large. We have been very gratified to see how the members of our denomination have responded to needs around the world. In a recent effort, the earthquake in Turkey, members of the PCA through their churches gave $217,000.00.

We have also begun to emphasize, at the imperative of the church, ministries that combine evangelism with physical and social needs. This word and deed emphasis has energized some in our denomination who had little interest in missions to this point. We are particularly pleased with the placement of Dr. Brian and Julie Riedel to do urban poor church planting in Lima, Peru, including ministry among street boys. We

<a id="ga28-p561"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=563 printed_page=561 -->

believe that this dual emphasis will continue to grow and continue to be a blessing to Mission to the World and to the PCA.

We also have continued to see indigenous leadership take more and more responsibility in such fields as Mexico, Ukraine, and the Philippines. This has been gratifying and we believe also gives a direction for the future as we seek to follow the leading of God’s spirit.

Our emphasis on training is developing nicely and we are particularly pleased with the way in which Bill Goodman has taken hold of this emphasis within Mission to the World. We believe that not only are missionaries going to learn from the Global Church Planting Center, but also it will give many of our missionaries an opportunity to share their expertise with others through the training events.

Finally, we have been overwhelmed with the response to short-term missions. The church continues to request this emphasis. It is short-term missions more than any other emphasis within the mission world which is sometimes blurring the lines of who a missionary is. We believe that short-term missions is going to be our best source of recruiting career missionaries, and also developing long-term support within our churches for the work for our church-planting teams. As we stated, all of this change comes at a very high cost and it is one of the reasons we need prayer partners holding us up in prayer.

The area retreat which we had in September for our missionaries in Asia was the best we have ever had, even though logistically and in terms of health, it was the most difficult we have ever had. It took many hours to get to Chiang Mai, Thailand, and quite a number of our missionaries became ill due to either something we ate or a virus that was going around. We had been praying for a year and had listed the development of family within Mission to the World as a major concern for 1999. God answered those prayers. Even though the word family was never mentioned once in any of the talks or seminars given during the Asian conference, a number of missionaries came up and expressed their gratitude that they felt like they were part of a growing family emphasis. It never ceases to amaze us how God answers prayers even sometimes in ways that are not obvious to us.

The LEAD conference, our annual gathering of field leadership, was also a productive time. The teaching both by Tim Keller and Mark Gornik was helpful to our missionaries. All of us grew in our understanding of what a church-planting movement is. It is our prayer that we will be able to continue this relationship with the Redeemer Presbyterian Church. New York and the emphasis which Tim Keller has seem to be a very good fit for our training of new missionaries. Following the instruction on church-planting movements, we asked our field personnel to critique the Atlanta office and our administrative operations. It is always difficult to hear constructive criticism, but we are determined to change the way we do business so we can be more responsive to our missionaries, our churches, and the national leaders with whom we are working. We

<a id="ga28-p562"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=564 printed_page=562 -->

are now working on a redesign that will help us respond to the needs we have found in
our work processes.

Christ’s College is a great ministry with more converts per capita than possibly any
other ministry in Taiwan. The Board is actively working on a strategic plan by which to
govern Christ’s College. In the November Board meeting a mission statement, which
reads as follows, was approved:

Christ’s College Mission Statement

Christ's college is a bilingual institution of higher education based on biblical truth
and dedicated to:
1. Evangelizing students to a saving faith in Jesus Christ and discipling them in the
Christian faith in a loving and nurturing environment.
2. Providing quality education that integrates faith a nd learning to equip students in
Christian character and competent professional abilities, as lifelong learners.
3. Training students to apply a Christian world and life view in order to become
servants of christ and competent christian leaders in church and in society.

the board is now in the process of developing strategic objectives which reflect this
mission statement. This process should be completed in the May 2000 meeting. We
will then expect the president to present an action plan by November 2000 in order to
move the college toward our desired goal

god is the god of all history and event he change our world is experiencing is under
His sovereign guidance Missiologists argue that in the next 25 years, more people will
come to faith in Jesus Christ than any other 25-year period God will undoubtedly use
the process of change to bring about his eternal plan. The future is very bright when
we acknowledge our own weakness and walk into the future with Him.

In 1999, there were 499 career missionaries, 136 two-year mtwimpact missionaries,
162 internship participants, and 5,000 short-term summer missionaries. The breakdown
of the career missionaries is as follows:
Church Planting 313
MTW-Direct 279
Urban 254
Hinterland 17

Other 129

ASIA/EUROPE HIGHLIGHTS
ASIA
Australia
Last year the Australian team was working toward combining two theological
seminaries which are associated with the Westminster Presbyterian Church, one in
Brisbane and one in Perth, with two other theological seminaries to enable them to get
accreditation. That has been accomplished in both cases and the seminaries are moving
ahead.

the university ministry team that has come out of a campus outreach background from
Briarwood Presbyterian Church has a tremendous outreach in Brisbane. They also
made a trip to perth to see how a ministry team might be put to work there.

India
David Fiol, in his commitment to bring nationals into control as soon as possible, has
reached the point of turning over grace academy completely to indian leadership an
Indian national has filled the headmaster position, previously held by David. He will be
in place by April 1st.

Japan
Between the teams, three new churches have been started in Japan. The newly formed
Presbytery in Chiba continues to function and grow in its work. The university
ministry, led by Fred Reid, is growing by leaps and bounds. Fred is committed to
nationalizing that work as soon as possible. The goal is that when Fred leaves for his
HMA in 2001 that university ministry team will be completely national and they will
begin work on a new campus.

<a id="ga28-p564"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=566 printed_page=564 -->

Myanmar
The work in Myanmar (formerly Burma) with reformed presbyterian church of
Myanmar continues to grow under Rev. Tial Thanga. Paul Taylor, members of the
Philippine team, and several Filipino pastors taught there again this year helping Tial
train his elders.

The two Burmese students studying in the Philippines continue to do well, as do the two
who are studying in India.

Philippines
an exciting new ministry has begun in the philippines to the street kids of Manila.
Originally the vision started with Renee Johnson. She quickly helped the team, the
Philippine Presbyterian Church, and the Philippine pastors catch a vision for the
hundreds of thousands of street kids. As a result, a new project has been established
and the first short-term teams will be going there this year.

Taiwan
Christ's College (Taiwan) continues to do well. We were able to add some full-time
PCA staff to the teaching roster. Plans have begun for building a new girls' dormitory.
this is desperately needed as many of the rooms used for the girls' dorm have up to ten
students in them.

Thailand
We rejoice that we have a team leader to begin the work in Thailand. David Veldhorst
and his wife, Jan, should be heading toward Thailand, if all goes well, by the fall. Our
relationship with Park Cities Presbyterian Church in Dallas, Texas, has continued to do
well as we've worked together in the northern part of Thailand.

EUROPE/CENTRAL ASIA
Bulgaria
The team in Bulgaria continues to progress. Craig Sheppard has laid a strong
foundation in publishing, teaching men to become elders, and leading Bible Studies.
One student has completed this training and is continuing toward a M.Div. and
ordination He and Craig have begun the first worship service.

Central Asia
The earthquakes in Central Asia added to the team's ministry, but at the same time were
very stressful. We had many churches and people involved in temporary hospitals. We
are also involved in the building of pre-fabricated homes for those who lost everything.
This has given us inroads into the community and acceptance by many. We are also
seeing significant growth in the area of evangelism and discipleship.

<a id="ga28-p565"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=567 printed_page=565 -->

England
One new man has been recruited and approved for helping David Cross do church
planting for the evangelical presbyterian church of england and Wales. In addition,
our relationship with the International Presbyterian Church and trying to reach out to
Central Asians has continued to grow in a positive direction.

France
The school that is connected to the Lyon (France) church has opened its doors to the
first 50 or so students. The city government has been very helpful in getting the school
started. In fact, they have been part of the main press behind the team doing it. It has
given us great presence and community acceptance.

Germany
we have begun two new church plants in the eastern part of Berlin, Germany. Those,
like all of european church planting, are going slowly but steadily. In the western part
of the country, Will Traub working with pastors frustrated with the state church has
helped begin the first "Confessing Church" in the city of Neuwied. Meanwhile, there
are five other churches in various cities that are joining the movement. one of their
goals, in order to keep this going, is the establishment of a seminary, since there is
literally no Reformed evangelical seminary available in Germany.

Portugal
We've seen some steady growth in Portugal. Another small church has asked to be part
of the reformed Fellowship. There is interest from several other European countries to
send workers to Portugal to help plant a Reformed church.

Romania
we have had two meetings with members of the PCA interested in seeing us minister in
Romania. The meetings have been good, and they have begun working on a purpose
statement and goals. The next meeting will be in Romania this spring.

Slovakia
The relationship between the work in Slovakia and Covenant College has continued to
grow. In addition, the relationship with the university has continued to be very positive.
We continue to look for workers to help the Lesondaks with the outreach.

Spain
Throughout the year several different evangelistic activities were held in Spain. They
were quite successful, considering the slow growth of christianity in Spain. out of a
series of evangelistic dinners, a group of 23-30 people continue to be involved in some
form of disciples hip and home bible study.

Sweden
Gary Johnson, team leader for the Sweden team, continues to recruit. many of the
people approved for his team are Swedes who came to the United States to study and

<a id="ga28-p566"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=568 printed_page=566 -->

are heading back to Sweden. The team had a very successful summer, returning to Sweden with some additional Americans to help with baseball camps and friendship outreach. Our goal is to have the first permanent missionaries on site by summer.

## Ukraine

The work continues to grow in Ukraine. Our biggest problem is lack of enough workers to meet all of our needs. One high point last year was the Ukrainian government's decision that we should inherit a church building that was once a Reformed church. The building is located downtown in a very accessible location and many people are familiar with it. We are in a capital campaign to raise the funds needed for renovation. The building will give us credibility and presence in the city.

# AFRICA/LATIN AMERICA HIGHLIGHTS

## AFRICA/MIDDLE EAST

### Senegal

The Senegal church-planting team has invited two new missionary candidates to join them. The national churches are growing, people are coming to Christ, and there is a growing number of national men (ten in 1999) in training for leadership positions.

### Cote d'Ivoire

Cote d'Ivoire suffered a coup d'etat in 1999. The Lord was gracious and the country did not suffer much upheaval. There are seven national leaders involved in training, two of whom are close to completing their studies. 1999 saw the beginning of the formation of a national presbytery. The national leadership is learning the dynamics of a Presbyterian form of church government.

### South Africa

Dr. Paul Gilchrist traveled to South Africa in early 1999 where he had the opportunity to preach, speak on the radio, and interact with several ministry partners. His visit was much appreciated and a great encouragement to many. Overall, the work in South Africa continues to grow. Our primary involvement at this time is in the area of theological training. However, God is opening doors for university ministry and mercy ministry. WIC is raising funds this year that are earmarked to help with a mercy ministry project in Port Elizabeth, South Africa.

### Sensitive

Jonah and Jennifer have returned to their field of ministry and are getting re-established. Pray for the Lord to help them make the necessary adjustments to life in their country, and that the relationships from which they've been separated for several years will be quickly regained such that the work will experience renewed vigor and make an impact in this sensitive country.

<a id="ga28-p567"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=569 printed_page=567 -->

We have seen several new missionary families and singles placed into ministry this
year. God has graciously answered prayer for some important needs, such as teachers
for missionary children. Though the work is slow, the Lord is bringing people to
Himself and preparing men for leadership in the growing churches. There are many
challenges in these places of the world but god is showing himself faithful and
continues to meet the many needs our missionaries face.

Muslim Ministry Network
A new Muslim Ministry Network was launched during 1999. the focus of this network
is to expand the awareness and involvement of the PCA for muslim ministry in the us
and around the world. The network will host a seminar for the 2000 General Assembly.
Commissioners are encouraged to contact ( ) if they are
interested in attending this seminar.

Africa AIDS Ministry Team
mt wi sin the midst of developing anew ministry whose primary focus i said sin the
African continent. Several MTW missionaries are dialoguing, exploring ideas and
asking the Lord to lead in developing this ministry. Pray for the Lord to give wisdom
and clear direction. This ministry will necessitate some team changes as well as
placement issues. it may also require additional medical training for some of our
missionaries who are sensing God’s call on their lives to be apart of this new endeavor.

LATIN AMERICA/CARIBBEAN
Ecuador
Ecuador is reeling from tremendous economic upheaval. At the same time, there are
two volcanoes that are creating quite a mess in the country. The airport has had to be
shut down numerous times due to volcanic ash. Schools are in a constant quandary
about whether or not to hold classes, etc. please pray for wisdom in the midst of these
issues. Also pray for God’s mercy and grace for our missionaries who are living in
these circumstances and struggle with the impact of these circumstances on their
ministries.

Hinterland
The Hinterland ministry team is presenting MTW with new goals and challenges to
expand the work in the andean region of south america the lord is raising up
several men who aspire to be leaders in the church. The teams are organizing different
types of pastor and leadership training efforts. They are also mentoring several
potential leaders whoa re desirous of beings en tout as church planters into some
un reached parts of peru and Ecuador. Pray for wisdom and the resources which are
needed as these endeavors continue to grow, and pray for the leaders whom God is
raising up for ministry in His church.

Lima
In addition to our existing urban church-planting team under the leadership of bob
Woodson, we have placed the first members of an urban poor church-planting team

<a id="ga28-p568"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=570 printed_page=568 -->

(mercy ministries) into Lima, Peru, in 1999. Other members are involved in raising
support and trusting the Lord to have them on the field before year-end. one of the
primary foci of this team is church planting through reaching street children with the
gospel. the first years of ministry are primarily focused on language learning and
cultural adaptation. However, it is already apparent that people will fall quickly and
naturally into ministry as they interact with the poor. Pray for wisdom, patience and
stick-to-itiveness that is needed to acquire the fundamentals (language and culture) in
these early stages. Pray for the team members as they make major life adjustments in
learning to live and minister with the urban poor.

Mexico
Learning to partner and have healthy church-planting partnerships is an ongoing
process. MTW sees both encouraging and discouraging signs along the way. God is
stretching our faith as we must be completely dependent upon Him for any kind of
success in these relationships. It is evident that these are extremely healthy models for
ministry and mission. The partnerships in Mexico continue to see fruit. New churches
are being started, people are coming to Christ and young leaders are growing in their
faith and in their ministry calling. Some, however, see that God has not gifted nor
called them as church planters so we see some setbacks. The team in Mexico would ask
you to joint he min praying for the lord of the harvest to raise up more laborers. Men
who aspire to leadership in the church seem to be increasingly few. Pray for the Spirit
to move among His people in Mexico and raise up godly men who will lead His church.

Haiti
The Haiti church-planting endeavor will see a significant increase in PCA church
members coming on short-term trips in 2000. These short-term missionaries will be
helping build church buildings, a clinic and a christian day school. Pray for safety and
pray for these missionaries to be a bright light ina dark country. Pray for Charles
Amicy as he hosts these groups. Also pray for him as he trains and mentors several
young men for leadership in the church some of these are already preaching and
helping with new church plants.

South America
The South American continent has a new regional director. MTW asked Dr. Gary
waldecker to assume the oversight of the urban church-planting teams in South
America as well as the relationship with a major church-planting partner, the
presbyterian church of brazil (IPB). Pray for Gary and Phyllis as they travel and work
with the MTW church-planting teams. This is a significant increase in responsibility for
Gary. previously he was overseeing just the southern cone of south America. Pray for
wisdom, for safety, and for discernment as he faces issues such as Colombia security,
economic crisis, volcanoes in Ecuador, and the launching of anew ministry effort with
the urban poor in the continent.

<a id="ga28-p569"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=571 printed_page=569 -->

RECOMMENDATIONS

The Committee on Mission to the World makes the following recommendations to the
Twenty-Eighth General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America:

1. that the month of October, 2001 beset as ideas a month of prayer for the “ 10/40
Window” and world evangelization, and that God would send many more laborers
to His harvest field.
2. that the General Assembly urge the churches to set aside a portion of their giving
for the suffering peoples of the world, to that end be it recommended that a special
offering for relief and mercy (Compassion Ministry) be taken during 2001 to be
distributed by MTW.
3. That the General Assembly set aside Sunday, November 19, 2000, as a day of
prayer and fasting for the persecuted church worldwide.
4. That Dr. Paul D. Kooistra, having been evaluated to have performed with excellence
in the position of coordinator of mission to the world during the 1998-1999
program year, and having been commended by the Committee on Mission to the
World for his exceptional performance in serving as both the spiritual and
administrative leader of mission to the world for the past year, be recommended to
the General Assembly of the PCA for reelection as Coordinator.
5. that the proposed budget of MTW, as presented through the Administrative
Committee, be approved.
6. that the minutes of the meeting of march 10-12, 1999 be accepted.
7. that the minutes of the meeting of october 6-8, 1999 be accepted.

Respectfully submitted,

Shelton P. Sanford, III, Chairman
Committee on Mission to the World

SPS/jht

MINUTES OF THE
NOMINATING COMMITTEE OF THE
TWENTY-EIGHTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

The General Assembly’s Nominating Committee convened in Atlanta, Georgia, at the
Westin Atlanta Airport Hotel on Saturday, March 25, 2000. in the absence of the
Chairman, the Nominating Committee was called to order by Secretary David Moon at
10:00 AM. RE Ed Hackenberg was selected as acting Chairman and opened the
meeting with a word of prayer.

The roll call was made by the Secretary Twenty-nine were present, including TE L
Roy Taylor, PCA Stated Clerk, as a visitor.

Ascension TE Nick Protos (2000)
Calvary TE John Armstrong (2000)
Central Carolina RE Harold Jones (2002)
Central Florida RE Tom Bingham (2000)
Central Georgia TE John C. Jagoditsch (2002)
Covenant TE Laurie Jones (2000)
Evangel TE Frank Barker (2001)
Fellowship TE Scott Hill (2000)
Heritage RE John Haldeman (2000)
Korean Southeast TE Young Pal Cho (2001)
Louisiana TE J. Paul Lipe (2001)
Mississippi Valley TE Mike Ross (2002)
New Jersey RE Dan Barr (2001)
New River RE Harry Musser (2001)
North Florida TE William Lyle
North Georgia RE Bob Blaylock (2001)
Pacific NW RE Don Robertson (2002)
Philadelphia TE Erwin Morrison (2001)
Pittsburgh TE Lawrence Roflf (2001)
Potomac TE Tom Osterhaus (2001)
Rocky Mountain RE Bill Nardin (2000)
Southeast Louisiana RE Edwin M. Hackenberg (2001)
Southern Florida TE Lynn Downing (2001)
Southwest Florida TE John Graham (2000)
Southwest RE David Moon (2001)
Susquehanna Valley TE John MacRae
Tennessee Valley RE Roger Ingvalson (2000)
Warrior TE Tom Kay, Jr. (2000)
Westminster RE Joe Reynolds (2002)

<a id="ga28-p576"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=578 printed_page=576 -->

Romans 12:1-8 and Hebrews 10:19-25 was read by Ed Hackenberg This was followed
by a season of prayer

acting chairman ed hack en berg reviewed the responsibilities of the committee per
agreement of the nominating Committee, late submissions will be disregarded other
than submission of alternates from the previous year. copies of the previous year’s
biographies for the alternates were provided.

Motion made, seconded and passed for the Stated Clerk’s Office to automatically
include the previous year’s alternates names and add these biographies as nominees in
the Rainbow Book.

A correction was made to the ballots that Mark Hartzell, on the Committee on Mission
to North America, made that he is now a teaching elder rather than a ruling Elder.

Subcommittee assignments were distributed and the main meeting divided into the
subcommittees for deliberations. Subcommittees dismissed for deliberations at 10:40
AM with instruction to reconvene as a body at 12.00 for lunch instructions
Reconvened and dismissed for lunch at 12:00.

Reconvened from lunch at 1:13 PM. reports of the subcommittee were presented, the
nominees and biographical sketches are attached.

The Committee elected RE David Moon, Southwest Presbytery, Chairman and TE Jack
Jagoditsch, Central Georgia Presbytery, secretary of the 2001 GA Nominating
Committee.

next meeting of the nominating committee will beat General Assembly on
Wednesday, time and place to be announced.

Next year’s spring meeting will be March 24, 2001.

New Business:
Motion to require recommendations for each nomination in the Rainbow Book was
ruled out of order a sit is already required, though it is not always included.

Meeting closed in prayer by Dr. Frank Barker at 2:28 PM.

Respectfully submitted,

/s/ Edwin Hackenberg, Chairman

Is/ RE David Moon, Secretary

<a id="ga28-p577"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=579 printed_page=577 -->

class of 2004

Curtis McDaniel, Nashville

Alternates
Michael A. Milton, C. Georgia William Joseph, Jr., SE Alabama

D. Biographical Sketches:
RE Edwin Hackenberg: SE Louisiana. BA in Business Administration, US Air Force
pilot and retired airline pilot. Served as board secretary for a Christian school
and served on various committees in local church, on presbytery and GA level.
GA Administration Co. 95-99, including 1 year as V-Chairman and 1 year as
Chairman; chairman of search Co. for the Stated Clerk 97-98. Presently serving
as stated clerk of se louisiana Presbytery.
RE William Joseph, Jr.: SE Alabama. served as moderator of presbytery twice,
moderator of the 7th GA, delegate to the first GA in 1973, was member of the

<a id="ga28-p578"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=580 printed_page=578 -->

first GA level Admin. Co. and has served several times since. Served on PCA Office Building subcommittee for several years, and as chairman of subcommittee to pay off the PCA Office Building mortgage. Operated an Architect and Engineering business for 45 years and has served as Montgomery County Commissioner for 35 years, presently as chairman.

TE G. Fredric Mau: Palmetto. Senior Pastor of Faith PCA in Ballentine, SC. Qualified as church planter by MNA’s Assessment Center. Former moderator and recording clerk of Warrior Presbytery, former parliamentarian of Covenant Presbytery. Has gifts of administration, communication, strategic organization and implementation. Has served on GA AC previously.

TE H. Curtis McDaniel, Jr.: Nashville. Pastor of Clarksville PCA of Clarksville, TN. A certified Teacher-Trainer in Evangelism Explosion IV. Served on a number of local community boards and projects, on SE Alabama Presbytery – including moderator, and adjunct professor of Systematic Theology at Birmingham Theological Seminary.

TE Michael A. Milton: Central Georgia organizing pastor, Kirk O’ the Isles PC, Skidway Island, GA. Former Linguist, Navel Intelligence; former manager of a Fortune 500 organization, adjunct professor in Practical Theology at Knox Seminary. Has served on Presbytery level MNA and MTW committees and as Interim Administrator at Knox Seminary, and is currently serving as an alternate on the GA level AC.

D. Biographical Sketches:
RE Jack Bagwell: Fellowship. On session at Westminster PC in Rock Hill, SC, and as
a biology professor at York Technical College has been nominated numerous
times for York Tech’s President’s Award for Excellence in Teaching.
Developed and taught York Tech’s first Internet Course and is a speaker at
conferences, teaching people how to get started in developing Internet based
courses. also serves as a contract educational consultant fora publishing firm
TE D. Marion Clark: North Florida. pastor of faith pc in Gainesville, FL. Has served
as chairman of candidates and internship committee of philadelphia presbytery
for 6 years, consolidating two committees and streamlining the process of
interviewing and overseeing candidates and interns. Has written, reviewed and
edited most of the public materials while on staff at Tenth PC in Philadelphia.
Served 5 years as principal of city center Academy, a college-preparatory high
school.
RE Steve Fox: SE Alabama. president of printing and publishing company dealing
with national ministries. Has served previously on GA CE committee - 1 year
as V-Chairman and 2 years as Chairman, served one term on MNA committee,
currently serving on GCP Board Served as moderator on the Presbytery level,
and, for 10 years, on CE committee
RE Robert Rogland: Pacific NW. Has a doctorate in science education from Harvard
U., is a gifted and popular teacher of high school students, and author of a study-
guide on Romans. Has served previously on GA CE committee, Chairman of
review of presbytery records committee and is a licentiate of presbytery who
does pulpit supply.
TE James L. Shull: MS Valley. pastor of first pc in Philadelphia, MS. Educational
background is in Christian Ed. Served on GA level CE committee from 1988-
92, including serving on GCP Board, also has served on Presbytery level CE
committees desires to seethe body of christ discipled in the word of God, so
they may bethe salt and light of our culture.

<a id="ga28-p580"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=582 printed_page=580 -->

TE Richard Tyson: Philadelphia. pastor of calvary pc in willow Grove, PA. Has
written Sunday School materials and notes for teachers, developed a Leadership
Training Course, teaches New Member classes and leads Small Groups. Served
on CTS Board and various committees at the Presbytery level.

D. Biographical Sketches:
TE Renato Bemardes: Metro NY. pastor of com un ida dec r is ta pres bi teri ana of
Newark, NJ - the first portuguese speaking church to be part of the PCA. Feels

<a id="ga28-p581"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=583 printed_page=581 -->

It is our duty to continue to work towards transforming our regional church into a truly multi-cultural body of Christ. Has served in secular business as Manager of Operations, extensively involved in establishing new UPS centers and the direct management of people and resources. Originally from Brazil, graduated from Montclair State U [NJ] and Princeton Theological Seminary [MD], ordained in the PCUSA in 1985, and received into the PCA in 1989.

TE Gary R. Cox: Central Carolina. Senior pastor of Meadowview PCA in Lexington, NC. Has served as MNA’s first Regional Coordinator for the Southeast; has developed a model for regional “Key Leaders Retreats” and implemented an annual “Church Planters Conference; has served on Presbytery level MNA committee and is currently the Triad Regional Vice Chairman of CCP’s MNA committee. Is intimately acquainted with the issues involved in church planting and has a close working relationship with MNA staff. Has served for the past decade as a PCA Army chaplain and served during Operation Desert Shield/Desert Storm.

TE Mark Hartzell: Philadelphia. Director of Harvest USA and is TE and Evangelist for the PCA. Served for over 10 years in the PCA as an RE before becoming ordained as a TE.

RE Michael A. Russell: Evangel. Has spent 26 years in the printing business as president and owner. Has been an RE in church plants in the Birmingham area for over 22 years and has served on Presbytery MNA committee for 2 terms, currently serving on Evangel’s Examinations Committee.

RE William Thetford: Warrior. Served with Arab World Ministries as Financial Director and Executive Administrator of Trinity PC. Served on Presbytery Examination Committee and MNA Committee. Currently in the real estate business and is centered in home building and property management.

RE Michael L. Wilson: South Texas. An attorney and has served 6 years as an RE at Bay Area PC in Webster, TX. Has been actively involved in the S. Texas Presbytery – moderator and member of Candidates and Credentials Committee. Appointed to serve on Texas State Board of Public Accountancy, elected twice as member of Galveston Independent School District Board of Trustees and has served as President; has served as President and Director of the League City Industrial Development 4B Corp. Has been very active on a number of public boards and committees. Currently serving as an Alternate on GA level MNA Committee.

## D. Biographical Sketches:

TE Allan M. Barth: Metro NY. Pastor of North Shore Community Church of Oyster Bay, NY, and served previously as Minister of Education at Cedar Springs PC in Knoxville, TN. Has served as a church planter. Has been active in Presbytery committee work and, on GA level, has served on the Nominating Committee.

RE Donald Blackburn, Sr.: Central Georgia. Owns his own company. Has served as RE at First PC, Macon, GA, for over 10 years. Served on GA level MTW committee and MTW rep. on the PCA Foundation Board. Has been on several missions trips with local congregation missions committee.

TE Roy P. Carter: Nashville. Senior Associate Pastor at Christ PC in Nashville, TN. Has served on the Ministers Committee on presbytery level; has trained pastors in the former Yugoslavia, participated in evangelistic work in Greece, worked with church planters in Mexico City, has served as Chairman of the Ukraine Partnership, and is currently leading church’s efforts to work with pastors and youth leaders in Northern Ireland.

<a id="ga28-p583"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=585 printed_page=583 -->

PJi Edward Lang: Potomac. Work experience has been in administration and human
resources, more recently began working part-time at Chesapeake Seminary
where they have developed seminary training programs to assist missionaries in
the development of seminaries in other countries. Have supported worldwide
missions and missionaries for many years, has seen his children go on short term
missions trips. During 1999, served a san advisory member of CMTW.
TE Dwight Linton, North Georgia. Has served as Missionary in Korea for 25 years,
coordinator of ethnic church Development, and Consultant on Cross-cultural
Ministry' for CE&P.
RE Robert Massengill: Grace. owner of a business consultation firm, former owner of
wholesale grocery distribution company, and former bank president. Has
actively served on CMTW, been on the board of directors for african bible
College, served on State Executive Co. and former chairman of fellowship of
Christian Athletes, and numerous charitable and civic organizations.

D. Biographical Sketches:
RE Dwight Allen: North Georgia. stated clerk of Presbytery, has also served many
years on CC Board, served as CC rep. on the GA AC, and on GA level has
served on CCB and Review' of presbytery Records. Has been active on
Presbytery' level. Has extensive business experience and is currently retired.
RE Robert Avis: Missouri. Chairman, ag edwards Capital, Inc. Has served as US
Navy pilot and has degree as a civil engineer. Has served on the CC Board for 2
terms and anxious to serve another term.
TE Arthur Broadwick: Central Georgia. associate pastor of independent pc in
Savannah, GA. Has been active in Presbytery and GA level committees, has
served a number of years previously on cc Board. Has sewed on Board of
Westminster Christian School in Savannah, GA, involved in starting the
Robinson Township Christian School in Pittsburgh, PA, and served on
President’s council of the reformed presbyterian theological Seminary' in
Pittsburgh, PA. Has also been involved in starting a Bethany Christian Services
Center in Pittsburgh, PA., and served as Secretary' of that Board.
TE Dennis Johnson: South Coast. Seminary' professor and associate pastor of New'
Life PC in Escondido, CA. Has served on GA level and many Presbytery' level
committees, including moderator and recording clerk. Has served in ministerial
and teaching positions a swell as served as academic dean of westminster
Seminary' in Escondido, CO, and Accreditation Liaison Officer at WTS in
California.
TE A1 Lutz: Palmetto. pastor of o katie pc in Bluffton, SC. Has served on CC Board

MINUTES OF THE NOMINATING COMMITTEE
OF THE TWENTY-EIGTH GENERAL ASSSEMBLY
OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH OF AMERICA
The General Assembly’s Nominating Committee convened in Tampa, Florida, at the
Tampa Convention Center on June 21, 2000. The meeting was called to order at 5:43
pm byte harry Long, Chairman, and was opened in prayer by the Chairman The roll
was taken by the Chairman and the Committee members with their Presbytery as
follows:

TE Mike Ross Mississippi Valley
TE John McRae Susquehanna Valley
TE John Graham Southwest Florida
TE Tom Osterhaus Potomac
RE Tom Bingham Central Florida
TE John Armstrong Calvary
RE Woody Camp Nashville
TE Scott Hill Fellowship
RE William Billings Great Lakes
TE J. Paul Lipe Louisiana
RE Ed Hackenberg Southeast Louisiana
TE Erwin Morrison Philadelphia
TE Harry Long James River
RE David Moon Southwest

<a id="ga28-p595"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=597 printed_page=595 -->

TE Harry Long, Chairman, explained the responsibilities of the committee are to
determine the eligibility of the nominees received from the floors

RECOMMENDATIONS:
1 three nominations were ruled out of order two were rejected as there was
already an SJC member from his Presbytery [BCO 15-4] One must be off one
before he is eligible to serve on another GA committee [BCO 14-1(12)]
2. All nominees, where there are on floor nominees presented in opposition, are to
be declared elected
3 The following is our report for contested positions:

for class of 2004

Nominating Committee Nominee Floor Nominee

TE Richard Tyson, Philadelphia TE Tim Miessler, Calvary

background is in Christian Ed Served on GA level CE committee from 1988-
92, including serving on GCP Board, also has served on Presbytery level CE
committees desires to seethe body of christ discipled in the word of God, so
they may bethe salt and light of our culture
TE Richard Tyson: philadelphia pastor of calvary pc in willow Grove, PA Has
written Sunday School materials and notes for teachers, developed a Leadership
Training Course, teaches New Member classes and leads Small Groups Served
on CTS Board and various committees at the Presbytery level.
TE Brian Abshire: pacific northwest pastor of faith pc in Spokane, WA Published
author and columnist who has lectures before members of the parliaments of
Australia, south africa and zambia ph din sociology of religion (Greenwich
University, Haden).
TE Tim Miessler: Calvary. pastor of beech st pc in Gaffney, SC. Eight years as
executive manager of various college book stores. served as vp of production
of an electronic publishing company using LOGOS format Has an excellent
personal library of Puritan, Presbyterian and Reformed books

Seminary in Escondido, CO, and Accreditation Liaison Officer at WTS in

are cc graduates has served on MTW board and vice moderator of synod
prior to RPCES joining and receiving
TE David Brown: Northern California. one of the PCA ‘s first pastors on the West

in starting a Christian school, which they operated for 11 years He has served
on numerous committees of commissioners at GA. He has been moderator of

TE Kevin Scogen: tennessee valley pastor of reformed presbyterian church of

and then associate at Faith Presbyterian Church, Tacoma, WA.

adult teaching ministries of both churches. he is a graduate of columbia
university college of physicians and surgeons in new york and attended

# MISSION TO THE WORLD

For Class of 2004

|  **Nominating Committee Nominee** TE Roy P. Carter, Nashville TE Allan M. Barth, Metro New York RE Donald Blackburn Sr., C. Georgia RE Robert Massengill, Grace For Alternate TE Dwight Linton, North Georgia RE Ed Lang, Potomac | **Floor Nominee** TE Carl Bogue, Ascension TE Jim Jones, Louisiana RE Edwin N. Moore, Palmetto RE Goug Hayman, SW Florida TE Bill Hay, Evangel RE Timothy L. Murr  |
| --- | --- |

TE Allan M. Barth: Metro NY. Pastor of North Shore Community Church of Oyster Bay, NY, and served previously as Minister of Education at Cedar Springs PC in Knoxville, TN. Has served as a church planter. Has been active in Presbytery committee work and, on GA level, has served on the Nominating Committee.

RE Donald Blackburn, Sr.: Central Georgia. Owns his own company. Has served as RE at First PC, Macon, GA, for over 10 years. Served on GA level MTW committee and MTW rep. on the PCA Foundation Board. Has been on several missions trips with local congregation missions committee.

TE Roy P. Carter: Nashville. Senior Associate Pastor at Christ PC in Nashville, TN. Has served on the Ministers Committee on presbytery level; has trained pastors in the former Yugoslavia, participated in evangelistic work in Greece, worked with church planters in Mexico City, has served as Chairman of the Ukraine Partnership, and is currently leading church's efforts to work with pastors and youth leaders in Northern Ireland.

RE Edward Lang: Potomac. Work experience has been in administration and human resources, more recently began working part-time at Chesapeake Seminary where they have developed seminary training programs to assist missionaries in the development of seminaries in other countries. Have supported worldwide missions and missionaries for many years, has seen his children go on short term missions trips. During 1999, served as an advisory member of CMTW.

TE Dwight Linton, North Georgia. Has served as Missionary in Korea for 25 years, Coordinator of Ethnic Church Development, and Consultant on Cross-cultural Ministry for CE&amp;P.

RE Robert Massengill: Grace. Owner of a business consultation firm, former owner of wholesale grocery distribution company, and former bank president. Has actively served on CMTW, been on the Board of Directors for African Bible

<a id="ga28-p599"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=601 printed_page=599 -->

College, served on State Executive Co. and former Chairman of Fellowship of Christian Athletes, and numerous charitable and civic organizations.

TE Carl Bogue: Ascension. Graduate of Muskingum College, Pittsburgh Theological Seminary. Th.D. from Free University of Amsterdam. Ordained by United Presbyterian Church, USA, in Akron, Ohio 1969. Helped organize Ascension Presbytery when forced to leave UPCUSA. Served as pastor for 25 years and on various Presbytery and GA committees, including MTW.

TE Bill Hay: Evangel. Pastor for 34 years. Participates as pastor to MTW church planting team in Chile, and has traveled there for past 3 years. Church he pastors has one of the highest per capita giving records for worldwide missions.

TE Jim Jones: Louisiana. Ordained in 75 as MTW missionary to France. Chaplain for Boy Scouts of America. Stated clerk of presbytery. Th.M and Th.D.

RE Edwin N. Moore: Palmetto. Re at Grace Presbyterian in Aiken, SC. Graduated from Clemson. Served on Presbytery MTW Committee. Engineer at Savannah River Site. Author of *Our Covenant Heritage*.

RE Doug Hayman: Southwest Florida. Purchasing Manager for physical rehabilitation hospital. Under care as Candidate for the ministry. Served on Presbytery committees, clerk of session and interim moderator of session, Youth Director and pulpit committees.

RE Timothy Murr: Grace. Very active elder involved in SS ministry. Practiced law 2 decades and has outstanding reputation.

development participant, new broker coordinator and growth leader,
demonstrating his financial wisdom, skills and abilities. Has served on several
local boards in the community. Is a gentleman farmer in his spare time.
RE Gary D. Campbell: Missouri. Sr Vice-President and Chief Investment Officer for
Commerce Bank’s Investment Management Group Chartered Financial
analyst and member of the St. Louis Financial Analyst’s Society. Member of
i ar class of 2000 and eligible for re-election chairman of iar investment
Committee

for class of 2004

TE A1 Bennett: SE Alabama. pastor of mill brook pc in Millbrook, A. Has served 5
years as Regional Trainer for Florida for CE&P and GCP, has been active in the
ministry of rh a scamp speaker and morning bible study leader. Has served on
CE committee on the Presbytery level.
TE Dan Steere: N. Georgia. pastor of first pc in Summerville, GA. Prior to pastorate,
served as the counseling and assistant director of a christian camp, 6 years as a
college professor, and 14 years in Christian School teaching and administration.
Currently serving on Presbytery’s Shepherding Committee.
RE John B. Haldeman Jr . Heritage Retired from career in customer services in 1992.
Since then has been registrar at Brandywine YMCA Camp. Member of
Presbytery Record Review Team. member of nominating Committee.
moderator of presbytery in 1999.
RE Jim Holcomb: Western Carolina. Retired electrical/civil engineer from Atlanta,
GA. Ruling elder in Smyrna, ga and member of board of directors on
Covenant Christian School, Smyna Ridge Haven resident and a construction
and maintenance voluteer.

## For Alternate

**TE Mark Hartzell, Philadelphia**
**TE Jim Bowen, Southern Florida**

**TE Renato Bernardes:** Metro NY. Pastor of Comunidade Crista Presbiteriana of Newark, NJ – the first Portuguese speaking church to be part of the PCA. Feels it is our duty to continue to work towards transforming our regional church into a truly multi-cultural body of Christ. Has served in secular business as Manager of Operations, extensively involved in establishing new UPS centers and the direct management of people and resources. Originally from Brazil, graduated from Montclair State U [NJ] and Princeton Theological Seminary [MD], ordained in the PCUSA in 1985, and received into the PCA in 1989.

**TE Gary R. Cox:** Central Carolina. Senior pastor of Meadowview PCA in Lexington, NC. Has served as MNA’s first Regional Coordinator for the Southeast; has developed a model for regional “Key Leaders Retreats” and implemented an annual “Church Planters Conference; has served on Presbytery level MNA committee and is currently the Triad Regional Vice Chairman of CCP’s MNA committee. Is intimately acquainted with the issues involved in church planting and has a close working relationship with MNA staff. Has served for the past decade as a PCA Army chaplain and served during Operation Desert Shield/Desert Storm.

**RE Michael L. Wilson:** South Texas. An attorney and has served 6 years as an RE at Bay Area PC in Webster, TX. Has been actively involved in the S. Texas Presbytery – moderator and member of Candidates and Credentials Committee. Appointed to serve on Texas State Board of Public Accountancy, elected twice as member of Galveston Independent School District Board of Trustees and has served as President; has served as President and Director of the League City Industrial Development 4B Corp. Has been very active on a number of public boards and committees. Currently serving as an Alternate on GA level MNA Committee.

**TE Mark Hartzell:** Philadelphia. Director of Harvest USA and is TE and Evangelist for the PCA. Served for over 10 years in the PCA as an RE before becoming ordained as a TE.

**TE Terry Baxley:** Potomac. Pastor of Cornerstone PC, California, MD. Chairman, Potomac Presbytery MNA for most of 90’s. Alternate to MNA this past year.

**TE Jim Bowen:** Southern Florida. Graduate of University of Florida and Reformed Theological Seminary. Organizing pastor of Grace Presbyterian Church, Stuart, Florida where he has served for 25 years. His congregation has oversight responsibilities for 2 mission churches. Served on various committee of presbytery including being moderator.

**TE Jack Graham:** Southwest Florida. Recently retired pastor of Westminster PC in Brandon, Fla. Pastored churches in Presbytery of Central Florida and Western Carolina. Served on various committees of presbytery as well as being Moderator. Served on various GA committees including nominating committee. Church planter in western North Carolina.

<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=604 printed_page=602 -->

church courts Has served 2 terms on SJC before, including during the time the
original Manual for the SJC was drafted. Has served on numerous committees
and commissions on both the Presbytery and GA level.
TE Stephen Arrick: Heritage. graduate of clear water christian college and reformed

RE Bruce Ferg: Southwest. BA and JD from University of Arizona. Army lawyer in Judge Advocate General’s Corps, Attorney General’s office 78-present, chief attorney Tucson unit, Criminal Appeals section. RE Fredrick Greco: Great Lakes. RE and Clerk of Session of Grace PC in Hudson, OH. Corporate attorney with law degree from U. of Mich. And Masters in Classics from U of Chicago.

RE Collie W. Lehn Jr.: Calvary. BA Furman University, MBA University of S.C. RE at Westminster since 82. Served on MNA committees of presbytery and GA. Trustee of Reformed

PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA FOUNDATION, INC.
REPORT TO THE 28th GENERAL ASSEMBLY

the PCA foundation is pleased to report that the lord blessed the work of the
Foundation during 1999. Total assets grew to $22 4 million as of december 31, 1999,
compared to $20.5 million at the end of 1998 and $16.6 million at the end of 1997

the total assets consist of advise & Consult Funds ($5.5 million), Charitable
Remainder Trusts ($8.6 million) and Designated Funds, Endowments, Gift Annuities,
Charitable Lead Trust receivables and miscellaneous assets ($8.3 million). The assets
of the Foundation are primarily “donor driven,” meaning that grants are either
recommended or designated by the donor

During 1999, the PCA Foundation made grants to Christian ministries totaling $3.5
million, an increase of $0 5 million or 16% over 1998. of the $3 .5 million, $1.5 million
was distributed to PCA churches, $1.2 million to PCA Agencies and Committees and
$0.8 million to other Christian ministries These amounts represent an increase in
grants over the previous year of 25% to PCA churches, 36% to the Agencies and
Committees, and a decrease of 14% to other Christian ministries.

The Foundation’s Advise & Consult Fund continues to be very popular. The Fund
ended 1999 with a balance of $5 5 million, compared to $4.4 million at the end of 1998
During 1999 there was $3.8 million in gifts into the Advise & Consult Fund and $2.7
million in distributions from the Advise & Consult Fund to Christian ministry. It was
through the Advise & Consult Fund that our donors played a key role in the recent
retirement of the mortgage o nt hep ca office building in Atlanta.

The Foundation continues to expand its marketing efforts to make its services known to
the PCA community, and to other christians a swell the purpose of the foundation is
to provide services to help Christians fulfill their stewardship responsibilities, and to
find God’s plan of stewardship for their estates. We also offer our services to PCA
Agencies and Committees, and to PCA churches.

The services the Foundation offers include Advise & Consult Funds, Charitable Trusts,
Designated Funds for churches and individuals, Endowments, Recommended
Endowments, Family Endowments, Bequest processing and Estate Design.

Designated Funds for churches is a relatively new program, and we are hopeful that a
number of our churches will participate. By setting up a Designated Fund with the
Foundation, a church specifies the intended use of the Fund, and controls the

<a id="ga28-p608"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=610 printed_page=608 -->

distributions The Foundation invests and administrates the Fund for the church, and
can process various types of gifts, including appreciated assets.

The Foundation will continue to strive to effectively meet the needs of its present and
future donors, as well as the PCA, its churches, and the Agencies and Committees.

By God’s grace, the result of our work will be a continual increase in the assets of the
PCA Foundation, which, in time, will mean more funds available for PCA ministries,
and for other Christian ministries as well.

This year is the first year that the Foundation is on a self-supporting basis. This is a
significant step forward, especially considering that as recently as 1996, approximately
one half of our support was from the other Agencies and Committees. Please continue
to pray for us in this regard, as we are now dependent upon fee income, earnings on
some of our funds, and contributions for our support.

we ask that you continue to pray for the board and staff of the foundation as they seek
to continue to lead the Foundation successfully into the future

Our recommendation is:

1. That the General Assembly approve our proposed 2001 budget as submitted.

We at the Foundation thank you for the opportunity to serve the PCA.

Respectfully Submitted,

Randel N. Stair
President

RIDGE HAVEN REPORT TO THE TWENTY-EIGHTH
GENERAL ASSEMBLY
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

Ridge Haven exists as a camps, conference, retreat center, and residential community
to proclaim the gospel to the lost, nurture God’s people in the truth of God’s word,
and provide a place of rest, refuge, and renewal so that God is glorified and God’s
people are challenged and equipped for service and ministry in His kingdom - The
Ridge Haven Purpose

Many do not know. . Many continue to ask. . What is Ridge Haven?
The Ridge Haven Tree provides the answer!

Ridge Haven is ..
a unique residential comm unity...
Awaiting the opportunity to serve you
Accessible, available, and affordable .
Host to almost 8,000 people per year...
Faithful and sound preaching and teaching .
one of the best investments and bargains in the PCA
A great home base for visiting the Blue Ridge Parkway ..
Swimming, singing, laughing, rocking, and star-gazing.
Summer and winter camps and year around conferences ..
A place where God has been pleased to dwell and bless His people .
surrounded by a multitude of opportunities in the pis g ah national forest .
Great food, warm fellowship, godly worship, and wholesome recreation..
Hiking trails, waterfalls, ropes courses, basketball, volleyball, and tennis.
A place for church, youth, college, WIC, men’s children and family groups ..
Comfortable and modern apartments, motel-style rooms, lodges and dorms...
a beautiful and quiet place in the mountains of western north Carolina...
the camps and conference center of the Presbyterian Church in America. .
More than you can imagine and better than you remember...

♦
I
Ridge Haven conferences for 2000-2001:
YoWAW (Youth World Awareness Weekend) - An annual CE/P, MNA,
MTW/Impact, Ridge Haven co-sponsored missions conference for
highschoolers.

<a id="ga28-p611"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=613 printed_page=611 -->

Love Life Marriage Seminar - A romantic weekend opportunity for couples to
receive biblically based teaching on the acclaimed Love Life book
written by dr ed wheat
Covenant Family Conference - Covenant Seminary and Ridge Haven co
sponsored vacation conference for families and individuals
Great American Family Campout - The PCA's premier tent camping event for
families and individuals
Keenager Conference - The PCA's senior adult Bible Conference for a unique
opportunity for age appropriate instruction in the Word, fellowship and
worship.
Year-end Blast - A Junior High -Senior High ski retreat between Christmas and
New Years.

♦ Ridge Haven summer camps:

Five JFJ camps for Juniors (students entering third - sixth grades)
Four Jr HiX camps for Junior Highs (students entering sixth - ninth grades)
A Great Adventure Week for Junior Highs
An RH+ camp for Senior Highs (students entering ninth - twelfth grades)
A Great Adventure Week for Senior Highs
A Senior High Missions Challenge (New)

♦ Thank you:

as the camps and conference center of the Presbyterian Church in America, we
are grateful for the help from all of the churches, groups, and individuals that have
shared with us and supported us this past year

♦ Challenges and opportunities:

The challenges and opportunities for the present year and 2001 are continuing to
increase. Present activities include completing the major water system upgrade
begun earlier this year and moving toward completion of anew engineering site
development plan detailing all that will be needed to bring Ridge Haven Southeast
to completion. To accomplish our goals we ask:

1. prayers for the successful completion of the water system upgrades, the financial
resources and wisdom for securing the site development plan, the Lord to continue
using Ridge Haven camps, conferences, and other ministries to bring many to
saving faith in the lord jesus christ and many to new levels of maturity,
commitment, ministry, and service, and that God would continue to sustain the
staff at Ridge Haven with wisdom, and strengthen, and purpose.

2. Cooperation and support in being sure that everyone in your church knows about
Ridge Haven, in communicating Ridge Haven information to your friends and

<a id="ga28-p612"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=28 pdf_page=614 printed_page=612 -->

others, and in inviting, attending, and sending these to attend Ridge Haven camps,
conferences, and other ministries

3. Help in providing for Ridge Haven’s needs by participating at the partnership
share level of $3.08 per member for the present year and raising the partnership
share level of church responses from 23% to more than 50% Sola Deo Gloria

Ridge Haven Recommendations to the Twenty-Eighth General Assembly:

1 That the 2000 budget be approved as submitted through the Administrative
Committee.

2 That the Assembly set the first Sunday in February as Ridge Haven Sunday.
